```
that one by the time you got to it.

BTW, I actually worked all day today in a GUI interface
written in Fujitsu Cobol ... working on the GUI itself.  It is
starting to be actually faster than working in their own
system.  Interesting trying to bootstrap a system written
in itself<g>.

Maybe they should try the same thing.  Their developers
would clean up a lot of the little stuff if they had to actually
code in the language they were developing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Ask Error in MS COBOL

- **From:** "Juni Hadi" <jnh@indosat.co.id>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdf18e$2dce8080$1d5b6464@jnh.indosat.co.id>`
- **References:** `<361289EF.EE36355D@erols.com> <6uukkv$g4j$1@news.igs.net>`

```

When I was running cobol Programming, I've this Error :
"Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory"...
Error Code : 114
But I've already succeeded compiling that program.

So....Is there anybody tell me what should I do ?
Thank you


Jnh
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ask Error in MS COBOL

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<000229da@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<01bdf18e$2dce8080$1d5b6464@jnh.indosat.co.id>`

```
Dear Juni,

JH> When I was running cobol Programming, I've this Error :
JH> "Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory"...
JH> Error Code : 114

This error code reports a general protection fault as caused for example  
by invalid pointers.

Do you get this error during compilation or at runtime? If the compiler  
fails with this error, check your DATA DIVISION for redefinitions on items  
that are not the same size.

If it's an runtime error of your program your runtime system or your  
program may be corrupted. If it's not, it's quite hard to find the  
problem. Does it occur during animation also? Do you excessively use  
LINKAGE SECTION items?

Best regards


Sven
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ask Error in MS COBOL

- **From:** Haluk Okur <spbis@sbs.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361B997B.3E52@sbs.de>`
- **References:** `<361289EF.EE36355D@erols.com> <6uukkv$g4j$1@news.igs.net> <01bdf18e$2dce8080$1d5b6464@jnh.indosat.co.id>`

```
Juni Hadi wrote:
>
> When I was running cobol Programming, I've this Error :
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jnh

I'm not sure about the error code, but the error message makes me
think that you are trying to access an array member with a subscript
too big for the OCCURS-clause - maybe not initialized at all.

This kind of errors (runtime errors) can't be detected by the
compiler so the program would compile OK as in your case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ask Error in MS COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361B8172.18F8D476@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<361289EF.EE36355D@erols.com> <6uukkv$g4j$1@news.igs.net> <01bdf18e$2dce8080$1d5b6464@jnh.indosat.co.id> <361B997B.3E52@sbs.de>`

```
Haluk Okur wrote:
> 
> Juni Hadi wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> too big for the OCCURS-clause - maybe not initialized at all.

Try using the "BOUND" compiler directive to see if this is the problem -
the compiler will insert extra code which checks the subscript before
accessing the array. This extra code will cause a "Subscript out of
range" error instead.

Alternatively ... what happens when you debug the program ?

Cheers, Kev.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ask Error in MS COBOL

- **From:** "kennet" <kennet@post11.tele.dk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vi4j3$21as$1@news-inn.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<361289EF.EE36355D@erols.com> <6uukkv$g4j$1@news.igs.net> <01bdf18e$2dce8080$1d5b6464@jnh.indosat.co.id>`

```
Are you using Micro Focus Cobol on a PC? If so: I used to get this error all
the time but when I upgraded my PC with more RAM the "error" went away. So
maybe your program is working OK, but takes up to much RAM.

Kennet

Juni Hadi skrev i meddelelsen
```

#### ↳ Re: Test your COBOL II knowledge at new website!

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36143bfc.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<361289EF.EE36355D@erols.com>`

```
I liked the test.

I noticed that some of the questions appeared difficult... but then all but one answer contained glaring syntax errors.  I think this is a great way to test someone's experience.  More experience programmers will take a very broad look at the problem and eliminate any obvious causes right off the bat.  A less experienced programmer will focus in on the details immediately.

I also liked the fact that you didn't get hung up on "text bookish" questions.  Since most code isn't written from scratch, there's little need to memorize what section goes under what division etc etc.

By the way, there is no top 10 for COBOL... what gives?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Test your COBOL II knowledge at new website!

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36169E39.1127@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<361289EF.EE36355D@erols.com> <36143bfc.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
rmch wrote:
> By the way, there is no top 10 for COBOL... what gives?

I scored 4.46 on the first test and 4.51 on the second, but it was about
midnite.  Several of the same questions are on both tests, perhaps for
corroberation (sp?).  It was not a bad test, but I don't know how
response time would be during normal business hours when employers might
want people to take the test.  There are several dial-in services
(non-internet) offering testing in many mainframe subjects and PC areas
as well.  I'm not sure what value the internet adds.

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: Editor PLEASE

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3613e6e8.0@glitch.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6uur63$ngo$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald

Like everone else, we think our Cobol editor is best - shipped with Casegen.

Its a full 32 bit Windows editor, has Cobol syntax highlighting, displays in
RED if you are outside Field A or B, has search and change capability, full
cut/copy/paste, works with "huge" files, allows you to change the case of a
line or block of lines or string and comment/uncomment lines and blocks.
Also its very fast, not overladen with unnecessary features.

It also has unlimited undo/redo capability and you can even change the
colours!

Like all software it takes time to appreciate.

However, I am afraid it is not free, its $50 US including the complete
"Casegen COBOL for Windows" product.  However, I could be pursuaded to email
you a copy of the editor.

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd










Donald Tees wrote in message <6uur63$ngo$1@news.igs.net>...
>The editor on the Fujitsu Cobol Version 4 is driving me nuts.
>It is completely unworkable on any program over two pages
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Editor PLEASE

- **From:** Karl Wagner <kwagner@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3612FBDC.94AA7015@sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<6uur63$ngo$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I'm partial to kedit (http://www.kedit.com). It uses a REXX-based macro
language. Visual SlickEdit is also a fine editor with a C-like macro language
including a GUI painter for customizing the editor (http://www.microedge.com).
Both have language-sensitive editing features including colorization,
auto-typing (type 'If' and get the matching 'END-IF'. The macro languages of
both allow for complete customization of the editor so it can become and IDE and
program launcher.

For the ultimate 'free' editor you could try GNU Emacs from
(http://www.cs.washington.edu/homes/voelker/ntemacs.html). It's a little too
scary for my blood but others swear by it (or was that 'swear at it').
```

```
When they were giving the CDs away for free, and not even 
charging for postage they have a quite valid reason to not
supply a full set of printed manuals.

With Fujitsu version 4 manual sets are available for around
$US 180.  As the software CD Enterprise version is around
this price, again, the manuals being given away for free
with the CD would mean operating at a loss.

Buy the manulas, buy the software.  If you can put up with
screen based doscuments then save some money.

I bought manuals for Fujitsu version 3 and 4, they are 
adequate but rather sparsely printed.  I would prefer a
much denser print and finer paper - but then it may only
be the size of a couple of MF manuals.  The information
could be improved.
```

<a id="t-0080219aa8"></a>
## Thread: How to open a file with Fujitsu COBOL v3 / Win NT 4 ? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to open a file with Fujitsu COBOL v3 / Win NT 4 ?

- **From:** "Luc Mertes" <luc.mertes@ibsr.be>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6uvorr$ahs$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```
I have tried to download the manuals of Fujitsu COBOL at this URL
http://www.adtools.com/products/downloadCOBV3.htm
But my explorer (MS) reports an error and stop.

My question is : where (and how) to give the complete name of a file I want
to open (such as "c:/my documents/data.001") in my source file ?

Thanks to all of you

Luc
```

#### ↳ Re: How to open a file with Fujitsu COBOL v3 / Win NT 4 ?

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3614094C.C75DB5D7@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<6uvorr$ahs$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```
ooh!  i know! i know!  finally a question i can handle!
in your ENVIRONMENT DIVISION, INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION, FILE-CONTROL:
SELECT MY-FILE
    ASSIGN TO "c:\my documents\data.001".
you can also name files from your working-storage section should your
program have to create and use files based on user input.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to open a file with Fujitsu COBOL v3 / Win NT 4 ?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<6v151v$872$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6uvorr$ahs$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net> <3614094C.C75DB5D7@mindspring.com>`

```

;<)
```

#### ↳ Re: How to open a file with Fujitsu COBOL v3 / Win NT 4 ?

- **From:** skyraven@home.com (Sue L)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3614af85.1456491@news>`
- **References:** `<6uvorr$ahs$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```
After being deep in thought on Thu, 1 Oct 1998 13:26:37 +0200,, I
noticed "Luc Mertes" <luc.mertes@ibsr.be> wrote:
>I have tried to download the manuals of Fujitsu COBOL at this URL
>http://www.adtools.com/products/downloadCOBV3.htm
>But my explorer (MS) reports an error and stop.
>My question is : where (and how) to give the complete name of a file I want
>to open (such as "c:/my documents/data.001") in my source file ?

done in Adobe Acrobat so you'll need to download it in order to read
them. Here's the web site that you can down load it from..
http://www.adobe.com/prodindex/acrobat/readstep.html

Hope this helps.

Sue
```

## Thread: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files? — 3 this month / 25 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-a0c3264b40)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** as999@torfree.net (Adrian Boldan)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F068zE.Lsn.0.bloor@torfree.net>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-emzwlvQYJNNo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36102863.A861F912@mindspring.com>`

```
skidmike (skidmike@mindspring.com) wrote:
: speaking of which, can i have an indexed file used by more than one
: program and depending on how each respective program uses it, define
: access as sequential in one program and dynamic in another?

Why not? If you code for the DD name DISP=SHR.
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** "Walter Levine" <walterlevine@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v4sdd$g1a@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>`

```
You detect END-OF-FILE from the return of the "FILE STATUS". It is a PIC XX,
and returns "10" when EOF occurs.
I usually do it as follows:
SELECT ...
    FILE STATUS IS CUST-FILE-STATUS
.
.
.
01 CUST-FILE-STATUS            PIC XX.
    88 END-OF-CUSTFILE         VALUE IS "10"
.
.
.
OPEN INPUT CUS-TFILE
READ CUST-FILE NEXT RECORD.
PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-CUSTFILE
    <PROCESS RECORD>
    ...
    READ CUST-FILE NEXT RECORD
END-PERFORM.

FOR ME, THIS IS THE MOST EFFICIENT AND ELEGANT WAY TO DO IT

Don Tarshes wrote in message <360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com>...
>Does anyone know the correct way to test for the End of File condition
>in indexed files? My compiler (MFCOBOL) objects to this way:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>Don
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Testing for End of File condition of indexed files?

- **From:** rbolin <rbolin-nospam@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361B0F1C.6E7D81D7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<360D6B72.7CF2@popd.ix.netcom.com> <6v4sdd$g1a@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```
The original code shown that was used with a sequential file will work for an
indexed file with a slight modification - change the 'AT END' clause to
'INVALID':

 MOVE SPACE TO FILE-FLAG.
 PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
      READ MY-FILE NEXT
            INVALID          MOVE "E" TO FILE-FLAG
            NOT INVALID PERFORM DISPLAY-FILE
     END-READ
 END-PERFORM.


Walter Levine wrote:

> You detect END-OF-FILE from the return of the "FILE STATUS". It is a PIC XX,
> and returns "10" when EOF occurs.
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Don
```

<a id="t-0d957696c2"></a>
## Thread: Multi Threading within MicroFocus Cobol — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-0d957696c2)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Threading within MicroFocus Cobol

- **From:** "Leigh Hebblethwaite" <leigh@hebblethwaite.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v0quf$3el$1@newsreader1.core.theplanet.net>`
- **References:** `<6uso69$jh7$1@news01.iafrica.com> <3611F35E.5AE8C28C@mfltd.co.uk>`

```

Kevin Digweed wrote in message <3611F35E.5AE8C28C@mfltd.co.uk>...
>Graeme Conn wrote:
>>
>> I require a method to start another thread while the current thread is
still
>> executing. I need to simulate a CICS START TRANSACTION in MicroFocus
Cobol
>> environment running under NT .
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Kev.

I believe the HP/UX version of MF COBOL has thread support courtesy of HP.

Standard MF COBOL has a thing called 'Concurrency Support'. It's documented
in Chapter 14 of the 'Programmer's Guide to Writing Programs' manual (at
least in my copy). It's a sort of multi-threading implemented by the RTS.
It's not very powerful but it may be what you're after.
```

#### ↳ Re: Multi Threading within MicroFocus Cobol

- **From:** "Dick McMullen" <dmcmulle@exicorp.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3614e453.0@207.226.241.101>`
- **References:** `<6uso69$jh7$1@news01.iafrica.com>`

```
Graeme,

I know of at least two options:

1)  Look at the "CBL_" multi-threading functions, which are documented in
the HELP system.  Using these functions you can start threads, monitor them,
kill them, etc.

2)  Use the x"91" / 35 CALL to launch a new NT process.  This is the
functional equivalent of opening a new application.  I am using this
successfully to run batch processes via command files and other functions.

If you want some code snippets send me a note off-list.

Dick McMullen


Graeme Conn wrote in message <6uso69$jh7$1@news01.iafrica.com>...
>I require a method to start another thread while the current thread is
still
>executing. I need to simulate a CICS START TRANSACTION in MicroFocus Cobol
>environment running under NT .
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Message-ID:** `<36142170.B4B2FFC5@mindspring.com>`

```
with a sore back and the remains of a six-pack in the fridge, here's the
type of "bug" we mechanics face so you don't feel so bad about missing a
period at the wrong place.
the vehicle:  87 volvo 240.  (off to a bad start already)
customer's request:  front struts (just got worse)

9 am  pulled the volvo into my bay and removed the front wheels.
      because the spindles, hubs, and rotors are made into the
strut          assembly, they have to come off together.  makes for
about 80 lbs.
9:05 am  because of the brake hose/steel line design, the caliper has
to          come off with the strut.  (add 5 or 6 pounds and bleeding
the           brakes after the job--not easy on a volvo)
9:06 am  one bleeder screw on each front caliper is broken off. 
the             thought of prayer comes up.
9:40 am  the left strut assembly is off the car, in the
spring                   compresser (one mistake CAN be fatal) and i am
waiting for the          new strut cartridges to arrive.
10:00 am  the new cartridges arrive and the left strut is
re-assembled            and re-installed on the car.  (back pain begins)
10:18 am  right side strut removed, dis-assembled, new
cartridge                  installed and put back on the car.
10:41 am  stripped threads found on the left outer tie rod end. 
thread           repair efforts to no avail.  ordered new tie rod end.
11:00 am  idiot parts man sends INNER tie rod end instead of what
i               ordered.  foul language ensues (could be heard in next
county)
11:28 am  correct part arrives and the car is put back together
and               the brakes bleed ok.  (thank god!)
11:45 am  test drive yields horrendous noise from passenger side
strut,           suspect cap nut.
12:15 am  right front strut removed, dis-assembled, inspected
and                 re-installed.  no problems found.
12:42 am  second test drive results in same noise
12:24 am - 5:00 pm  removal, adding and removal of shims and
washers,              and reinstallation finally reveals the POSSIBILITY
of a                 defective strut cartridge.
6:00 pm - i go home with that volvo STILL on the rack waiting for a
new           strut cartridge to arrive tomorrow so i can begin the
process           anew.
my back is killing me.  my hands hurt like hell.  and one question rings
in my mind.  would it have been so bad to have made a third atom bomb in
the 40's and dropped it on Sweden?
```

```
Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>>kennet <kennet@post11.tele.dk> wrote:

Oh, I ain't much on book-larnin', really... I jes' read it oncet on th'
back of a cereal-box.

... well, no, not really... I was trained as a Classical Western Scholar,
exposed to the Trivium and the Quadrivium, and a goodly chunk o' Western
Civ rests on Bible-teachings.  I'm sufficiently familiar with Hebrew and
Ancient Greek so that I can confound folks with my own misinterpretations.

> You refer to an incident
>when Abraham, grandfather of Jacob (Israel) the father of the Jews, had
>the *brass* to haggle with God Himself.

Ain't is a lovely bit, though?  A fundamental difference in the conception
of a Deity, far removed from The Big Bearded Guy On A Chair In The Sky, a
Deity which not only talks to men (common enough) but can bargain with
them... just *stunning*.

(another one of my favorites is the description of Abraham's purchase of
the Cave of Machpelah for Sarah's burial... the lessons from that
reverberate, loudly and livingly, across the millenia)

> No wonder his descendants have
>been so successful in haggling with the rest of us humans. :-)

Well, it makes for a... curious role-model, granted... but that talent is
not unique; it is the way most business is done in the Middle East.

DD
```

```
reverberated through history as very few other events.  Blood is probably

escalate into a nuclear confrontation.  The family feud to end all family
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing Procedures

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361626EB.1FA84826@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<6uvqun$k1$1@hermes.is.co.za>`

```
comp.software.testing?

FAQ:   (http://www.faqs.org/faqs/software-eng/testing-faq/).

1.    <http://www.soft.com/Institute/HotList>

2. The military standard is now the IEEE/EIA 12207 "Information
Technology-Software Life Cycle Process" and is packaged in three parts.

For additional information, check the following URL at 
http://www.stsc.hill.af.mil/doc/mil-std-498-cancel.html


3. HTTP://www.acomtech.com
They have a few test plan resources.

4.  http://members.tripod.com/~bazman/testplancontents.html

Get "Testing Computer Software" by Cem Kaner et al.  Very easy to read
and
understand and a definite read for newbies.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol & C/C++

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v21kj$fbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6v0m8i$hqf$1@news3.mr.net>`

```
In article <6v0m8i$hqf$1@news3.mr.net>,
  "A. Maxon" <amaxon@wrbc.com> wrote:
> I need to make Cobol calls from C/C++. I would appreciate any ideas or
> comments.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Interlanguage communication between C/C++ and COBOL depends very much on the
dialects of both languages, the version of the compilers and the operating
system you have to use. There are absolutely no general rules, these can vary
from 'no problem' to 'no chance, will not work'.

Post more details please, so you probably will get more usefull answers!

Cheers

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
In article <c9WP1.2801$Ge.9194301@ptah.visi.com>,
Peter Seebach <seebs@plethora.net> wrote:
>
>Imagine.  It's 1985.  You've got a spec for a 'date entry' device, and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>for the special date.

I would have picked 11/11/11, which wouldn't become a problem until
my 35th birthday, and wouldn't fudge ever if we implemented four-digit
years and rendered the 11s as either 0011 or 1111.

Saving space is one thing, but I hope that Prince song would have
brought enough attention to the fact that we won't be dead in 1999.

...and by the way, I wonder if there are any programs which fudged
on August 8, 1988?  Or July 7, 1977?  And if you can give me a
documented example of a date-related fudge revolving around June
6, 1966, I will move to Montana and live in a shack made entirely
from Lincoln logs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981004184506.05213.00011247@ngol04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6uv5o8$e5s$1@nnrp03.primenet.com>`

```

In article <6uv5o8$e5s$1@nnrp03.primenet.com>, nickb@primenet.com (Nick S
Bensema) writes:

>...and by the way, I wonder if there are any programs which fudged
>on August 8, 1988?  Or July 7, 1977?  And if you can give me a
>documented example of a date-related fudge revolving around June
>6, 1966, I will move to Montana and live in a shack made entirely
>from Lincoln logs.

In the drop date field (the field that indicates when a student dropped a
class), we had two "magic" values:
6/6/66 was used in our shop for cancelled classes (cancelled before the first
day of that class).
7/7/77 was used for terminated classes (cancelled after the class had met at
least once).

We have since added a flag field to indicate Dropped, Terminated, or Cancelled.

In the birth date field, we had one magic value:
8/8/88 was used to indicate no birth date given.

We have since expanded birth date field to 4 digits and now use spaces for "no
birth date supplied."

Why were magic dates used in the first place? Because for a while we used a
forth-generation package that gave us poor choices on fields: treat them as
alphanumeric (anything goes), or treat them as numeric (digits only).

We have since found other fields (not dates) that have been put in to use for
other purposes by the users without input from our department. The worst case
was about 8 years ago when one department decided to use the debt switches in
student records for non-debt purposes and then complained to us about students
not being able to register. 8(

Mark A. Young
```

```
Bill Lynch (wblynch@att.net) wrote:
: Dennis J. Minette wrote:
: > 
: (snip)
: > >The date routine I replaced last year did exactly that, plus it rejected
: > >out of hand any date after 1999, and was hard coded to reject any
: > >century as a non-leap year, plus, it was written in Assembler (and
: > >poorly, too). I suspect 99/09/09 was used for an open settlement date (I
: > >wasn't there when it was dreamed up) for two reasons: it's a valid date
: > >(which was a requirement), and it was sufficiently far in the future so
: > >no one, programmer or manager, dreamed it would be a problem (ha!).
: > >
: > >Bill lynch
: > 
: > I remember running across old securities industry COBOL programs circa 1970
: > that had PERP coded in the maturity date field

: I must be watching too many cop/lawyer shows! My first thought when I
: saw "PERP" was "perpetrator". 'Guess their software didn't insist on
: numerics in date fields?

: Bill Lynch :-)

Wouldn't the perp(etrator) in this case be the pgmr who causes the S0C7 by
not checking for "PERP"/non-numeric first when she/he uses this field?  And
in so doing, betraying the fact that not only are they not paying attention,
they're also apparently not testing very thoroughly, if at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Dennis J. Minette" <dennis_minette@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uZzCqCc79GA.53@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`
- **References:** `<6u9o9k$hkn$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <6uk8nq$l7s_002@p19-term2-and.netdirect.net> <360f9f78.0@122.122.122.1> <6up58i$ovg_005@p4-term3-and.netdirect.net> <FOVP1.2789$Ge.9177215@ptah.visi.com> <6up6r2$ovg_009@p4-term3-and.netdirect.net> <3611C7E6.ED43E1BE@att.net> <uOZ14pO79GA.196@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net> <361348ED.1C183821@att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch wrote in message <361348ED.1C183821@att.net>...
>Dennis J. Minette wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I remember running across old securities industry COBOL programs circa
1970
>> that had PERP coded in the maturity date field
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch :-)

I believe that virtually all references to the MATURITY-DATE-YYMM field in
question were for inequality as in

if maturity-date-yymm not =  todays-yymm
    don't bother with it
else
    prepare to mature the bond.

Both date fields were group items over a pair of subordinate PIC 99's (YY
and MM.)

And I think yield to maturity calcs were done only if MATURITY-DATE was
numeric.

The security master file update edited for, and allowed, input of "PERP"
(for perpetual bonds) in the MATURITY-DATE group item and otherwise edited
the MM part for month values ranging from 1 through 12.  As I recall, the YY
year value part of the data element was edited only for numeric so the
maturation process would presumably not have been affected by Y2K if the
code had managed to survive that long (which it didn't - it was totally
rewritten in the early '80's.)

I believe the programs  involved were originally developed by IBM's Service
Bureau Corp in the early  '60's.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parts of a program that break in Y2K?

- **From:** Mathias Koerber <mathias@singapura.singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year2000,alt.folklore.computers
- **Message-ID:** `<6uvhed$jjj$3@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`
- **References:** `<6u9o9k$hkn$1@nnrp02.primenet.com> <360952C1.6B4C@arrow.com>`

```
In alt.folklore.computers Manzari <pmanzari@arrow.com> wrote:
: Nick S Bensema wrote:
:  

: You've identified several Y2K related problems.  

: Now, here are 10,000 Cobol programs.
: Step 1) Identify all date related code within those programs
: Step 2) Fix the programs
: Step 3) Test all of the programs... 

: Ready... Go... 

And then go throuh all the stored data in your archives and fix it, so that
the newly fixed programs can read the old data when it needs to.
How about data  you shipped to external parties long time ago, and may
need to read again in future? Will they fix the data-tape or expect you
to be able to read the old tapes and apply any fixes on the fly?

Then make sure that all your interfaces to(easy) and from(hard) external
systems are Y2K fixed. Make sure everyone has used the *same* solutions
you have, or data can still be corrupted. These things come up often
when dealing with banks, insurences, statutory boards, regulators etc.



: What? you have no documentation to tell you how to test the program?...
: Try to find someone who does... What?, the person who knew that part of
: the system retired ten years ago? Oh well ...What's that you say?...
: There are about 200 programs with no source code?...Some of the load
: module create dates do not match the latest version of the source
: code?... I guess that the source code that you have is not the latest
: production version... Let's hope there are no Y2K issues in those
: programs...Did you say that several dozen of your 20 year old production
: source modules no longer compile cleanly because some copybooks have
: disappeared over the decades? ... Did you remember the 150 VSAM files
: that use a 6 digit YYMMDD date as part of the key?... No?  Well, the
: deadline isn't going to change.

: Suddenly the simple solution no longer looks so simple.  Here's an
: example... pull your car out of the garage, polish it, then put it
: back.  Very simple.  Now, do the same to 10,000 cars while they're
: driving on the highway.
```

<a id="t-d900d4f3c1"></a>
## Thread: <MF Cobol, SQL Server v6.5, Error Code 10005 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### <MF Cobol, SQL Server v6.5, Error Code 10005

- **From:** "Tony & Kian Heffner" <heffner@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bded9d$b72beec0$956256d1@moon>`

```
Hello All,

Anybody had SQL error 10005 using MF Cobol 3.4.23 and Embedded SQL Toolkit
2.1?  From my research, this is occuring after 5 connections.  The
application I am working on employs the use of browse cursors, each of
which create a separate connection to the database.

On the server, I checked the obvious things like user connections in the
SQL Database configuration and SQL Server licensing under Licensing in the
control panel.

I've already placed a call with MicroFocus.  They came back saying
something to the effect that I have built the modules in either Shared mode
running in Exclusive mode or vice-versa.

Regards,

Tony Heffner
```

#### ↳ Re: <MF Cobol, SQL Server v6.5, Error Code 10005

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3618cb66.10454342@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<01bded9d$b72beec0$956256d1@moon>`

```
On 1 Oct 1998 23:48:49 GMT, "Tony & Kian Heffner"
<heffner@mindspring.com> wrote:

>
>On the server, I checked the obvious things like user connections in the
>SQL Database configuration and SQL Server licensing under Licensing in the
>control panel.

Have you checked the NT event log to see if you indeed are running out
of licenses? 5 connections sounds suspiciously like you're running
out.

You can increase the number of licences through the license manager -
you should of course have bought another licence pack but the licence
manager works on 'trust' rather than actual physical evidence that you
have bought another 5 licences.

Try increasing it, if that solves the problem either look at your code
again and/or buy some more licences.
```

<a id="t-e7b53c0905"></a>
## Thread: strange action of CALLed programs — 8 this month / 10 total

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-e7b53c0905)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36136264.955D54A7@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com> <6uuq7i$m03$1@news.igs.net>`

```
key-code is initialized in W-S with value zero.  remember key-code and
key-type are fields of TERM-KEY which is CRT STATUS.  in fujitsu v3 i
didn't have to move zeroes to them in the procedure division, just init
with VALUE ZERO in the W-S section.  in v4, i have to MOVE ZERO to them
in the procedure division or suffer annoying results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v00kt$pim$1@client2.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com> <6uuq7i$m03$1@news.igs.net> <36136264.955D54A7@mindspring.com>`

```
skidmike wrote in message <36136264.955D54A7@mindspring.com>...
>key-code is initialized in W-S with value zero.  remember key-code and
>key-type are fields of TERM-KEY which is CRT STATUS.  in fujitsu v3 i
>didn't have to move zeroes to them in the procedure division, just init
>with VALUE ZERO in the W-S section.  in v4, i have to MOVE ZERO to them
>in the procedure division or suffer annoying results.

From your description, it would seem that version 3 was not conforming to
the ANSI standard.  A CALLed program is to be in its last state (e.g.
working-storage variables unchanged) unless:

1.  The CALLed program contains the INITIAL clause in the PROGRAM-ID
paragraph, or

2.  The CALLed program was CANCELed.

Perhaps the version 3 behavior was changed to conform to the standard in
version 4.  If Fujitsu provides any "what's new" document, this might
(should) be listed, since, as you have determined, it does affect program
behavior.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn717dom.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com> <6uuq7i$m03$1@news.igs.net> <36136264.955D54A7@mindspring.com>`

```
On Thu, 01 Oct 1998 07:07:16 -0400, skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> wrote:
>key-code is initialized in W-S with value zero.  remember key-code and
>key-type are fields of TERM-KEY which is CRT STATUS.  in fujitsu v3 i didn't
>have to move zeroes to them in the procedure division, just init with VALUE
>ZERO in the W-S section.  in v4, i have to MOVE ZERO to them in the procedure
>division or suffer annoying results.

You might want to start using the INITIALIZE verb religiously. The nice about
INITIALIZE is that if you have an item like this:

01 My-Record.
  03 My-PicX    PIC X(256).
  03 My-Integer PIC 9(4).
  03 My-Counter PIC 9(9) COMP.
  03 My-Number  PIC S9(5)V9999.

A single INITIALIZE My-Record will correctly set all of the numbers to zero and
alpha* to spaces. Additionally, there's some options that allow you to
replace different types of data with arbitrary values:

INITIALIZE My-Record,
  REPLACING NUMERIC DATA BY 3141592
            ALPHABETIC DATA BY "ABCD"
            ALPHANUMERIC-EDITED DATA BY "You get the idea".



----
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

#### ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-R6odyGdhskdO@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com>`

```
On Wed, 30 Sep 1998 23:05:38, skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> 
wrote:

> ok, i moved all of my source over to fujitsu 4 and compiled the
> project.  the main program when [f2] is pressed calls the "create
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> this?

Probalbly the way the called programs are initialized - put a "VALUE" 
clause in the Working-Storage item and use IS INITIAL after the 
program name on the PROGRAM ID line of the called program.  That will 
properly initialize any Working-Storage entries when the call is made.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361408C2.4589693A@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-R6odyGdhskdO@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  CRTEST01 IS INITIAL.  ?

I've never seen the "IS INITIAL" clause in the program id line in the
books i have.  i stopped the strange behavior by adding the move zero
command, but i'll go back and use the is initial.  thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v2hiv$7g9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-R6odyGdhskdO@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <361408C2.4589693A@mindspring.com>`

```

skidmike wrote in message <361408C2.4589693A@mindspring.com>...
>IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
>PROGRAM-ID.  CRTEST01 IS INITIAL.  ?
>
>I've never seen the "IS INITIAL" clause in the program id line in the


The "is initial" clause forces the data division to be reset to the
initial state each time it is called.  It is the same as if you did
a "cancel" after each call.  If you do not use it (or cancel), then working
storage value will hold their value from one call to the next.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-lhPLkdDo5PKV@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3612B941.F1E94A93@mindspring.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-R6odyGdhskdO@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <361408C2.4589693A@mindspring.com>`

```
On Thu, 1 Oct 1998 22:57:06, skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> wrote:

> IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
> PROGRAM-ID.  CRTEST01 IS INITIAL.  ?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> command, but i'll go back and use the is initial.  thanks.

It's ANSI 85 COBOL standard  - older books may not have it.  You have 
it coded correctly in the above example.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strange action of CALLed programs

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298274.69596.6332@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-R6odyGdhskdO@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
> 
> > ok, i moved all of my source over to fujitsu 4 and compiled the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > this?

This is perfectly normal behaviour for a called program that
is called twice without an intervening CANCEL (or one that
works).  When a called routine is first called it is loaded
into memory and the WS areas have the values specified by
the VALUE clauses, where these don't exist the memory is
initialised to some arbitrary value.

When the program exits it still remains resident, a 
subsequent call does _not_ cause the ws values to be
reset, they are left as they were on exit.  This is a
good thing as it allows the routine to remain active for
being called to do a variety of tasks.

To reset the memory of the called routine you need to
CANCEL it so it disappears from memory, then the next CALL
will reload it.

If you do have a CANCEL then it is not working.
```

```
Through the Looking-Glass, chapter VI (with italics in the original
rendered by bracketing asterisks):

	"But 'glory' doesn't mean 'a nice knock-down argument," Alice
	objected.

	"When *I* use a word," Humpty Dumpty said, in rather a scornful
	tone, "it means just what I choose it to mean--neither more
	nor less."

	"The question is," said Alice, "whether you *can* make words
	mean so many different things."

	"The question is," said Humpty Dumpty, "which is to be master--
	that's all."
```

```
riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston) wrote:

>In message <<6utd6u$pkg$1@clarknet.clark.net>> docdwarf@clark.net  writes:
>> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

Are you sure that it didn't occur at the Mad Hatter's Tea Party...I
believe that the dialog went something like this when the Mad Hatter
asked Alice to solve a riddle:


`Do you mean that you think you can find out the answer to it?' said
the March Hare. 

`Exactly so,' said Alice. 

`Then you should say what you mean,' the March Hare went on. 

`I do,' Alice hastily replied; `at least--at least I mean what I
say--that's the same thing, you know.' 

`Not the same thing a bit!' said the Hatter. `You might just as well
say that "I see what I eat" is the same thing as
"I eat what I see"!' 

`You might just as well say,' added the March Hare, `that "I like what
I get" is the same thing as "I get what I
like"!' 

`You might just as well say,' added the Dormouse, who seemed to be
talking in his sleep, `that "I breathe when I
sleep" is the same thing as "I sleep when I breathe"!' 

`It IS the same thing with you,' said the Hatter, and here the
conversation dropped, and the party sat silent for a
minute, while Alice thought over all she could remember about ravens
and writing-desks, which wasn't much. 



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Unix->MVS file transfer; MF COBOL

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36133d82.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36104E32.A0E@swbell.net>`

```
Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <36104E32.A0E@swbell.net>...
>Is there a way to transfer a MF COBOL file, with binary data and
>variable length records, to MVS?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>can show that it is absolutely unavoidable.


Write an MVS program that can read the all-character file,
where I assume that the comp fields are stored in display
format. Then move the fields one at a time to a binary MVS
file record. Write that record to an MVS file defined the
normal way.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unix->MVS file transfer; MF COBOL

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36142637.259B@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<36104E32.A0E@swbell.net> <36133d82.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <36104E32.A0E@swbell.net>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> normal way.

That's a sensible solution, but we have non-sensible requirements.

As I tried to explain in the passage you quoted, upper management
has assured the recipient of the file that he will not have to 
change *any* of his processing, not even to insert a trivial 
reformatting operation.  The file from Unix must arrive ready to
run, complete with block descriptor words and record descriptor
words, with no further alteration needed.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

```
"Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:

>Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <36104E32.A0E@swbell.net>...
>>Is there a way to transfer a MF COBOL file, with binary data and
>>variable length records, to MVS?

If you're using COBOL Workbench or Mainframe Express from Micro Focus,
then they both include very powerful data file conversion and transfer
tools.

For conversion use the Data File Convert tool - this will
"intelligently" convert the necessary data from ASCII/ANSI to EBCDIC.
The decision is controlled by the user, but there are tools to assist
you by browsing record layouts, indicating which fields are to be
converted, specifying rules to determine different record layout
within the same file etc. The convertor can also convert between
different file formats - e.g. Record Sequential to VSAM. This is in
both Workbench and Mainframe Express.

For transfer, MFXFER is provided with Workbench which will transfer
source and data files, optionally doing the codeset conversions as
part of the process. The Mainframe Access Drag & Drop tool (an option
to Workbench, but a standard part of Mainframe Express) provides the
same functionality and a lot more.

Contact your MF rep for more information or take a look at the website
: http://www.microfocus.com

Regards,
Mark Warren
Micro Focus
Micro Focus Ltd., The Lawn, Old Bath Rd., Newbury, Berks. UK RG14 1QN
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unix->MVS file transfer; MF COBOL

- **From:** mkw@mfltd.co.uk (Mark Warren)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3617b11f.12313647@hyperion>`
- **References:** `<3614afd0.11978237@hyperion>`

```
Oops - should always re-read notes to ensure there complete before
sending (slaps own wrist).

I should have pointed out that the tools mentioned are only available
on Windows platforms. However it would be possible to move files
around from UNIX assuming you have Workbench or Mainframe Express on a
machine which can access the UNIX file system using something like NFS
or Samba.

Regards,
Mark Warren
Micro Focus
Micro Focus Ltd., The Lawn, Old Bath Rd., Newbury, Berks. UK RG14 1QN
```

```
Sue L wrote:
> 
> After being deep in thought on Wed, 30 Sep 1998 16:14:26 -0400,, I
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> for that.

I think our new mf2fsc converter will be able to help.
We have a Fujitsu Preprocessor mode in it.
When you run converter in this mode, 
it takes MF input and produces FSC output with full inlining.
Also the output file has #FILE and #LINE directives,
so both Fujitsu compiler and Fujitsu debugger 
will work in terms of lines of original MF file.

In fact, it creates an illusion that Fujitsu compiler
parses and executes MF Cobol program "directly".

Fairly soon you will be able to say something
like "cobol32 /mf file.cbl" and you will have your MF
application running under FSC, whille you can continue
maintaining it as an MF applcation.

Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems
http://www.siber.com/sct/
```

```
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MDI Forms -- Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36152080.12729974@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<6usj4v$i77$1@medousa.forthnet.gr> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-zhnzK1HpVhLY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <3613ac49.15214493@news.enter.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-r7Wdsnh65S3v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>On Thu, 1 Oct 1998 16:23:15, rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe) wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No...multi-panel windows are a single window made up of multiple
panels.  The distinction is that the multi-panel window in sp2 has the
appearance of being constructed from a single panel, when in fact it
is constructed of multiple panels.  Gives you a bit of flexibility.

An MDI Window is indeed multiple panels, but more along the lines of a
main "parent" window which contains one or more "child" windows.
They're not actually parent/child windows, but they appear to be a
similar construct.

I have a neat little COBOL program which I will send you offline.  It
can be run as an MDI-type application or it can be run as a Tab Dialog
Box application by just changing the panel file.

Just take a look at the readme.txt file when you get it.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-a7c1accf74"></a>
## Thread: sound — 1 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-a7c1accf74)._

#### ↳ Re: sound

- **From:** Martyn Woerner <mw@unspam.mfltd.unspam.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36133B73.DEB@unspam.mfltd.unspam.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<361211f9.0@news.mountain.net>`

```
Patrick Lykins wrote:
> 
> Can anyone tell me if their is a way to put sound into a CoBOL program?
> 
> I really don't care how crude it is, if it's just a series of signal tone
> beeps, it is better than nothing.

With MF COBOL running under Windows, try the windows API.  Check if
sound is available and if so, just play away.  This sort of thing - mind
you need to use the copyfile derived from the windows.h file using the
h2cpy utility to pick up the right datatypes and constant values.  If
you need more info, let me know.

      *>   Next, check if sound is available
           move SND-ASYNC to sound-mode
           if sound-enabled                    *> Do we want to try?
               set sound-enabled to false
               call ms-winapi sndPlaySound using
                       by reference init-run-sound
                       by value     sound-mode
                       returning win-return-bool
               on exception
      *>           This is not a basic Windows routine but one from
      *>           the MMSystem, so it may not have been linked in.
                   add MB-OK MB-ICONINFORMATION giving tmp-int
      $if env not = "16bit"
                   add MB-SETFOREGROUND to tmp-int
      $end
                   call ms-winapi MessageBox using
                       by value screen-hand
                       by reference "The sound routine doesn't seem " &
                                 "to be here, but no matter."   & x"00"
                       by reference "TicTac - Running Silently" & x"00"
                       by value tmp-int
                   end-call
               not on exception
                   if win-return-bool = val-true
                       set sound-enabled to true
                   end-if
               end-call
           end-if
and then ...

           move SND-ASYNC to sound-mode
           add SND-NOSTOP to sound-mode
           if sound-enabled
               perform with test after until win-return-bool = val-true
                   call ms-winapi sndPlaySound using
                           by reference init-game-sound
                           by value     sound-mode
                           returning win-return-bool
                   if win-return-bool not = val-true
                       perform zb000-yield
                   end-if
               end-perform
           end-if
           move SND-ASYNC to sound-mode

and working storage has stuff like ..

       77  sound-mode                  usage UINT    value SND-ASYNC.
       77  win-return-bool             usage BOOL.
       78  init-run-sound  value sound-path & "initrun.wav"  & x"00".
       78  init-game-sound value sound-path & "initgame.wav" & x"00".
       01  screen-data value low-values.
        03 screen-hand                 usage HWND.





Martyn.

To reply, remove the two "unspam." from the address.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Editing programs on a network

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn717d7o.66o.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<6u8d1u$cku1@puligny.idt.cdc.fr> <6u8rs3$75d@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <6ua9fv$rah$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <01bdecc4$1e19e2c0$239669c0@mprata>`

```
On 30 Sep 1998 22:51:30 GMT, prata <prata@mail.teleweb.pt> wrote:
>My question is: Anyone knows if its possible to use an software that
>permits to develop on Win95 without the emulators? i.e. can i write
>programs on win95 environment with an automatic update of unix system?

If your Unix admin is willing to install Samba on the Unix system, this would
allow you to see the Unix box in Win95's network neighborhood. You could then
map the share containing your code to a drive letter.  You'd then be able to
edit those files on the Win95 box and your changes would be seen by anyone on
the Unix box as soon as you saved the file. Since the Acucobol objects are
portable, any compiles you do under Win95 would also be useable on the Unix
box.

The downside to this approach is that all your edits are "live".  If you want
to do development locally and then send the changes in once they're complete,
I'd suggest evaluating a source management tool such as CVS.


----
The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.
```

```
At the place where I once work, these pictures were each a member in a
directory file with a record length of 133 chars.  The first character of
each line was the carriage control.  For each printed line of Mona it may
have taken 4 lines from the data file.  We used iebgener to print it.  To
recreate it, you need a printer that uses the carriage control otherwise
you are out of luck.  Oh yeah, the 4 lines would be printed on top of each
other, before advancing one paper print line.
```

<a id="t-4a9ce09b5e"></a>
## Thread: L S 6 W K M C Re: Short-circuiting — 1 msgs

### L S 6 W K M C Re: Short-circuiting

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298273.5884.10226@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

 
>> In each language it is necessary to understand its particular
>> evaluation order, there is _no_ substitute.  In C, for example,
>> there are 15 levels precedence, each specifying the grouping,
>> and 15 levels of associativity.
 
> It has escaped your notice (why am I not surprised?), but it is
> not computer languages which establish evaluation order in
> mathematical expressions, but universally recognized
> mathematical practice.
 
In mathematical expressions it is exactly the 'mathematical
language' that establishes evaluation order.  Different
mathematical languages (and there are several) may establish
their own order. Polish Notation and RPN, for example, have
different orders of evaluation than infix notation.  Your idea
that there is a 'universally recognised practice' is flawed by
your apparent belief that there is only _one_ system.
 
Computer languages do, in fact, establish the evaluation order
that _they_ require.  Some languages do strict left-to-right
evaluation regardless of any priorities.  Other languages use
one or other of the mathematical notations, or some other
ordering that is appropriate.
 
> This has absolutely nothing to do with what I was talking about.
 
'Mathematical Practice' has nothing to do with what anyone was
talking about, so why did you introduce it ? (except as a
misunderstanding about the points raised with C).
 
The fact that you misunderstood the issue with C language
evaluation does tend to show that you don't even understand the
terminology of C, let alone the issues involved.
 
Now looking at the core part of your arguments:
 
>> There isn't a programmer alive who has written a lot of code
>> in several languages who hasn't at one time or another
>> written a statement using the syntax of a wrong language.
 
and:
 
>> A practice such as you suggest is absolutely guaranteed to
>> increase the probability of writing broken code, and there is
>> nothing in this world you can do to eliminate that, except
>> avoid the practice altogether.
 
Just to summarise, the 'practice' that I suggested was:
       1) testing indecies before use
       2) using short-circuit evaluation in Cobol 85
 
In Cobol the short-circuiting of evaluations is a non-issue.
If you code correctly and do not know of short-circuiting
then whether evaluations are actually done or not is irrelevant
 
However, you brought in "several languages".  In other langauages
(notably C, but others as well) the issue of short-circuited
evaluations is _vital_ to the correct coding of the program.
You need to know that they occur and when they occur, you need
to know the standard for each language.
 
You cannot "avoid the issue" if you "code in several languages".
 
> No, I work hard at developing habits which will serve me, not
> trick me.  This is because I am smart enough to know that I am
> not perfect. Apparently you aren't that smart, because it is a
> sure bet you aren't perfect.  Not that you would agree on that,
> of course.
 
>> This is a fact, not an opinion.
 
You seem to work hard at 'abuse', 'intellectual bullying',
'pomposity', perhaps these do generally serve you well.
 
 
> Only seemingly obsolete to someone who hasn't a clue about the
> real issue here.  You are apparently unaware (as you are of so
> many other things) that many, many MIS shops do *not* use the
> latest compiler?
 
Fully aware, and that is _exactly_ why I qualified all my
comments about Cobol to ensure that it was recognised as applying
to Cobol-85.  However, just because some MIS shops still use
older standards does not mean that everyone should continue to
write Cobol that is a quarter of a century old.
 
If they use Cobol-85 then they should use END-~ and EVALUATE
and Functions (if they have the 89 addendum) and also short-
circuit evaluations.  Or they should at least be able to
recognise these because they will come across them.
 
Your idea that they should be avoided, and that knowledge of
them should be avoided is flawed.
 
 
> Richard, I have been examining code from compilers for a long
> time. I have been studying and programming in COBOL, and many
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the beginning, ...
 
You really do come across as a ludite here.  The order of
evaluation of logical expression _is_ embodied in the Cobol
Standards and is thus guaranteed in any conforming compiler.
It is in the Cobol-85 standard, and in the 74 standard.  It
was also in the -68 standard but some changes were made in
respect of the order of evaluating NOT when going to -74.
 
If you had taken a few minutes looking at the specification of
Cobol instead of burying yourself in the machine code output from
some specific implementations you may have a better idea of
what constitutes standard Cobol.
 
> ... I would agree that you could test limits and end
> conditions in the same statement.
 
This is actually a somewhat different issue to that of 'order of
evaluation', but you seem to have them somewhat confused.
 
All compilers _are_ required (in order to be standard) to
follow the appropriate order of evaluation rules which you think
are missing.
 
The issue of whether bound checking can be used in the same
conditional as the use of the index is subject to
'short-circuiting' the evaluation, the correct ordering, while
important, is not enough.  This _is_ guaranteed in Cobol-85.
 
> But the fact is that there are still a lot of compilers out
> there which do not guarantee that.
 
You are referring to ANSI-74 and -68 compilers ?  These compilers
also do not have END-~, EVALUATE, reference notation, in-line
performs, and many other features.  Do you avoid all these too ?
 
In fact, if you had looked closely, the example was an in-line
perform.  This was obviously Cobol-85, it couldn't have been an
earlier standard, and so testing the bounds in the same condition
is entirely appropriate.
 
> And once code is written, who knows where it might end up?
> You assume that I avoid certain practices because I don't
> understand them.  The fact is that I avoid them because I
> do understand them, and I understand the ramifications of
> using them.
 
Well clearly not in this case.
```

<a id="t-cdaef6ab06"></a>
## Thread: Color Change — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-cdaef6ab06)._

#### ↳ Re: Color Change

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298273.14757.23647@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3612dbaa.0@news.mountain.net>`

```
In message <<3612dbaa.0@news.mountain.net>> "Patrick Lykins" <lynkins@access.mountain.net> writes:
> 
> I have one last question, is their anyway to change color in CoBOL?

That will depend entirely on what is required to have its 
colour changed: printouts ? the programmer ? the source
code ?

If it is Cobol DISPLAYs to screen then there are several
ways to do it and this depends on the environment and the
compiler used.

With MicroFocus (or MS Cobol) you can do it with SCREEN
SECTION, with DISPLAY .... FOREGROUND-COLOUR BACKGROUND-COLOUR
(or even COLOR), or with CBL_WRITE_SCR_CHARS_ATTR (or
similar). or CALL x"A7", or configuring the ADIS 
routines.

In some environments you can send control sequences to
the screen to effect the colour changes.

Or you can provide a configuration file withh appropriate
settings.

Other compilers may have similar facilities or different
ones.  This may also depend on how you are displaying
to the screen (eg ANSI DISPLAY, extended DISPLAY or
Windows API).
```

## Thread: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric

- **From:** Claus Heiko Niesen <cniesen@iastate.edu>
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu>`

```
Hello! 

I was wondering what the official steps of converting a PIC S9(4) COMP
and PIC S9(9) COMP to numeric and alpha numeric are. 

Currently I am moving the PIC S9(9) COMP to PIC S9(9) and then to PIC
X(9).  I'm aware that I do lose the negative sign when I'm doing this
and that the COMP field could potentially contain a larger number than
S9(9).  However, I'm not sure if COBOL utilizes the whole byte to store
such a large number and so if I have to worry about it.

I would greatly appreciate if someone could tell me how to do the
conversion properly.  It would also be nice to know the conversion of
PIC S9(9) COMP-3 to numeric and alpha numeric.

Your help is greatly appreciated,
  Claus Niesen cniesen@iastate.edu
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981002181430.04701.00001688@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu>`

```

In article <36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu>, Claus Heiko Niesen
<cniesen@iastate.edu> writes:

>I would greatly appreciate if someone could tell me how to do the
>conversion properly.  It would also be nice to know the conversion of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

AFAIK, in the environment that I am currently using (IBM OS/390/LE), a straight
move of the PIC S9(9) COMP to a PIC X... will expand the fields to display
format and produce a readable number.  The same is true for COMP-3.  I may be
wrong but in some of the most recent research efforts into other people's code
has shown that this is how some folks side-step the need to add extra
re-definitions of fields in their programs.  Personal preference would be to
have a redefine of the receiving PIC X field to receive the COMP/COMP-3 as PIC
S9(9) SIGN SEPARATE TRAILING.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00

```

> 
> In article <36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu>, Claus Heiko Niesen
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> format and produce a readable number.  The same is true for COMP-3.  I may 

The COMP to PIC X won't work.  It will not be converted.  Move it to 
PIC 9 redefined as Pic X or as a subordinate element to a group field 
and it will work.
```

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)

```
In message <<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu>> Claus Heiko Niesen <cniesen@iastate.edu> writes:
> 
> I was wondering what the official steps of converting a PIC S9(4) COMP
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

Is this homework ?

You move a PIC S9(9) COMP into a larger numeric field, or
even an edited numeric:

            01  Floatie-Signed      PIC -(11)9.

            MOVE Comp-Number    TO Floatie-Signed
```

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> In message <<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu>> Claus Heiko Niesen <cniesen@iastate.edu> writes:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is this homework ?

No, I never got a formal explanation of this subject in school or
anywhere else.  In fact in my COBOL class I never heard about COMP and
COMP-3.  Now that I am out of school and writing code that should be of
good quality I think that I should know how to handle this case
properly.  Unfortunately  the manuals that I have available do not
contain much information about this subject.

BTW the .edu extension of my e-mail address does not indicate that I am
a student; it indicates that I am employed by the university.  If you
need proof look at http://www.adp.iastate.edu/staff.html.

*shakes his head about all the unfriendliness in this newsgroup toward
students*

> You move a PIC S9(9) COMP into a larger numeric field, or
> even an edited numeric:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>             MOVE Comp-Number    TO Floatie-Signed

I was wondering why the numeric field in which you move the PIC S9(9)
COMP has to be larger.  I tried on the IBM MVS system to move a number
that has more than 9 digits into the PIC S9(9) COMP field but it would
not let me do this.  I know that in 8 bytes you can store numbers with
more than 9 digits, but there is no possibility that this happens when
only COBOL is being used (or am I missing something).

This weekend I dialed-in and tried some of the suggestions that have
been posted here as well as other possibilities.  I decided to do the
following in the future when moving a computational field to an
alphanumeric field:
      01  WS-WORKING-STORAGE.
          12  WS-COMPUTATIONAL               PIC S9(9) VALUE +31 COMP.
          12  WS-NUMERIC-EDITED              PIC -9(9) VALUE +0.
          12  WS-NUMERIC-EDITED-REDEFINED 
                 REDEFINES WS-NUMERIC-EDITED PIC X(10).
          12  WS-ALPHANUMERIC                PIC X(10) VALUE +0.
      
      MOVE WS-COMPUTATIONAL                  TO WS-NUMERIC-EDITED
      MOVE WS-NUMERIC-EDITED-REDEFINED       TO WS-ALPHANUMERIC
I choose not to use the "SIGN [LEADING / TRAILING] SEPARATE" since I did
not see any code at work that used this statement, in the hope that the
next person can maintain my code easier.

I still have not found a simple way to move an alphanumeric field to a
computational field.  Here the problem is to move an alphanumeric field
to a signed numeric field.  I probably just end up using the routine at
work that is used to edit numeric values that the user enters to an
alphanumeric field since the output of the routine is signed numeric.

Thank you to everybody that replied. I appreciate it a lot. 
Do not forget that the students of today maintain your code of tomorrow
and somehow they, too, have to learn all this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanume

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cT2S1.26$Hz2.23160@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu> <3298275.4949.29209@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36180342.27DB9C18@iastate.edu>`

```
Claus Heiko Niesen wrote:
>
>I still have not found a simple way to move an alphanumeric field to a
>computational field.  Here the problem is to move an alphanumeric field
>to a signed numeric field.

If you know the exact format of the number, you can redefine the field
using that PIC and move from that field to a numeric field.

If you know the number is a valid format, you can use FUNCTION NUMVAL or
FUNCTION NUMVAL-C to extract it.  Be careful with non-integers, because
the returned format is floating point, and on machines which use binary
floating point, you can lose precision through binary/decimal rounding.
Also, if the number is invalid, the program can abort.

If you want a COBOL routine to extract a free form number from an
alphanumeric field, returning an error flag if the number is not valid,
download GETNUM.ZIP under 'COBOL Programs' from my web page below.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanume

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00

- **References:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu> <3298275.4949.29209@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36180342.27DB9C18@iastate.edu> <cT2S1.26$Hz2.23160@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>Claus Heiko Niesen wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>the returned format is floating point, and on machines which use binary
                     --
Correction: that should be *may* be floating point (e.g. Micro Focus).
Thanks to Thane Hubbell for bringing it to my attention. :-)

>floating point, you can lose precision through binary/decimal rounding.
>Also, if the number is invalid, the program can abort.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>download GETNUM.ZIP under 'COBOL Programs' from my web page below.
```
