###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanume

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct6.143855.19310@giant>`
- **References:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu> <3298275.4949.29209@kcbbs.gen.nz> <36180342.27DB9C18@iastate.edu>`

```
In article <36180342.27DB9C18@iastate.edu>,
Claus Heiko Niesen <cniesen@iastate.edu> wrote:
>
> In fact in my COBOL class I never heard about COMP and COMP-3.

This is bad news.

It appears that some academics can't bring themselves to teach CoBOL even
on the rare occasions when their department says they shall.

The difference between internal and external representation is important
in most computer languages, and CoBOL has an unusually versatile set of
external representations.


[...]
> I was wondering why the numeric field in which you move the PIC S9(9)
> COMP has to be larger.  I tried on the IBM MVS system to move a number
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> only COBOL is being used (or am I missing something).

I'd say you're missing the course entitled "Introduction to Computing",
or some such, which should have been a prerequisite to the CoBOL course. 
If you expect to get a semester's worth of information from such a
limited medium as Usenet, you are expecting too much.

All computer languages define an abstract machine, which the compiler
then implements with the facilities of the real machine you are using.

The mainframe on which you are working has built-in fixed-point
two's-complement binary types: a two-byte "halfword" that can hold
-32,768 to 32,767, a four-byte "fullword" that can hold -2,147,483,648 to
+2,147,483,647, and an 8-byte "doubleword" that can hold
-9,223,372,036,854,775,808 to +9,223,372,036,854,775,807.

When you say PIC S9(4) COMP, you are asking the compiler to set aside a
halfword.  When you say PIC S9(9) COMP, you are asking the compiler to
set aside a fullword. When you say PIC S9(18) COMP, you are asking the
compiler to set aside a doubleword.

It may seem odd at first that PIC S9(5) COMP is a fullword, not a
halfword.  After all, the five-digit number 12345 can fit in a halfword.
But, not _all_ five-digit numbers can fit in a halfword.

If the compiler set aside a halfword for PIC S9(5) COMP, sure as shooting
some wise guy would try to store 54321 in it, which wouldn't fit.  In
decimal, only -9999 to +9999 can be _guaranteed_ to fit.  -32,768 to
-10,000 and 10,000 to 32,767 are wasted.  Likewise, in decimal, only
-999,999,999 to +999,999,999 can be _guaranteed_ to fit in a fullword. 
-2,147,483,648 to -1,000,000,000 and 1,000,000,000 to 2,147,483,647 are
wasted.  This is an important thing to know about internal vs. external
representations.

Although mainframe hardware can handle 31 decimal digits, CoBOL has an
overall limit of 18 digits, because IBM was outvoted at the time. 
Therefore, it is relatively easy to use all five digits in a halfword
when you need them, and all ten digits in a fullword, but displaying the
ninteenth digit of a doubleword is difficult in CoBOL.

Obviously, the CoBOL abstract machine lets you specify other than PIC
S9(4) COMP, PIC S9(9) COMP, and PIC S9(18) COMP, but these are the only
real ones on the mainframe, and all the others must map to them.  The
others are of little use on the mainframe without the TRUNC option, which
will kill you.

It would be nice if PIC S9(2) COMP mapped to a byte, which it could do,
since -99 to +99 will fit quite nicely.  However, the mainframe does not
have built-in byte arithmetic, and IBM was unwilling to do the slight
extra work of simulating byte arithmetic, a reluctance that has caused
the waste of thousands upon thousands of hours over the years.

I'll let someone else do COMP-3 this time.


[...]
>      01  WS-WORKING-STORAGE.
>          12  WS-COMPUTATIONAL               PIC S9(9) VALUE +31 COMP.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>      MOVE WS-NUMERIC-EDITED-REDEFINED       TO WS-ALPHANUMERIC

I'm sure that, one-on-one, your question could be answered in a short
time, but in this medium, unfortunately, your example is as puzzling as
your original article.  Leaving aside the minor errors, it's not clear
why you think you need to redefine PIC -9(9) as X(10), and if for some
reason you did, it would be better to let it flow upward than to
explicitly redefine.  The second MOVE, from PIC X(10) to PIC X(10), has
no purpose that I can see.  I can only repeat the question others have
asked, namely, why do you think you need to do this?


[...]
> I still have not found a simple way to move an alphanumeric field to a
> computational field.

You don't need to do this, either.  All computational input to CoBOL
programs is in numeric fields on punched cards.  :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XRnR1.2579$T5.3529208@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu>`

```
Claus Heiko Niesen wrote:
>
>I was wondering what the official steps of converting a PIC S9(4) COMP
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>PIC S9(9) COMP-3 to numeric and alpha numeric.

If I understand your post, you seem to be having a little difficulty
with some of the concepts here.

You don't "convert COMP to numeric", because COMP is a subset of numeric.
A numeric item is one on which you can perform numeric operations, ADD,
SUBTRACT, COMPUTE, etc.  A COMP item (or COMP-3, etc.) is a numeric item
which is formatted in some specific way, determined by the hardware
platform and compiler.  Numeric fields which are *not* defined as one of
the COMPUTATIONAL forms is inherently DISPLAY, meaning the digits could
be printed or displayed, because each digit is stored as a byte.  For all
numeric fields in COBOL, to 'convert' one type to another, you simply
move the data from the field defined one way to a field defined the other
way, and COBOL will do the necessary conversion.

An 'alphanumeric' field is simply a generic DISPLAY type field which has
no other attributes associated with it.

To convert a field defined as

  03  FIELD1      PIC S9(9) COMP (or COMP-3, or COMP-anything).

to DISPLAY, do this:

  03  FIELD2-ALPHA.
      05  FIELD2      PIC S9(9).

or

  03  FIELD2        PIC S9(9).
  03  FIELD2-ALPHA  REDEFINES FIELD-2 PIC  X(9).

  MOVE FIELD1 TO FIELD2.

But the digit which has the sign would print as an alphabetic character,
not a numeric digit.  The only difference between the two is that the group
item above will not edit properly if moved to an alphanumeric edited field.
To get around that, you can use either of these approaches:

  03  FIELD2-ALPHA.
      05  FIELD2      PIC S9(9) SIGN [LEADING / TRAILING] SEPARATE.
or

  03  FIELD2        PIC S9(9) SIGN [LEADING / TRAILING] SEPARATE.
  03  FIELD2-ALPHA  REDEFINES FIELD-2 PIC  X(10).

Either of these would give you a + or - for the sign in a separate character,
the total field length would be 10.  But the usually preferred method is to
simply use a numeric edited field:

  03  FIELD2      PIC -9(9).
  03  FIELD2-ALPHA  REDEFINES FIELD-2 PIC  X(10).

  03  FIELD2      PIC 9(9)-.
  03  FIELD2-ALPHA  REDEFINES FIELD-2 PIC  X(10).

  03  FIELD2      PIC 9(9)+.
  03  FIELD2-ALPHA  REDEFINES FIELD-2 PIC  X(10).

  03  FIELD2      PIC -(10).
  03  FIELD2-ALPHA  REDEFINES FIELD-2 PIC  X(10).

  03  FIELD2      PIC ----,---,---.
  03  FIELD2-ALPHA  REDEFINES FIELD-2 PIC  X(10).

or any of a multitude of possibilities.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric

- **From:** "Charlie Ebert" <kd5ob@theshop.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_7SS1.145$bC3.91436@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu> <XRnR1.2579$T5.3529208@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
COMP is not COMP.

Micro focus has COMP-6 which is exactly what Burrow's COMP is.
They also have COMP-3 which is exactly what IBM COMP is.

The difference is in unsigned COMP numbers.

IBM COMP a 9(02) COMP VALUE 2 would be hex X"00002F" or three bytes.
Burrows COMP  a 9(02) COMP VALUE 2 would be hex X"0002" or two bytes.

The Burrows COMP is more efficient on the drive space frankly.

Fujitsu COBOL only supports the IBM or Micro Focus COMP-3 format.
I've asked the Fujitsu people if they would support the COMP-6 Micro Focus
format
but I haven't received an answer.  They may do this in a future version.

Thanks
Charlie



Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Claus Heiko Niesen wrote:
>>
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>Either of these would give you a + or - for the sign in a separate
character,
>the total field length would be 10.  But the usually preferred method is to
>simply use a numeric edited field:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qj0T1.1748$2K1.1768648@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<36152C7F.7BA2@iastate.edu> <XRnR1.2579$T5.3529208@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <_7SS1.145$bC3.91436@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Charlie Ebert wrote:
>
>Micro focus has COMP-6 which is exactly what Burrow's COMP is.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Burrows COMP  a 9(02) COMP VALUE 2 would be hex X"0002" or two bytes.

I wonder if you made an unintentional mistype there? :)  I don't know
about Micro Focus COMP-6, because my MF COBOL 3.2 does not support
it.  But in true Burroughs COMP, a 9(02) COMP field would be one byte,
not two.  In fact, if you defined this

    03  TEST-GROUP.
        05  TEST-1    PIC  9(03) COMP VALUE 123.
        05  TEST-2    PIC  9(03) COMP VALUE 789.

the hex value of TEST-GROUP would be X"123789" in 3 bytes.  Both TEST-1
and TEST-2 would occupy a byte and a half, or '3 digits' in Burroughs
terminology.  Outside a group, or before a display type field, or at
the end of a group, you would get a one digit (half byte) FILLER after
an odd length series of COMP fields.  And you only get a sign when you
specify a sign.  If Micro Focus does all this, I will be impressed! :-)

The reason for this behavior is that Burroughs mainframes address a half
byte as easily as a byte, though the architecture is vastly different
among them.  At the hardware instruction level, there is as much or more
difference between any of the Small, Medium and Large systems as between
any of them and say, an IBM mainframe.  At the operating system and
software level, they are very compatible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COMP to numeric and alphanumeric

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981012012825.04700.00003451@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<_7SS1.145$bC3.91436@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```

In article <_7SS1.145$bC3.91436@newsfeed.slurp.net>, "Charlie Ebert"
<kd5ob@theshop.net> writes:

>IBM COMP a 9(02) COMP VALUE 2 would be hex X"00002F" or three bytes.
>Burrows COMP  a 9(02) COMP VALUE 2 would be hex X"0002" or two bytes.
>

Actually IBM's COMP PIC 9(2) would be X'0002', two bytes long. COMP on IBM's
mainframe is binary: halfword binary (COMP PIC 9(1) through COMP PIC 9(4)), or
full word binary (COMP PIC 9(5) through COMP PIC 9(9)).

IBM's COM-3 PIC 9(2) would be X'002F' (packed decimal, two bytes because one
byte wouldn't hold both sign and two digits, but two bytes is).

Mark A. Young
```

---

## Thread: Cross-platform CICS COBOL — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Cross-platform CICS COBOL

- **From:** "Steve King" <steve@quetzal.clara.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v3dbf$jut$1@eros.clara.net>`

```
Is there a software product that allows development and testing of CICS
COBOL programs on a PC network (NT-based)? It must be possible to then take
the COBOL source and re-compile on a MVS system for productionising (i.e. no
development work should be done on the mainframe).

Alternatively, has anyone got PC COBOL programs to talk to MVS CICS COBOL
programs, perhaps using CICS/NT?

My company is considering various options for replacing our existing 3270
terminal/MVS CICS COBOL systems with PC GUI/CICS COBOL, so I would welcome
any suggestions. We currently use DB2 databases and native VSAM files.

Thanks,

Steve
```

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

then utilze VSAM files (built into CICS/NT), DB2 (if you get DB20NT, ot UDB, as it is
known now.  Also, Visual Age for COBOL 2.2 supports access to remote DLI databases.

## Thread: General image of COBOL - Tirade Alert. — 19 msgs

### General image of COBOL - Tirade Alert.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YdVVmrp5DJd9@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

I know that "COBOL: Unleashed" is now available in the bookstores.  

So, being interested in the book, I took a trip to my local "Hastings"
bookstore.

They have a TON of computer books.  From C to JAVA - they have it all.

But NO COBOL BOOKS.  You think, so?  COBOL isn't being touted today 
and so sales of COBOL books will be slow - so why stock the book - you
can just ORDER it if someone wants it.  

Well, that doesn't wash - not when you stock a book like: Using 
Microsoft INTERDEV.

What the heck is that?  Anyone ever heard of it?  Is there a greater 
demand for this than COBOL?  

There *IS* a serious STIGMA to COBOL - what can we do to eliminate 
it????
```

Well Thane my copy of Cobol Unleashed was delivered yesterday by my
UPS guy. And I like it a whole lot. I want to congratulate you and the
others
for some real good work.

As for Cobol, I don't think it is a stigma as much as a demand for
more perceived "hot" topic books. C, Java, C++, Perl, etc., all of
these are perceived as being hot and book stores will only stock books
that are hot or they think they can sell.

For example, I went to my local B&N to pick up the following book,
There's Nothing in the Middle of the Road but Yellow Stripes and
Dead Armadillos by Jim Hightower. First I searched the store and
then I asked a sales person where I might find this title. The look
I received was priceless. After some thought the same sales person
said "I think that is out of stock. We can order it for you." Well I
ordered
the book but it just shows that if the managers don't think they can sell
the sucker in two minutes they will never stock it.

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>
>I know that "COBOL: Unleashed" is now available in the bookstores.  
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>demand for this than COBOL?  

Thane:

Interdev is the "next generation" of their Front Page product from
what I understand.  It is basically a web page development tool.

I'm not really very familiar with it, but I'll bet that the pages
developed with it don't run very well on Netscape Navigator.

The reason that the bookstore stocks it is because everyone is "gaa
gaa" over the Internet.  It really doesn't matter to the bookstore
owner that the vast majority of business applications DON'T run on the
Internet.

The bookstore owner is primarily concerned with satisfying the
voracious appetites of the buying public when they are in a feeding
frenzy.  The current feeding frenzy involves just about anything to do
with the Internet.

The reason for the feeding frenzy changes every few years.  It always
seems to involve "the technology to end all technologies." yet somehow
life goes on and new technologies continue to emerge.  Interesting how
COBOL just keeps plugging away despite the feeding frenzy.

>There *IS* a serious STIGMA to COBOL - what can we do to eliminate 
>it????

The stigma has been attached to COBOL for about 30 years now and it
never has gone away.  Yet, COBOL continues to maintain the strongest
position in business data processing.

The "pundits" have predicted the demise of COBOL for many years.  The
"experts" scoff at the language and declare it to be "bad."  This
"bad, dead" language still maintains its dominance despite the
predictions of the self-appointed industry leaders.

It's similar to the story of the bumblebee.  It goes something like
this:

The world's leading Aerospace engineers claim that based upon the
principals of physics and aerodynamics, it is impossible for the
bumblebee to fly, but the bumblebee never studied physics nor
aerodynamics and therefore continues to fly.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: General image of COBOL - Tirade Aler

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298275.4688.29060@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<361524d7.13841612@news.enter.net>`

```
In message <<361524d7.13841612@news.enter.net>> rtwolfe@flexus.com  writes:
> 
> The world's leading Aerospace engineers claim that based upon the
> principals of physics and aerodynamics, it is impossible for the
> bumblebee to fly, but the bumblebee never studied physics nor
> aerodynamics and therefore continues to fly.

Actually the Aerospace engineers do know about Reynold's
Number so they haven't made that claim in the last 60 years
or so   ;-)

The original is that, in the 1930s, an aeronautical engineer
demonstrated that the then current calculations did not
scale well, something that model aircraft designers knew
already.  The engineer was Neville Shute Norway, the 
solution was Reynold's Number.  The Bumble Bee (otherwise
and more correctly known as the Humble Bee) obviously
knew as much as Reynold did.

The importance of this was most apparent when the aeronautical
calculations were required for supersonic flight a short
time later.
```

>
>Well Thane my bookstore carries A copy of "Teach Yourself COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>works.  

Very unlikely - what precedence is there for that. He may *BUY* something that 
works. (and then of course add 'features')

>Meanwhile the plan is just to keep COBOL out of the limelight.
>Unless of course it's negative publicity.

No. The industry thrives on buzzwords and all too often new programmers see old 
languages or indeed old anything as being outdated. There is a tendancy to 
invent a new wheel when the old ones worked perfectly well.

In the case of Microsoft, they don't own the old wheels but they do own most 
of the carts they go on. Billy would much rather you put his wheels on it too.

Java is interesting. It's not a terribly good wheel but it goes on anyones 
cart.

#### ↳ Re: General image of COBOL - Tirade Alert.

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> it????

Yeah, who's fault is it that COBOL is stigmatized?  I say blame
Microsoft, whether it's true or not.  In one  computer dictionary they
give the definition of COBOL - ". . . COBOL is a language used primarily
for business applications for large mainframe computers. (Now you know
which language is responsible for screwing up your bank accounts.) 
Developed in the 60s by several computer companies and the U.S.
Department of Defense, the language shows the influence that the
drug-crazed, free-love era had on technology. . ."

Using COBOL in a sentence:  "Verily, I say unto you, COBOL is the Latin
of computer languages; dead but still used in crusty, nongrowing
environments."

The definition of MAINFRAME includes the sentence "Mainframe computers
also are called the dinosaurs of the computer industry."

There's only one way to eliminate this stigma.  When somebody sneers at
what you do, act like you're having a drug-crazed flashback and get
violent.

kitty
```

#### ↳ Re: General image of COBOL - Tirade Alert.

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3616478b.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YdVVmrp5DJd9@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Good to hear your book is finally out, although I will probably go through
the same problems getting it.  But then, there's always Amazon!

>What the heck is that?  Anyone ever heard of it?  Is there a greater
>demand for this than COBOL?
>
>There *IS* a serious STIGMA to COBOL - what can we do to eliminate
>it????

I think we're looking at two major problems:  one is that big booksellers
have to move product and therefore will carry "hot topic" books, as others
stated.

Second, and most importantly, is the ubiquity of the PC and the notion that
certain languages are "PC" or "mainframe" centric... the seeds of which were
sewn with Bill's invention of BASIC for the Altair and then subsequently for
almost ALL home PCs.   Obviously since a growing generation of kids
(including myself) didn't have access to mainframe COBOL, we were left
playing with VIC-20s, Apples, and Commodore 64s (all running Microsoft BASIC
of course)... and then C "came along" to replace ASSEMBLER... wow!  What
power at 4mHz!!!

The upshot is (IMO) that the combination of the availability of PCs to the
population in the 80s (definitely helped along by the growing demand for
better games) and the growing young generation that were weaned on BASIC and
C, fed each other.  I went into college with a PC centric mindset.  It
really wasn't until then that the mainframe world of COBOL and JCL was
opened up to me.  But how else was it supposed to be?

>There *IS* a serious STIGMA to COBOL - what can we do to eliminate
>it????

I'm glad that schools aren't pumping out COBOL programmers.  One of the
reasons I'm not too interested in BASIC and C anymore is that every kid and
his dog has access to these technologies and if kid programmers are the same
as when I was younger, they will probably be major contenders on these
platforms before they're 18!   The fact that the demand for COBOL
programmers exceeds its supply is a great thing... and in the end we win...
even if we lose at the bookstore.

RH
```

#### ↳ Re: General image of COBOL - Tirade Alert.

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3618cd6c.10972547@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YdVVmrp5DJd9@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
On 2 Oct 98 16:37:40 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>
>I know that "COBOL: Unleashed" is now available in the bookstores.  
>
>So, being interested in the book, I took a trip to my local "Hastings"
>bookstore.

How big is this 'Hastings' bookstore? I know my local Blackwells had
it but this bookstore is three storeys high and has at least 1000
books.

>
>They have a TON of computer books.  From C to JAVA - they have it all.

Surely the 'V*' section would be large too.

>
>But NO COBOL BOOKS.  You think, so?  COBOL isn't being touted today 
>and so sales of COBOL books will be slow - so why stock the book - you
>can just ORDER it if someone wants it.  

Surprising actually. What with the perceived panic over COBOL and Y2K.

>
>Well, that doesn't wash - not when you stock a book like: Using 
>Microsoft INTERDEV.

Hot stuff at the moment. It's MS's internet development platform for
developing Active Server Pages. ie. Proprietary MS locked in
technology that only works on MS's internet servers. You must have
come across web pages with .ASP as an extension?

The page is generated on the fly from a database and HTML fragments.

>
>What the heck is that?  Anyone ever heard of it?  Is there a greater 
>demand for this than COBOL?  

Amongst the Microsoft obsessed yes. It's not without it's merits but
you have to buy in to having completely MS tech on your server.

>
>There *IS* a serious STIGMA to COBOL - what can we do to eliminate 
>it????

Shut up about it, get on with the job. The new stuff will pass by and
die or find it's niche. Why anyone would want to use ASP and Microsoft
IIS is slightly beyond me though. Linux and Apache are far more stable
than the MS products and of course the same database engines we've
always used with COBOL are being used with the MS server.

The only thing I find interesting about Interdev and Microsofts
Internet server stance is that they are shoving the processing back on
the server and running a very thin client. Sound familiar?

[and I don't mean Java]
```

## Thread: Help with DECLARATIVES — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with DECLARATIVES

- **From:** Peter Niepel <PeNiepel@niepel.ka.uunet.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361502C2.2AECAFA6@niepel.ka.uunet.de>`

```
Hello everybody,

I need help with the following Statement:

011460  DECLARATIVES.
011470      
011480  LABEL-1-PRUEF SECTION.
011490        USE BEFORE BEGINNING FILE
011500        LABEL PROCEDURE ON SILABEL-E
011510        MOVE SPACE TO ZW-BAND
011520        MOVE VOL-NR  TO ZW-BAND
011530        GO TO E1.
011540
011550  E1.
011560
011570  END DECLARATIVES.

This code is in a COBOL 75 program and I have to transform it to a COBOL
II statement. The compiler made a complain about the word 'BEFORE'.
The program runs in a MVS-job and the statement reads a label from a
tape.
Can anybody give my an advice how I can transform it ?? 

Thanks a lot 

bye Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with DECLARATIVES

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981002181432.04701.00001689@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<361502C2.2AECAFA6@niepel.ka.uunet.de>`

```

>I need help with the following Statement:

…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

DECLARATIVES".

Let me know if you have trouble finding it.

#### ↳ Re: Help with DECLARATIVES

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct3.070931.19305@giant>`
- **References:** `<361502C2.2AECAFA6@niepel.ka.uunet.de>`

```
In article <361502C2.2AECAFA6@niepel.ka.uunet.de>,
Peter Niepel <PeNiepel@niepel.ka.uunet.de> wrote:
>
> 011460  DECLARATIVES.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> label from a tape.

Your manual is your friend!  The LABEL declarative is an IBM extension. 
In IBM CoBOL for MVS & VM the syntax is:

    USE [GLOBAL] AFTER [STANDARD] [BEGINNING] [FILE]
                                  [ENDING]    [REEL]
                                              [UNIT]

    LABEL PROCEDURE [ON] {file-name} ...
                         {INPUT    }
                         {OUTPUT   }
                         {I-O      }
                         {EXTEND   }


Notice that a label declarative is executed _after_ beginning a file, not
_before_, otherwise there would be no label to process.
```

---

Warren,

I can endorse your recommendation of PFE.  I've used it extensively in
writing and revising COBOL programs for several years, through several
earlier versions, and it just keeps getting better with each iteration.

Dennis Minette
CCCO

Warren G. Simmons wrote in message <361451B9.7DB7AA87@inficad.com>...
>Hi group,
>With the various references made tp favorite editors, I have found one that
is free that
>may be of interest to some of you. Certainly, it might be a good reference
to the FAQ.
>
>I've taken the liberty to paste in the content of it's read me file.  The
addition of several
>templates to it should make it very useful.
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>- Fully-remappable keyboard, including two-key operation similar to
MicroEMACS
>  and mapping of Alt keys to functions
>- Keystrokes and menu commands can be recorded in replayable keyboard
macros
>  which can be collected into libraries
>- Files can be printed either in total, by line range, or selected text
only
>- Automatic line indenting and removal of trailing spaces
>- Automatic configuration of edit options depending on file type
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>PFE may be used in any way, for any purpose, at no cost. In may be
distributed
>by any means, provided that the original files as supplied by the author
remain
>intact and no charge is made other than for reasonable distribution costs.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>PFE may not be distributed with any commercial product without a prior
license
>agreement with the author
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I would very much appreciate hearing from you if you use PFE and find
problems,
>or if you can think of ways it could be improved - and even (or is that
>'especially'?) if you just think it's great. Even if the facility you would
>like to see appears to be of interest only to you, tell me about it - you'd
be
>surprised how many ideas in that class have a much wider appeal.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I will try to reply to all the mail I receive, but it may take a week or so
if
>I'm very busy.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>   pfe0701p.zip      The 32-bit edition for Windows NT 3.51 and 4.0 on
PowerPC
>                       platforms
>   pfe0701.zip       The 16-bit edition for Windows 3.1x
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>The definitive source for new releases, news and useful information is the
PFE
>Home Page on the World Wide Web; the URL is
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>intended ones, not all facilities will be available in some combinations.
For
>the best result, you should use the versions recommended here:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>If you've been using 0.06.002 or earlier of the 32-Bit Edition under
Windows 95
>or Windows NT, then you'll probably want to take some installation steps
before
>you start to run the new version of PFE.
>
>Version 0.07.001 of PFE uses the Windows Registry to store configuration
>information rather than the PFE32.INI initialisation file; this allows it
to
>support per-user configuration data and offer some extra facilities.
>
>The INI2REG utility allows you to convert existing initialisation files
into
>the Windows Registry and explains the new facilities. You should read the
text
>file UPGRADE.TXT that is part of the distribution set for complete details
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Version 0.07.001 of PFE now sets up associations for the file types you
want to
>use it with itself, using the Options Preferences command. You should begin
>by using this command to define your associations, as this will guarantee
that
>they are correctly defined, and also enable some context menu support for
the
>32-Bit Edition. Note that your manually-defined associations will not be
shown
>by PFE when you do this.
>
>If you're upgrading from version 0.05.007 or earlier, it's best to delete
your
>existing initialisation files (PFE.INI or PFE32.INI) and begin again with
the
>new release.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>For the answers to some of the common questions that users have, see
FAQ.TXT.
>Looking in here can save you a lot of worry and experimentation, and save
you
>from posting your question to newsgroups and mailing lists.
>
>The CHANGES.TXT file tells you what's changed from earlier releases. If you
>find something unexpected going on, look in here too - it could be a
feature
>rather than a bug.
>
>The PROBLEMS.TXT file tells you about any major problems that were
discovered
>too late to be addressed in this release, and what you can do if you hit
them.
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>There are a small number of files in the PFE distribution. You should find
the
>following items:
>
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
>There is no setup program for PFE, as it doesn't really need one.
Installation
>is done by simply copying the supplied files:
>
>32-bit Edition:
>
> Copy pfe32.exe, pfe.hlp, pfedos32.exe to a suitable directory. This need
not
> be on your path; but it's important that all the files are in the same
> directory.
>
> Next, read upgrade.txt carefully, and if you wish, use the ini2reg utility
to
> convert your existing initialisation file PFE32.INI into the Windows
Registry
>
>16-bit Edition:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>It's a good idea to keep the 16-bit and 32-bit editions in the same place;
then
>you will need only one copy of the help file.
>
>After you've installed PFE, the best thing to do is start it up, then use
the
>Options Preferences command to explore the customisation options and set it
up
>to run as you prefer.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Go into MicroFocus COBOL help;
Click on STANDARD COBOL REFERENCE;

  COBOL VERBS;
  DISPLAY;

This shows extended options that you can use with the DISPLAY command.

#### ↳ Re: Console Colors in MF Personal Cobol

- **From:** "Dick McMullen" <dmcmulle@exicorp.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3614eb95.0@207.226.241.101>`
- **References:** `<6uo747$ct5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Try setting the run-time switch +a1

This changes the stdout device so it is redirectable via command line
redirection (program > display.out) and changes the color from brown to
white.  At least it did for me on an older release of MF COBOL.

Dick McMullen

<6uo747$ct5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...

- **Message-ID:** `<01bdeda6$2f2bde00$8e0785d0@default>`
- **References:** `<01bdeb18$24b005e0$LocalHost@default>`

```
The educational version of MicroFocus COBOL is pretty good, but it won't
create any executables.  Also I don't know if they are still giving it away
for free, but you used to be able to download a demo version of Fujitsu
COBOL from www.adtools.com.

If you buy COBOL for Dummies it comes with a CD that has the fujitsu
compiler on it, plus to or three other demo version.

processor and distributor of agricultural and food products and
employes over 80,000 employees in more than 1,000 locations in 72
countries. This dynamic company is in need of a AS/400 Technical
Support Specialist to join their I/T Technical Team.

responsible for ensuring excellent service to our customers across

technical skills to join our infrastructure team in maintaining
highly reliable and cost effective operating environments required to

be quickly resolving customer problems elevated from level 1 support
and actively participating on projects associated with the AS/400
environment.

Tech Support, good/effective communication with wide range of people
skills, experience with AS/400 OS fixes and upgrades.  Working
experience with Client Access, Robot, Autotune is a plus.

attain your career goals.  Medical, Dental, Vision, Life, Short and

(matiching 7% up to 15% pay), tuition reimbursement, 10 paid vacation

></PRE></HTML>

Very ingenious, indeed, and it does appear to work, but is there any IBM doc
that indicates that this trick is supported?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How Many COBOL Programs Does It Take To Change A Light Bulb?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vfmdn$r32$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6utn2c$ql6@newsb.netnews.att.com> <19981002214744.22187.00001556@ng99.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981002214744.22187.00001556@ng99.aol.com>,
  theodp@aol.com (Theo DP) wrote:

snip

> >The real key here is to use RECORDING MODE U and RECORD CONTAINS 0, the
> >combination of which forces the program to create the proper DCB at
> >runtime. It is interesting that this combination will also allow you to
> >read any length FIXED lrecl with the same FD.

snip

> Very ingenious, indeed, and it does appear to work, but is there any IBM doc
> that indicates that this trick is supported?
>

I didn't find any IBM documentation that indicates that this trick is
supported! But I didn't find any doc that indicates that this is forbidden
either!

It would certainly be interesting what Mr. Klein's "usually reliable source"
says about it!?!

I just found the following texts:

'All record formats except U can be blocked.' (Chapter 3.2 of 'DFSMS/MVS
Version 1 Release 4') --> conclusion: U is unblocked!

'... you must provide an area at least as large as the block size of the data
set.' (Chapter 3.2.4, same manual)

 'If you specify format-U, you can read any format.' (Chapter 3.7.3, same
manual)

'For format-F and format-U files, the definitions of QSAM logical record and
COBOL logical record are identical.' (Chapter 1.8.1.2 of 'COBOL MVS & VM V1R2
Programming Guide')

'Layout of Format-U Records:  Each block on external storage is handled as
a logical record' (Chapter 1.8.1.2.4, same manual)

My conclusion: If I read a record as 'U', I can do so, but I have to handle
the record-lenght myself.

Cheers

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

## Thread: MF Cobol under SCO Unix with Fileshare — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol under SCO Unix with Fileshare

- **From:** grwallace@taynet.co.uk ()
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v3eie$lh5$1@phys-ma.sol.co.uk>`

```
We are trying to get a medium-sized application (up to 50 users) to
run under Fileshare on a SCO Unix platform.

When about 30 users log in we get errors and the whole system falls
over.

We pay MF maintenance and, in general, we find that their products
work.

We would be pleased to here from anyone out there who may have a
similar system, and who may have something to say on ths subject

regards,

Gordon Wallace

grwallace@taynet.co.uk
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol under SCO Unix with Fileshare

- **From:** nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick & Carol Lucas-White)
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36162c5a.2365197@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6v3eie$lh5$1@phys-ma.sol.co.uk>`

```
On Sat, 03 Oct 1998 04:53:25 GMT, grwallace@taynet.co.uk () wrote:

>We are trying to get a medium-sized application (up to 50 users) to
>run under Fileshare on a SCO Unix platform.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You don't say what sort of errors, are they cobol error messages, SCO
messages or what ?

I had a problem with the Unix conecting to the 'net everytime it fired
up the MF CCI but I traced this to a Morningstar PPP filter file
needing the entry MFCOB PASS in it so that it did not have to identify
the service being used with the Domain name server. Apart from that
I've not experienced any other problems. Are all the users logging in
with the same login/password as this may cause additional problems.
```

<a id="t-a727e18086"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu 4.0 vs MF NetExpress & MF Resolve

- **From:** grwallace@taynet.co.uk ()
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v4m7q$muu$1@phys-ma.sol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<6ur3gb$hp1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Clive.Allwork@gems8.gov.bc.ca wrote:

>Fujitsu 4.0 Enterprise 189.00 (CDN)
>MF NetExpress 5,220.00 (CDN)
>MF Revolve 4,5670.00 (CDN)

>Anyone have any really good reasons for choosing the Micro Focus products vs
>the Fujitsu compiler?  Obviously there is a LARGE price difference.

>Some of our requirements:
>1) EASE OF USE
>2) INTEGRATION WITH VISUAL BASIC
>3) SUPPORT FOR ORACLE DATABASES

>-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
>http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum


In answer to your newsgroup problem I can only speak from our own
experience.

NetExpress is a strange product when compared to the older MF
Workbench offerings, but it's one 
great strenth is in VB integration.

Linking from COBOL to VB is not the easiest thing to do, as there are
problems with transferring data
between the two - indeed we have written a program generator to write
most of the code required
(in both COBOL and VB) to pass complete COBOL FD definitions between
the two.

We would make our program generators available if there was sufficient
commercial interest,
but this seems lacking for now.

If I never use NetExpress for anything else (and that is a
possibility!) the fact that you can step
straight from the VB debugger into the COBOL animator makes the
product worth having - essential,
even, I would think.

I hope that this helps.
```

### Cobol Programmers Needed in Greater Cleveland Ohio area

- **From:** exexesrc@aol.com (Exexesrc)

- **Message-ID:** `<19981003070950.18741.00001917@ng113.aol.com>`

```

I am looking for experienced Cobol Programmers for Direct Hire positions in
Cleveland, Ohio. These are permanent full time positions with outstanding
companies. The industries are Insurance, and Financial. Relocation assistance
is being offered along with a salary range of up to $65,000. You must be a US
Citizen or Green Card holder and currently residing in the US and willing to
relocate to Cleveland, Ohio. Please do not contact me if you are located
Overseas and would like to relocate back to the States. You must have at least
1 year of programming experience in a Mainframe environment to be considered
for these outstanding positions.

Must have current experience with Cobol, MVS, JCL, CICS, IDMS, or revelant
mainframe database experience. Degreed good, but not required if you have
extensive Cobol Programming experience.

You will be responsible for coding, testing, and maintaining programs and
systems.

Fax, or Email your resume to us with contact information and email address.

Thank you,
Dawn Palumbo
Excalibur Executive Consulting and Search Firm
<http://members.aol.com/exexesrc/intro.html>
Fax. (216) 941-3776
Tel. (216) 941-3332 (Mon-Sat, 9am-4pm - EST)

<a id="t-ad9f972673"></a>
## Thread: What my computer said: — 1 msgs

### What my computer said:

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v6al7$2kd$2@news.igs.net>`

```
Imagine if you will ... the leader of the fifth invader force speaking to
the commander in chief ... reporting what was found upon visiting the planet
Earth.


"They're made out of meat."

"Meat?"

"Meat. They're made out of meat."

"Meat?"

"There's no doubt about it. We picked several from different parts of the
planet, took them aboard our recon vessels, probed them all the way
through. They're completely meat."

"That's impossible. What about the radio signals? The messages to the
stars."

"They use the radio waves to talk, but the signals don't come from them. The
signals come from machines."

"So who made the machines? That's who we want to contact."

"They made the machines. That's what I'm trying to tell you. Meat made the
machines."

"That's ridiculous. How can meat make a machine? You're asking me to believe
in sentient meat."

"I'm not asking you, I'm telling you. These creatures are the only sentient
race in the sector and they're made out of meat."

"Maybe they're like the Orfolei. You know, a carbon-based intelligence that
goes through a meat stage."

"Nope. They're born meat and they die meat. We studied them for several of
their life spans, which didn't take too long. Do you have any idea
the life span of meat?"

"Spare me. Okay, maybe they're only part meat. You know, like the Weddilei.
A meat head with an electron plasma brain inside."

"Nope. We thought of that, since they do have meat heads like the Weddilei.
But I told you, we probed them. They're meat all the way
through."

"No brain?"

"Oh, there is a brain all right. It's just that the brain is made out of
meat!"

"So... what does the thinking?"

"You're not understanding, are you? The brain does the thinking. The meat."

"Thinking meat! You're asking me to believe in thinking meat!"

"Yes, thinking meat! Conscious meat! Loving meat. Dreaming meat. The meat is
the whole deal! Are you getting the picture?"

"Omigod. You're serious then. They're made out of meat."

"Finally, Yes. They are indeed made out meat. And they've been trying to get
in touch with us for almost a hundred of their years."

"So what does the meat have in mind?"

"First it wants to talk to us. Then I imagine it wants to explore the
universe, contact other sentients, swap ideas and information. The usual."

"We're supposed to talk to meat?"

"That's the idea. That's the message they're sending out by radio. 'Hello.
Anyone out there? Anyone home?' That sort of thing."

"They actually do talk, then. They use words, ideas, concepts?"

"Oh, yes. Except they do it with meat."

"I thought you just told me they used radio."

"They do, but what do you think is on the radio? Meat sounds. You know how
when you slap or flap meat it makes a noise? They talk by
flapping their meat at each other. They can even sing by squirting air
through their meat."

"Omigod. Singing meat. This is altogether too much. So what do you advise?"

"Officially or unofficially?"

"Both."

"Officially, we are required to contact, welcome, and log in any and all
sentient races or multibeings in the quadrant, without prejudice, fear, or
favor. Unofficially, I advise that we erase the records and forget the whole
thing."

"I was hoping you would say that."

"It seems harsh, but there is a limit. Do we really want to make contact
with meat?"

"I agree one hundred percent. What's there to say?" `Hello, meat. How's it
going?' But will this work? How many planets are we dealing with
here?"

"Just one. They can travel to other planets in special meat containers, but
they can't live on them. And being meat, they only travel through C
space. Which limits them to the speed of light and makes the possibility of
their ever making contact pretty slim. Infinitesimal, in fact."

"So we just pretend there's no one home in the universe."

"That's it."

"Cruel. But you said it yourself, who wants to meet meat? And the ones who
have been aboard our vessels, the ones you have probed? You're
sure they won't remember?"

"They'll be considered crackpots if they do. We went into their heads and
smoothed out their meat so that we're just a dream to them."

"A dream to meat! How strangely appropriate, that we should be meat's
dream."

"And we can mark this sector unoccupied."

"Good. Agreed, officially and unofficially. Case closed. Any others? Anyone
interesting on that side of the galaxy?"

"Yes, a rather shy but sweet hydrogen core cluster intelligence in a class
nine star in G445 zone. Was in contact two galactic rotations ago,
wants to be friendly again."

"They always come around."

"And why not? Imagine how unbearably, how utterly, cold this galaxy would be
if one were all alone with no-one to talk to but meat."

THE END.
```

<a id="t-f77c0b67e3"></a>
## Thread: HELP! College Student --binary bit manipulation — 1 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-f77c0b67e3)._

#### ↳ Re: HELP! College Student --binary bit manipulation

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6v6hee$qst$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6uob21$54q@news.southeast.net>`

```
Jason,

    Conceptualize exactly what an 'NI' (AND-IMMEDIATE) accomplishes. Example
in IBM ASSEMBLER ===> NI    BYTE, B'01000000' with 'BYTE' equal to X'FF'
before the instruction. Subsequently, you'll wind up with a X'40' (A SPACE
IN EBCDIC).

    Now, in COBOL, set up a table of S9(04) BINARY fields, with the
following values:

Occurs 08 ===> 128
Occurs 07 ===> 064
Occurs 06 ===> 032
Occurs 05 ===> 016
Occurs 04 ===> 008
Occurs 03 ===> 004
Occurs 02 ===> 002
Occurs 01 ===> 001

03 WS-TBL OCCURS 8 TIMES PIC S9(04) BINARY.

Let's say you want to turn-off the 8th-Bit (leftmost) in a field called
WS-BYTE, which has a value of X'C0'. Move WS-BYTE to the low-order of a
redefined halfword, ensure the halfword's high-order byte is X'00' and
SUBTRACT the value found in occurrence 08 in the table, which is decimal 128
(X'80'). The result will be X'40', which is B'01000000'. You've just issued
an 'AND-IMMEDIATE' but in COBOL.

03    WS-HALFWORD    PIC S9(04)    BINARY
03    WS-HALFWORD-X    REDEFINES WS-HALFWORD PIC X(02).
03    WS-BYTE PIC X(01) VALUE X'C0'.

MOVE LOW-VALUE TO WS-HALFWORD-X (1:1).
MOVE WS-BYTE TO WS-HALFWORD-X (2:1).
SUBTRACT WS-TBL (8) FROM WS-HALFWORD.

WS-HALFWORD-X now equals X'0040', which is decimal 64. Now, MOVE
WS-HALFWORD-X (2:1) to WS-BYTE and you've got your answer.

You can now use the table components to turn-on and turn-off bits in a given
byte.

HTH....

WOB,
Atlanta

Jason Bogin wrote in message <6uob21$54q@news.southeast.net>...
>I have a project that requires us to change certain bits in a single byte.
>For example: Prompt the user for what bit to change (1-8), prompt for 1 or
0
>and then we must come up with the mask and change the original and finally
>write it to disk.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
On Sun, 04 Oct 1998 19:20:48 GMT, Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>Thanks for all the info!  So I guess it's OK to give your credit card
>number online, huh?  I ordered Unleashed from Amazon; Bookpool was
>cheaper, but I didn't have all the info they required at my fingertips.

Even unecrypted credit card transfers are no more risky than giving your card
to a possibly disreputable waiter at a restaraunt. With even export-grade
encryption, it's *much* safer. The cost in computer time to crack an SSL
session is higher than the limit on most cards...
```

## Thread: embedded sql — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### embedded sql

- **From:** "Jean Davison" <jeandavison@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rlRR1.1248$Ar1.1408778@news.rdc1.sdca.home.com>`

```
need help  with cobol embedded sql school proj. can anyone help? I've done
what I can, but am stuck on a few problems. Any help would be greatly
appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: embedded sql

- **From:** "J���rg Laarmann" <J.Laarmann@online.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361A70F2.5568359E@online.de>`
- **References:** `<rlRR1.1248$Ar1.1408778@news.rdc1.sdca.home.com>`

```
Hi Jean!

Which problem do you have?
Which computer system and and which operation system do you use?
Which database do you want to use?
In general, using embedded SQL in cobol is vry simple. You can almost use your

"normal" SQL-Statements. The greatest different is in achieving the data. It
is like reading a sequential file. You have to do a little more work during
compile time, becuase you have to tell then DBMS which data you want to access

via your program.

Have a nice day
Joerg

Jean Davison schrieb:

> need help  with cobol embedded sql school proj. can anyone help? I've done
> what I can, but am stuck on a few problems. Any help would be greatly
> appreciated.
```

if <condition> then

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IF

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-D5O03Y4PZYvM@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6v952n$go9$1@news.netvision.net.il> <6v99cv$k2h$1@camel19.mindspring.com>`

```
On Mon, 5 Oct 1998 02:02:47, SkidMike 
<skidmike@~NOSPAM~mindspring.com> wrote:

> if <condition> then
>     <do something>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-evaluate.  is evaluate preferred over if?

Different purposes with some overlap.

As a GENERAL rule, if you have an IF IMMEDIATELY after an ELSE with no
other intervening code - consider using Evaluate instead.
```

Kennet

<a id="t-a5066f0a95"></a>
## Thread: how to determine string length ? — 4 this month / 38 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-a5066f0a95)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981004173810.05213.00011230@ngol04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<1998Sep28.074201.19290@giant>`

```

In article <1998Sep28.074201.19290@giant>, cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
Westbury) writes:

>    01 WorkArea.
>       02 FILLER PIC X(01) VALUE LOW-VALUE.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    END-PERFORM

If the FILLER had a value of SPACE instead of LOW-VALUE, I could see how that
logic would work.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298277.68050.7584@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<19981004173810.05213.00011230@ngol04.aol.com>`

```
In message <<19981004173810.05213.00011230@ngol04.aol.com>> mark0young@aol.com  writes:
> In article <1998Sep28.074201.19290@giant>, cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
> Westbury) writes:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mark A. Young

If it had a value of SPACE then the logic would not work.
LOW-VALUE is not SPACE and thus would terminate the perform
when Max20 was all spaces.

There are (IMHO) better ways of doing this though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ef2S1.16$Hz2.10009@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1998Sep28.074201.19290@giant> <19981004173810.05213.00011230@ngol04.aol.com>`

```
Mark0Young wrote:
>
>Chris Westbury writes:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>that logic would work.

It would not work if FILLER was a space, because Ptr would eventually
become negative and blow the program if Max20 was all spaces.  The idea
is that by putting a value in the search range which will terminate the
loop if no actual match is found, you obviate the need for a limit test.
This method is also useful in sequential table searches (e.g. SEARCH with
no ALL).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to determine string length ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct6.081754.19309@giant>`
- **References:** `<1998Sep28.074201.19290@giant> <19981004173810.05213.00011230@ngol04.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981004173810.05213.00011230@ngol04.aol.com>,
mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young) wrote:
>
> In article <1998Sep28.074201.19290@giant>,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> how that logic would work.

One common kind of loop executes two conditionals per iteration at run
time, one for the terminating condition and one to protect the loop from
running off into nowhere.

The idea of a sentinel loop is to arrange the data so that only one
conditional per iteration need be executed at run time.  In other words,
the terminating conditional is arranged so that it also prevents the loop
from running off into nowhere.

A previous poster was looking for the last non-space in MAX20.  The
terminating conditional is easy.  But what if MAX20 is all spaces?  A
simple loop would then fall off the beginning of MAX20.  The poster
therefore used the common dual-conditional loop in which the second
conditional protects against PTR passing zero.

However, if you arrange it so that the byte immediately preceding
MAX20(1:1) is guaranteed not to be a space, then you don't need the
second conditional at all!  The loop will automatically stop when PTR is
zero without your having to test it at run time.

If the FILLER were SPACE, we would be right back where we started.  If
PTR ran off the beginning of MAX20, it would not be stopped by the
FILLER, and it would then run off the beginning of WORKAREA.
```

<a id="t-67943038e1"></a>
## Thread: Eztrieve and comp-3 data? — 1 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [1998-09](1998-09.md#t-67943038e1)._

#### ↳ Re: Eztrieve and comp-3 data?

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981003220819.08204.00007165@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>`

```

In article <360496AB.ECF293C4@bcpl.net>, Steve Young <syoung@bcpl.net> writes:

>				eff-date     34    8   P    0
>

The "8" is the length in bytes, so it represents 15 digits (not 16 that another
message mentioned--the right half of the rightmost byte is a sign, not a
digit).

If the COBOL COPY book contains PIC S9(8) COMP-3, it is a _five-byte_ field (8
half-bytes containing digits, followed by a sign half-byte, but since only
integral number of bytes may be present in a field, there is a leading
half-byte of zeroes, so that makes 10 half-bytes or 5 bytes). (It is also 5
bytes even if unsigned--the sign in that case would be X'F', at least on IBM
mainframes.)

So the Easytrieve-Plus equivalent of COMP-3 PIC S9(8) is __ 5 P 0 (where the __
should be replaced by the starting location, in decimal).

Mark A. Young
```

<a id="t-3d39278271"></a>
## Thread: COBOL 74 — 18 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COBOL 74

- **From:** quiteman25@aol.com (Quiteman25)
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981005173410.11769.00007076@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```

My school is beginning to annoy me with the quality of computers they have in
the  labs, the materials they hand out, and now the software they present to
the students.

My current course is Data Structures using Cobol, and while I realize the
emphasis isn't on the Cobol language itself I do however feel slighted that
they only offer us an ancient Micro Focus compiler that runs in DOS and really
seems to suck. IMO.

I have to ask you pro types what you think of a school that would only use
COBOL 74 on the MICROFOCUS 1986 compiler.  (They even stick to this set-up for
the actual Cobol programming classes.  They don't even support Cobol 85 or
extended 85.)

Again, I completely understand the purpose of the class but it doesn't sit well
with me that we are using such antiquated software/hardware.  I am also
wondering what I'm missing out on because of the compiler and specifications of
74 vs cobol 85.

I asked them why we were using what we are and the explanation that I got was
that on alot of business systems COBOL 74 is the norm.  Yet I see no one
mention it in this news group.

I'd appreciate any valid comments.

Thanks,

~Q
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 74

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vbi1k$gp9@sjx-ixn2.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981005173410.11769.00007076@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```

Quiteman25 wrote in message
<19981005173410.11769.00007076@ng-fa1.aol.com>...
>
  <much snippage>
>
>I asked them why we were using what we are and the explanation that I got
was
>that on alot of business systems COBOL 74 is the norm.  Yet I see no one
>mention it in this news group.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I know that it will outrage many readers of this newsgroup, but ANS'74
syntax *is* still quite common in IBM mainframe shops.  This occurs whenever
the older (currently unsupported but still in relatively common use) OS/VS
COBOL compiler is used with the LANGLVL(2) compiler option.  What may
surprise you, is that ANS'68 syntax is STILL in common usage when the
LANGLVL(1) compiler option of OS/VS COBOL is in use.  (There was a thread
about this a few weeks ago in the newsgroup.)

For IBM mainframe shops, the move from OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II or
COBOL/370 or COBOL for MVS & VM or COBOL for OS/390 & VM (the *4* compilers
later than the one that your teachers think is common) was a quite painful
migration.  It involved a LOT more than just upgrading a few syntax
features.  Therefore, many shops were (and still are) slow in upgrading.

P.S. Even with the newer compilers, when you compile with the CMPR2 compiler
option, then you are still (basically) limited to ANS'74 syntax.

Just to make one more thing clear.
   I think that there is NO EXCUSE for using a 1985 version of the Micro
Focus product and any of the more recent (currently supported) versions
would allow you to select whether you want ANS'74 or ANS'85 or IBM
compatible syntax or whatever.  You school shouldn't even use cost as an
excuse, because Micro Focus has programs to make it quite affordable for
schools to get and maintain their more current compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 74

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361A5987.3E87@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<19981005173410.11769.00007076@ng-fa1.aol.com> <6vbi1k$gp9@sjx-ixn2.ix.netcom.com> <361f3ac8.7385934@news.apk.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 5 Oct 1998 17:34:55 -0500, "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:

[ much snippage; not sure if the attribution is right ]

> >Just to make one more thing clear.
> >   I think that there is NO EXCUSE for using a 1985 version of the Micro
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the concepts of data structures.

If the point of the course is to teach the principles of data
structures, then COBOL '74 is not a good choice of language, and
neither is COBOL '85.

I understand the term "data structures" to include things like linked
lists, stacks, queues, and various species of trees.  It is awkward
to implement these kinds of things in a language which lacks pointers
and dynamic allocation of memory.  You can allocate objects from
static arrays, using array subscripts instead of pointers, and in
some cases I've done exactly that.  However, the resulting machinery
is clumsy enough to obscure the principles which the course is trying
to teach.

In fairness, even COBOL '74 would be better than primitive dialects of 
FORTRAN or BASIC.  Other dialects of COBOL (up to the upcoming standard) 
don't support pointers and dynamic allocation of memory either, except
as vendor extensions.  Even with these extensions the syntax for 
pointers is clumsier and more error-prone than it is in C or Pascal, 
but the extensions at least give you an option you wouldn't otherwise 
have.

For teaching data structures per se I'd rather use something like
C or Pascal, depending on the expected background of the students.
If the point of the course is more specifically to teach COBOL 
implementations of data structures, then COBOL '74 is a defensible
choice because it is approximately a subset of later dialects.  Code
which works in COBOL '74 will work with any later COBOL, possibly with
some minor tweaks.  Still, it would be instructive to use a dialect
where pointers and dynamic memory allocation were available.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 74

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vjpbl$l9v@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981005173410.11769.00007076@ng-fa1.aol.com> <6vbi1k$gp9@sjx-ixn2.ix.netcom.com> <361D546A.5B3B@aol.com>`

```

Kitty Carr wrote in message <361D546A.5B3B@aol.com>...
  <snip>
>
>Mr. Klein, I have a question, or two.  We are one of those shops who
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Kitty,
  What you are asking about is one of those things that you can ONLY know
for sure with hind-sight.  Certainly, going from DOS/VS COBOL with
LANGLVL(1) on VSE to NOCMPR2 with COBOL for MVS & VM on MVS is doing about 6
migrations - all in one giant step.

The advantage of doing this type of giant-leap migration is that WHEN
EVERYTHING WORKS CORRECTLY, then you only need a single step of conversion
and a single time of "testing" to verify that your applications are all
working as expected.

The disadvantage of this type of migration, is that when ANYTHING GOES
WRONG, then you don't know which of the steps caused the problem.  Was it
the VSE to MVS changes?  Was the LANGLVL(1) to ANS'85 changes that caused
the problems?  Was it something else (JCL? CICS? other?)

Personally, I would be petrified of the specific migration that your shop is
going thru - as it is changing SO MANY things ALL AT ONCE.  On the other
hand, I could imagine (a very EXPENSIVE) set of experts who could be hired
to do the migration in question.

As far as the COBOL itself goes, I would estimate that the "best" LANGLVL(1)
DOS/VS COBOL program and the best NOCMPR2 COBOL for MVS & VM (or even VS
COBOL II) program that were intended to do the same thing would have well
over 80% and maybe as much as  95% identical source code.  COBOL just
hasn't/doesn't change that much.   The problem is that what has/does change
requires an understanding of the changes - and from what you have said
previously, I do NOT think that is true at your shop (or the hired company
that is trying to  do the migration for you).

Does this help? Address your question?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ve19f$fkq@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981005173410.11769.00007076@ng-fa1.aol.com> <6vdono$971$1@front1.grolier.fr>`

```

Manu wrote in message <6vdono$971$1@front1.grolier.fr>...
>I think your school doesn't want to spend money for buying an up to date
>compiler.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Incorrect, IBM most certainly does still support the ANS'74 dialect of
COBOL.  Even if IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (IBM's most recent COBOL product),
the compiler option "CMPR2" provides ANS'74 support.

No only are there shops still using the OS/VS COBOL compiler (even though it
is unsupported) but many shops use CMPR2 - particularly to avoid File
Status=39 problems.

FYI, *all* releases of VS COBOL II (not COBOL II) are labeled as NOT year
2000 ready (although there are no know problems with Release 4.0 - and it is
announced for support thru March of 2001).

Finally, DOS/VS COBOL (which also has the LANGLVL(1) support for ANS'68 as
well as ANS'74) is still supported - but will go out of support before Jan
1, 2000.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 74

- **From:** quiteman25@aol.com (Quiteman25)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981006173903.09342.00007415@ng109.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6ve19f$fkq@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```

Thanks very much for the responses.

The fact that the course is about data structure is irrelevant.  That is review
for me, cobol itself is not.  I aim to take full advantage of the time I spend
in the class to learn as much as possible about COBOL itself.

My main question is 'what am I missing by only having an old compiler and only
using COBOL 74'?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 74

- **From:** "Ted Pack" <tedpacknospam@thevision.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdf1ac$d8dbe300$6f01d2d1@sranmojg>`
- **References:** `<19981005173410.11769.00007076@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
Easy answer - give the school $100,000 to upgrade the lab or stop
complaining. The first job you get probably won't come complete with a red
convertible and a window office, either.

Thomas Chippendale didn't have power tools and he managed to do all right.
Verbs are verbs -- if you use more than ten you are showing off. I've made
a comfortable living for 20 years with OPEN, CLOSE, READ, WRITE, IF,
PERFORM, MOVE and COMPUTE. My corporation manufactures, stores and sells
its products.

Learn the basics, by which I mean thinking. The tools you use don't matter
as much as the skills with which you use them. I do woodwork as a hobby,
and have $2,000 worth of power tools in my garage. Believe me, Thomas
Chippendale, working with "Tools 1780", was about 100 times better than me
working with "Tools 1990".
```

>2) Is that some kind of emulation environment on PC that I could use  IMS,
>TSO/DB2,ICCF  ?


Sorry but it is not possible. There is no chance. Before this question I
have another; "why emulate this environmment in a PC" ??

I believe that you are a person interested in to learn COBOL in OS/390 and
TSO environment and, it is possible if you are to ready to begin as trainee
programmer in an enterprise with this systems.
But, don�t create confusion in your mind. PC and mainframes are differents
in radical form.
In future, a PC environmment can be emulate in OS/390, but there is no way
to invert this situation.

Good luck

>

<a id="t-68a3da8077"></a>
## Thread: The future of Cobol — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### The future of Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com>`

```
I am writing a paper for school, discussing technologies such as Cobol
still being viable, and sometimes preferable to some of the more modern
technologies.

So I am looking for references and ideas.  Anybody have any favorites
they would like to share?
```

#### ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vbcdc$sm4@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com>...
>I am writing a paper for school, discussing technologies such as Cobol
>still being viable, and sometimes preferable to some of the more modern
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>they would like to share?

It is NOT a question of COBOl being 'viable',
nor does new automatically equate to better.

No new programming language or database access methodology
is yet capable of replacing IBM's duo of COBOL and IMS DB/DC.
Even if something were to be developed, why SHOULD we convert?
Because it is new?  Don't insult the intelligence of IS managers.
There is  the issue of the mission critical nature of the existing databases
and the existing COBOL software and the existing systems software
and database backup and reorg programs, etc etc etc.

Companies are reluctant to upgrade desktops from Win 3.1 to Win 95,
or from Win 95 to Win 98.

What makes anyone think they will cavalierly undertake a humungous
conversion
from IMS to...to...what? Conversion to what?  There is no 'viable'
alternative.
Any suggestion that COBOL is not viable is vaporware-level thinking.
I haven't even mentioned the Return On Investment pros and cons.

There are two camps here.  Those of us who deal with mission-critical,
enterprise-level systems 7-by-24, and those who do not.

The former are acutely aware of the desirability of improving systems and
procedures
in a very careful, methodical, risk-limiting manner.

The latter seem to be focussed on change for the sake of change.  That is
wrong.
Change is to be explored, researched and embraced when it makes sense.

Just don't propose a feeble hypothesis such as 'COBOL is not viable' as a
solution
to anything.  Or even as an idea, if you have no documented 'viable'
alternative.

Good luck with your paper.  Just don't go charging at the wrong windmill.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36196834.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com> <6vbcdc$sm4@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Recently, while on contract with CP Rail, I was introduced to their mission
critical "Integrated Train Management" software that was used in their
network management centre.  It pretty much had the same function as an
air-traffic controlling system.  The front end was Delphi and the back end
was IMS!


Hugh Candlin wrote in message <6vbcdc$sm4@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>...

databases
>and the existing COBOL software and the existing systems software
>and database backup and reorg programs, etc etc etc.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298278.75352.3394@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36196834.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
In message <<36196834.0@news.cadvision.com>> "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com> writes:
> critical "Integrated Train Management" software that was used in their
> network management centre.  It pretty much had the same function as an
> air-traffic controlling system.  The front end was Delphi and the back end
> was IMS!

This sounds like client-server with the IMS on a server machine.
In many cases the client software acts much like an intelligent
terminal emulator.  It will present an appropriatly pretty
screen and will do field validation, even help and selections
but eventually the dat is sent to the server where the actual
processing is done.

Delphi and VB as good at being front ends, and for simple 
systems, but suffer when scaled up to larger numbers of
users and more complex data.  For example using the the
simpler PC databases may result in table locking (rather than
record or row locking) presenting serious performance and
usability issues.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361b3fc0.171312514@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<36196834.0@news.cadvision.com> <3298278.75352.3394@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On 6 Oct 98 20:55:52 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
wrote:

>Delphi and VB as good at being front ends, and for simple 
>systems, but suffer when scaled up to larger numbers of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>usability issues.

That's more a problem with the 'free' databases you get such as
Interbase and JET. Any larger system and you use MS SQL Server, Oracle
etc. Those aren't without problems either but it's a matter of picking
your technology.

Of course, most COBOL's don't come with any kind of database engine so
the arquement that COBOL is good or not for C/S is based on how easy
it is to plug it into other systems. Delphi and VB are much easier in
my experience here for thin clients.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-0emudskog4gG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<36196834.0@news.cadvision.com> <3298278.75352.3394@kcbbs.gen.nz> <361b3fc0.171312514@news.freeserve.net>`

```
On Wed, 7 Oct 1998 10:25:08, scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. 
Murray) wrote:

> That's more a problem with the 'free' databases you get such as
> Interbase and JET. Any larger system and you use MS SQL Server, Oracle
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I really hate to be picky  - but who says a database engine is a 
pre-requisite for Client Server.  I have a friend who has been doing 
Client/Server under DOS on a Novell network for years - (before it was
called client server).  He has a centralized program that runs on the 
server that does all of his file I/O, and the applications on the work
stations communicate with this server.  Using NATIVE COBOL indexed 
files, and no third party database.  This *is* client server, and 
there is no outside "database" involved.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361b7aa4.186388312@news.freeserve.net>`

```
On 7 Oct 98 14:47:17 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:


>
>I really hate to be picky  - but who says a database engine is a 
>pre-requisite for Client Server.  

I don't think any did though we were talking of about a particular
installation.

>I have a friend who has been doing 
>Client/Server under DOS on a Novell network for years - (before it was
>called client server).

Heck, I've been doing distributed computing using COBOL I/O for years.

>  He has a centralized program that runs on the 
>server that does all of his file I/O, and the applications on the work
>stations communicate with this server.  Using NATIVE COBOL indexed 
>files, and no third party database.  This *is* client server, and 
>there is no outside "database" involved.

Much as I agree, it doesn't scale well without some significant
engineering work. In much the same way, I wish people wouldn't use
database engines for small systems - the overhead is a killer. As I
said, choose your technology carefully.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36197B16.BAD7A77D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com> <6vbcdc$sm4@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I have over 20 years of COBOL experience, with an emphasis on IDMS.  I
am frequently a Cobol apologist, but my masters program was heavy into
object oriented methodologies.  The professors sometimes mention
"obsolete" technologies such as relational databases.  Well, I was a
physics major as an undergraduate, and I "see" the physical structure of
a hierarchical databases more easily than the mathematical view of
relational databases.  I think object oriented is vastly overrated.

But this is for my capstone, where it can pay off to say something
different from everybody else.  But if I am this different, I had better
back it up!


Hugh Candlin wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> nor does new automatically equate to better.
```
