- **Message-ID:** `<6vdcbf$bpn$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com> <6vbcdc$sm4@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <36197B16.BAD7A77D@home.com> <36197199.878A3B22@earthlink.net> <361A1749.113E9951@home.com> <KbpS1.784$2K1.667255@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
While I tend to agree (and after all, underneath it is
generating procedural code), I think that Richard hit
the nail on the head the other day when he said OO was
essentially a bottom up methodology.  There are times
when a top down approach is best, and other times
when a bottom up approach is best. I think it would
be a mistake to write off OO within Cobol, as it *could*
provide a very vital approach to building toolsets.

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Howard Brazee wrote:
>...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hear! Hear!  Well said!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn71ki8i.h0q.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com> <6vbcdc$sm4@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <36197B16.BAD7A77D@home.com> <36197199.878A3B22@earthlink.net> <361A1749.113E9951@home.com> <KbpS1.784$2K1.667255@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <6vdcbf$bpn$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Tue, 6 Oct 1998 11:27:32 -0400, Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>when a bottom up approach is best. I think it would be a mistake to write off
>OO within Cobol, as it *could* provide a very vital approach to building
>toolsets.

Yes - OOP is a great way of hiding ugly details. How many Win32 API programmers
do you know? Compare that with the number of programmers who use something like
MFC. MFC isn't even a very good library but it's *much* easier than messing
with the raw API.

The downside is that OOP is basically compiler-enforcement for good practice
and must be planned for appropriately. Many of the complaints/failures blamed
on OOP are really failures to use it properly. Not that this is a new
problem...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361B4B89.256542FF@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com> <6vbcdc$sm4@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <36197B16.BAD7A77D@home.com> <36197199.878A3B22@earthlink.net> <361A1749.113E9951@home.com> <KbpS1.784$2K1.667255@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
> ...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

IMHO, if people would stop thinking about OO as something alltogether
different and new, and start thinking more about OO as an extension to
established practices like structured programming and ER, then the whole
picture would get a lot clearer. I would not like to go so far as to say
that OO is the solution to everything (that would'nt be true), but it does
have some nice things that solve problems inherent both in SP and ER. Used
right, it can only enhance programmers abilities to produce better
products. OO is only one step in the evolution of programming practices,
it was'nt the first step and it sure won't be the last. But let us not
disregard the positive things it has just because of the hype around it.

It *is* overrated, though.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff

PD: Whats wrong with pascal? Delphi is very nice and a heck of a lot
easier than C++.
```

#### ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Fg3S1.30$Hz2.32424@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>I am writing a paper for school, discussing technologies such as Cobol
>still being viable, and sometimes preferable to some of the more modern
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>they would like to share?

Check out the following two front page articles:

  Wall Street Journal, April 30, 1998 "Pulling the Plug".

  Info World, June 29, 1998 "Return of the Mainframe".

In brief, recent failure rates for new systems has been about 45%,
from cost and time overruns and failure to meet design goals.  Much of
this is because of the cost and complexity of using client server and
GUI technology.  Many companies are beginning to react to the impact
of this on their bottom line.  Personally, I am convinced that we will
see a growing groundswell of such reaction from companies as these
facts become more widely known and appreciated.  Part of this reaction
is a return to more reliable and cost effective technologies like COBOL.

Another trend: use of COBOL for C/S development.  In the late 80's and
early 90's, there was a huge rush to the new technologies, and away
from COBOL.  But by 1996, surveys showed that, even in C/S development
where COBOL has not been considered strong, COBOL had taken a very close
second place to VB is language of choice, with C a more distant third.
One of the reasons for this, besides cost and reliability, is that
companies have found that mainframe based servers leave any PC based
server in the dust, both in performance and reliability.  Mainframe
operating systems, instead of being uprooted and rewritten every couple
of years for the API du jour, have been steadily evolved and improved
over decades.  The difference that makes in reliability is truly
impressive.
```

>> Another trend: use of COBOL for C/S development.  In the late 80's and

>Too bad it wasn't the front page of PC....... mag(s) but then "hype"
>sells a lot better than "truth".

Quite true. I've heard the same thing commented on about OS/2 - the magazines
preferred to play with Windows because a review like "We installed it and
turned it on. We started to get some useful work done. 6 months later it was
still working and we never had to reboot." would sell very few magazines
whereas Windows always has new patches, bugfixes and workarounds to hype up. I
consider it troubling that so many people would ignore a language that just
works because it's not trendy enough...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The future of Cobol

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3619e48d.82429347@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<3618347B.D2E7B5DE@home.com> <Fg3S1.30$Hz2.32424@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Mon, 05 Oct 1998 12:50:45 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>In brief, recent failure rates for new systems has been about 45%,
>from cost and time overruns and failure to meet design goals.  Much of
>this is because of the cost and complexity of using client server and
>GUI technology.  

I wouldn't have thought that was the actual cause. More than likely,
it's bad estimating. You are hampered a bit by Microsoft as they often
throw a spanner in the works where something is supposed to work but
just plain doesn't. Dial-Up Networking API comes to mind.

Certainly where I work, work is done in Delphi, VB, VC, Java, COBOL,
C, Oracle, MS SQL and loads more I've bound to have forgot but we very
rarely miss targets or fail to predict correctly. Where we do, it's
rarely a function of the language chosen.

I think the problem is often that the analysts on these systems have
little experience of GUI and C/S rapid development as the toolsets
change rapidly themselves. This has matured a bit lately (at least
until Java) but it's less easy to predict than a straight COBOL
development job.

>Many companies are beginning to react to the impact
>of this on their bottom line.  Personally, I am convinced that we will
>see a growing groundswell of such reaction from companies as these
>facts become more widely known and appreciated.  Part of this reaction
>is a return to more reliable and cost effective technologies like COBOL.

There is nothing particularly unreliable about other technologies
given the right staff and experience.

>
>Another trend: use of COBOL for C/S development.  In the late 80's and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>second place to VB is language of choice, with C a more distant third.

This stems from peple realizing that they need a strong backend to an
application. Whereas the server part may be written in a more robust
language for data handling, the client tends to be written in a more
graphically oriented language still.

>One of the reasons for this, besides cost and reliability, is that
>companies have found that mainframe based servers leave any PC based
>server in the dust, both in performance and reliability.

That's a sweeping statement! Whereas I agree totally, you have to also
add in cost.

>  Mainframe
>operating systems, instead of being uprooted and rewritten every couple
>of years for the API du jour, have been steadily evolved and improved
>over decades.  The difference that makes in reliability is truly
>impressive.

Yeah, but look what it's competing against in the PC world. The
Microsoft OS is a kuldge on a kludge of a quick hack OS that was never
intended to be used running on esentially the same hardware built in
the early 80's to a design that was out of date in the late 70's.

And your surprised the mainframe is more reliable ;-)
```

## Thread: IBM MVS/TSO or OS/390 dynamic allocation of dataset name — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM MVS/TSO or OS/390 dynamic allocation of dataset name

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.com (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981005201537.26585.00003572@ng97.aol.com>`

```

I'd like to find out if there is a way to dynamically allocate/change the
dataset name referenced in a JCL DDname.  My objective is to have a single FD
in the COBOL program to reference either a LOADLIB or PRINT PDS for input to
allow browsing of a user specified member for KEY strings to be extracted and
placed in an output report file.

I've seen a couple of different posts on this basic thread.  The sample
altering DDnames works get, but is not a fit for the specific project that I
have in mind.  There was another sample that had inline Assembler code (doesn't
work well with the environment COBOL for MVS).  The assembler sample would have
been ideal (if I knew how the dataset is being allocated) assuming that the
dynamic allocation allows for DISP=SHR or DISP=OLD .
```

>It is possible that you need look no further than ISPF itself. The
Utilities
>Menu (which is option 3 in most shops), has a submenu option that allows
you to
>list the members of a PDS (usually function X), and you can send it to your
>current .LIST dataset. When you do that hit PF1 to get an exact DSN.


Once you get the list of members on screen, type "SAVE MEMLIST" this should
save what is in the current dataset list to a flat file as I believe:
USERID.MEMLIST.DATASET.  I may be wrong about the extension. From the DSLIST
screen type HELP SAVE to find the specifics. This may have the info
mentioned above. All of the files matching the selection criteria on the
first DSLIST screen will be listed, not just what is on screen.
```

<a id="t-bba7baa3e8"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and C — 4 msgs

### COBOL and C

- **From:** "Mike Kozelka" <mkozelka@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vb2fi$lr7@newsops.execpc.com>`

```
        I'm trying to link to a C library of routines on a PC.  I don't know
anything about C.  Below is the C structure I'm supposed to use.  I
think I know what some of these items are; see the Linkage Section
following.
        My question is: What are the COBOL definitions of the items in the
Linkage Section that have the '?' ?.  Any help would be appreciated.

              C Structure:
typedef struct z_stream_s {
    Bytef    *next_in;  /* next input byte */
    uInt     avail_in;  /* number of bytes available at next_in */
    uLong    total_in;  /* total nb of input bytes read so far */

    Bytef    *next_out; /* next output byte should be put there */
    uInt     avail_out; /* remaining free space at next_out */
    uLong    total_out; /* total nb of bytes output so far */

    char     *msg;      /* last error message, NULL if no error */
    struct internal_state FAR *state; /* not visible by applications */

    alloc_func zalloc;  /* used to allocate the internal state */
    free_func  zfree;   /* used to free the internal state */
    voidpf     opaque;  /* private data object passed to other routines */

    int     data_type;  /* best guess about the data type: ascii or binary
*/
    uLong   adler;      /* adler32 value of the data */
    uLong   reserved;   /* reserved for future use */
} z_stream;

typedef z_stream FAR *z_streamp;

                        COBOL Linkage Section

       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  Z-STREAM-STRUCTURE.
           05  NEXT-IN              POINTER.
           05  AVAIL-IN             PIC 9(04) COMP-5 VALUE 0.
           05  TOTAL-IN             PIC 9(08) COMP-5 VALUE 0.
           05  NEXT-OUT             POINTER.
           05  AVAIL-OUT            PIC 9(04) COMP-5 VALUE 0.
           05  TOTAL-OUT            PIC 9(08) COMP-5 VALUE 0.
           05  ERR-MSG              POINTER.
           05  INTERNAL-STATE       POINTER.
           05  ALLOC-FUNC           ?
           05  FREE-FUNC            ?
           05  OPAQUE               ?
           05  DATA-TYPE            PIC 9(04) COMP-5 VALUE 1.
           05  ADLER32              PIC 9(08) COMP-5 VALUE 0.
           05  RESERVE-IT           PIC 9(08) COMP-5 VALUE 0.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Z-STREAM-STRUCTURE.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and C

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn71i8c4.h0q.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<6vb2fi$lr7@newsops.execpc.com>`

```
On Mon, 5 Oct 1998 09:05:26 -0500, Mike Kozelka <mkozelka@execpc.com> wrote:
>        I'm trying to link to a C library of routines on a PC.  I don't know
>anything about C.  Below is the C structure I'm supposed to use.  I
>think I know what some of these items are; see the Linkage Section
>following.

What compiler are you using? 

>        My question is: What are the COBOL definitions of the items in the
>Linkage Section that have the '?' ?.  Any help would be appreciated.

>    alloc_func zalloc;  /* used to allocate the internal state */
>    free_func  zfree;   /* used to free the internal state */
>    voidpf     opaque;  /* private data object passed to other routines */

These are all custom types that have been declared somewhere in your C program,
probably in the header files. You can use a tool like grep to search through
the source to find out just what these are declared to be.  

>           05  INTERNAL-STATE       POINTER.

As a side comment, the C code referred to this as *state. It might be a good
idea to keep the names the same to avoid confusion. 
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and C

- **From:** Scott McKellar <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361961D9.2699@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<6vb2fi$lr7@newsops.execpc.com>`

```
(posted and mailed)

Mike Kozelka wrote:
> 
>         I'm trying to link to a C library of routines on a PC.  I don't know
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>        PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Z-STREAM-STRUCTURE.

As Chris Adams has already pointed out, the C datatypes in question
(alloc_func, free_func, and voidpf) are not defined by the C language.
They are presumably defined somewhere in a header file that came
with your library.

However, the following are reasonable guesses:

alloc_func and free_func are probably function pointers.  The
equivalent in COBOL, in dialects which support it, is called a
procedure pointer.  If your dialect doesn't support it then you
can't use them from COBOL anyway; just declare FILLER of the
appropriate sizes -- either two bytes or four, depending on the 
compiler and memory model.

A voidpf is probably a void pointer, i.e. a generic pointer to an 
unspecified datatype.  COBOL pointers are untyped (in the few 
dialects I'm familiar with, anyway), so just call it POINTER.

Keep in mind that C does not guarantee that the members of a struct
are contiguous in memory.  The compiler is at liberty to insert padding
bytes wherever it pleases in order to maintain byte alignment. 
Intel-based compilers typically give the user a compile-time option to 
suppress padding bytes.  You'll need to find out how this option was 
set for whatever C code you're interfacing to.  In the presence of
padding bytes it may take some experimentation -- preferably with the
help of a C programmer -- to determine the appropriate offset for 
each member.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

#### ↳ parameter passing intro was Re: COBOL and C

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36248e70.10521378@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6vb2fi$lr7@newsops.execpc.com>`

```
On Mon, 5 Oct 1998 09:05:26 -0500, "Mike Kozelka"
<mkozelka@execpc.com> wrote:

>        My question is: What are the COBOL definitions of the items in the
>Linkage Section that have the '?' ?.  Any help would be appreciated.

A SECOND GLANCE (i wrote this after thinking long about it)

look up CBL_Byte stream functions.
quite frankly, i think the C programmer is giving you the runaround.
COBOL has byte stream functions. the only real parameters that your
C program might have that the CBL doesn't would be the opaque
function pointer and best-guess data. for that, you would have to
define opaque as external, and verify it exists upon running your
COBOL program.


A FIRST GLANCE (an answer to your initial question)

probably all are procedure pointer.

voidpf is probably a void pointer to function. however, the other two
are not vpfs, so my best guess is that they take parameters.

however, i would make certain the parameters you pass are matched
exactly by reference or value, the correct USAGE, and exactly as the C
and COBOL parameter standards given by both compilers. make sure to
write comments in your source code for parameter passing, and how
parameter passing works from the COBOL and C compiler in
your COBOL code. parameter orders for C are pascal or C based, and you
should include docs on both, as well as the default method.

alittle more about usage. data and code might in the COBOL compiler be
defined with less or more space than that in the C compiler. matching
this data 1:1 is an absolute necessity. you can write error checking
code for verifying your first time run of the data, but if the
function pointers are passed wrong, your machine will crash anyways.

one thing to make certain is the two outside data variables are
verified the first run through. thus:

    struct internal_state FAR *state; /* not visible by applications
*/

should be verified as should
    int     data_type;  /* best guess about the data type: ascii or
binary
*/

this will make certain your function pointers at *least* take up the
right amount of space (as they are sandwiched between those two
variables), which is 1 method of verifying they are passed correctly.

this assumes the sub-structures are not aligned on boundaries.

now, pretend you want to assign a value to be passed to your alloc.
you first must make certain you are passing the correct *size*
variable. (for instance, the FAR variable tells me you r using a
segmented architecture)

get your C programmer to tell you the size of the function pointers.

printf ( "%d bytes",	sizeof(	functionpointertype)	);

where functionpointertype = alloc free or opaque should give you an
idea of what size of function pointer you are dealing with.

another note, make certain that you include info on returned
variables, and verify at least 1 returned variable.

on some stack based machines, variables may be changed, but returned
variables may not be removed from the stack, and cause the machine to
crash. make a comment in your source code that this is a possibility,
under the environment, object computer section.

and finally , after you get your cobol program running, assert the
program you connect to's date has not changed.
a changed date may mean changes in the structure types.

if it is changed, halt the computer, send a message to the operator
with info to the C source code file name (if possible) or company name
and phone number (if not), and offer to continue the program, or halt
the machine, with a variable that doesn't ask until the next time the
cobol prog is run.

now your cobol program is bullet proofed and commented.

So lets look at the steps involved:

a>verify correct USAGE clauses

b>verify data is passed
	1>on boundaries or not
	2>by reference or value
	3>tests same value in called and calling programs
c>verify code pointers are sandwiched between two data statements
d>verify code pointers are correct size
	1>document all far and near types in COBOL source
e>verify and document returned variables
f>verify stacks position before and after called function

thats a 1 way parameter pass. 2 way parameter passing requires
you to verify which data/code is memory managed.



gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

## Thread: Test your COBOL II knowledge at new site - top ten list has been updated! — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Test your COBOL II knowledge at new site - top ten list has been updated!

- **From:** Michael Littman <mikelittman@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36195046.FDB2A3E7@erols.com>`

```
DearCOBOL II programmer,

Per the requests of those of you who already took the beta test of our
COBOL II knowledge test (thank you!!!), we have updated the 'top ten'
list of best scores to date.

We are still beta testing our web-based COBOL II knowledge test.  The
format is
multiple choice.  To take the test, simply go to www.123apply.com and
select the link,
"Take a beta test".  The rest should be self-explanatory.  Before taking
the test, check out
the 'top ten' list to see the best scores to date!

Upon completion of the test, you will be scored 1-5 (1-Novice,
2-Beginner,
3-Intermediate, 4-Advanced and 5-Master).  The test is actually
comprised of two tests
of 20 questions each (that's two chances to make the top ten!).  Any
feedback you can
provide us on the quality of the questions and the test itself would be
greatly appreciated.
You will be given the opportunity to provide feedback on each question
as it is
administered as well as at the end of the test.

The test is delivered in our production environment, so things might
seem a little 'strange'
prior to taking the test.  However, any information you provide will be
kept anonymous
(unless you make the top ten - we figured the bragging rights would make
it worth it!).

If you have any problems getting to the site, or have any questions in
general, please
feel free to contact me.  We hope that you enjoy the test and look
forward to your
feedback!

Good luck!

Mike
```

<a id="t-d77cfe05ae"></a>
## Thread: Creating PDS members from a batch program — 5 msgs

### Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** don_weigend@email.com (Donald Weigend)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103_907704975@hen.arinet.com>`

```
Greetings All,

Does anyone have any COBOL code examples that create members in a PDS as output. I would like to enhance a 
program that generates JCL for several batch jobs. This program currently stores all the JCL together in a single 
sequential file, I then have to split these jobs up manually. I would appreciate any help anyone can give.

Donald Weigend
SPR Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O1xS1.253$Ex.2816277@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<1103_907704975@hen.arinet.com>`

```
>Does anyone have any COBOL code examples that create members in a PDS as
output. I would like to enhance a
>program that generates JCL for several batch jobs. This program currently
stores all the JCL together in a single
>sequential file, I then have to split these jobs up manually. I would
appreciate any help anyone can give.


If you wish to do it in REXX batch it would be a simple matter. I have never
done anything like that in COBOL. The REXX would go something like this:

/* REXX */
ARG FLAT_FILE_IN PDS_OUT
"ALLOC FI(JCLIN) DA('"FLAT_FILE"') SHR REUSE"
"EXECIO * DISKR JCLIN (STEM JCLIN.L. FINIS"
"FREE FI(JCLIN)"
"ALLOC FI(JCLOUT) DA('"PDS_OUT"(DUMMY)') SHR REUSE"
DO A=1 TO JCLIN.L.0
    PARSE VALUE JCLIN.L.A WITH PRE'//'JOBNAME' JOB 'SUFF
    JOBNAME=STRIP(JOBNAME)
    IF PRE=''&LENGTH(JOBNAME<=8)&LENGTH(JOBNAME)>0 THEN DO
       "EXECIO 0 DISKW JCLOUT (FINIS"
       "FREE FI(JCLOUT)"
       "ALLOC FI(JCLOUT) DA('"PDS_OUT"("JOBNAME")') SHR REUSE"
    END
    JCLOUT.L.1=JCLIN.L.A
    "EXECIO 1 DISKW JCLOUT (STEM JCLOUT.L."
END
"EXECIO 0 DISKW JCLOUT (FINIS"
"FREE FI(JCLOUT)"
RETURN


This REXX  could be placed in a control card library and could be executed
with JCL like this:

<<<<<standard jobcard>>>>>
//*******************************************************************
//PARMTEST  EXEC PGM=IKJEFT01,
//      PARM=('MAKEMEMS XXX.FLAT.FILE.IN XXX.PDS.OUT')
//SYSEXEC   DD DSN=XXX.CONTROL.LIB,DISP=SHR
//SYSTSPRT  DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSTSIN   DD DUMMY

This will run the REXX contained in XXX.CONTROL.LIB member name MAKEMEMS
passing XXX.FLAT.FILE and XXX.PDS.OUT as the values for the above ARGuments
FLAT_FILE_IN and PDS_OUT. The members created by the REXX will be the
jobnames on the job card statement: //X1234567 JOB .... would create member
X1234567, all lines following the jobcard will be output to that member
until EOF or another jobcard is found.

Hope this helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** nullify@aol.com (Nullify)
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981007174144.15208.00007508@ng144.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<1103_907704975@hen.arinet.com>`

```

Donald,

You can do this with IEBUPDTE, after minor modification to your COBOL source.

Your COBOL program can continue to write multiple output members to a single
sequential file, but you also write an IEBUPDTE control statement before each
member.

You then use the sequential file as SYSIN input to IEBUPDTE, which uses the
control statements and JCL records to create multiple members on the SYSUT2
PDS. The PDS can be new or already in existence. There's an example of this in
the DFSMS Utilities Manual (MVS Utilities as was).

We had a similar requirement ourselves, and this proved to be the ideal
solution.

Julian Smailes
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361F75EF.66DC0A7E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<1103_907704975@hen.arinet.com>`

```
If you have a known number of JCL's then just use separate DD names and
PDS(MEMNAME) on the DD or am I missing something.

IEBCOPY will allow you to 'backup' a PDS into a sequential file and then
'restore' it.  The way it does this is use a special line between each
member.  If you read the manual or do a backup it would be simple to
create the nessecary lines and then run it through IEBCOPY, no special
code and a standard utility.

HTH
Ken

Donald Weigend wrote:
> 
> Greetings All,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> SPR Inc.
```

<a id="t-db6f73cef6"></a>
## Thread: Creating PDS members from a batch program — 4 msgs

### Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** don_weigend@email.com (Donald Weigend)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104_907704981@hen.arinet.com>`

```
Greetings All,

Does anyone have any COBOL code examples that create members in a PDS as output. I would like to enhance a 
program that generates JCL for several batch jobs. This program currently stores all the JCL together in a single 
sequential file, I then have to split these jobs up manually. I would appreciate any help anyone can give.

Donald Weigend
SPR Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <pdorfma@ucs.att.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361A8C52.B33DE2E3@ucs.att.com>`
- **References:** `<1104_907704981@hen.arinet.com>`

```
Donald Weigend wrote:
> 
> Greetings All,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> SPR Inc.

Donald,

Others may point you in the right direction as to how to do it strictly
in COBOL (it is going to be tricky, to say at least). However, if you
are lucky to have the SAS System installed at your site, check the
procedures PDS, PDSCOPY, and SOURCE. With these, you can do everything
you need and a lot more (including creating inputs for IBM utes, if
necesary). Since these procs are MVS-specific, they are not listed in
the SAS Procedures Guide, but rather in the SAS Companion for the MVS
Environment. The text is amply supplied with examples, so it will not
take you long to get it up and running, even if you have never heard of
SAS before. By the way, the SOURCE procedure is quite useful in
producing a file listing of PDS members with all the satellite
information (almost exactly the way you can see it in 3.4).

Good luck.

Kind regards, Paul.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** PPP <ABCDE@ddd.ddd.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361EC7F2.B3D@ddd.ddd.com>`
- **References:** `<1104_907704981@hen.arinet.com>`

```
Donald Weigend wrote:
> 
> Greetings All,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> SPR Inc.

I've a rather stupid method which may be usefull to you.
The material I need are
A PDS
2 program, 
	PGM A your utility program,
	PGM B my program.
2 JCL, 
	JCL B - to run PGM B and submit the JCL A 
	JCL A - your JCL to execute PGM A but the DD statement are replaced by
symbol.
 e.g. //OUT1     DD DSN=PROJECT1.TASK1.LIB(&S),DISP=SHR 

Details

PGM B will need to accept the member name of the resulting "report"
either from the input card are stored in an DSN.
Then PGM B will read to JCL A and replace the symbol(&M) with the member
name.
Finally PGM B will output the formatted JCL A to "internal reader".

When you want to run JCL A, simply submit JCL B.

Hope you can understand what I written.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Creating PDS members from a batch program

- **From:** "AM" <AM69107@glaxowellcome.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdf5ec$cac8b4c0$c58c3398@us0071826>`
- **References:** `<1104_907704981@hen.arinet.com> <361EC7F2.B3D@ddd.ddd.com>`

```
IEBUPDTE

produced with COBOL II and COBOL FOR MVS.

<a id="t-72fca4dc1a"></a>
## Thread: Cobol for 0S/390 VSAM-Filest. 39 — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Cobol for 0S/390 VSAM-Filest. 39

- **From:** Hans-Georg Decker <Decker@kzvk.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3619FD1D.7E8451D7@kzvk.de>`

```
Hi,

in our company we try to go from IBM-COBOL II to COBOL for
OS/390 & VM.

Now I have a problem:

In a program I have a VSAM-File with a variable
record-length (a fix part plus a part with OCCURS DEPENDING
ON).  Under COBOL II it works fine.

I changed no cluster-information.

The test under the run-time modules of COBOL for OS/390
results an filestatus 39 from the OPEN-statement for that
Vsam-file. When I compile the program with the new compiler
I get the same result.

Did anything have had the same Problem? Who can help me?

Does anyone in germany use the new compiler too?

Geetings Georg
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol for 0S/390 VSAM-Filest. 39

- **From:** John Calahan <jcalahan@magpage.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vdpdl$6vo$0@204.179.92.50>`
- **References:** `<3619FD1D.7E8451D7@kzvk.de>`

```
Georg,

I've experienced the same problem recently. To correct this, add this line
to your file's FD statement in your program:

        RECORD VARYING FROM 1 TO XXXX CHARACTERS

where XXXX represents your maximum record length.

Hope this helps,

John 

On Tue, 6 Oct 1998, Hans-Georg Decker wrote:

> Hi,
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Hans-Georg Decker <Decker@kzvk.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361B6ACC.2635DECE@kzvk.de>`
- **References:** `<3619FD1D.7E8451D7@kzvk.de> <6vdpdl$6vo$0@204.179.92.50>`

```

> Georg,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi John,

the code in the program is

FD RBESTT
      RECORD CONTAINS 4160 to 25146 CHARACTERS.

Do you have had the same clause before and then changed it to RECORD VARYING
...?

Thanks,

Georg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol for 0S/390 VSAM-Filest. 39

- **From:** John Calahan <jcalahan@magpage.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vg5j3$o5l$0@204.179.92.50>`
- **References:** `<3619FD1D.7E8451D7@kzvk.de> <6vdpdl$6vo$0@204.179.92.50> <361B6ACC.2635DECE@kzvk.de>`

```
Georg,

If you've already got that line in there, the next thing I'd look at is
how your VSAM cluster is defined. The definition should be 4 bytes longer
than the record description in the program (COBOL ignores the RDW at the
beginning of both VSAM and QSAM files in its record layouts, from what I
can see). If that doesn't work, let me know. 

John 

On Wed, 7 Oct 1998, Hans-Georg Decker wrote:

> John Calahan schrieb:
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

(CIDF) and Record Descriptor Field (RDF) handle the variable nature of the
records automatically through Access Method Services (AMS)?

I would think that  RECORDSIZE (4160 25160) would be what was required (i.e.
no adjustment of lrecls due to using variable length records, as would be
done with physical sequential files).

>John

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol for 0S/390 VSAM-Filest. 39

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Hans-Georg Decker <Decker@kzvk.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361CA7F0.827018F5@kzvk.de>`
- **References:** `<3619FD1D.7E8451D7@kzvk.de> <6vdpdl$6vo$0@204.179.92.50> <361B6ACC.2635DECE@kzvk.de> <6vg5j3$o5l$0@204.179.92.50>`

```
Hi John,
hi Chris,

thank you for your answers and for your help -

Now I have found the solution:

File-status 39 wasn't an errror in the record-length or
cluster-definition.
The error results from an empty alternate-index.

For explain (I asume you are interested):

In the Programm, I load (with open output) a VSAM-File (by
the way: with variable recordlength).
This file has an alternate record too. First I create the
file (cluster) with IDCAMS and NULLFILE and
then run BLDINDEX. Past it works very fine - but now we are
testing the new Cobol-Compiler :-(

How did I found the error: In the SELECT-statement I coded
the optional data-name-2 in the file-status-claus
and the 3 fields with COMP in the working-storage section.
I got the return-code 04 and the reason-code 100.  For
interpreting this codes you have
to see the manual DFSMS/MVS Macro Instructions for Data
Sets.
Caution ==> 04 means: >Data set was opened successfully, but
an attention message were issued<.
                    But the file wasn't opened!!!!

Now I have removed the alternate-key clause in the program
and run the BLDINDEX after the
program.

So, I hope my english (incl. grammar) was good enough and
you do understand me!

Greetings from Cologne
Georg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol for 0S/390 VSAM-Filest. 39

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361b8cdf.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<3619FD1D.7E8451D7@kzvk.de> <6vdpdl$6vo$0@204.179.92.50> <361B7E1B.2362A7BF@kzvk.de>`

```
Hans-Georg Decker wrote in message <361B7E1B.2362A7BF@kzvk.de>...
>John Calahan schrieb:
>
>> Georg,
>>
>> I've experienced the same problem recently. To correct this, add this
line
>> to your file's FD statement in your program:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>now I have had time to try with your clause ... VARYING FROM 4160 to 25146
...
>
>(4160 Byte is the fix part plus max.1500 x 14 Byte for the occurs part
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I get no compiler-error, only this run-time-error. Is this an problem with
the
>new
>run-time-modules.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Um, forgive me if I did this wrong (or if the 25150 above is just a typo on
your part when entering this info):

1500 x 14 = 21000
21000 + 4160 = 25160

(25146 or 25150) <> 25160, and you've been typing in 25146 and 25150.  Try
changing to 25160 and see what happens.
```

## Thread: Portland OR: Systems Analyst — 1 msgs

### Portland OR: Systems Analyst

- **From:** azad@europa.com (AZAD)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361a40f6.63441918@news2.europa.com>`

```
IEF/Composer Programmer/Analyst

 
Scope of work:	This is an excellent opportunity to join a team 
		responsible for the creation, development and 
		maintenance of ERD's. Also, candidate will orchestrate

		appropriate DB2 tables, package and implement
solutions,
 		Perform system and integration testing, process and 
		event modeling and mentor new employees new to

<a id="t-bb337efee0"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu V4.0 slower than MF NetExpress? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu V4.0 slower than MF NetExpress?

- **From:** dlmax@netcom.com (David L Maxwell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dlmaxF0FAKM.BL6@netcom.com>`

```
I received my copy of Fujitsu COBOL V4.0 a few days ago, and have been
running compiler timing tests to determine if Fujitsu COBOL V4.0 is
faster than Microfocus NetExpress or Visual Object COBOL.

I was shocked to find that Fujitsu V4.0 COBOL was running 6 to 7 times
slower than either NetExpress or Visual Object COBOL.

My results (300Mhz Micron Pentium II / Windows 95):

NetExpress V1.0             9.39  secs  size of exe:  5632

Fujitsu V4.0                65-70 secs  size of exe:  41472

I later discovered that when the Fujitsu version was run from either
the "RUN" command line, or WINEXEC the run time is 8.89 sec.

Can anyone explain why there is such a large difference?  When the Fujitsu
version is run from the MS-DOS Prompt, it completes in anywhere between
65 and 70 seconds. The NetExpress and Visoc versions run in the same times
regardless of where they were launched.

David Maxwell
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4.0 slower than MF NetExpress?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-IYDQsH6oXhsG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<dlmaxF0FAKM.BL6@netcom.com>`

```
On Tue, 6 Oct 1998 20:36:21, dlmax@netcom.com (David L Maxwell) wrote:

> I received my copy of Fujitsu COBOL V4.0 a few days ago, and have been
> running compiler timing tests to determine if Fujitsu COBOL V4.0 is
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I SUSPECT it is excess overhead as the DOS box emulator tries to load 
the runtime DLL files - there are more associated with Fujitsu than 
with Netexpress.  

If you create an ICON on the desktop for the application, how does it 
perform?

Did you compile with optimization?  That can significantly speed up 
execution.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu V4.0 slower than MF NetExpress?

- **From:** dlmax@netcom.com (David L Maxwell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dlmaxF0FoGx.75y@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<dlmaxF0FAKM.BL6@netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-IYDQsH6oXhsG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-IYDQsH6oXhsG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>, 
redsky@ibm.net says...
>
>On Tue, 6 Oct 1998 20:36:21, dlmax@netcom.com (David L Maxwell) wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It runs in 8.89 seconds.

>Did you compile with optimization?  That can significantly speed up 
>execution.

Yes, Global optimization was on.

I created a seperate directory for the application and loaded all
the runtime files there.  I then rebooted the system to eliminate
the possibility of DLL's hanging around.  I started the application
from the DOS prompt and it ran in 9.02 seconds.  I started it again,
and again it ran in 8.92 seconds.  On the third and subsequent runs
the times ranged from 75 seconds to 90 seconds.

I have an old 100Mhz 486 running windows 95, so I tried running the
application there with the same results.  However the run times were
very much longer.

I have noticed one other strange behavior.  While waiting for the 
application to finish I clicked repeatedly on the Icon in the
Windows start bar, and this causes tha application to finish
much earlier than the 75 to 90 seconds.  Sometimes it will complete
in under 20 seconds.  It all depends upon when the clicking begins.

Strange Huh?

David Maxwell
>
```

<a id="t-0929c86d79"></a>
## Thread: redefines puzzlement — 32 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### redefines puzzlement

- **From:** higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
is there a rule about a redefined field being assumed to be
alphanumeric??
the reason i ask is the code below (chopped out of viasoft smart-test),
where the program thinks money temp is pic x(6), even though with the
definition right below it on the display is (essentially) pic 9(6),
which is redefined.

i hope this copies fairly well.

 01 MONEY-TEMP                      PIC X(6)
      VALUE > ...... <
----------------------------------------------- (after = is ws
def's;before = values)
01  MONEY-TEMP.
    04  MONEY-DOLLARS      PIC 9999.
    04  MONEY-CENTS        PIC 99.
01  MONEY  REDEFINES MONEY-TEMP.
    04  CAL-MONEY         PIC S9999V99.

so, does the act of redefining money-temp change it?? when this program
compares further down, the compare fails because <different-field> pic
9(6) is *never* going to equal money-temp pic x(6)!!!!

argh!!!!
suzie
```

#### ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** "tmj" <tmj@spam.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vggfg$9ag@musky.state.wi.us>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
CAL-MONEY is signed where MONEY-DOLLARS & MONEY-CENTS are not.

CAL-MONEY = X'F9F9F9F9F9C9'
MONEY-TEMP = X'F9F9F9F9F9F9'

higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us wrote in message
<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>...
>is there a rule about a redefined field being assumed to be
>alphanumeric??
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BLPS1.1292$2K1.1427895@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
Suzie wrote:
>is there a rule about a redefined field being assumed to be
>alphanumeric??
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>9(6) is *never* going to equal money-temp pic x(6)!!!!

Suzie, the REDEFINES has nothing to do with it.  Data item MONEY-TEMP
is inherently alphanumeric because it is a group item.  The same goes
for data item MONEY.  Redefining a data item does not change that data
item in *any* way.   Redefining a data item provides a way to create
one or more *other* data items which describe the same memory location
in a possibly different way.  Above, when you refer to data items
MONEY-TEMP or MONEY, they are essentially identical.  If you refer to
data item CAL-MONEY, you refer to the exact same memory location, but
the description of how the item is treated (the PICTURE) is different.
When you refer to MONEY-DOLLARS you refer to the first four characters
of the same area as a 4 position numeric field.  When you refer to
data item MONEY-CENTS, you refer to the last two characters of the same
area, as a 2 position numeric field.
```

#### ↳ Common error

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361bd10a.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
If you reference the top level of a data group, you will be referencing it
in alphanumeric format regardless of how the group is defined underneath it.
So yes, MONEY-TEMP would be referenced as PIC X(6) not as PIC 9(6) as you
would think.  The redefinition has nothing to do with it... that's just
redefining MONEY-TEMP as one value with a decimal place.


higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us wrote in message
<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>...
>is there a rule about a redefined field being assumed to be
>alphanumeric??
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vglap$btl@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
Money-temp is a GROUP item.  In (almost) all cases a group item is treated
as alphanumeric - regardless of the class/category of the elementary items
that are below it.

REDEFINES has nothing to do with this.

higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us wrote in message
<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>...
>is there a rule about a redefined field being assumed to be
>alphanumeric??
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Group Items are alphanumeric (was: redefines puzzlement)

- **From:** "Art Perry" <arthur.perry@eds88.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vgo6s$7kp$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <6vglap$btl@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I have seen a copy book where the group item was defined as PACKED-DECIMAL,
and the elementary items below it were not given a usage clause, ie:

PACKED-GROUP        PACKED-DECIMAL.
    ITEM-1          PIC 9(05).
    ITEM-2          PIC 9(05).

It was obvious that each of the individual items were PACKED-DECIMAL, but I
am curious if the group level is still alphanumeric.  I suppose it must
be...

-Art

William M. Klein wrote in message <6vglap$btl@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>...
>Money-temp is a GROUP item.  In (almost) all cases a group item is treated
>as alphanumeric - regardless of the class/category of the elementary items
>that are below it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Group Items are alphanumeric (was: redefines puzzlement)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h41T1.1759$2K1.1782760@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <6vglap$btl@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <6vgo6s$7kp$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Art Perry wrote:
>I have seen a copy book where the group item was defined as PACKED-DECIMAL,
>and the elementary items below it were not given a usage clause, ie:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>be...

Usage specifiers at the group level specify the usage for elementary items
subordinate to the group item.  They do not change the usage of the group
item itself, or subordinate group items.  Group items are always inherently
alphanumeric.  If you specify a usage on an elementary item subordinate to
a group item with a usage, they must be the same.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Group Items are alphanumeric (was: redefines puzzlement)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oLlT1.25$ce.68777@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <6vglap$btl@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <6vgo6s$7kp$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <h41T1.1759$2K1.1782760@news2.mia.bellsouth.net> <6viglf$mgk$2@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Art Perry wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>>If you specify a usage on an elementary item subordinate to
>>a group item with a usage, they must be the same.
>
>I hope the compiler will tell me if they are not ...  ?

It will if the compiler is COBOL 85 compliant, because that is a clearly
defined requirement in the COBOL 85 standard, paragraph 5.14.3(2) :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Group Items are alphanumeric (was: redefines puzzlement)

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981007220622.06996.00008494@ng111.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6vgo6s$7kp$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```

You have received good comments already with which it is hard to disagree.
However, ...

I would like to add.

In modern COBOL X(6) fields (elementary OR group items) are naturally compared
with 9(6) fields.
This is 1998, you know.  Modern compilers will convert the ! X(n) ! field to
numeric by signing it (within a temporary area).

If you do an X(n) elementary field compare to an X(n) or a group level, then
the details under the group (REDEFINES or whatever) do not bring about a
conversion. A character compare is  permitted by the standard.

But if one of the comparands is numeric elementary, then the character field is
converted! and a numeric test performed.  This is 1998.

The X(n) field gets moved to a temp area and a sign (if necessary) is
constructed to match the presence or absence of the sign on the numeric
elementary item that determines the context of the compare.

As far as the other question that entered this thread. Specifying
PACKED-DECIMAL (or for ye olde folkes, COMP-3) at the group level is just
syntactic sugar. 

A group item is alphanumeric in type; 
end of story.

The syntax shorthand is specified at the group level, but applies to the
elementary items. But that is a very good question (Gold Star for Art).

Back to the real issue, ...
the redefinition of a 9(n) field as a S9(n) is not only confusing, it is
dangerous. It can engage many very esoteric issues depending upon settings of
the prefered sign compile option.

But more to the point of the original posters enquiry. There is a real strong
possibility of mis-compares.  The sense of the original enquiry was that
possibly the 9(n) versus X(n) distinction will cause a problem. Actually,
modern COBOL should cover that, unless you get a negative number in there, and
then only in that sense X(n) can never equal S9(n) where the S is negative.
COBOL will promote types to sign, it will not demote the types by stripping
signs.

The problem will be that in the situation where you have one defintion signed
and the other unsigned, the field clearly COULD get populated three different
ways: one unsigned, one signed positive, and one signed negative.  You are in
trouble with that if you compare X(n) group to X(n) group.

Unsigned will not match signed positive if you manage to trigger an X to X
compare. Unsigned will also naturally not match signed negative on an X to X
compare.

Focusing on the final digit of the fields:
  x'Fn' is not equal to x'Cn'
and
  x'Fn' is not equal to x'Dn'

The compiler will do what it can for you if you compare at the elementary data
item (it can not ignore the fact that negative is not equal positive).
But if you truely compare at the group level, you are definitely on your own,
as far as the results of moving to fields variously with their original
defintion, or with their alternate definition.

In short, the advice you have received so far, is good, although I am not sure
that each poster is aware that modern COBOL promotes data types to signed.  And
more to the point, you are into a world of unnecessary mysteries if you
redefine unsigned fields as signed.

You have not posted enough code for anyone to make a conclusion in this
specific case, but once you get used to all these shell games you become
somewhat jaded. Any definition of unsiged by signed is potentially suspect. The
remarkable fact is the the NOPFD (no prefered sign) actual scrubs the mistake
out more times than not. 

You can get into trouble with such an unsigned/signed redefintion under a (PFD)
prefered sign compile assumption even at the elementary level, and frequently
so when you do compares at group level that trigger X to X compare
instructions.

Best of luck with this, it is a valuable technical area to master thoroughly.



















Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Group Items are alphanumeric (was: redefines puzzlement)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1T1.1761$2K1.1784996@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6vgo6s$7kp$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <19981007220622.06996.00008494@ng111.aol.com>`

```
Moral: never lie to the compiler.  If you simply make sure to use a PIC
which correctly matches the data, you can be virtually certain that the
compiler will handle it the way you expect.  If you try to fudge it, you
are playing Russian Roulette with your data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Group Items are alphanumeric (was: redefines puzzlement)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct12.091153.19324@giant>`
- **References:** `<6vgo6s$7kp$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <19981007220622.06996.00008494@ng111.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981007220622.06996.00008494@ng111.aol.com>,
rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:
>
> In modern COBOL X(6) fields (elementary OR group items) are naturally
> compared with 9(6) fields.  This is 1998, you know.  Modern compilers
> will convert the ! X(n) ! field to numeric by signing it (within a
> temporary area).

Sorry, but you've got this backward.

When a nonnumeric elementary data item or a nonnumeric literal is
compared to a numeric, the _numeric_ operand is treated as if it had been
MOVEd to a temporary elementary alphanumeric data item of the same size.

123456 is equal to 123456, and "123456" is equal to 123456.

0123456 is equal to 1232456, but "0123456" is not equal to 123456.

"123456 ", "123456  ", and so on, are all equal to 123456.

Furthermore, the sign is stripped from the numeric operand (not added to
the nonnumeric operand) by the standard CoBOL MOVE rules:

    $ copy sys$output demo.cob
    IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
    PROGRAM-ID.  DEMO.
    DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  FILLER.
        05  WS-X-6   PIC  X(6)  VALUE "123456".
        05  WS-S9-6  PIC S9(6)  VALUE -123456.
    PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    0000-MAIN.
        IF   WS-X-6 = WS-S9-6
        THEN DISPLAY "EQUAL"
        ELSE DISPLAY "NOT EQUAL"
        END-IF.
    9999-EXIT.
        STOP RUN.
    ^Z
    $ cobol demo.cob
    $ link demo.obj
    $ run demo.exe
    EQUAL
    $

When a group item is compared to a numeric, the numeric operand is
treated as if it had been MOVEd to a temporary group item of the same
size.  In this case, however, the sign is _not_ stripped by the MOVE
rules:

    $ copy sys$input demo.cob
    IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
    PROGRAM-ID.  DEMO.
    DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  FILLER.
        05  WS-GROUP-X.
            10  FILLER  PIC  X(6)  VALUE "12345O".
        05  WS-S9-6     PIC S9(6)  VALUE -123456.
    PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    0000-MAIN.
        IF   WS-GROUP-X = WS-S9-6
        THEN DISPLAY "EQUAL"
        ELSE DISPLAY "NOT EQUAL"
        END-IF.
    9999-EXIT.
        STOP RUN.
    ^Z
    $ cobol demo.cob
    $ link demo.obj
    $ run demo.exe
    EQUAL
    $
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

```

does it comform to.
Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
In article <361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us>,
higgish@dhfs.state.wi.us wrote:
>
> 01  MONEY-TEMP.
>     04  MONEY-DOLLARS      PIC 9999.
>     04  MONEY-CENTS        PIC 99.

OK, you've probably got this part, but it never hurts to begin at the
beginning.  All three of these fields are USAGE DISPLAY; MONEY-TEMP is a
group item, class alphanumeric.  The other two are elementary items,
class numeric.

There are two kinds of comparisons.  When both operands are numeric,
their algebraic values are compared.  However, when one or both of the
operands is non-numeric, each operand is considered a string of
alphanumeric characters and is compared according to the program's
collating sequence.  In other words, a PIC 9(6) can be equal to a PIC
X(6) in a non-numeric comparison.  For example, try this:

    $ copy sys$input demo.cob
    IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
    PROGRAM-ID.  DEMO.
    DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  FILLER.
        05  WS-PIC-9-6  PIC  9(6).
        05  WS-PIC-X-6  PIC  X(6).
    PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    0000-MAIN.
        MOVE  123456  TO WS-PIC-9-6.
        MOVE "123456" TO WS-PIC-X-6.
        IF   WS-PIC-9-6 = WS-PIC-X-6
        THEN DISPLAY "EQUAL"
        ELSE DISPLAY "NOT EQUAL"
        END-IF.
    9999-EXIT.
        STOP RUN.
    ^Z
    $ cobol demo.cob
    $ link demo.obj
    $ run demo.exe
    EQUAL
    $

Now, why are you doing this?  Obviously, to extract the dollars and cents
from the field:

    $ copy sys$input demo.cob
    IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
    PROGRAM-ID.  DEMO.
    DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  FILLER.
        05  MONEY-TEMP.
            10  MONEY-DOLLARS  PIC  9(4).
            10  MONEY-CENTS    PIC  9(2).
    PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    0000-MAIN.
        MOVE    "123456" TO MONEY-TEMP.
        DISPLAY MONEY-DOLLARS " DOLLARS AND " MONEY-CENTS " CENTS".
    9999-EXIT.
        STOP RUN.
    ^Z
    $ cobol demo.cob
    $ link demo.obj
    $ run demo.exe
    1234 DOLLARS AND 56 CENTS
    $


> 01  MONEY  REDEFINES MONEY-TEMP.
>     04  CAL-MONEY         PIC S9999V99.
>
> so, does the act of redefining money-temp change it??

The act of redefining does not change the attributes or the content of
MONEY-TEMP.  However, MONEY now occupies the same space as MONEY-TEMP, so
if you MOVE something to MONEY at run time, you will change the contents
of MONEY-TEMP, and vice versa.  In order for a non-numeric comparison to
work, you have to take the sign into account:

    $ copy sys$input demo.cob
    IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
    PROGRAM-ID.  DEMO.
    DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  FILLER.
        05  MONEY-TEMP.
            10  MONEY-DOLLARS  PIC  9(4).
            10  MONEY-CENTS    PIC  9(2).
	05  MONEY   REDEFINES  MONEY-TEMP.
            10  CAL-MONEY      PIC S9(4)V9(2).
    PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    0000-MAIN.
        MOVE +1234.56 TO CAL-MONEY.
        IF   MONEY-TEMP = "12345F"
        THEN DISPLAY "EQUAL"
        ELSE DISPLAY "NOT EQUAL"
        END-IF.
    9999-EXIT.
        STOP RUN.
    ^Z
    $ cobol demo.cob
    $ link demo.obj
    $ run demo.exe
    EQUAL
    $

I'm just guessing here, but you might have moved something to CAL-MONEY
and then compared to MONEY-TEMP without taking the sign into account. 
This might be more what you want:

    05  CAL-MONEY              PIC  9(4)V9(2).
    05  MONEY-TEMP  REDEFINES  CAL-MONEY.
        10  MONEY-DOLLARS      PIC  9(4).
        10  MONEY-CENTS        PIC  9(2).

Or maybe:

    05  CAL-MONEY              PIC S9(4)V9(2) SIGN IS LEADING SEPARATE.
    05  FILLER      REDEFINES  CAL-MONEY.
        10  FILLER             PIC  X(1).
        10  MONEY-TEMP.
            15  MONEY-DOLLARS  PIC  9(4).
            15  MONEY-CENTS    PIC  9(2).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant>`

```
This is only peripherally related to the topic at hand, but I just thought
I'd ask.  I have the following...

01  CARD-DATA.
    05  CD-YEAR          PIC 9(4).

01  MISC.
    05  CURRENT-YEAR     PIC 9(4) VALUE 1998.
    05  SELECT-YEAR      PIC 9(4).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     ACCEPT CARD-DATA.
     IF CD-YEAR IS EQUAL TO SPACES
         MOVE CURRENT-YEAR TO SELECT-YEAR
         GO TO 100-DATA-OK.
     IF CD-YEAR NOT NUMERIC
         DISPLAY 'INVALID DATA'
         GO TO 999-EXIT.
     MOVE CD-YEAR TO SELECT-YEAR.
     GO TO 100-DATA-OK.

Now, on both the compilers we have at work, DOS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II
there is no problem.  It just seems weird to me that a numeric picture
(CD-YEAR) could truely evaluate to spaces.  Since CD-YEAR is of usage 
DISPLAY rather than some COMPUTATIONAL picture are it's only restrictions
that you can't directly move a non-numeric value to it and that it you
attempt a mathmatical calculation on it while it is non-numeric it won't
work?  I use the "NOT NUMERIC" comparison all the time, but I've never
(until now) attempted to compare a numeric picture with a non-numeric
value.  But, as I said, it seems to work, so I'll keep it unless someone
can tell me a good reason not to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981010134954.06032.00009600@ng05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>`

```

If you are executing a mainframe compiler in a manner that generates COBOL 85
semantics then ...

>>
01  CARD-DATA.
    05  CD-YEAR          PIC 9(4).

....
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
...
     IF CD-YEAR IS EQUAL TO SPACES
         MOVE CURRENT-YEAR TO SELECT-YEAR
         GO TO 100-DATA-OK.
  
<<
Does not perform a test for SPACES (x'40').  Instead, this is an elementary
level compare, and the alphanumeric literal is promoted to a numeric,

x'40404040' is moved to a temp, and then converted to x'F0F0F0F0'. 
It is then packed. The numeric value that was input by the ACCEPT is PACKED and
(and a sign is possibly forced, depending upon the prefered sign compile
option).

So if x'40404040' was input, it gets packed as x'00000004' and (probably) then
forced to x'0000000F'. The test that looks like a test for SPACE on the surface
will work when it is actually spaces, because zero is equal to zero.

If you enter non numeric data, there is a distinct possibility of an S0C7.
Eespecially on the 'more friendly' newer compiler. (The compare packed CP
instruction will blow on the non numeric data).

It would be much much much better to test the group level for spaces (01 
CARD-DATA. ). You could do that before the numerics test and sustain your
current algorithm.  

You say that the compilations have 'no problem.'  But did you test?  Send in
some non-numerics and let us know what happens.  SEND in characters 0000 and
see if that doesn't trip up your program, convincing it that it has found
spaces (clue; your algortihm has a vulnerability you don't know about, beyond
the mere possibility of ABEND).

Try inputting values that have characters S through Z, Try inputting real fun
stuff like "<@#!". Hey, try typing +000, stand back, hit ENTER, cover your
head. (the x'4E' will get converted to x'FE' , and nomare where the E goes, I
bet ir blows).

Try entering -000. The x'60' will get converted to x'F0', and again your
program will be tricked into thinking it has spaces.

Test, test, test.  Getting bagels from the compiler don't mean nothin'.

Best Wishes 
   
Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

RKRayhawk wrote:
> 
> If you are executing a mainframe compiler in a manner that generates COBOL 85
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> <<

[stuff deleted]
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct16.163036.19339@giant>`
- **References:** `<361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com> <19981010134954.06032.00009600@ng05.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981010134954.06032.00009600@ng05.aol.com>,
rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:
>
> If you are executing a mainframe compiler in a manner that generates
> COBOL 85 semantics then ...
[...]
> this is an elementary level compare, and the alphanumeric literal is
> promoted to a numeric, x'40404040' is moved to a temp, and then
> converted to x'F0F0F0F0'.  It is then packed.  The numeric value that
> was input by the ACCEPT is PACKED and (and a sign is possibly forced,
> depending upon the prefered sign compile option).

You have invoked the Sacred Name C*B*L 85, but you did not check the said
standard before posting.  The general rule is that a numeric comparison
is used only when _both_ operands are class numeric.

First, SPACES is a figurative constant, not a nonnumeric literal, though
in this particular case the result would be the same.

Second, when a figurative constant other than ZERO is compared to an
external decimal integer elementary item, a _nonnumeric_ comparison is
used. 

In a nonnumeric comparison, the _numeric_ value that was input by the
ACCEPT is treated as if it were MOVEd to an alphanumeric elementary item
of the same size.  If this value had a sign, the sign would be stripped,
by the ordinary CoBOL MOVE rules.  The result is then compared directly
to SPACES.


[...]
> It would be much much much better to test the group level for spaces
> (01  CARD-DATA. ).  You could do that before the numerics test and
> sustain your current algorithm.

This is good advice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<708kod$74k@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com> <19981010134954.06032.00009600@ng05.aol.com> <1998Oct16.163036.19339@giant>`

```

Chris Westbury wrote in message <1998Oct16.163036.19339@giant>...
  <much snippage>
>
>If this value had a sign, the sign would be stripped,
>by the ordinary CoBOL MOVE rules.
  <more snippage>

T'aint true!  This is the normal IBM MAINFRAME COBOL MOVE rules.  The rules
of COBOL say NOTHING about how you move a field that is supposed to be
numeric but actually is NOT to an alphanumeric field.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vognn$s9@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant> <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>`

```
For those running on ANY IBM mainframe compiler (OS/VS COBOL, DOS/VS COBOL,
VS COBOL II, COBOL/VSE, COBOL/370, COBOL for MVS & VM, or COBOL for OS/390 &
VM), PLEASE look at your setting for the ZWB compiler option - and read the
documentation on what this does.  It has significant impact on the handling
of the sign-nibble (and hence X'40' vs X'F0') values when doing comparisons
between some numeric and non-numeric fields.

And for those outside the IBM mainframe world,  It is well documented (if
hidden a little) that one of these is definitely NOT ANSI conforming.
(Unfortunately, as with so many IBM extensions/deviations - the non-Standard
variation is the most common one among actual IBM users.)



Frank Swarbrick wrote in message <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>...
>This is only peripherally related to the topic at hand, but I just thought
>I'd ask.  I have the following...
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>work: frank.swarbrick@1stbank.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** cbailey@galstar.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vp7p3$725$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant> <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>`

```
The main reason I can think of to change the IF statement is that at some time
in the future the compiler will be "fixed" to not accept a "space" check on a
numberic field.  Why not make life easier and redefine the 9(4) as X(4) and
check that for spaces?  Cliff
--------------------------------

In article <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>,
  Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:

…[33 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct11.075144.19322@giant>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant> <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>`

```
In article <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com>
Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:
>
> 01  CARD-DATA.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> picture (CD-YEAR) could truly evaluate to spaces.

Every time a compiler writer responds to programer pressure to remove a
"fussy" error message in the interests of making that programer's job
seem easier, there is a corresponding loss of clarity that makes some
other programer's job seem harder.

Think of the PICTURE clause as a promise you make to the compiler, not a
promise the compiler makes to you.  It's not the compiler's job to
enforce the PICTURE, and, as you know, if you break your promise, you may
get an abend.

In this example, it is actually no more difficult to write standard
CoBOL:

       ACCEPT CARD-DATA.
       IF CARD-DATA IS EQUAL TO SPACES
           MOVE CURRENT-YEAR TO SELECT-YEAR
           GO TO 100-DATA-OK.

Of course, there are usually more fields in the card, and before IBM
unilaterally relaxed the rule against numeric/space comparison, we had to
write it like this:

    01  IN-REC.
        05  IN-FLD001.
            10  IN-FLD001-9-4  PIC 9(4).
        05  IN-FLD002.
            [etc.]

        IF   IN-FLD001     = SPACES     MOVE IN-DEF001 TO IN-FLD001.
        IF   IN-FLD001-9-4 NOT NUMERIC  GO TO IN-ERR001.
        MOVE IN-FLD001-9-4 TO ...
```

```
Chris Westbury wrote:
>
>Think of the PICTURE clause as a promise you make to the compiler, not a
>promise the compiler makes to you.  It's not the compiler's job to
>enforce the PICTURE, and, as you know, if you break your promise, you may
>get an abend.

That's a good way to put it, Chris.  I often say "Don't lie to the compiler."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Garry Dell" <Garry_dell@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eTbegdT99GA.80@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant> <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com> <1998Oct11.075144.19322@giant>`

```

snipped
>>      IF CD-YEAR IS EQUAL TO SPACES
>>          MOVE CURRENT-YEAR TO SELECT-YEAR
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>



I certainly wouldn't write this code this way. I would try something like
the following, a little bit easier to follow and maintain.

IF CD-YEAR = SPACES OR CD-YEAR IS NUMERIC
    MOVE CURRENT-YEAR
        TO SELECT-YEAR
    PERFORM 1000-DATA-OK
ELSE
    DISPLAY 'INVALID DATA'
    PERFORM 999-EXIT.

I don't like GOTO's so if it were possible I would change them to a
performs.

this is a little bit easier to read and maintain.

Let me know what you think of this
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3620EF7D.6DF0@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant> <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com> <1998Oct11.075144.19322@giant> <eTbegdT99GA.80@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`

```
Garry Dell wrote:
> 
> snipped
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Evaluate Cd-Year
    When Spaces
        perform 1000-DATA-OK (although I question this)
    When Numeric
        perform 1000-DATA-OK
    When Other
        perform 1100-INVALID-YEAR
End-Evaluate

The move statement would be in the 1000-DATA-OK paragraph.
If necessary, a condition could be set in the 1100-INVALID-YEAR routine,
and then tested after the Evaluate statement
ie: If Invalid-Cd-Year
        go to 9999-EDIT-CD-YEAR-EXIT
    End-if
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct16.163319.19340@giant>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <3620EF7D.6DF0@netbox.com>`

```
In article <3620EF7D.6DF0@netbox.com>,
Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com> wrote:
>
> In article <eTbegdT99GA.80@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>,
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> The move statement would be in the 1000-DATA-OK paragraph.

Well, lessee what we got here.  Mr. Swarbrick posts eight (8) lines of
code that he encountered in a system he is remediating.  It looks to be
pretty average code, neither unusually clear not deliberately obfuscated.

Then comes a guy who hates GO TOs so much, he takes them out on the
company's nickel.  Of course, the code doesn't work any more; if you
enter a numeric value it will be ignored and the default will be used;
but by gum those pesky GO TOs are gone, so the broken code _must_ be
easier to follow and maintain.

Now comes a guy who quotes both the original code and the broken code. 
Does he _read_ the lousy eight lines of the original code and fix the
broken code?  Not on your life!  He takes the seven lines of broken code
and rearranges them into eight lines of broken code, adding new errors in
the process (e.g. NUMERIC is not a valid WHEN phrase).

If either of these two gentlemen is doing remediation, perhaps this Y2k
thing _is_ something to worry about, after all.  ;-).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362141E0.C3E@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <1998Oct9.143011.19317@giant> <361F9C51.5B96@sprynet.com> <1998Oct11.075144.19322@giant> <eTbegdT99GA.80@upnetnews02.moswest.msn.net>`

```
Garry Dell wrote:
> 
> snipped
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Let me know what you think of this

Well, actually, the code is already written.  I'll I'm trying to do is
make it Y2K compliant.  It's a goofy piece of code because what originally
existed was something like this:

01  CARD-DATA.
    05  CD-DATE.
        10  CD-DECADE        PIC 99.
        10  CD-YEAR          PIC 99.

Then CD-DATE was checked for a value of spaces.  Following that, CD-YEAR
was checked for numerics.  Etc.

CD-DECADE was never used anywhere at all, and CD-DATE was only used in the
aforementioned check for spaces.  And, of course, CD-DECADE is meaningless
anyway.  So I just made CD-YEAR a PIC 9(4), made it the 05 level, and got rid
of CD-DECADE and CD-DATE.  I'm going to put CD-DATE back, though, as a PIC X(4)
and make CD-YEAR a redefine of it.

As for not using GOTO's, the program has GOTO's all over already, so one more
won't make much difference...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefines puzzlement

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct12.094035.19325@giant>`
- **References:** `<361BB767.7FCD13F4@dhfs.state.wi.us> <362141E0.C3E@sprynet.com>`

```
In article <362141E0.C3E@sprynet.com>,
Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:
>
> 01  CARD-DATA.
>     05  CD-DATE.
>         10  CD-DECADE        PIC 99.
>         10  CD-YEAR          PIC 99.


Yes, that's the way to write it in standard CoBOL.

In a well-constructed DATA DIVISION, the reader can see at a glance
everything that you plan to do later on in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.

CARD-DATA has been defined with exactly the attributes needed to ACCEPT
the input record.

CD-DATE has been defined with exactly the attributes needed to test the
input field for spaces.

CD-DECADE and CD-YEAR have been defined with exactly the attributes that
the programer originally thought s/he needed.


[...]
> CD-DECADE was never used anywhere at all, and CD-DATE was only used in
> the aforementioned check for spaces.  And, of course, CD-DECADE is
> meaningless anyway.  So I just made CD-YEAR a PIC 9(4), made it the 05
> level, and got rid of CD-DECADE and CD-DATE.  I'm going to put CD-DATE
> back, though, as a PIC X(4) and make CD-YEAR a redefine of it.

No, the only thing wrong is that CD-DECADE and CD-YEAR no longer match
what you want to do with them later on in the PROCEDURE DIVISION, so
they're the only things you have to change.  The rest is OK:  

    01  CARD-DATA.
        05  CD-DATE.
            10  CD-YEAR          PIC 9999.

CARD-DATA is still there so you can ACCEPT the input record.

CD-DATE is still there so you can test the input field for spaces.  

CD-YEAR now matches what you are now doing with it.

Personally, I try to minimize the number of different names for the same
thing.  When CD-DATE had a breakdown, the name made sense.  But now that
CD-DATE and CD-YEAR are the same thing with different attributes, I might
change the names like this:

    01  CARD-DATA.
        05  CD-YEAR-FIELD.
            10  CD-YEAR          PIC 9999.

or like this:

    01  CARD-DATA.
        05  CD-YEAR-X-4.
            10  CD-YEAR-9-4      PIC 9999.

In Y2k code, I include the date format in the variable name itself, so
the reader doesn't have to search back to the DATA DIVISION:

    MOVE        DD OF WS-DATE-CCYYMMDD  TO DD  OF WS-DATE-DDMMMYY
    MOVE WS-MMM(MM OF WS-DATE-CCYYMMDD) TO MMM OF WS-DATE-DDMMMYY
    MOVE        YY OF WS-DATE-CCYYMMDD  TO YY  OF WS-DATE-DDMMMYY

so I would probably end up with something like this:

    01  CARD-DATA.
        05  CD-DATE-CCYY-X.
            10  CD-DATE-CCYY-9   PIC 9999.
```
