<a id="t-b91356a557"></a>
## Thread: Leap Years? Who Needs 'Em? — 13 msgs

### Leap Years? Who Needs 'Em?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<6vgfk4$af$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```

Came across the following whilst doing a bit of Y2K remediation:

IF TABLE-MO (JURIS-SUB, RATE-SUB, TYP-SUB, MONTH-SUB) =
                           (4 OR 6 OR 9 OR 11)
    MOVE 30 TO WRAR091-TO-DAY
ELSE
    MOVE 31 TO WRAR091-TO-DAY.
IF TABLE-MO (JURIS-SUB, RATE-SUB, TYP-SUB, MONTH-SUB) = 2
    MOVE 28 TO WRAR091-TO-DAY.

What is it trying to do?  It is trying to force the end-of-month date to a
field based on something buried in a four-dimensional array.

What does it succeed in doing?  It succeeds in *never* giving February 29
days.

When was it written?  DATE-WRITTEN is 12 Nov 1984... so, about 14 years
ago.

When was it last modified?  Last mods appear to have been done 16 Jan
1997 (found in MODCODE, or 'comments' area); the Prod load module was
created then, as well.

What does a Good Y2K Remediator do about it?  Notify the Project Lead and
suggest that the Appropriate User generate a ChangeReq... if *that* much.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Leap Years? Who Needs 'Em?

even when not exact in february.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Leap Years? Who Needs 'Em?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00

francois millet wrote:
>> ...
>> IF TABLE-MO (JURIS-SUB, RATE-SUB, TYP-SUB, MONTH-SUB) =
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>1984?


I believe the COBOL 74 standard merely required that at least three
dimensions be supported.  I know for a fact (because I just checked the
manual :) that Unisys A Series COBOL 74 permits up to 48 dimensions,
one for each possible level number 02 - 49.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap Years? Who Needs 'Em?

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00

According to one post I saw recently, a 1974 COBOL compiler allowed 48
dimensions (1 dimension for each data division level from 02 to 49).
Unisys, I think.

Shmuel (Seymour J.) Metz wrote in message <36265BD1.1E3FAE3B@nsf.gov>...
>francois millet wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Reply to host nsf (dot) gov, user smetz

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Leap Years? Who Needs 'Em?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<706a0r$hrf@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6vgfk4$af$1@clarknet.clark.net> <VA.0000004b.008ab72a@francoim> <36265BD1.1E3FAE3B@nsf.gov> <36267250.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
The correct answer was given a while ago - but just to clarify it (with the
cross-postings),

The ANS'74 COBOL Standard required that a compiler must support *AT LEAST* 3
levels (or dimensions).  There was no requirement that it support exactly 3
levels - nor that supporting more than 3 levels be treated as an extension
(via flagging).

Some '74 compilers did support only 3 levels, others supported 16 or 25
levels - or the reported 48 levels.  During the development of the '85
Standard, there was a proposal that a  compiler be required to support 48
levels until it was pointed out that the fastest then available computer
would take several days (or some other large time amount) simply to
initialize such a table - if it only had 2 bytes at the "bottom" of the
levels (i.e. 2 ** 48).

I never verified this, but it ended up with the current Standard requiring
AT LEAST 7 levels - with (again) no upper limit.

Chris Osborne wrote in message <36267250.0@news2.uswest.net>...
>According to one post I saw recently, a 1974 COBOL compiler allowed 48
>dimensions (1 dimension for each data division level from 02 to 49).
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

>On Wed, 7 Oct 1998 13:06:15, "Kay Jenkins"
>> Does anyone know where I can find a profiler for Cobol on the VAX/VMS
>> platform/OS ?
>What is a profiler?


it is a program that collects and shows run-time statistics for a program,
such as for each line how many times it was executed or how long it took.
Very useful for locating bottlenecks and untested areas.
```

#### ↳ Re: looking for a profiler for the VMS platform...

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct9.075945.19316@giant>`
- **References:** `<01bdf1f3$6d02f820$a051d5d0@rd0495.wiztec-usa.com>`

```
In article <01bdf1f3$6d02f820$a051d5d0@rd0495.wiztec-usa.com>,
"Kay Jenkins" <jenkins_k@wiztec-usanospam.com> wrote:
>
> Does anyone know where I can find a profiler for Cobol on the VAX/VMS
> platform/OS ?

First, get the system manager's tuning manual and the guide to VMS
performance management and make sure your cluster is up to snuff.

Then, review your program's I/O strategy.  The compiler must translate
your CoBOL I/O statements into RMS, and a poor I/O strategy will outweigh
the most careful code.  Start with the overview in chapter 20 of the
CoBOL user manual, and then go on to chapter 3 of the file applications
guide.

Only after you have tuned your system and your I/O is it worth profiling
your code with the VMS Performance Coverage Analyzer (PCA).  PCA doesn't
come free with VMS, but it might already be on your system.  If not, you
can get it fairly quickly.
```

#### ↳ Re: looking for a profiler for the VMS platform...

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361dfad2.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<01bdf1f3$6d02f820$a051d5d0@rd0495.wiztec-usa.com>`

```

## Thread: MVS Cobol question....... — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** "Jaye Snidow" <Jaye.Snidow@123EDS.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
This is my first posting here and I would like to ask the various experts
gathered here a question that was referred to me by a colleague.
We are not running MVS Cobol onsite and do not have any access to reference
materials as yet. Is the following possible?

Given:

10   FIELD-A                      PIC  9(09).
.
.
.
ADD + 1 TO FIELD-A(3:1) .


I don't believe this is possible based on the fact that you would be trying
to add 1 to an address reference as opposed to the actual value. Am I
correct or simply less informed? TIA.


Jaye Snidow
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** "Jay Moseley" <jayNO_SPAMmoseley@ibm-dot-net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wnlzbfryrlvozarg.f0h4480.pminews@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Jaye,

     The field that results from reference modification implicitly has a
class and category of alphanumeric. Therefore you are correct, you cannot
perform computations directly on the field as in your example.

Regards,
Jay Moseley, CCP





On Wed, 7 Oct 1998 16:26:46 -0400, Jaye Snidow wrote:

>This is my first posting here and I would like to ask the various experts
>gathered here a question that was referred to me by a colleague.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vh7au$5ck$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Jayne,

    Fields accessed via Reference Modification are always treated as EBCDIC
DISPLAY. When adding a value to a DISPLAY-NUMERIC field, the compiler
normally generates a PACK, MVN (Sign-Nibble), AP, UNPK and OI (Zone-Nibble).

    If you're unsure, use the LIST,NOOFFSET Compile Parameters and verify
the generated ASSEMBLER instructions.

    My conclusion would be that you'd get an 'E' Level Error on the Compile.
But why would you want to do this in the first place? I can't see the
benefit nor reason.

    HTH....

Regards,

WOB,
Atlanta

Jaye Snidow wrote in message <6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>...
>This is my first posting here and I would like to ask the various experts
>gathered here a question that was referred to me by a colleague.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** "Jaye Snidow" <Jaye.Snidow@123EDS.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6viftc$iia$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Thank you all for the various responses to my question. It has solved a
large headache for my colleague. The actual field he was working with is 16
bytes (zoned decimal) and he needs to be able to manipulate each byte within
the field. I had originally recommended that he redefine the field and break
each byte down for individual manipulation but he felt it would be easier to
do it the way mentioned below. Thanks to the responses I got, I also know
why he got a compile error about performing math on alphanumeric fields.
Thanks again.


Jaye Snidow

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361D75EE.3D2000C8@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com> <6viftc$iia$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
Here's an alternative:

10   FIELD-A                      PIC  9(09).
10   FIELD-N                      PIC  9.
..
..
..
MOVE FIELD-A (3:1) TO FIELD-N
ADD + 1                      TO FIELD-N
MOVE FIELD-N         TO FIELD-A (3:1)

John Trifon

> Thank you all for the various responses to my question. It has solved a
> large headache for my colleague. The actual field he was working with is 16
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vhhmu$5rp@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```

Jaye Snidow wrote in message <6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>...
>This is my first posting here and I would like to ask the various experts
>gathered here a question that was referred to me by a colleague.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Several others have told you that what you propose is (as you suspect)
illegal.  It would, however be possible (but pretty convoluted) to get what
you want in a single step - as long as you are working with an unsigned
DISPLAY numeric field.  I think (but haven't tried to compile this) that you
would need to have the following code:

 Given:

 10   FIELD-A                      PIC  9(09).

then a procedure division statement of.

 Compute Field-A = ((Function NumVal (Field-A (1:2)) * (10 ** 7)) +
                                 (Function NumVal (Field-A (4:))  +
                                 (Function NumVal (Field-A (3:1)) + 1
  End-Compute

What did you want to have happen when the 3rd digit was 9? If you want it to
go back to 0, then you would need to change the last  line of the compute
statement to include a MOD statement (I think).

Obviously, it would be a million time easier (and clearer) just to code

  10  Field-A   Pic 9(09).
  10 redefines Field-A
      15           Pic X(02).
      15  Add-Byte   Pic 9.
      15           Pic  X(06).

Add 1 to Add-Byte.
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1998Oct10.091050.19321@giant>`
- **References:** `<6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`

```
In article <6vgit7$414$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>,
"Jaye Snidow" <Jaye.Snidow@123EDS.com> wrote:
>
> 10   FIELD-A                      PIC  9(09).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> trying to add 1 to an address reference as opposed to the actual value.

Reference modification defines a subset of a data item by specifying its
leftmost character and its length.  Only data items whose USAGE is
DISPLAY may be reference-modified.  The result of reference modification
acts like an elementary data item of the same class and category as the
reference-modified data item.  So far, it would seem that you can do
this, but there's a joker: for reasons that are unclear to me, if the
reference-modified data item is numeric, numeric-edited, or
alphanumeric-edited, the result of reference modification is
alphanumeric.

So, FIELD-A is class and category numeric, and you can add one to it, but
FIELD-A(3:1) is class and category alphanumeric, and you can't add one to
it.

Sometimes the error message from the compiler can help clarify things:

    $ copy sys$input demo.cob
    IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
    PROGRAM-ID.  DEMO.
    DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  FILLER.
        10  FIELD-A  PIC 9(09).
    PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    0000-MAIN.
        ADD + 1 TO FIELD-A(3:1) .
    9999-EXIT.
        STOP RUN.
    ^Z
    $ cobol demo.cob
        9             ADD + 1 TO FIELD-A(3:1) .
                          1                   2
    %COBOL-F-ERROR  326, (1) Missing literal or data-name
    %COBOL-W-ERROR  297, (2) Processing of source program resumes at this point
    %COBOL-F-ENDNOOBJ, DEMO.COB;1 completed with 2 diagnostics - object deleted
    $

Oops!  You didn't test before posting, did you?  Oh, well, let's try again:

    $ copy sys$input demo.cob
    IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
    PROGRAM-ID.  DEMO.
    DATA DIVISION.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  FILLER.
        10  FIELD-A  PIC 9(09).
    PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    0000-MAIN.
        ADD +1 TO FIELD-A(3:1).
    9999-EXIT.
        STOP RUN.
    ^Z
    $ cobol demo.cob
        9             ADD +1 TO FIELD-A(3:1).
                                1
    %COBOL-F-ERROR  277, (1) Operand must be a numeric data-name
    %COBOL-F-ENDNOOBJ, DEMO.COB;2 completed with 1 diagnostic - object deleted
    $

Now, here's the original CoBOL thought pattern:

    10  FIELD-A            PIC  9(09).
    10  FILLER  REDEFINES  FIELD-A.
        15  FIELD-A-1      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-2      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-3      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-4      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-5      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-6      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-7      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-8      PIC  9(01).
        15  FIELD-A-9      PIC  9(01).

    ADD 1 TO FIELD-A-3.

Notice that there's no subscripting, indexing, or reference mod at all. 
In a well-constructed DATA DIVISION, the reader can see at a glance
everything that you plan to do later on in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.  I
started with the simplest case because the example you gave contains only
constants and needs nothing more.  If you need more, the next step might
be:

    10  FIELD-A            PIC  9(09).
    10  FILLER  REDEFINES  FIELD-A.
        15  FIELD-A-9      PIC  9(01)  OCCURS 9  INDEXED BY FIELD-A-9-NDX.

    PERFORM VARYING FIELD-A-9-NDX ...

            ADD 1 TO FIELD-A-9(FIELD-A-9-NDX) ...

and so on.

You can pull out subfields of any size in plain old CoBOL without
reference modification:

    10  FIELD-A            PIC  9(09).
    10  FILLER  REDEFINES  FIELD-A.
        15  FIELD-A-123    PIC  9(03).
        15  FIELD-A-456    PIC  9(03).
        15  FIELD-A-789    PIC  9(03).

    ADD 1 TO FIELD-A-123.

and, of course,

    10  FIELD-A            PIC  9(09).
    10  FILLER  REDEFINES  FIELD-A.
        15  FIELD-A-9-3    PIC  9(03)  OCCURS 3.

    ADD 1 TO FIELD-A-9-3(1).

Reference modification is a useful feature for processing variable
fields, but you should not use it merely to save typing fixed fields in
the DATA DIVISION, because then you lose the single most valuable
self-documenting feature of CoBOL.
```

## Thread: on-topic legal question — 8 msgs

### on-topic legal question

- **From:** SkidMike <skidmike@~NOSPAM~mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com>`

```
about the muffler shop system again.  i am close to putting a demo
version up on my website for other meineke shop owners to check out
(yeah, i'll post the url here 'cause you guys can torture it a little
and give me some pointers) but for the final version i need something. 
i need to obtain an on-line catalog from our exhaust parts supplier
(maremont) and convert it to use with my program.  i've been advised (by
someone i know at meineke corporate) to ask them for one, as they have
them on cd-rom.  however, what am i in for as far as legal issues like
paying them money if i ever get to the point where i sell the software
with the maremont catalog included?
```

#### ↳ Re: on-topic legal question

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361CB9B6.D9AADB9A@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com>`

```


SkidMike wrote:

> about the muffler shop system again.  i am close to putting a demo
> version up on my website for other meineke shop owners to check out
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> with the maremont catalog included?

I think you are getting into a number of legal issues. One way to go with
this is before you go to far seek legal advice. You will likely need proper
contracts drawn up between you and the various shops and between you
and the supplier of the parts catalog. In addition, you will be running into

several tax issues which can get messy.

All of this said, I am not a lawyer/accountant and I think your best bet
is to seek professional advise concerning these issues. It will cost you
money but in the long run it will be money well spent, IMO.
```

#### ↳ Re: on-topic legal question

- **From:** stevewie@apk.net (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361dd9a2.6421137@news.apk.net>`
- **References:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com>`

```
On Wed, 07 Oct 1998 22:35:24 -0400, SkidMike
<skidmike@~NOSPAM~mindspring.com> enlightened us:

>about the muffler shop system again.  i am close to putting a demo
>version up on my website for other meineke shop owners to check out
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the maremont catalog included?

I'm not sure if this applies, but I write mostly new programs for the
company I work for and I am not allowed to sell those programs to
anyone.  They become the property of the company that employs me and
while they can sell them if they wish to, I cannot.  If I tried to, I
could be fired and sued for a number of different things including
disclosure of trade secrets.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Todays tip for annoying people:  While making presentations, occasionally bob your head like a   
parakeet.
 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: on-topic legal question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VL6T1.1822$2K1.1939287@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com> <361dd9a2.6421137@news.apk.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
>SkidMike wrote:
>>about the muffler shop system again.  i am close to putting a demo
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>disclosure of trade secrets.

It depends on what they pay you to do.  If your company pays you to
write programs, the programs belong to them.  If they pay you to fix
mufflers and you write programs, the programs belong to you. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: on-topic legal question

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vj6o4$cet$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com>`

```
I would find out the price, and just ask to become a dealer
for their CD.  That would be by far the easiest way to do
it.  You can resell anything that you have legally purchased,
including someone else's CD.  The problem arises if that
catalogue is unavailable ... if they only give it away to dealers
for example.

SkidMike wrote in message <6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com>...
>about the muffler shop system again.  i am close to putting a demo
>version up on my website for other meineke shop owners to check out
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the maremont catalog included?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: on-topic legal question

- **From:** notrleech@sprynet.com (Raymond W. Leech, III)
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.10871b08d666ed3e989680@news.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com> <6vj6o4$cet$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Just my two cents. I needed to do something similar years ago. This 
particular vendor was much too difficult to deal with for resale or 
licensing.

To solve the problem, I simply included the conversion routines in the 
software and made it clear in the documentation where the client could 
purchase the data if they desired and how to run my conversion.

It depends on how easy your vendor is to work with, how much effort you 
want to invest and how inconvenient it is to the client this way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: on-topic legal question

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vl5cr$2sc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com> <6vj6o4$cet$1@news.igs.net> <MPG.10871b08d666ed3e989680@news.sprynet.com>`

```
Yes, that approach can work as well.  I currently have software
that requires Excel to use ... I simply tell the customer to get
a copy.  In a lot of cases, they can get a copy cheaper than
I can.

Raymond W. Leech, III wrote in message ...
>Just my two cents. I needed to do something similar years ago. This
>particular vendor was much too difficult to deal with for resale or
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

#### ↳ Re: on-topic legal question

- **From:** "Art Perry" <arthur.perry@eds88.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vigle$mgk$1@news.ses.cio.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com>`

```
SkidMike wrote in message <6vh8e5$k3g$1@camel15.mindspring.com>...
>[snip]  ... what am i in for as far as legal issues like
>paying them money if i ever get to the point where i sell the software
>with the maremont catalog included?

I could see where they might require licenses for copies of their catalog,
but, if they want to sell more automotive parts, why would they want to
hinder you or take money from you?

I would think that you would want to update the catalog database from time
to time.  If you do get to a point where you sell your software, perhaps you
might just include an upload utility that will load the updated catalog
database into the software.  You could work out a deal with the parts
supplier where the user could get the updated catalog data from them in a
format compatible with your system.

Good luck to you!

-Art
```

---

<a id="t-66609f796e"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol's 32bit CCIIPX bad performance — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol's 32bit CCIIPX bad performance

- **From:** Scott Dickson <scottdickson@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361B7F25.514F9DBC@ibm.net>`

```
We use Micro Focus Cobol's Fileshare NLM in our production environment.
The live system uses 16bit cobol (version 3.2.50 with v. 3.2.20 cciipx
modules).  We have been desperately trying to upgrade to 4.0.32 (32bit)
but CCIIX32 performance is 22 times slower on a Dell PowerEdge 4200
server with dual 333 PIIs and 256mb RAM.

10,000 reads with 16bit takes 25 seconds versus 9min 32sec using 32bit.

This is ludicrous and must be attributable to some setting or settings.
I am using the same COBOL.DIR for both, the same FHREDIR.CFG for both
and everything else is exactly the same.

MF tech support tells me they should perform about the same, but when
they ran my tests, they had the 32bit outperforming 16bit by a 4 to 1
ratio.

This problem has gone unresolved for 5 months now.  Any suggestions?

Anyone who has any experience with cciipx and fileshare NLM?

Scott
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol's 32bit CCIIPX bad performance

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<000229db@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<361B7F25.514F9DBC@ibm.net>`

```

Dear Scott,

SD> We use Micro Focus Cobol's Fileshare NLM in our production
SD> environment. The live system uses 16bit cobol (version 3.2.50 with v.
SD> 3.2.20 cciipx modules).  We have been desperately trying to upgrade to
SD> 4.0.32 (32bit) but CCIIX32 performance is 22 times slower on a Dell
SD> PowerEdge 4200 server with dual 333 PIIs and 256mb RAM.

SD> 10,000 reads with 16bit takes 25 seconds versus 9min 32sec using
SD> 32bit.

What about the client side? 16Bit or 32Bit? Windows 95 or NT? Graphical or  
textmode UI?

Best regards

Sven
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF Cobol's 32bit CCIIPX bad performance

- **From:** Scott Dickson <scottdickson@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361CC2A5.9BE48DDE@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361B7F25.514F9DBC@ibm.net> <000229db@ftn.de>`

```
Everything we have done is on a Win95 client.  Whenever I refer to 16bit, it
is not the Operating system, but the Cobol product from Micro Focus.

Our 16bit cobol client performs very well with Fileshare.nlm.  The 32bit cobol
client does not, it takes 1min 49seconds to do 10,000 reads (of the same
record) while the 16bit cobol client does the same in 43seconds.

We purchased a new dual processor server, and the 16bit cobol clients time
dropped to 25 seconds.  However, the 32bit cobol's time was a horrific 9min
32seconds.  I just today learned from MF that CCIIX32.NLM is completely
untested on the newer dual processor machines.

Sven Brueggemann wrote:

> Dear Scott,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sven
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Cobol's 32bit CCIIPX bad performance

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00024843@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<361CC2A5.9BE48DDE@ibm.net>`

```
Dear Scott,

SD> > SD> 10,000 reads with 16bit takes 25 seconds versus 9min 32sec using
SD> > SD> 32bit.
SD> > What about the client side? 16Bit or 32Bit? Windows 95 or NT?
SD> Everything we have done is on a Win95 client.  Whenever I refer to
SD> 16bit, it is not the Operating system, but the Cobol product from
SD> Micro Focus.
SD> Our 16bit cobol client performs very well with Fileshare.nlm.  The
SD> 32bit cobol client does not
SD> However, the 32bit cobol's time was a horrific 9min 32seconds.  I just
SD> today learned from MF that CCIIX32.NLM is completely untested on the
SD> newer dual processor machines.

I strongly doubt your server causes the trouble. As far as I can do a  
diagnosis from here, I would blame Windows 95. 32Bit textmode performance  
is extremely low on Win95 - you wouldn't like to use a 32Bit PANELS  
application under Win95. On NT it's faster than 16Bit (not as good as on  
Unix, but not bad).

Please check your test suite for any screen i-o and do some tests without  
DISPLAYS. And ask Microfocus whether they tested your suite on Windows NT  
or on Windows 95.

BTW: Which Runtime do you use? Try running the INT or GNT with RUN instead  
of linking the program.

If you have any results, I would appreciate your keeping me up to date.

Best regards


Sven
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Cobol's 32bit CCIIPX bad performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn71sh9f.d0.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<361CC2A5.9BE48DDE@ibm.net> <00024843@ftn.de>`

```
On Thu, 08 Oct 1998 21:11:00 +0100, Sven Brueggemann
<Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de> wrote:
>diagnosis from here, I would blame Windows 95. 32Bit textmode performance  is
>extremely low on Win95 - you wouldn't like to use a 32Bit PANELS  application
>under Win95. On NT it's faster than 16Bit (not as good as on  Unix, but not
>bad).

FYI, I've found the same thing to be true with 32-bit file I/O. Win32 code
running on Win95 can be as much as four times slower than NT or even DOS/WIN16
under Win95. Win32 on NT, OOTOH, is only around two times slower than Linux.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Cobol's 32bit CCIIPX bad performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott Dickson <scottdickson@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361E17FB.542FEBE7@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361CC2A5.9BE48DDE@ibm.net> <00024843@ftn.de>`

```
The only screen i-o is the display of the time before the test and the display
of the time after the test.  I have run into the poor text video performance
myself and know better.  Also, we only use .gnt code (in both production and
development) as we are the end user of the system.  I've animated the program
(I know performance is much worse and I don't use any times from animated
runs) and I have isolated the performance problem to the actual READ
statement.  I can actually time the interval from when I Step at the READ
until control comes back to me (for the 32bit cobol).  Under 16bit, it is
instantaneous, like it is for every other line of code.  MF is totally in
control at that point, so I can't programmatically affect the performance
(within the program).

According to MF development, there may be a problem with the CCIIX32.NLM on a
server with a second CPU.  They stated "There is a slim possibility that the
presence of the second CPU in the new server is affecting the threading
algorithms of the NOS, even with SMP disabled.  The newer CCI modules use a
different threading model to the older CCI modules".  At this point, we are
awaiting Lanalyzer software to trace the IPX traffic before, during, and after
the tests.

Sven Brueggemann wrote:

>
> I strongly doubt your server causes the trouble. As far as I can do a
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sven
```

<a id="t-aa5d3fb97c"></a>
## Thread: okay, what now? — 5 msgs

### okay, what now?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vjqmh$j1d$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I have a rather strange problem that I have been fiddling
with for half a day, and I was wondering if anybody can point
me in a new direction.  It is Fujitsu Cobol, Version 4

I need to update a screen, plain screen section type of data,
every ten seconds or so.  The application will run in the
background, so to speak, and present an airport style
schedule ... the data being referenced constantly for work
in other windows.

The approach I decided to use was to write a stub in
power Cobol with a single timer on a form. When the timer
times out, I call a subroutine that is a Cobol program
with a screen section.  On the first pass, it displays the
background, then the data, as well as a flag saying that
it has been there. It then exits. Ten seconds later, it is
called again, sees it is not the first pass, updates the
screen, etc.  All works perfectly, and exactly as I require.

Until I want to stop it.  Now, the main form is
not visible, only the "screen" of the subprogram.  If I click
on its "stop" tab, I get a windows illegal reference. It then
stops. It stops quite gracefully.  However, I could do
without the "this program has produced an illegal reference,
please call your software vendor" message.  The only way
that I have been able to make it stop correctly is to make
the form visible, and have a stop button on it.  The "stop"
control on the screen causes an error, and it appears
that I cannot turn it off(either error or control).  Oh yes,
if I put an "accept" statement into the subroutine, then
it appears that I can stop it.  Of course, if I do that, then
it no longer works.

Any ideas?  About the only thing I can think of at this stage
is to turn it around backwards ... make the current subroutine
into the main program, and attempt to write a power Cobol
subroutine that is a timer.
```

#### ↳ Re: okay, what now?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-KgviNyQ3gUqE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6vjqmh$j1d$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Fri, 9 Oct 1998 02:10:14, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> 
wrote:

 
> Any ideas?  About the only thing I can think of at this stage
> is to turn it around backwards ... make the current subroutine
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I know Fujitsu V3 does not support it, but what about V4? - The Accept
with TIMEOUT that MicroFocus has.  

Optionally you could write your own timer loop - or call the "Sleep" 
api function that has been discussed here in some detail.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: okay, what now?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vlg3n$4n5$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6vjqmh$j1d$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-KgviNyQ3gUqE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>I know Fujitsu V3 does not support it, but what about V4? - The Accept
>with TIMEOUT that MicroFocus has.

As far as I know, they do not have it.  Of course, you never know
with Fujitsu ... I'll give it a try.  That timeout would solve a lot of
problems.  I am somewhat worried, actually, about the lack of
exception handling under Fujitsu.  The "stop" button at the upper
right hand corner is another that worries me.  It seems to be
the equivalent of a power switch ... like press here and destroy
this system by aborting without closing files.  As far as I can tell,
there is absolutely no way to disable or trap it.


>Optionally you could write your own timer loop - or call the "Sleep"
>api function that has been discussed here in some detail.
>
I don't want the loop; I need this to be as low a priority as I can
get. I am also not too worried about data harm, as the files are
all opened in read only mode.  I may just have to fall back on the
sleep API, though the timer function as a subroutine should
be the same thing.  I am finding the incompatibilities between
the power Cobol and the Cobol97 rather frustrating, to tell the
truth.  You do not expect to have conversion problems between
what is supposedly two variations of the same compiler, yet
intermixing Fujitsu regular Cobol and Fujitsu power Cobol is
proving to be a huge pain in the ass.

I hope that in the next version they can decide what a line
of source code looks like. The line number/tab idiocy is enough
to drive one to drink.  It seems to be their major design problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: okay, what now?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-yu1HKs3WzzcF@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<6vjqmh$j1d$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-KgviNyQ3gUqE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <6vlg3n$4n5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Fri, 9 Oct 1998 17:22:01, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> 
wrote:

---- snipped a bunch-o-stuff ----
 
> I hope that in the next version they can decide what a line
> of source code looks like. The line number/tab idiocy is enough
> to drive one to drink.  It seems to be their major design problem.
> 

I'll have to confess to not having these problems.  When I jumped to 
Fujitsu, I was already using a GUI interface with Realia COBOL - Since
I use SP2 and it supports both compilers, the move was virtually 
painless.  I had to convert some of the realia specific stuff I was 
using for file I-O, and a few calls used to read the directory 
structure, but the CBL calls handled that.  I am not done yet - but I 
know I CAN do it.  By using the GUI overall I avoid the problem of the
text mode window and the X close button that terminates the 
application.  Wish I could help you with that.  

I think your problems with Power COBOL vs COBOL 97 may be related to 
the OO syntax in Power COBOL - although I thought the same compiler 
was under the hood.  Interesting.  

Looks like the Sleep API call is your best answer.
```

>I think your problems with Power COBOL vs COBOL 97 may be related to
>the OO syntax in Power COBOL - although I thought the same compiler

    Actually, I think they are the same compiler.  I think what is
happening is that the project manager, which actually writes the
PowerCobol code, is setting a whole bunch of compiler options
differently, and then setting the code up in very specific ways
so that it will work.

>Looks like the Sleep API call is your best answer.

Though I have a post from Fujitsu saying the same thing, I get

what library or linkage options are required to make it work.

<a id="t-410709f5b5"></a>
## Thread: How to terminate accept with Escape in Fujitsu — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to terminate accept with Escape in Fujitsu

- **From:** peter.derichs@rocketmail.com (Peter)
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1086bd756905b65989680@news.wxs.nl>`

```
I'm researching the migration from MF-COBOL to Fujitsu-COBOL. I need to 
be able to get the escape-key as TERMINATOR. 
Anyone know how???
```

#### ↳ Re: How to terminate accept with Escape in Fujitsu

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vj6db$abm$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1086bd756905b65989680@news.wxs.nl>`

```

Peter wrote in message ...
>I'm researching the migration from MF-COBOL to Fujitsu-COBOL. I need to
>be able to get the escape-key as TERMINATOR.
>Anyone know how???

You can define all the keys in a keyboard file. Search the
manual for the file extension KBD.  (The sample file given
is called COBOL.KBD)

You can set the terminator keys in the environment as well,
but it is better (in my opinion) to set up the file, then specify
the file in the environment instead.  That allows you to nail
them all in one place.  After I/O, it will put the exception
keystrokes into the key status register, and you can look at
them.  If you have a problem, tell me and I will post an example.
```

<a id="t-044634b7be"></a>
## Thread: entry statement in cobol — 3 msgs

### entry statement in cobol

- **From:** narasing@yahoo.com (narasingarao)
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<907881023.17886@wren.supernews.com>`

```
Hi,
     I am facing a typical problem during compilation of cobol programs having entry statements.
What is the differece between the ENTRY statement  and CALL statement in COBOL ?
What is the right way to link the programs having the CALL as well as the ENTRY statements ? 
 the situation is progA calling progB and prog C and prog B has the ENTRY statement.
The load module is ABC.I have made change to B now how do I link the programs to get the new executable load ABC?
I have a linking procedure which accepts the following  parameters

//sysin dd *
include load(program1)
include prodlib(main program)
entry xxx
NAME member(R)
/*
//load dd dsn= "load library"  





   -**** Posted from Supernews, Discussions Start Here(tm) ****-
http://www.supernews.com/ - Host to the World's Discussions & Usenet
```

#### ↳ Re: entry statement in cobol

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981008191958.29057.00003581@ng124.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<907881023.17886@wren.supernews.com>`

```

 narasing@yahoo.com writes ...

>Hi,

Hi, yourself

>
>     I am facing a typical problem during compilation of cobol programs
>having entry statements.

Not so typical. ENTRY statements aren't that common in COBOL programs, I don't
believe.

>
>What is the differece between the ENTRY statement  and CALL statement in
>COBOL ?

An ENTRY statement defines a callable point in a program other than the main
procedure. A CALL statement can name the program-id (to enter at the procedure
division), or it can name the name identified in the ENTRY statement.

So CALL goes out to a program point. That point is the program-id or an
alternate ENTRY point.


>
>What is the right way to link the programs having the CALL as well as the
>ENTRY statements ? 

Several ways. Basically, if the module with the ENTRY point you want is brought
in through the linkage editor's automatic call resolution (i.e.: look in
SYSLIB) , the ENTRY point will be found automatically. Otherwise, you will have
to implicitly INCLUDE the module.

>
> the situation is progA calling progB and prog C and prog B has the ENTRY
>statement.

Wait a minute. What name does the ENTRY statement identify? Do you mean: A
calls B, and A calls C and that B contains the entry point C also?

>
>The load module is ABC.I have made change to B now how do I link the programs
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>//sysin dd *

You mean 

//SYSIN  DD  *

>
>include load(program1)
You mean
     INCLUDE DDNAME1(PROGB)

where DDNAME1 is the name of the DD statement pointing to where PROGB is found

>
>include prodlib(main program)

You mean
     INCLUDE DDNAME2(PROGA)

where DDNAME2 is the name of the DD statement pointing to where PROGA is found
(note these may be the same)


>
>entry xxx
>
 You mean
     ENTRY PROGA

>NAME member(R)

You mean
     NAME PROGA(R)

>
>/*                                     - not needed

>
>//load dd dsn= "load library"  

Which load library? Where PROGA is found? Where PROGB is found? Either case, it
should be all caps, no quotes, and add ,DISP=SHR; based on earlier notes, you
probably mean

//DDNAME1  DD  DSN=library_name,DISP=SHR

There are lots of places today where lowercase is accepted. JCL is not one of
them. Precision of communication is important, it seems to me, in order to
understand any problem you describe.

Cheers,



Regards


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: entry statement in cobol

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3622aa58.12511103@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<907881023.17886@wren.supernews.com>`

```
narasing@yahoo.com (narasingarao) wrote:

>Hi,
>     I am facing a typical problem during compilation of cobol programs having entry statements.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The entry statement defines where in the procedure code of the called
program is to start executing. The CALL would be to that entry point,
using the same name to match up.

The only times I've ever used this delibrately was in a i/o subroutine
(which I didn't have any say in designing) where we called the  main
entry point to open the file, and other entry points for read,
rewrite, write and close. Probably the most common use of entry
statements is for specialized evironments like IMS, DB2 or CICS where
you get something like 

PROCEDURE DIVISION. 
	ENTRY DLITCBL USING xxxxxx. 
PARAGRAPH1.

and then a little program stub is link edited to the program to handle
some environmental issues. 

In your example, assuming static links (NODYNAM) the only reason for
an entry statement would be to define the point where the program was
supposed to start executing when the load module is executed.  All the
other calls should be automatically resolved by the linkage editor
matching the calls to the corresponding entry points in the called
program..

You should be able to dump the need for an entry statement simply by
changing the load order or including a linkage editor ORDER statement
so that the primary module is first in the load module and assuming
that you want to start executing at the beginning of the procedure
division. 

			Scott Peterson
```

<a id="t-a6a6474441"></a>
## Thread: RFD: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol — 33 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RFD: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

- **From:** "Scott Woodward" <scottw@southpacfinance.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** news.announce.newgroups,news.groups,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org>`

```
                     REQUEST FOR DISCUSSION (RFD)
              unmoderated group comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

This is a formal Request For Discussion (RFD) for the creation of a
world-wide unmoderated Usenet newsgroup comp.lang.cobol.acucobol.
This is not a Call for Votes (CFV); you cannot vote at this time.
Procedural details are below.

Newsgroup line:
comp.lang.cobol.acucobol	All aspects of the use of Acucobol.

RATIONALE: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

Acucobol is a company based in San Diego CA. Currently all users and
developers use email to communicate ideas and problems to Acucobol
(now known as Acucorp). These ideas and problems however are not
released to the general populous of Acucobol users. This presents a
problem for both Acucobol and the Users.

The purpose of creating this comp.lang.cobol.acucobol newsgroup is to
have a central point of contact for all users. Discussions involving
Acucobol-specific information would be greatly enhanced by this group
due to the world wide use of Acucobol.

Due to the high volume of traffic in comp.lang.cobol and the variety
of Cobol languages, it would seem logical to start to create subgroups
of Cobol.

We've received comments that comp.lang.cobol isn't being used because:

A significant portion of the traffic deals with mainframe COBOL.  What
open-systems COBOL traffic there is tends to deal with MicroFocus, RM
or Fujitsu.

Here are some reasons in favor of a comp.lang.cobol.acucobol:

Currently, most developers cannot easily come in contact with other
developers other than by attending Acucorp's yearly developer's
conference.

Acucobol contains significant extensions to the COBOL standard.
Discussion of these extensions would be of little interest to
comp.lang.cobol readers who are not Acucobol developers.

There are a number of Acucorp products which are used in conjunction
with Acucobol that do not directly involve COBOL. Because these
products tend to be used in larger, more complex systems they also
tend to cause more discussion than the base language. Such discussion
would be only marginally on topic in comp.lang.cobol.

CHARTER: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

This newsgroup is for discussion of all aspects of the use of
Acucobol.  Some examples of relevant topics are:

  Coding techniques
  Learning and teaching materials
  Discussions on coding
  User Support
  Technical Support
  User Discussions
  General Information

The primary purpose of this group is to pass information between the
users of Acucobol. Explicitly discouraged are the posting of
commercial advertisements, any kind of flaming and personal arguments,
as well as the posting of binaries of any kind.

END CHARTER.

PROCEDURE:

This is a request for discussion, not a call for votes.  In this phase
of the process, any potential problems with the proposed newsgroups
should be raised and resolved.  The discussion period will continue
for a minimum of 21 days (starting from when the first RFD for this
proposal is posted to news.announce.newgroups), after which a Call For
Votes (CFV) may be posted by a neutral vote taker if the discussion
warrants it.  Please do not attempt to vote until this happens.

All discussion of this proposal should be posted to news.groups.

This RFD attempts to comply fully with the Usenet newsgroup creation
guidelines outlined in "How to Create a New Usenet Newsgroup" and "How
to Format and Submit a New Group Proposal".  Please refer to these
documents (available in news.announce.newgroups) if you have any
questions about the process.

DISTRIBUTION:

This RFD has been posted to the following newsgroups:

  news.announce.newgroups, news.groups,
  alt.cobol, comp.lang.cobol

Proponent: Scott Woodward <scottw@southpacfiance.com.au>
```

#### ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org>`

```
Personally, I think that this is one of the worst ideas that I have seen in
a long time - and I intend to encourage as many people as I know to vote
against this when (if) this ever comes to a vote.

I understand that concern about many of the posts in comp.lang.cobol not
being relevant to AcuCOBOL users.  HOWEVER, many of the posts are relevant
and most particularly are relevant either to "Standard" COBOL or to
cross-compiler issues.

I would certainly be very happy if more AcuCOBOL interested people posted in
the existing newsgroup - and would hope that they start doing so soon.  If
the number of current "off-topic" posts is a problem, it would certainly be
reasonable for such users to put "AcuCOBOL" in their subject line so most
"newsgroup filters" could find these posts (more quickly or exclusively).

PLEASE, please,
    Don't start breaking up the COBOL newsgroups into product-specific
newsgroups.  This will destroy one of COBOL's most important strengths - its
portability.

P.S. Should anyone think I have a "conflict of interest" - I will be totally
up front and indicate that I have worked for Micro Focus and I have headed
the GUIDE (IBM mainframe users) COBOL project.  I also currently maintain
the COBOL FAQ (distributed twice a month in these newsgroups).  I don't
personally see any conflict, but do want to follow the road of "full
disclosure" when actively opposing this proposed newsgroup.


Scott Woodward wrote in message <907917797.22206@isc.org>...
>                     REQUEST FOR DISCUSSION (RFD)
>              unmoderated group comp.lang.cobol.acucobol
…[93 more quoted lines elided]…
>Proponent: Scott Woodward <scottw@southpacfiance.com.au>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<6volom$7cc$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```


William M. Klein wrote:

> Personally, I think that this is one of the worst ideas that I have seen in
> a long time - and I intend to encourage as many people as I know to vote
> against this when (if) this ever comes to a vote.

Mr. Klein, I have to agree with you that this is not a very good idea. I would
not go as far to say it was one of the worst ideas but it comes very very close.

>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "newsgroup filters" could find these posts (more quickly or exclusively).

I would not mind seeing how many people are not posting to this group because
AcuCobol is not a "major" topic of conversation. Personally I think that
including
AcuCobol in the subject is a good way to identify questions related to the
product
and if AcuCobol(Acucorp) is interested in supporting its users base they can
freely
post responses in this group just like Fujitsu.


>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> portability.

Can't argue with this point. It is one of the reasons I like Cobol!

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> disclosure" when actively opposing this proposed newsgroup.

Based upon my current thoughts I would also have to vote no on the
establishment of this group. It has the potential of thinning out the knowledge
base and water down comp.lang.cobol until it has almost no value.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298283.16572.24121@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In message <<6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
> 
> I would certainly be very happy if more AcuCOBOL interested people posted in
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Absolutely agree.  Having their own group won't stop them getting
'off topic' posts.  Headhunters and spammers will simply add 
the new group to their crossposting list and then there will
be double the noise.  The solution is to raise the signal
level and ignore the noise.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

- **From:** boonlee@melbpc.org.au (Boon Lee)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36208f5d.3852959@news.melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I fully agree with what Mr. Klein  has said  below. COBOL is the only
programming language that has been available  and is still availlable
across all computer platforms since 1970 (if not earlier). It is not
identified with any particular platform(and it must be borne in mind
that many platforms have come and gone). By now even a  layman in the
street has heard of cobol AND THAT it is not associated with any
computer vendor. It simply is a language that has  always been used
and is still being used today because it is a very good language for
specific aplications and that is true across all computing platforms.
That is its strength.

It is not tue that  comp.lang.cobol is of interest only to mainframe
(meaning IBM etc) developers only. Until recently, I was a cobol
developer in a HP3000 environment, now in a openvms environment and I
have always found this newgroup useful and relevent. There is only one
cobol standard; there may be extensions (which are easily bridged),
but only one standard and that forms the core/base  of the cobol
language across all platforms. Looking at the charter of the proposed
newsgroup, I (and I am sure other cobol practioners too) would be
interested in their postings.   I would also be equally sure that
acucobol developers will find the postings of this newsgroup relevent
and useful for the most part.

As a cobol pratitioner, I definitely wish every success for acucobol;
however in the same vein, I would think that any future success of
acucobol will  go hand in hand with the continuing strength of cobol
and therein lies my arguement that postings with regard to acucobol
should be made in only one single cobol newsgroup,  this
one:comp.lang.cobol..

 

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Personally, I think that this is one of the worst ideas that I have seen in
>a long time - and I intend to encourage as many people as I know to vote
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
       <snip> <cut because the server does not allow the orginal
message to be longer than my message>
      <snip>
     <snip> 
===================================================

Boon Lee
Melbourne PC User Group

E-mail : boonlee@melbpc.org.au

===================================================
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups

- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Thanks William,

	I do appreciate your comments, but the main purpose of this RFD is NOT to
break up the COBOL community but to actively create an area which can boost
the input and ease of access for specific problems
(At the moment related to Acucobol, but there could be others...) 

	Some things which are discussed are not at all related to COBOL but are
related to products which Acucorp
produce in conjuction with the COBOL product.


Regards

Scott Woodward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol SB comp.lang.acucorp.usage

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<6vtadt$pdc$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <01bdf5c4$a8046f60$874f6ccb@xxxx>`

```


Scott Woodward wrote:

> Thanks William,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Scott Woodward

Hi Scott,

I have given some thought to your RFD post and I still think that an
establishment of a subset "Cobol" group(s) would simply have a
negative effect upon comp.lang.cobol.  The strength of
com.lang.cobol is from the unity of the group and from the
diverse knowledge base which supplies all of the great
answers to various questions.

      The rational of your RFD is week at best and does not justify
the establishment of a new news group. You do make some good
points and IMO the best way to address these issues is the
establishment of comp.lang.acucorp.usage. This group would
deal with the non-Cobol issues that are specific to the Acucorp
product.

       Scott, IMO changing the name of the news group would
help focus he group on issues concerning the usage of Acucorp
products and not the usage of Cobol. This would draw Acucorp
users to a singe group and any users can go there to get the
latest information concerning Acucorp.

Respectfully,
Jeff Farkas
JeffreyFarkas@earthlink.net

COBOL FAQ:
http://pw2.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

FAQ(S):
http://www.htmlhelp.org/faq/

"I calculated the odds of this thing succeeding versus the odds of
it doing something incredibly stupid. . and. . I went ahead anyway"
{Crow from MST3K}
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol SB comp.lang.acucorp.usage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn724tlg.ck.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <01bdf5c4$a8046f60$874f6ccb@xxxx> <6vtadt$pdc$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Mon, 12 Oct 1998 12:23:18 -0400, Jeff Farkas
<JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net> wrote:
>       Scott, IMO changing the name of the news group would
>help focus he group on issues concerning the usage of Acucorp products and not
>the usage of Cobol. This would draw Acucorp users to a singe group and any
>users can go there to get the latest information concerning Acucorp.

First, the disclosure: Although Mr. Woodward doesn't work for Acucorp and
suggested the idea to us, we have offered our help on his RFD since we'd been
discussing similar ideas.

Would something like a comp.(sys|soft-sys).acucorp or comp.lang.cobol.acucorp
be more palatable? We do have a number of products which are definitely
Acucorp-specific. Placing them under comp.lang.cobol would reflect the fact
they are related to COBOL and not a separate language (which makes me dubious
about comp.lang.acucorp.usage). 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol SB comp.lang.acucorp.usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups

- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <01bdf5c4$a8046f60$874f6ccb@xxxx> <6vtadt$pdc$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <slrn724tlg.ck.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

Chris Adams wrote:

> On Mon, 12 Oct 1998 12:23:18 -0400, Jeff Farkas
> <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> The opinions above are my own and may or may not match those of my employer.

Mr. Adams,

  I tend to agree with the others that the establishment of a Cobol sub group would

not be a very good idea. If it were set up why not set up comp.lang.cobol.fujitus,
comp.lang.cobol.<any vendor>, etc.

 The question is how should Acucorp communicate with its user
base and not break up the core users in comp.lang.cobol??

I tend to think that comp.soft-sys.acucorp or alt.acucorp would be
a method to  accomplish this if a news group must be set up.
But the best way to go would be the establishment of a mailing list
and/or a FAQ on  Acucorps home page.

 IMO, if it is really necessary to set up a new news group I think that
alt.acucorp would be the way to go. But I still think that monitoring
comp.lang.cobol would be the best bet in the long run. The amount of
traffic is not that high and if it does get to be that much setting up
alt.acucorp would be the easiest way to go!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** denebeim@deepthot.ml.org (Jay Denebeim)
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<705obq$ra7$1@marvin.deepthot.ml.org>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <slrn72c9r5.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <705a37$5j$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <slrn72cc9o.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

```
In article <slrn72cc9o.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>,
Chris Adams <cadams@acucorp.com> wrote:

>I don't know any more than you do - the last email I received was on
>Monday.  He's probably busy; AFAIK this is being done in his spare
>time.

Then he has insufficient quantities of spare time to propose a
newsgroup.  A proponant should be prepared to spend at least 1/2 hour
per day on news.groups, and that's if the group is non-controversial
which this one is not. Anything less and the chances of his proposal
passing are pretty dismal.

I'm very uncomfortable about a newsgroup for a specific product.
Especially when it talks about tech support and so on.  Usenet is
public, it's also resource intensive.  A company really shouldn't
expect 100s of thousands of machines world wide to provide resources
to it for free.  Web pages are much more economical, even a publicly
accessable news server at your company doesn't cost much.  You guys
are making money off of your product, you should be supporting it.

Jay
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn72cvlb.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <slrn72c9r5.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <705a37$5j$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <slrn72cc9o.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <705obq$ra7$1@marvin.deepthot.ml.org>`

```
On 15 Oct 1998 15:10:18 -0600, Jay Denebeim <denebeim@deepthot.ml.org> wrote:
>I'm very uncomfortable about a newsgroup for a specific product.
(That'd be more accurately stated as "a specific language dialect and related
products")

There would seem to be ample precedent for it in the comp.lang hierarchy, let
alone the rest of the comp.* groups. A cursory search might find:

comp.lang.basic.visual.*
comp.lang.clarion
comp.lang.clipper
comp.lang.pascal.borland
comp.lang.pascal.delphi.*

It'd be fairly accurate to say that Delphi is to Pascal as Acucobol is to
COBOL.

>Especially when it talks about tech support and so on.  Usenet is
>public, it's also resource intensive.  A company really shouldn't
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>are making money off of your product, you should be supporting it.

We already do.  It's also true that we have nothing to do with the RFD other
than considering it a good idea.  

We aren't asking anyone to support our products for us and I had already been
recommending the alternatives (mailing lists, web forums, public news servers)
before we received Mr. Woodward's initial message. One of the first things I
sent him was a the document I was working on. It would be unreasonable to
expect us not to support his efforts.
  
We try to make it as easy as possible for our customers to get in contact with
us as possible. Any of our customers can contact us at no cost on the phone, by
our BBS, our website (http://www.acucorp.com/) or email. When an Acucobol user
(Mr Woodward) contacted us about his intention to propose a newsgroup, we gave
him some information he requested and decided that we'd commit to having
someone monitor that group, similar to the way I currently monitor alt.cobol
and comp.lang.cobol. We also agreed to send an email to our customers so that
interested parties could get in contact with Mr. Woodward.


Chris
```

Adams) wrote:


>There would seem to be ample precedent for it in the comp.lang hierarchy, let
>alone the rest of the comp.* groups. A cursory search might find:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL.

Agreed. I just don't think the traffic warrants a seperate group
unlike the other languages which require greater discussion and help
and that creating a new group will create the amount of traffic.

For arguements sake, we should also have...

comp.lang.cobol.ansi
comp.lang.cobol.ibm.osvs
comp.lang.cobol.ibm.vs
comp.lang.cobol.microfocus.netexpress

etc.

The 40-50 posts a day currently in comp.lang.cobol would get split
pretty thinly I think and we'd lose the cross implementation nature of
the group which is after all what we spend most of our time talking
about.

Only group I'd be in favour of is comp.lang.cobol.jobs then we could
perhaps get rid of (most of) the stupid job posts and docdwarfs
boilerplate.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Scott Woodward" <scottw@southpacfinance.com.au>
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdfc19$5027a7c0$42206ccb@xxxx>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <slrn72c9r5.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <705a37$5j$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <slrn72cc9o.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <705obq$ra7$1@marvin.deepthot.ml.org> <slrn72cvlb.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <36272340.306789730@news.freeserve.net> <slrn72f0hv.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <36278e6d.10344194@news.freeserve.net>`

```
Thanks to all who have posted responses to my RFD. Please let me attempt to
address some of your concerns about this proposal, to better illustrate
where I am coming from and to encourage futher discussion.

As I have said before and as has been amply demonstrated in previous
postings, precedents for this type of sub-group have been set before and
appear to be working to everyone's satisfaction. I am not setting a
precedent here in suggesting that a sub-newsgroup of a newsgroup be
created.

What I am proposing is, let me again stress, a *user* group. I do not
represent Acucorp in any way, shape or form. I actually find it somewhat
insulting that some apparently see my part in this proposal as being some
sort of "patsy" for Acucorp. Please rethink. Suggestions for news servers,
web sites etc. to be promulgated by Acucorp are all good ideas, however
they will not serve my specific purpose, which, I repeat, is simply to
create an unmoderated forum where I and other Acucobol *users* around the
world can freely exchange information and ideas. 

In regard to the claims that I haven't the time to respond to my own RFD, I
find it surprising that some feel there should be an "arbitrary" amount of
time allocated to dealing with RFD responses which, no disrespect intended,
sometimes do not warrant a considered response. Let me assure everyone that
I have been closely monitoring all responses. Everyone is entitled to
express their opinion and I do not feel that I necessarily need to agree or
refute at this stage of the game. Don't misinterpret this as a lack of due
care or attention. 

Aside from my prime argument for this newsgroup, this proposal could IMHO
actually benefit the perception of Cobol in its exposure to the wider world
through newsgroups. By this I mean that the existence of subgroups could
help to illustrate the greater variety of cobols being used around the
world apart from the "garden variety" cobol which is stereotypically seen
as being a little dated. Surely this can only be a positive thing for Cobol
in general?

Why not help push Cobol out onto the world stage for the 21st Century?
Cobol as I see it will only continue to expand and extend in the future,
and I can see further potential for diversification of cobol newsgroups in
the future. If we want the Cobol community to grow and develop along with
this expansion, why is it seen as so negative by some to have a sub-group
on the subject of Cobol? 

I urge you to consider some of the positives of a proposal such as this
rather than focusing so much on the perceived negatives which, I believe,
are being overstated. Thanks again for your responses and I look forward to
reading more.

Regards
Scott Woodward
Proponent
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Arn Kratzer" <arn@casemaker.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,news.groups
- **Message-ID:** `<5z8X1.1032$Rj4.3927739@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <slrn72c9r5.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <705a37$5j$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <slrn72cc9o.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <705obq$ra7$1@marvin.deepthot.ml.org> <slrn72cvlb.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <36272340.306789730@news.freeserve.net> <slrn72f0hv.cuc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <36278e6d.10344194@news.freeserve.net> <01bdfc19$5027a7c0$42206ccb@xxxx>`

```


>
>What I am proposing is, let me again stress, a *user* group. I do not
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


While I could argue for various vendors being "forced" to support their own
COBOL user groups, having them in an open format, grouped together, could
create some synergy, as problems in one group could be solved by
conversations in another.  (Much the way that an answer to a thread question
can be often found by looking at other threads.)  Allowing cross COBOL
platform communication is a good thing!  Making it easy to read comments
from many COBOL types is also very nice.

And, by being out here, away from vendors, we do have more freedom, etc.

Also, I do agree that dealing with COBOL "related" matters is very
important.  In some cases, users may be reluctant to bring up an issue that
is "not" COBOL, but only occurs in certain kinds of COBOL systems.  Having a
"special chatroom" for that subset is very helpful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,news.groups,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vtk5r$7s1$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <6vohmn$icp@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-zrdidXXyUDHN@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36221d45.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
>>> Personally, I think that this is one of the worst ideas that I have seen
>in
>>> a long time - and I intend to encourage as many people as I know to vote
>>> against this when (if) this ever comes to a vote.


I might be running against the crowd here, but I am not
all that against sub-groups forming.

First of all, I think that there are specific issues that are
vendor specific.  JCL and all of that sort of sub-discussion
have almost nothing to do, for example, with Cobol. Similarly,
issues having to do with GUI design and specific integrated
development tools and their usage have almost nothing to
do with Cobol, they have to do with vendors of Cobol compilers
and how they package their product.

Secondly, I think it gives out conflicting signals.  We bitch
and complain in here about how Cobol is not "popular" even
though it is the most common language.  Then we say that
there is not enough interest for a second newsgroup, and that
having two discussions on the language at the same time
is going to weaken it to the point it may kill off discussion. If
you look at any other language, there are probably a dozen
different groups ... how come?  Maybe because they are
popular, and maybe they are popular because talking about
them in new ways is encouraged.  Perhaps, for example, we
should have a newsgroup JUST for learning OO.  Maybe that
is part of our problem ... we are becoming "against change".

Last but not least, I cannot see the harm.  That it would decrease
the number of postings is ludicrous.  It simply means that the
postings are sorted into a somewhat more efficient threaded
structure. If it does not work out, then it will be gone soon enough.
Newsgroups become empty and die off all the time. In fact, I can
see a need for several more newsgroups for topics that might
last only a year or two.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.acucobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** denebeim@deepthot.ml.org (Jay Denebeim)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,news.groups,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vu3rr$ldk$1@marvin.deepthot.ml.org>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-zrdidXXyUDHN@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36221d45.0@news1.ibm.net> <6vtk5r$7s1$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <6vtk5r$7s1$1@news.igs.net>,
Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>We bitch and complain in here about how Cobol is not "popular" even
>though it is the most common language.

I don't think that's true anymore.  C has been the most popular for
new development for quite awhile now.  Granted there's several Sagans
of legacy cobol code, but I suspect that by now C has surpassed it in
total lines of code.  There's alot more programmers out there now than
there used to be.  If you include all procedure oriented languages (C
& friends, Pascal & friends, plus the bazlillion proprietary ones) I
don't think there's any question.  (IMHO, YMMV, etc)

Jay
```

       Does anyone know if IBM supports Report Writer for COBOL for
OS/390?
      Thanks very much for any help.

```

#### ↳ Re: Report Writer

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981011135516.17653.00006790@ng31.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<361E1AFB.3378D75A@edinfor.pt>`

```

><HTML><PRE>Subject: Report Writer
>From: "Drï¿½. Emilia Coelho" <ecoelho@edinfor.pt>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

Report Writer was dropped with the introduction of COBOL II.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

- **From:** Rex Widmer <rwidmer@sound.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36211A12.6A72@sound.net>`
- **References:** `<361E1AFB.3378D75A@edinfor.pt> <19981011135516.17653.00006790@ng31.aol.com>`

```
Yes REPORT WRITER was dropped from the compiler with the introduction of
VS COBOL II in 1984 or so, but the SPC Systems REPORT WRITER PRECOMPILER
was released with an IBM part number in about 1985, and supports all of
the VS COBOL II, COBOL/370, COBOL for MVS and VM and COBOL for OS/390
and VM compilers.  This can be used as either a transparent compiler
shell or as a "get rid of report writer syntax, give me just equivalent
COBOL" tool.

Check IBMLINK or your IBM rep for details.

The pre-compiler works quite well
Theo DP wrote:
> 
> ><HTML><PRE>Subject: Report Writer
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Report Writer was dropped with the introduction of COBOL II.
```
