###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36237636.B27716@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36222169.0@news2.uswest.net> <19981012130155.16096.00000095@ng107.aol.com> <36236210.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```
> 
> That practically begs me to ask you to detail the nature of your dislike.
> So I'll ask: Why do you dislike Report Writer?

I also dislike Report Writer.  I think this is mainly because
programmers don't think about it the same way as they do the rest of
Cobol IO.  It is almost a different language.  Why not use a different
language optimized for that task?

Maintenance programmers have to know standard COBOL.  But they are often
unfamiliar with Report Writer.  This unfamiliarity causes problems.  So
why not standardize on doing output for reports the same way all of the
time - using the same type of logic used for all the rest of the output!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7004dv$hhh@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36222169.0@news2.uswest.net> <19981012130155.16096.00000095@ng107.aol.com> <36236210.0@news3.uswest.net> <36237636.B27716@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36237636.B27716@home.com>...
>>
>> That practically begs me to ask you to detail the nature of your dislike.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>time - using the same type of logic used for all the rest of the output!

As a strong proponent of Report Writer, I do want to add my understanding of
WHY so many programmers (and entire programming staffs) HATE report writer.
In my opinion (at least for IBM mainframe shops), it is because:

1) The original OS/VS COBOL (ANS COBOL?) implementation was quite "buggy"

2) The original documentation was almost entirely incomprehensible

3) The original compiler interface would give you a compiler error for the
1st error - and then stop checking.  This meant repeated compiles (in the
days of punched decks - no less) to get a clean compile.

4) There were originally almost no "debugging" tools that were targeted at
report writer code.

This, along with the above mentioned fact that training of IBM mainframe
COBOL programmers almost always omitted report writer, made this a little
used, little understood, and little desired feature.  Furthermore, this
became a "viscous circle" as new programmers came along, there were no
experienced programmers to help them with report writer, so new programmers
didn't learn it and thought it must be "bad" and/or "hard".

All of these technical problems SHOULD be fixed by now - but (like COBOL
itself among "academics") report writer has such a poor reputation that few
COBOL programmers look at its power and usefulness.

I will be MOST happy when it becomes required (and enhanced) via the
Standard and more and more new COBOL programmers become familiar with it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r5QU1.329$DN2.3456214@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36222169.0@news2.uswest.net> <19981012130155.16096.00000095@ng107.aol.com> <36236210.0@news3.uswest.net> <36237636.B27716@home.com> <7004dv$hhh@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I was taught report writer in college and I hated learning it, using it and
maintaining it. I have worked in many shops and none use report writer. The
same code in COBOL can be written in a report writing utility, such as
EZtrieve, Dyl280, and even DF/SyncSort in just a few lines. Building it in
to COBOL will simply make already long and complicated programs longer and
even more complicated.
Leave report writing to a language built for it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3624b66d.0@news2.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36222169.0@news2.uswest.net> <19981012130155.16096.00000095@ng107.aol.com> <36236210.0@news3.uswest.net> <36237636.B27716@home.com> <7004dv$hhh@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <r5QU1.329$DN2.3456214@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message ...
>I was taught report writer in college and I hated learning it, using it and
>maintaining it. I have worked in many shops and none use report writer. The
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Syncsort's Sortwriter facility can be very powerful, but has it's own
limitations.   One of the biggest is internal maintenance.  Just looking at
any type of Syncsort instruction tells you nothing about the data, only
where Syncsort itself has been told to look for that data.  There is no way
to tell what any field is (at least not in the version we have).

EZtrieve is a massive CPU hog (but not as bad as SAS), and I haven't seen
Dyl280.  I'll admit SAS is even faster to code than report writer, and from
what I've seen of EZtrieve, it is also faster to code in.

I'm going to have to say that I find Report Writer far closer to COBOL that
either EZtrieve or SAS.  Both of these languages require a higher learning
curve than Report Writer, especially SAS (okay, writing a PROC PRINT
statement to do basic printouts is a breeze, but learning enough about SAS
to reliably control everything . . ., well, I did it on my own, so I had a
tough time of it).

There are tons of places that use ordinary COBOL to do report generation,
and do not have report writing tools to help them out.  When Report Writer
becomes standard again (and hoping that it won't be as buggy as the
original; though that's a compiler vendor issue), everyone who goes to the
new COBOL (admitting that there will be many who won't go to it) will have a
report writing tool.

Although I'm getting into the issue of modularization of code, using Report
Writer directly in your COBOL program allows you to generate report details
lines in the same read-to-write pass that will encompass all other
processing.  If you finish processing a file, and then start a separate
report writing package, a completely new set of I/O is required.
```

```
sufficiently.

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3624D515.1E881E75@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36222169.0@news2.uswest.net> <19981012130155.16096.00000095@ng107.aol.com> <36236210.0@news3.uswest.net> <36237636.B27716@home.com> <7004dv$hhh@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <r5QU1.329$DN2.3456214@storm.twcol.com> <3627c2db.6771817@news.apk.net>`

```
What's interesting is that there are lots of shops which have standards
which actively discourage or even ban COBOL sorts.  I understand the
extract/sort/report methodology and approve of it, and understand the
problems of integrating too many functions together.  That's part of the
reason I am a Cobol apologist against the OO crowd.  But COBOL sorts
don't seem complicated - maybe they use the same arguments I have
against Report Writer.


> I had the same experience.  That is, I learned it in college, but I've
> never seen it used outside of school.  I didn't hate it, but I
> certainly concur that using Eztrieve or Dyl280 is much easier if all
> you want to do is read a file and report its contents.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** B W Spoor <bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3624E032.312FE8E8@fridaycs.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36222169.0@news2.uswest.net> <19981012130155.16096.00000095@ng107.aol.com> <36236210.0@news3.uswest.net> <36237636.B27716@home.com> <7004dv$hhh@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <r5QU1.329$DN2.3456214@storm.twcol.com> <3627c2db.6771817@news.apk.net> <3624D515.1E881E75@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> What's interesting is that there are lots of shops which have
> standards
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > you want to do is read a file and report its contents.

I make quite extensive use of both the COBOL Sort verb and Report Writer
for print/report programs. The in-house standard is to use Report Writer
for ALL reports.

Once you get the hang of it, it is very quick and easy to create a
report program, complete with headings, footings, sub-totals & totals in
a very few lines of procedure division. The Report Section does take a
bit of writing, but IMHO far less than is required in the Procedure
Division to handle page breaks, control breaks, totals etc.

I found the manual very hard to understand (and I am far from a novice
programmer - 25 years this summer just past, mainly Cobol), but
perservered and have now been using it for about 8 years - I wouldn't
like to write a report without it.

It does have it's drawbacks and can be a little inflexible, but most
things can be handled with a little ingenuity, similar to writing it the
'standard way' - you just need to look at it differently, basically from
the data/output required aspect rather than from how to process the
data.

Combined with the SORT verb, it is extremely powerful - Extract, Sort
Report. I find that I can create short, easily readable code for reports
that require data manipulation.

I can't answer for the relative effeciency/inefficiency of the internal
sort and report writer,  but I believe that with the power of the target
machines, speed & maintainabilty of code is generally more important.

There will be exceptions to this, I remember years ago testing a
report/file rebuild where we estinmated it's run time to be 4 days from
extrapolating the test data - this needed to run as part of the
overnight batch in one night - it had to be rewritten for run time
rather than nice code.

Brian
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)

```
Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>What's interesting is that there are lots of shops which have standards
>which actively discourage or even ban COBOL sorts.  I understand the
>extract/sort/report methodology and approve of it, and understand the
>problems of integrating too many functions together. 

Most of the COBOL sort standards like that date back to VS/COBOL Even
with the using/giving options it was much slower than a JCL sort. 

What most people missed was that you could write a JCL SORT with
e15/e35 exits written in COBOL to handle the extract and reporting and
produce the needed output with extremely simple COBOL routines.

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298286.67086.6935@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36237636.B27716@home.com>`

```
In message <<36237636.B27716@home.com>> Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> writes:
> > That practically begs me to ask you to detail the nature of your dislike.
> > So I'll ask: Why do you dislike Report Writer?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> time - using the same type of logic used for all the rest of the output!

I dislike Report Writer for an entirely different reason,
it is still code.  If the user wants a small change in 
the report it still has to be recoded and recompiled.
Even report generators that spit out Cobol source need
to have compilations.  This removes the user from 
control of the process.

I developed my own reporting program in Cobol that
reads a script and outputs a report based on that
script.  It has limits but will do two file (master/
transaction eg) and 4 levels of control breaks, will
output in the order of any field(s), has 20 selelection
control.

Or use Chrystal Reports.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<df9V1.695$DN2.4126254@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36237636.B27716@home.com> <3298286.67086.6935@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
>I dislike Report Writer for an entirely different reason,
>it is still code.  If the user wants a small change in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>control of the process.


I whole heartedly agree. When users want small changes you may have to
recompile the entire program and continue to test. Using soemthing akin to
your program such as DF/Syncsort, EZtrieve, or DYL280 you simplify the
entire process. Users rarely have any comprehension as to what their changes
are going to do to the entire report process. They ask for small changes and
those small changes take days to implement in COBOL: sort sequences, adding
fields to a report, or even adding "PAGE 1 OF 20". These changes look simple
to the average human being, but imagine if it took you a week to develop the
COBOL report and now you have to rexamine the entire process to figure out
how to implement what seems like a simple change.

EXTRACT
FILTER
SORT
REPORT

I will believe it is the only way until someone proves there is an easier
way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

In my current conversion, the existing reports will be the last.
The approach for me, in the future, will to simply export collections
of data into Excel, and let the user do whatever they want with it.
I plan to supply a couple of standard templates for the basics,
but that will be about it.  Even something like a customer listing
can be done that way for anything under 3 or 4 thousand customers.
100 megabytes of memory does make a difference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AA2W1.84$IF6.940659@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36237636.B27716@home.com> <3298286.67086.6935@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
>I dislike Report Writer for an entirely different reason,
>it is still code.  If the user wants a small change in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>control of the process.


I whole heartedly agree. When users want small changes you may have to
recompile the entire program and continue to test. Using soemthing akin to
your program such as DF/Syncsort, EZtrieve, or DYL280 you simplify the
entire process. Users rarely have any comprehension as to what their changes
are going to do to the entire report process. They ask for small changes and
those small changes take days to implement in COBOL: sort sequences, adding
fields to a report, or even adding "PAGE 1 OF 20". These changes look simple
to the average human being, but imagine if it took you a week to develop the
COBOL report and now you have to rexamine the entire process to figure out
how to implement what seems like a simple change.

EXTRACT
FILTER
SORT
REPORT

I will believe it is the only way until someone proves there is an easier
way.
```

```
how can you expect them to realise the impact upon you of their

to take and cost. Eventually the users apologised for asking for complex
changes which only took me 5 minutes to do. Talk to them (if you are
allowed to do so).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Russell Strope" <RussStrope@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdfa39$4b7b65e0$15cf430c@RussStrope>`
- **References:** `<36237636.B27716@home.com> <3298286.67086.6935@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
I beleive in keeping your programs simple and minimizing the number of
functions that are being performed.  I do not recommend having a program
perform a bunch of tasks and then do an internal sort and have report
writer create a report.   It is tough enough to find good programmers, let
alone finding ones who know report writer.

I have my shop use a report generator, Easytreive, to produce all reports. 
Even rookie programmers can produce complex control break reports in a day.
 They enjoy using it and do not even want to code report logic in a
program.  Easytreive is just not limited to report generation. You can also
create programs to update sequential, VSAM, and DB2 and do it in a fraction
of the time it would take in cobol. 

I even have the users sit down with the programmers to help create the
reports and the breaking.  This utility allows you move fields around,
alter the control breaks with little effort.  The programs can run as
instream sysin so you don't have to wait for a compile to show the results
to the user sitting right there.  TRY THAT IN COBOL!!!!!

UTILITIES SAVE YOU TIME, EFFORT AND MONEY....USE THEM TO YOUR
ADVANTAGE!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
```

## Thread: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs — 39 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

- **From:** Paul Hardy <paul.hardy.uhold@pwgsc.gc.ca>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
Hi;

I am having a bit of a problem with VSAM files...the problem is as
follows;

When running a JCL stream using the program compiled with COBOL/OS the
program ends successfully; however; when I recompile the program in
COBOL for MVS using LE when the program tries to open the VSAM files, it
returns a File-status of 35.

Below is the file definition and the JCL of the VSAM file, the step just
prior to the running of the program, so you note that the VSAM data file
is empty....

JCL;

//BA41280  DD DSN=BMADIS.STST.OA.RG.BA41280.CL,
//            DISP=OLD
//*
//BA41281  DD DSN=BMADIS.STST.OA.RG.BA41281.CL,
//            DISP=OLD
//*

Definition of the VSAM file;

IDCAMS  SYSTEM SERVICES

   DEFINE CLUSTER -
               (NAME (BMADIS.STST.OA.RG.BA41280.CL) -
                VOLUME(PRVSAM) -
                TRACKS(100 5) -
                RECORDSIZE(204 204) -
                NUMBERED ) -
          DATA -
               (NAME(BMADIS.STST.OA.RG.BA41280.DA))
IDC0508I DATA ALLOCATION STATUS FOR VOLUME PR2005 IS 0
IDC0001I FUNCTION COMPLETED, HIGHEST CONDITION CODE WAS 0


This is the definition of the file in the program;


*
     SELECT TEMP-FILE         ASSIGN TO BA41280
        ORGANIZATION IS RELATIVE
        ACCESS IS RANDOM
        RELATIVE KEY IS BATCH-RELATIVE-REC-NO
        FILE STATUS IS TEMP-FILE-STATUS.
*
     SELECT TEMP-FILE2        ASSIGN TO BA41281
        ORGANIZATION IS RELATIVE
        ACCESS IS RANDOM
        RELATIVE KEY IS TEMP-FILE2-REC-NO
        FILE STATUS IS TEMP-FILE2-STATUS.

*
 FD  TEMP-FILE
     LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.
 01  TEMP-FILE-REC.
     05 TEMP-REC-TYPE    PIC X.
     05 FILLER           PIC X(203).
*
 FD  TEMP-FILE2
     LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.
 01  TEMP-FILE2-REC.
     05 TEMP2-REC-TYPE   PIC X.
     05 FILLER           PIC X(203).

In the procedure
OPEN I-O TEMP-FILE.
DISPLAY 'TEMP-FILE-STATUS= ' TEMP-FILE-STATUS.
MOVE 'Y' TO TEMP-FILE-OPEN.
OPEN I-O TEMP-FILE2.
DISPLAY 'TEMP-FILE2-STATUS= ' TEMP-FILE2-STATUS.


Any help you could provide would be appreciated.....

Paul Hardy
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

- **From:** Kdr3 <kdr3@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361E7986.1E64C25A@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
I just had the same problem. My solution was to put a dummy record into the
file, then run the cobol program. I put a key value of zeroes into the file
and then the cobol II program was able to run successfully. I added code in
the program to delete this dummy record.
I've included the syncsort JCL I used for the dummy records:

//DUMMYREC EXEC PGM=SYNCSORT,COND=(0,NE)
//SYSOUT  DD SYSOUT=*
//SORTIN  DD *
000000  0000000 00
/*
//SORTOUT  DD DSN=THE.VSAM.FILE,
//         AMP=('BUFNI=3','BUFND=4'),
//         DISP=SHR
//SYSIN   DD *
  SORT FIELDS=COPY
/*

If there is a better solution, please let me know.
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>

```
Try opening the file for output in the beginning of the program,, closing
it,
then opening it for I-O.  I think the problem is that the HURBA is set to
zero.
This should also save the trouble of insertind and deleting a dummy record.

OPEN OUTPUT TEMP-FILE
CLOSE TEMP-FILE.
OPEN I-O TEMP-FILE     etc.





Paul Hardy wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

- **References:** `<361EAE86.8208E776@ix.netcom.com> <19981009222705.07837.00009249@ng17.aol.com>`

```

Theo DP wrote in message <19981009222705.07837.00009249@ng17.aol.com>...
>
>><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>This should also save the trouble of insertind and deleting a dummy
record.
>>
>>OPEN OUTPUT TEMP-FILE
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

  <much snippage>
>
>Just IBM and the COBOL Standards folks making Y2K (unnecessarily) that much
>more difficult!
>
>Check out the COBOL for MVS file status documentation - the stuff that used
to
>work one way doesn't work that way anymore!
>
>Your "fun" is just beginning...

Don't blame this one on the Standard.  IBM did this all by themselves (with
a rather convoluted definition of "available files" for VSAM).  However, as
I recall (I haven't looked it up) this is well documented in the IBM
Migration Guide.  Please *DO* have one of those handy whenever you see OS/VS
COBOL to COBOL for this-and-that file status changes.  Most of them are
pretty well documented (as are the "required" solutions).

P.S.  Anyone who is JUST NOW doing a migration from OS/VS COBOL to the '85
Standard shouldn't be blaming IBM or the Standards people for making Y2K "so
hard".  If you haven't done a migration to an '85 Standard compiler more
than a decade and a half after it became available, I think there is another
problem going on - rather than Standards or IBM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vp90t$258@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<361EAE86.8208E776@ix.netcom.com> <19981009222705.07837.00009249@ng17.aol.com> <6vogem$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <36200E86.4484@erols.com>`

```

The Goobers wrote in message <36200E86.4484@erols.com>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>
>[snippolino]
>
>> P.S.  Anyone who is JUST NOW doing a migration from OS/VS COBOL to the
'85
>> Standard shouldn't be blaming IBM or the Standards people for making Y2K
"so
>> hard".  If you haven't done a migration to an '85 Standard compiler more
>> than a decade and a half after it became available, I think there is
another
>> problem going on - rather than Standards or IBM.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

If you were still running a PC application that worked today with DOS 3.0,
would you worry?  What if you think you might ever want to install a new
print driver to the PC?  How about any other "interface"?

The problem with OS/VS COBOL was that IBM announced in 1984 (yes I mean "84"
not "94") that they only guaranteed support for another 3 years.  Then in
the early 90's they did announce a 2 year drop of support and made it clear
that it MIGHT not work with future operating systems, file systems,
databases, hardware, etc.  Given this much warning, you DO need to start
thinking about "upgrading" a not yet broken application.

P.S.  Interestingly enough, IBM *does* say that existing OS/VS COBOL
compiled programs (Load modules or object code - but object modules do have
some new linking requirements) that are not changed in any way and which do
not attempt to interface with any of the new things WILL continue to run "as
they did in the past" - ASSUMING (and it is a big assumption) that they
didn't use any "undocumented" features (that used to work but were never
documented as working).  Therefore, if you really do have existing OS/VS
COBOL programs that you don't want to change and don't want to enhance and
don't want to work with new stuff, you shouldn't have any problems.  (Again,
assuming they were originally coded to correctly handle Y2K issues.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981011000808.17274.00006592@ng29.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6vp90t$258@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```

><HTML><PRE>Subject: Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs
>From: "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
></PRE></HTML>

The problem with this particular upgrade was that it essentially guaranteed
that your existing programs would no longer work - new file status codes,
elimination of Report Writer, more restrictions on variable length files,
elimination of existing keywords, etc. Hard to blame people for not beating
down the door to upgrade under these circumstances...

It seems that backwards compatibility is a lost concept these days. 

Microsoft seems to have perfected this - my current client has "legacy"
applications written in VB3-4-5 that wait to be converted while they start to
cut their teeth on VB6 - four very incompatible releases of the same software
(some with different versions for WIN16 and WIN32) in about five years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3620CC0B.1C00@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<361EAE86.8208E776@ix.netcom.com> <19981009222705.07837.00009249@ng17.aol.com> <6vogem$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <36200E86.4484@erols.com> <6vp90t$258@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> The Goobers wrote in message <36200E86.4484@erols.com>...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> would you worry?

In the spirit of Devilish Advocation and Tight Budgets:

Given the abovequoted axiom... no, as long as it ain't broke it doesn't
need fixing.

> What if you think you might ever want to install a new
> print driver to the PC?  How about any other "interface"?

What if pigs learn to fly; should we all buy heavy umbrellas now?  Hey,
when it needs to be dealt with it needs to be dealt with.

> 
> The problem with OS/VS COBOL was that IBM announced in 1984 (yes I mean "84"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> thinking about "upgrading" a not yet broken application.

You need to allocate budget for something which cannot be proven to
enhance this quarter's Bottom Line... something like Y2K, no?  Stretch
your imagination and consider the following interchanges:

1984:

CIO: IBM's gonna drop support for OS/VS COBOL in three years; we should
upgrade.
CFO: Come back in three years.

1994:

CIO: IBM's dropped support for OS/VS COBOL; we should upgrade
CFO: Does everything still work?
CIO: Yes, but...
CFO  But, nothing... come back when we Really Need It.

> 
> P.S.  Interestingly enough, IBM *does* say that existing OS/VS COBOL
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> assuming they were originally coded to correctly handle Y2K issues.)

... and when you *do* try to upgrade remember that programs using things
like TRANSFROM, ON (interger) and AFTER POSITIONING will have to be
rewritten... adding time and budget to something which does not enhance
this quarter's Bottom Line.

When did IBM drop support for macro CICS?  How many shops are upgrading
from CICS 2.1 *only* because of Y2K problems?  If it ain't broke...

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

- **From:** "BR-549" <dsrush@webzone.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
The COBOL II Reference manual gives the following explanation to a VSAM status code of 35 :
 
 An OPEN statement with the INPUT,    
 I-O, or EXTEND phrase was attempted  
 on a non-optional file that was not  
 present.   
 
Either your file is not there or is not recognized.      

I have never seen a VSAM relative file format used in a production environment.  What are you doing with it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net>`

```
BR-549 wrote:
>     ---------------------------------------------------------------
> The COBOL II Reference manual gives the following explanation to a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Either your file is not there or is not recognized.

I didn't see the original question, but if you attempt to open an empty
file as input using COBOL II or later you will get the '35' status.  That
is, unless you select the file as OPTIONAL (ie "SELECT OPTIONAL FILE-NAME
ASSIGN TO DDNAME, etc.)  If you do this it should be OK.  I can't remember
if it returns '00' or '05', but either way it's OK.

> I have never seen a VSAM relative file format used in a production
> environment.  What are you doing with it.

I obviously can't answer for him, but I can give you a useful example for
a relative file.  We purchased an ATM system package.  It's up 24 hours a day,
more or less.  Batch processing must be done while CICS is up.  Card traffic
is written to a relative record file that holds about 10 days worth of 
transactions batch processing starts at where it left off on the previous
day and processes the RRDS file until it reaches a record that is from an
earlier time.  Basically the RRDS is a circular file.  When it needs to write
to the next record after reach the end of the file it simply starts over at
the beginning of the file.

Anyway, it's a pretty clever way of doing it.  All of the maintenance log
files work the same way.

(There's a little more to it than what I've explained, but that's the basics
of it.  We even stole the idea for our inhouse written telephone transfer
processing file.)
```

```
from the header, that is.

What is VSAM?  Although very Cobol literate, it has always
been on PC's (at least since a about 1978 or so).  I used CICS
once back in the early seventies ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:


>What is VSAM?  Although very Cobol literate, it has always
>been on PC's (at least since a about 1978 or so).  I used CICS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

VSAM (Virtual Storage Access Method)  was a high performance access
method implemented under MVS to provide replacements for sequential,
ISAM and random access files. 

They used their own new catalogs and new utilities, replacing the old
MVT utilities for managing these files. 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stevewie@apk.net (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net>`

```
On Mon, 12 Oct 1998 07:40:15 GMT, scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott
Peterson) enlightened us:

>"Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It was also implemented in VSE.  The file structure of VSAM and ISAM
are very different as well.  Where in ISAM you have 3 distinct types
(i.e. index, data, overflow) in VSAM there is only one record type and
the index information is stored in the VSAM cluster definition.  

That's very short and sweet and if you want to know more, let me know
and I'll post full technical details.

Cheers,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Todays tip to annoy people:  Buy a large quantity of orange traffic cones and reroute entire   
streets.
 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3623335a.2918454@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net>`

```
stevewie@apk.net (SkippyPB) wrote:


>
>It was also implemented in VSE.  The file structure of VSAM and ISAM
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

WRONG!

A key sequenced VSAM data set consists of two components. an index and
a data component. The index contains the keys (very heavily
compressed) of the records and the relative byte address (RBA) of the
control inverval containing them. 

Overflow areas still exist, however they are not explicitly defined.
You define them by specifying the freespace percentage in the control
interval and control areas.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36247e00.22031718@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net> <3623335a.2918454@news3.ibm.net> <6vtn50$ocj@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:


>
>If we are getting into details, aren't there (now) 4 types of data sets?
>Don't "linear" data sets get counted as separate? (Really a question - not a
>statement.  I have never used them, but have read about them.)
>

Not really.  Linear came almost a decade later. It really isn't a
usable data organization from a use viewpoint. It was defined to store
DB2 tables.It mostly is a way of tell ing VSAM to get out of the way
because something else will be managing (i.e. DB2) will really be
managing the space. 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vtm33$8vb$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net>`

```

>It was also implemented in VSE.  The file structure of VSAM and ISAM
>are very different as well.  Where in ISAM you have 3 distinct types
>(i.e. index, data, overflow) in VSAM there is only one record type and
>the index information is stored in the VSAM cluster definition.


The file structure of Isam is very different on every platform, so that
the file structure of VSAM as implemented by IBM on a specific platform
is different than the file structure  of Isam as implemented by IBM
on a specific platform comes as no surprise.  BTW, I have never
seen an ISAM that had three distinct types.  I have seen one with two,
and I have seen one where there is only one record type and the
index information is stored in the Isam cluster definition, but never
a variation where there were three distinct file areas.

How is the Cobol program different?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36257f2e.22333778@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net> <6vtm33$8vb$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>seen an ISAM that had three distinct types.  
I have. 
Long, long ago. 

As I remember, and I may be wrong as we're talking about the late
seventies, ISAM reserved part of each cylinder for inserting records.
If you did a lot of inserts eventually you ran out of space. You could
explicitly define an overflow area to hold these records. Hitting that
too much drove performance through the floor because you were bouncing
all over the disk during sequential access. 

That was one of the major problems with the implementation of ISAM on
MVS. Constantly having to have outages to reorg the files.

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vtn4u$ocj@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net>`

```

>>
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Todays tip to annoy people:  Buy a large quantity of orange traffic cones
and reroute entire
>streets.
> Steve

Just for the non-IBM people who are reading this, please do not confuse ISAM
with isam!  (rather easy to do).  "ISAM" was a proprietary file system
available with earlier IBM mainframe systems.  "isam" (or "ISAM") is now a
generic term often used for anyone's "indexed" access method (For example,
"C-ISAM" is available on many UNIX systems).

When IBM mainframe people talk about "ISAM being obsolete" they are NOT
saying that using indexed file systems are obsolete - only that this
specific file system is.

(IBMers - please ignore the "generalizations" - but this is a confusion that
I often run into with people who don't know IBM mainframes or their current
facitlies.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362272C5.C0A5712B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net> <6vtn4u$ocj@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`

```
IBM tends to do that, for instance when they "invented" virtual memory,
leaving Burroughs users wondering what they have been using all these
years.

I once converted a Univac 9030 system to IBM.  They were very similar,
since the 9030 was basically the results of buying the rights to the RCA
design, and the 360 was basically the results of hiring the RCA
designers after Univac laid them off.

To keep the same functionality, I made converted all of our files to
VSAM.  Most of these were either flat or relative.  That may confuse
lots of people who use VSAM interchangeably with KSDS (the "isam"
portion of VSAM).  It has been a long time since I have seen a shop
using ESDS or RRDS (that's not right, the relative) VSAM files.  I think
the coming of databases has lessened the need for permament files within
the VSAM file management tools.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981016015426.21940.00000617@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<362272C5.C0A5712B@home.com>`

```

In article <362272C5.C0A5712B@home.com>, Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
writes:

>It has been a long time since I have seen a shop
>using ESDS

We use VSAM ESDS for intermediate files for file transfers (uploads and
downloads).

One of our vendor packages use a lot of ESDS clusters for data that is accessed
in batch sequentially.

CICS uses ESDS for the intrapartition dataset and DFHTEMP. (At least CICS 2.3
in VSE/ESA does.)

SQL/DS and DB2/VSE also use VSAM ESDS (or that is how it is defined to the VSAM
catalog) to hold the DBEXTENTs.

Mark A. Young
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362829E5.7758C5D0@att.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <36231f39.7075962@news.apk.net> <6vtn4u$ocj@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <362272C5.C0A5712B@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
(snip)
> To keep the same functionality, I made converted all of our files to
> VSAM.  Most of these were either flat or relative.  That may confuse
> lots of people who use VSAM interchangeably with KSDS (the "isam"
> portion of VSAM).  It has been a long time since I have seen a shop
> using ESDS

Stop by my shop sometime, we have four ESDS's online (in two CICS
regions) as message files, plus many in batch, usually copies of an
online KSDS that some program wants to read sequentially to generate a
report, or something. Providing the access is sequential and the dsorg
is VSAM, the program doesn't care if it's reading a KSDS or an ESDS.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3628840b.23578839@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au> <362142B9.685@sprynet.com> <6vrg7q$n60$1@news.igs.net> <3622b06d.2507074@news3.ibm.net> <6vtlja$8kd$1@news.igs.net>`

```
 "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>Yes, but how is the COBOL different?  That is a JCL change.

>>
>>They used their own new catalogs and new utilities, replacing the old
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Actually often the JCL did not have to change. Usually, however, it
did because of on naming conventions and JCL standards. 

Except for files that loaded (OPEN OUTPUT) ISAM files almost all IBM
Cobol programs written to use ISAM could read a VSAM keyed file with
no changes or recompilation necessary through a ISAM/VSAM
compatibility routine supplied by IBM and transparent to the
application. 

There was overhead to do this, but generally an ISAM program reading a
VSAM file would run 10 to 15 percent faster than it would reading an
ISAM file although there were exceptions. 

The changes to the COBOL program to make it a handle native VSAM files
were minor adjustments to the file definitions, eliminatinn or
modification of file-error declaratives and addition of
file-status-key checking for all I/O operations. I've seen programs
run twice as fast as their ISAM counterparts with these changes and
everything else identical. 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VSAM problems re; cobol/os vs cobol/mvs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36212b62.19845495@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<361E29B5.9DF6BF47@pwgsc.gc.ca> <361ed1b2.0@wznews.webzone.net> <361FA05A.58F6@sprynet.com> <6vqp6r$81e$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```
"ben" <robertshaweb@dnb.com> wrote:

>Am I missing something ... the example on the use of a RRDS file below seems
>absurd .. I have yet to find a good example of the filetype ... none of the
>books i have read give one either .
>
The RRDS file format is essentially a bunch of record slots accessible
by record number.  They were included to provide a replacement for the
random access or relative file methods supported in pre-VSAM days. 

I've worked on a few of these. They were intended for high performance
random access applications where sequential processing was not an
issue. Access was strictly by relative record number or by a
hardware-dependent cylinder/head CCHHRR algorythm. Essentially IBM
implemented the file structure and then pretty much ignored it as user
implemented. No utilities, no backup utilities, no record locking for
concurrent access. That all had to be handled by the developers.

One of the places I remember this being used a lot was for transaction
files that could be read and updated concurrently by CICS and batch
programs. One warehousing system I worked on used a transaction file
where everything received and shipped was recorded in it. Record one
on the file was used as a control record that was constantly updated
by each CICS transaction that updated the file with the number of the
last record written. When batch processing would start, we'd read that
record and use that to set our end-of file point  Then we'd look for
records that had not been previously extracted, write them out to
another file and update them as extracted. At the end-of-file record
number from the control record we'd stop reading that file and start
processing the extracted records.  This proved to be completely
bulletproof as the batch and online never processed the same records
at the same time.

When VSAM was developed IBM originally marketed it as a replacement
for all the older access methods. They provided sequentail, indexed
and relative file access methods.. The RRDS file type was made
available as an alternative for the non-hardware-dependent
implementations only. I know of a few people that converted the CCHHR
files to VSAM by using the CCHHRR into a key and rewriting the
applications using indexed files, but this was very uncommon. We never
found it nearly as clean or easy as converting ISAM applications to
VSAM. Concurrent access and record locking was still user
responsibility, and there were never any major performance or
reliability justifications to make it worth while. 

			Scott Peterson
```

## Thread: Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program. — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

- **From:** Ganesh Kudva <abc@somecompany.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361DE36A.D9357E79@somecompany.com>`

```
Hello,

I have the following SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

EXEC SQL SELECT  *
         INTO :DCLVar
         FROM  Table

         WHERE  Table_Var_1   =
                 :Variable-Defined-in-CopyBook

END-EXEC.

Here the "Variable-Defined-in-CopyBook" is a host variable defined in
CopyBook.  (  With the Statement "COPY CopyBook." in the LINKAGE SECTION
)

While Compiling the program, the PreCompiler throws up an error  telling
the vairable is UNDEFINED or UNUSABLE Host Variable.  There is no
problem if I use a locally defined variable in place of CopyBook
Variable.  Looks like the PreCompiler ignores the CopyBook variables.

Any Ideas ?

Thanks in Advance,
Ganesh Kudva.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

- **From:** "Anna-Karin Johansson" <akj@mbox302.swipnet.se>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NdmT1.5516$yM.6903871@nntpserver.swip.net>`
- **References:** `<361DE36A.D9357E79@somecompany.com>`

```
Use this instead of COPY CopyBook:

EXEC SQL
    INCLUDE CopyBook
END-EXEC.

Regards,

Anna-Karin Johansson

>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** DOUG-PARTCH@WORLDNET.ATT.NET
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00

- **References:** `<361DE36A.D9357E79@somecompany.com> <NdmT1.5516$yM.6903871@nntpserver.swip.net> <361DFA6E.FBC33B1@somecompany.com>`

```
In article <361DFA6E.FBC33B1@somecompany.com>,
  Ganesh Kudva <abc@somecompany.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the quick reply :-))
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

For reasons that go to deep to discuss on the COBOL forum, don't use "*" in
the Select statement. Instead, list only the columns of the table you will
need for the program. Otherwise, you could be faced later with possible
maintenance and performance problems.

Select "*" also is a taboo in most IMS shops.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vl0av$40i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<361DE36A.D9357E79@somecompany.com>`

```
In article <361DE36A.D9357E79@somecompany.com>,
  Ganesh Kudva <abc@somecompany.com> wrote:
> Hello,
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Ganesh

Just copy your definitions via the DB2 Preprocessor:

EXEC SQL INCLUDE CopyBook END-EXEC

The Library in which your CopyBook resides has to be defined onto the
Preprocessor via '//SYSLIB DD DSN=your.copybook.libaray,DISP=SHR'.
Unfortunetly, you cannot use REPLACING-Statements.

This is because COBOL doesn't know a separate preprocessor step as you can use
when compiling C and PL/I. That's life!

Cheers

Daniel

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981009123103.20648.00008670@ng88.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6vl0av$40i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

Ganesh,

Can you let us know if the SQL INCLUDE solved this problem.

I am curious as to why you would not be allowed to use Host Variables specified
in copybooks brought in by conventional copy statements. (That is with out
dependence on the preprocessor).

If your problem is solved already, then the following line of inquiry can't be
too useful. But It seems to me that the declaration of the copybook in LINKAGE
might be the issue for the precompiler. Although that is a surprise to me.

In other words, its not an UNDEFINED problem, it is perhaps an UNUSABLE data
item problem.

If location in the LINKAGE SECTION is the problem, then the data value(s) would
merely need to be moved to working storage, and the SQL SELECT reference that
working storage area from within the WHERE clause.

But actually, the COBOL SQL preprocessors I have used, seem to have no problem
with keys being in LINKAGE.  I could imagine a very advanced compiler reconning
that the address of a given 01 in LINKAGE is never resolved, and that would
make it and its lower level data items in fact unusable.  But that is very
advanced. 
Could that be your problem here? 
Do you resolve the address of the linkage section group that holds the Host
Variable you attempted to use in the WHERE clause?

Hope that helps. Please let us know how you succeeded.


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vll2b$2vj@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6vl0av$40i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981009123103.20648.00008670@ng88.aol.com>`

```
In several (some obscure) places, IBM documents that you cannot use COPY
statements for *either* EXEC CICS or EXEC SQL type programs that need to
reference certain types of COBOL/SQL or COBOL/CICS statements.

If you think about this, it sort-of makes sense.  For IBM (where
precompilers and/or translators are used), the "step" before the compile has
to handle all EXEC CICS statements or EXEC SQL statements - but it is the
step with the compiler that handles COPY statements.  Therefore, if the COPY
statement needs to be resolved to handle the EXEC statement's operands -
this causes a problem.

The EXEC SQL INCLUDE solution solves this for SQL.  For CICS, the only way
to handle this is to "translate" your CICS code and put the translator
OUTPUT into your COPY library.  This doesn't always work (for example if you
have an EXEC CICS HANDLE statement - don't try translating that in a
separate job from the rest of your program).

For other environments (than IBM mainframe), where the EXEC SQL and EXEC
CICS statements are handled in the same "step" as the compile, this is not a
problem.
RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981009123103.20648.00008670@ng88.aol.com>...
>
>Ganesh,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am curious as to why you would not be allowed to use Host Variables
specified
>in copybooks brought in by conventional copy statements. (That is with out
>dependence on the preprocessor).
>
>If your problem is solved already, then the following line of inquiry can't
be
>too useful. But It seems to me that the declaration of the copybook in
LINKAGE
>might be the issue for the precompiler. Although that is a surprise to me.

data
>item problem.
>

would

that
>working storage area from within the WHERE clause.
>

problem

reconning
>that the address of a given 01 in LINKAGE is never resolved, and that would
>make it and its lower level data items in fact unusable.  But that is very
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<361F7564.3245@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<361DE36A.D9357E79@somecompany.com>`

```
Ganesh Kudva wrote:
> I have the following SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Variable.  Looks like the PreCompiler ignores the CopyBook variables.

The DB2 precompiler executes and resolves all SQL *before* the COBOL
compiler executes, where the copybooks are merged into the program. 
Thus the precompiler has no knowledge of the copybook datanames.

Bob
```

```
<361DE36A.D9357E79@somecompany.com>...

Normally I wouldn't get on anyones tail for answering something like this,
but I must point out a couple of things. 
First, this guy is probably a contractor selling knowledge he doesn`t have
- lets face it even someone with basic SQL knowledge (and COBOL of course)
should have known the answer to the question, and probably getting paid a
lot of money for doing it. Not to mention keeping someone out of a job who
does know his/her rear fom their elbow.
Second (s)he doesn't even use a real address, which brought me to my first
conclusion and a second that they are scared someone might notice how
little they know and put it to the boss.
Of course I could be completely wrong and it may just have been a homework
assignment in which case all of the above must be rewritten.
All I need to do now is get some of you to do my job for me and I can get
back on the golf course.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help -- Problem with SQL Statement in my COBOL Program.

- **From:** Gianni Kiaris <mlc@tuttocitta.it>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362612F3.AFE6AAAA@datamat.it>`

```
Ganesh Kudva wrote:

> Hello,
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ganesh Kudva.

Hello,

excuse me for my bad English.

The PreCompiler ignores the CopyBook variables simple because
run before Compiler that explain the CopyBook.
It's necessary that all SQL variables must be defined local or inside
INCLUDE copybook.
It's possible to do several INCLUDE like the CopyBook COBOL,
in Working, Linkage or Procedure Division.

Ask me for every things

bye


Gianni Kiaris
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus Name Change - Madness surely ?

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <pc@microfocus.com>

```
Walter,

The following is written as an individual and should not be taken as
representing Micro Focus in any way.

A name change is being considered and this was made public in the
Intersolv acquisition announcements.  As far as I am aware no decision
on the new name has been taken at this time.

I believe one of the reasons for the Company/group name change is
that "Micro Focus" is closely linked with COBOL.  The new combined
group does much more these days and the name needs to reflect
this.  The new Micro Focus has revenues of approx. 400 million
dollars and over 2000 people World-wide.  We have product and
service offerings in a number of solution areas.

Who knows, may be Micro Focus will stay around as a brand name
for the COBOL products?

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
England.

Walter Levine wrote in message <6vnr9o$95d@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>...

>"very little" and the "focus" refers to "how much attention we pay to what
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)

```
On Mon, 12 Oct 1998 12:13:50 +0100, "Paddy Coleman"
<pc@microfocus.com> wrote:


>
>I believe one of the reasons for the Company/group name change is
>that "Micro Focus" is closely linked with COBOL. 

It wasn't the original idea of course. The name was supposed to
reflect the microcomputer as the focus of the company. 

Back when I started at Micro Focus 12 years ago there was still other
Micro Focus products around. A reasonable word processor driven by
function keys and semi-wysiwyg springs to mind. I must add that that
was when everyone knew Ctrl-K,Ctrl-Q was exit and dot matrix printers
was 'what you got'. It did have support for IBM 6670 lasers though you
had to send your file via a 3270 emulator. I wonder why it failed to
take off?

Problem was, the COBOL stuff was pretty succesful so other projects
got dropped by the wayside. Some projects seemed a bit mad to be
looking at but that probably reflected the management at the time ;-)
```

<a id="t-ff5702cfa5"></a>
## Thread: >>>>> HELP ! — 7 msgs

### >>>>> HELP !

- **From:** "Luc Mertes" <luc.mertes@ibsr.be>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```
I know this could seem elementary to you ...

I work with a PC / Windows NT 4.0 / Fujitsu COBOL 85.

In a COBOL program, I would like to test some bytes, in order for me to
check their binary value (hexa '0D' or dec '013' for instance).
I read the bytes with a MOVE TO ELEM (with ELEM defined as 01  ELEM
PIC X)

... Then, what do I have to do to be able to read the real value of ELEM (0
to 255 Decimal) ?

Thanks to all of you !

Luc
luc.mertes@village.uunet.be
luc.mertes@ibsr.be
```

#### ↳ Re: >>>>> HELP !

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vlemb$166$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```
COMP-5 is a 16 bit number, so the easiest way is to redefine
your base field as that, then look at it. The code would be as follows:

    01 dumy-name-one.
        02 filler                        picture x.
        02 ascii-character      picture x.
    01 dumy-name-two redefines dumy-name-one.
        02 binary-number        picture s9(5) comp-5.

    move zero to binary-number.   (make sure high order is hex zero)
    move character-data to ascii-character.
    move binary-number to numeric-value-of-character.

I think I might have the order of the filler and ascii character
in the above example reversed, I always end up doing it wrong
once and fixing it on the debug<g>, but that should get you there.
There is also an intrinsic function to do the same job. That is
in the manual under "intrinsic functions".

Luc Mertes wrote in message <6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>...
>I know this could seem elementary to you ...
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: >>>>> HELP !

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rfuT1.107$ce.312564@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net> <6vlemb$166$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>COMP-5 is a 16 bit number, so the easiest way is to redefine
>your base field as that, then look at it. The code would be as follows:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>        02 binary-number        picture s9(5) comp-5.

I think you want to make that s9(4) Donald, because -99999 to -32769
and 32768 to 99999 can't be stored in 2 bytes. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: >>>>> HELP !

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vmbmb$pu6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net> <6vlemb$166$1@news.igs.net> <rfuT1.107$ce.312564@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>
>I think you want to make that s9(4) Donald, because -99999 to -32769
>and 32768 to 99999 can't be stored in 2 bytes. :-)


That is correct, though from the Fujitsu docs, it appears that
comp-5 is a 16 bit regardless. Perhaps though, I am mistaken
and it is actually 32 bit.  That is the usage that they use internally
for indices, etc.  I'll test it in the morning, and get back to you.
```

#### ↳ Re: >>>>> HELP !

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981009131311.14582.00008398@ng-fa2.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```


Luc Mertes <luc.mertes@ibsr.be>
On Fri, 9 Oct 1998 16:29:05 0200


Asked about
>>I work with a PC / Windows NT 4.0 / Fujitsu COBOL 85.

In a COBOL program, I would like to test some bytes, in order for me to
check their binary value (hexa '0D' or dec '013' for instance).
I read the bytes with a MOVE TO ELEM (with ELEM defined as 01  ELEM
PIC X)

... Then, what do I have to do to be able to read the real value of ELEM (0
to 255 Decimal) ?
<<

I can tell you how to do what you want to do then comment about what you
specifically asked.

1) Hex
COBOL 85 permits hexadecimal literals, like
X'0D' and X'0A'. (The Following examples are in ASCII, not EBCDIC).

So having populated your data item
 01  ELEM PIC X.

you can code test like

   IF ELEM = X'0D'
      PERFORM 013-CARRIAGE-RETURN
   END-IF.

or perhaps
   EVALUATE ELEM
      WHEN X'0D'
          PERFORM 013-CARRIAGE-RETURN
      WHEN X'0A'
          PERFORM 010-LINE-FEED
      WHEN X'09'
          PERFORM 009-DETAB-TEXT
      WHEN X'0B'
          PERFORM 012-FORM-FEED
      WHEN OTHER
          PERFORM 099-PROCESS-IT
    END-EVALUATE.
      
the hex literals can go into other relationals, like

* You know you cold do the following
* with a test for a blank, but just for illustration
   IF ELEM < X'20'
      PERFORM 013-SKIP-PROTOCOL-CIPHER
   ELSE
      PERFORM 099-PROCESS-IT
   END-IF.


Incidentally you can code hexadecimal literals longer than one byte.  You can
put them in VALUE clauses of data items.

Or you could do something like

    INSPECT item-bigger-than-elem-say-x256
       CONVERTING x'0A0D'
       INTO ALL SPACES.
* I am a little unsure of the above syntax.    

using a literal or a fixed data item for the CONVERTING clause or the INTO
CLAUSE.

2) Decimal

But there is noway I know of to do this with decimal literals exactly as you
have layed oout your plan, COBOL does not have an ASCII function, like some
other languages.

However, the typical recommendation is to move the cipher to the lower byte of
a binary and then test the binary item,  as in

01 WORK-GROUP. 
     05 F       PIC X.
     05 ELEM PIC X.
01 ASCII-GROUP REDEFINES 
     WORK-GROUP. 
     05 ELEM-ASCII PIC S9(4) BINARY.

 
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
* initialize it atleast once in procedure div,
* or initialize it repeatedly in a loop,
* or transpose the redefintion above, and 
*    init it in WS.
    INTIALIZE ELEM-ASCII.

    PERFORM LOOP-LOGIC
      VARYING NNN....
.......


 LOOP-LOGIC.
    MOVE SOURCE-DATA(NNN:1)
        to ELEM.
   IF ELEM-ASCII = 13
      PERFORM 013-CARRIAGE-RETURN
   END-IF.
   
or
   EVALUATE ELEM-ASCII
      WHEN 13
          PERFORM 013-CARRIAGE-RETURN
      WHEN 010
          PERFORM 010-LINE-FEED
      WHEN OTHER
          PERFORM 099-PROCESS-IT
    END-EVALUATE.
or

* note value and relational operator varied
* from earlier example just for fun
* but should have same effect
   IF ELEM-ASCII <= 31 
     PERFORM 013-SKIP-PROTOCOL-CIPHER
   ELSE
      PERFORM 099-PROCESS-IT
   END-IF.


Best Wishes,




Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: >>>>> HELP !

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uuzT1.640$q3.4810597@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net> <19981009131311.14582.00008398@ng-fa2.aol.com>`

```

RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981009131311.14582.00008398@ng-fa2.aol.com>...
>
>But there is noway I know of to do this with decimal literals exactly as
you
>have layed oout your plan, COBOL does not have an ASCII function, like some
>other languages.
>

Actually, it does! Use the ORD intrinsic function. As in,

    01 ELEM PIC X.
    01 REAL-VALUE PIC 999.

    COMPUTE REAL-VALUE = FUNCTION ORD ( ELEM ) - 1

The adjustment of -1 is required to match the ordinal value of the character
(within the COBOL character set) to its numeric value. The complementary
intrinsic function is CHAR. As in,

    01 ELEM PIC X.
    01 DECIMAL-VALUE PIC 999.

    MOVE FUNCTION CHAR ( DECIMAL-VALUE + 1 ) TO ELEM.

The adjustment of +1 is required to map the numeric value to the position
within the character set.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: >>>>> HELP !

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ueFT1.232$ce.601478@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6vl6ip$7o7$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net> <19981009131311.14582.00008398@ng-fa2.aol.com> <uuzT1.640$q3.4810597@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
>
>RKRayhawk wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>within the character set.

Rick, that is a clever approach which should work for standard COBOL.
However, I would expect FUNCTION ORD to be affected by the ALPHABET
clause in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph of the CONFIGURATION SECTION.
The FUNCTION ORD description in the Intrinsic Function Module standard
refers to the collating sequence 'for the program' not 'for the system'.
This might be a problem for applications using ALPHABET.  Probably a
small number of programs, but a potential 'Oops!' to remember. :-)
```

### Application Development Manager Opportunity

- **From:** erikam@wlhandler.com

- **Message-ID:** `<6vlf79$qtk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


W.L. Handler & Associates
SEARCH CONSULTANTS

POSITION TITLE: Applications Development Manager.   Reports to a Director of
Institutional IT, and will manage a staff of ten professionals.

CLIENT OVERVIEW: Our client is a premier financial services company with more
than $65 billion in assets under management and 2,400 employees.  The Company
services more than two million investors with a family of more than 60 mutual
funds which have historically ranked among the best performers in the nation.
Two recent developments, a business partnership with a global financial
services company and a merger one year ago with another mutual fund company,
ranks them among the top few financial services companies in the world.  They
have always been an aggressive pioneer in the utilization of technology for
investment, customer service, customer information and communications.  They
are well known in their area for being very employee oriented, and continue to
lead their industry and maintain a cutting edge attitude with respect to
technology innovation.  The Institutional lines of business account for over
half of the Company�s assets under management, and is the fastest growing
segment of the business, forecasting 50% growth next year and a tenfold
increase in revenue over the next ten years.  This position is located at the
corporate headquarters in a very desirable metropolitan area in the Midwest,
considered one of the most attractive cities to move to in the U.S.

POSITION IMPACT: The Company is implementing a new enterprise-wide
Information Systems architecture, putting in place new development
methodologies, and creating new Web-based applications. The Applications
Development Manager will have significant impact on the user satisfaction,
service offerings, business processes and customer service capabilities of
the Company.  This position will manage a development and implementation team
aligned with a critical business function such as finance, sales and
marketing, customer information or investments analysis. He/she will manage
project delivery, plan and coordinate staffing requirements, scheduling,
review performance, quality, budget, organization, and standards of the
development team efforts. He/she will participate in the department planning
process.  Prepare and implement departmental goals and objectives and assist
in the development of the IT department budget.

PROFESSIONAL QUALIFICATIONS: The successful candidate will have a minimum of
six years of analysis, design, construction, testing, and implementation of
large complex mainframe on-line and batch applications.  Additional
development experience in the Intranet, client server, open systems, and
relational database arena would be a plus. We strongly prefer a minimum of
four years as a manager of highly competent IT personnel, with duties
including: administration of performance appraisals, providing performance
feedback and coaching, and identifying training and development plans for
staff.	The right person will have strong project management skills, and be
able to demonstrate proficiency in providing technical and business guidance
to technical and non-technical staff.  Experience in the financial services
industry is preferred.

PERSONAL CHARACTERISTICS: The successful candidate will have good
interpersonal skills including the ability to communicate at various levels
including staff and middle management.	He/she must be able to communicate
clearly and concisely, both verbally and in written form.  He/she will be
characterized as having good enterprise level project management skills and
be flexible in a continuously changing environment.  The ability to provide
technical and business guidance to technical and non-technical staff and to
generate imaginative or creative solutions in work related situations are
essential.

EDUCATION: A Bachelor�s degree in either computer science, mathematics,
information management or a related field is a requirement.

COMPENSATION: The client offers a very competitive base salary, attractive
bonus, excellent company-paid benefits plan, 401(k) program, and profit
sharing.  A generous relocation package will be provided by the company.

CONTACT: Erika B. Moorman by phone:  (770)805-5026 ext 235, fax at (770) 805-
5020 or by Email at erikam@wlhandler.com;  visit our web page at
www.wlhandler.com

A WORLD SEARCH GROUP MEMBER
Paris * London * Hamburg * Sao Paulo * Buenos Aires * New Delhi * Hong Kong *
Sydney * Lagos * Other Major Cities
2255 Cumberland Parkway, NW, Building 1500, Atlanta, Georgia 30339 * Phone
(770) 805-5000 Fax (770) 805-5011 * www.wlhandler.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-a64280cc7a"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol/hpux record unlock problem — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol/hpux record unlock problem

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@ramtecjr.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<907953548.12438.0.nnrp-07.d4e45f36@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Does anyone use MF cobol to process index files > 1gig on hp-ux?  We have a
file this size (in a bench mark test) which is opened for shared use.

If we update records near the end of the file, they do not get unlocked.
Records earlier on the file get unlocked normally.  We don't get any error
conditions.

The manual says that for windows etc, MF Cobol can handle files of 2gig, but
only 1 gig if shared.  However for unix it just says that it can handle 2gig
files.

I don't know if this is a bug in MF, in hp-ux or in the manual.  However I
do know this is not a bug in our code.

This bench mark is being run at a user site.  I'm waiting to get this file
back to test it on our machines under different versions of the os but in
the meantime I thought I might get some feed back.

We're using mf cobol 4.0.5 on hp-ux 10.20.

tia
Rick
```

## Thread: Microfocus COBOL error — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus COBOL error

- **From:** phight@flash.net (Patrick Hight)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,umbc.ifsm.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vlp92$c9e$1@excalibur.flash.net>`

```
I have encountered an error in MF COBOL for which i cannot locate a 
resolution.
The error description i receive is: "File is a directory." This 
runstream is executed in 4DOS and aborts consistently at the same 
program step (an internal sort?). When the job is restarted at this 
step, the runstream executes normally and completes without error. 
Nothing is changed to restart the runstream! This is a 
very-poorly-described Error 21.

Any and all help would be appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL error

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,umbc.ifsm.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vltrq$a3q$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6vlp92$c9e$1@excalibur.flash.net>`

```
If you have a network, you might try creating a drive to run
it on.  If the problem then disappears on a dedicated drive,
you might actual have a directory that the system is attempting
to open as a file.
```

<a id="t-6b84d14097"></a>
## Thread: MVS Cobol question....... — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### re: MVS Cobol question.......

- **From:** regy102@csc.canterbury.ac.nz (Brad Henry <B.Henry@CSC.Canterbury.AC.NZ>)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m9ZfeVpFruOw@cantva.canterbury.ac.nz>`

```
Jaye Snidow wrote:
>This is my first posting here and I would like to ask the various experts
>gathered here a question that was referred to me by a colleague.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>correct or simply less informed? TIA.

Jane,

I use DEC COBOL on a VMS system. The manual quite clearly indicates that
reference modification can be used on a display item - so yes you can
use FIELD-A(3:1) because PIC 9(09) is a display item (but if you had
PIC 9(09) COMP it won't get past the compiler because PIC 9(09) is NOT
a DISPLAY item).
I tried it in a program and the snag is that the term FIELD-A(3:1) is
alphanumeric not numeric
    and so  ADD   1  TO FIELD-A(3:1)  will not compile
    BUT     MOVE "7" TO FIELD-A(3:1)  will compile and execute as expected.

Hope this is of interest - but I am not really sure why you would want to
be doing such a curious thing. :)

Good programming,
Brad Henry.
```

---

- **From:** regy102@csc.canterbury.ac.nz (Brad Henry <B.Henry@CSC.Canterbury.AC.NZ>)
- **Date:** 1998-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5Lm5cnbtSh92@cantva.canterbury.ac.nz>`

```
Jaye Snidow wrote:
>This is my first posting here and I would like to ask the various experts
>gathered here a question that was referred to me by a colleague.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>correct or simply less informed? TIA.

Jaye,

I use DEC COBOL on a VMS system. The manual quite clearly indicates that
reference modification can be used on a display item - so yes you can
use FIELD-A(3:1) because PIC 9(09) is a display item (but if you had
PIC 9(09) COMP it won't get past the compiler because PIC 9(09) COMP
is NOT a DISPLAY item).
I tried it in a program and the snag is that the term FIELD-A(3:1) is
alphanumeric not numeric
    and so  ADD   1  TO FIELD-A(3:1)  will not compile
    BUT     MOVE "7" TO FIELD-A(3:1)  will compile and execute as expected.

Hope this is of interest - but I am not really sure why you would want to
be doing such a curious thing. :-)

Good programming,
Brad Henry.
```
