<a id="t-52ba5207ef"></a>
## Thread: couple of questions — 54 msgs

### couple of questions

- **From:** SkidMike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com>`

```
awhile back thane helped me out with full screen io.  now, i have a
further question.  i would like to know what is the acceptable practice
in this situation.  i rewrote the "create estimate" program (yes, again)
but it not only creates the estimate, it also handles screen i/o for the
customer in question.  my program's main routine is 2 routines.  A00000
is screen i/o for the repair estimate itself and B00000 is screen i/o
for the customer.  right now, it's under 500 lines so i would say it is
no nightmare.  A00000 and B00000 are contiguous (i.e. one leads to the
other, provided the right condition is met)  following those two and the
end-routine are the subroutines of A and B (A10000, A20000, A21000, etc
you get the idea)  is it acceptable to write this way or should the
estimate i/o and customer i/o be in separate programs?
```

#### ↳ Re: couple of questions

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298283.66605.14560@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com>`

```
In message <<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com>> SkidMike <skidmike@mindspring.com> writes:
> further question.  i would like to know what is the acceptable practice
> in this situation.  i rewrote the "create estimate" program (yes, again)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> for the customer.  right now, it's under 500 lines so i would say it is

Using names such as A000000 and B00000 is not a good idea.
There routines should have meaningful names - this is not
Basic.  Why are they not called Get-Estimate-Data and
Get-Customer-Data for example ?  Even if the codes you used
are prefixes then why have them ?  

Usually I find prefixes used where the _position_ of the
routine is deemed to be more important than its 
functionality.  This is the result of (IMHO) a view of the
program where 'the A parts must be done before the B parts'
instead of thinking about how the user wants to drive it
or how the data must be processed, ie a view of code sequence
being more important than user facilitiy.

> no nightmare.  A00000 and B00000 are contiguous (i.e. one leads to the
> other, provided the right condition is met)  following those two and the

You mean by 'drop through' ? <sharp intake of breath>   ;-)

It sounds like you have:

        PROCEDURE DIVISION.

             open files

        A000000.
             accept screen-1
             validate by
             if (....)
                  GO TO A00000.
        B0000000.
             accept screen-2
             etc
           
             WRITE ...
              CLOSE
             STOP RUN
             .

If this is the case then it will lead to problems as the program
expands.  It doesn't need to go into separate programs
necessarily, but it would need to be more modular.  You 
have established a fixed order of entry.   What if a new
screen was added because of 'trade discounting' for 
example, where certain customers were 'trade' (such as 
panelbeaters) and got a fixed discount rate.  They
would have a record on disk for their address that would
be permanent and this would also indicate their rate.

You would then want to get this data before the estimate,
not after.


> end-routine are the subroutines of A and B (A10000, A20000, A21000, etc
> you get the idea)  is it acceptable to write this way or should the
> estimate i/o and customer i/o be in separate programs?

I dislike the idea that routines are 'fixed' to their 'owners'
by having hierarchical prefixes or names.  All routines
are potentially available (or should be) to all other
routines.

I have seen a recommendation that all routines only ever
be called from one point and thus are immovably fixed on
the hierarchical tree by its prefix.  If the same functionality
is required in anothe part of the program then presumably
they just wrote a new identical block of code with a 
different prefix.

In general terms I would not fix the user interface to a
predefined sequence without good reason.  The customer
may give his name first, last, or in the middle of a 
transaction, or not at all.  The user should be able to 
do the input in any suitable sequence.  He should be
able to go back and change the data at any time.

Enter a few lines of estimate, take the customer name
which then indicates special pricing so the program 
changes the price, halfway through the address the
customer wants to add another item to the estimate so
back to the estimate then finish off the address.

The idea that the program should in parts 'A' and 'B"
and the order is start, A, B, end, will not give a
flexible user interface or happy users.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36211E2B.D309A6F7@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3298283.66605.14560@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> In message <<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com>> SkidMike <skidmike@mindspring.com> writes:
> > further question.  i would like to know what is the acceptable practice
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> being more important than user facilitiy.

The use of a prefix such as a000-startoff, b000-main, r000-read-some-file etc.  may not seem
to be useful in trivial programs, but it is invaluable in large programs,where listings run
into thousands of lines. It is a very useful aid in organizing and locating the performed
paragraphs/sections.

Drop thru code is really something you should avoid. It will cause nothing but problems.

        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
         A000-startoff.
             open files
             perform a000-... until ...
             perform b000-... until ...
            close files
            .
            .
            GOBACK.

       A000-.
           perform a110-
           perform r300-read-a-record
           perform a120-
            .
            .

       B000-.
            .
            .
            .

>
>
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> different prefix.

There is no need to duplicate functionality. A program should be able to be read top-down,
easily locating source statements, and not looking like a spaghetti factory.

>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> flexible user interface or happy users.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36226478.4A925737@att.net>`

#### ↳ Re: couple of questions

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_r1U1.919$ce.1135750@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com>`

```
SkidMike wrote:
>awhile back thane helped me out with full screen io.  now, i have a
>further question.  i would like to know what is the acceptable practice
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>estimate i/o and customer i/o be in separate programs?

Separating code into multiple programs is usually for one of these reasons,
in no specific order.

  Program is so big it is unwieldy to compile or edit (many thousand lines).

  To get around memory limitations (not as common these days).

  More than one person will be maintaining code simultaneously.

  Code needs to be CALLed instead of PERFORMed, so must be packaged as a
  separate program.  (In the proposed COBOL standard you can write your own
  functions, and they will have the same 'separate program' requirement as
  called subroutines do now.)

  Code needs to be executed from different programs.

  Logically it makes sense to do so.

If you do not have one of the above reasons to split the program, it is
usually simpler to keep it one program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298283.81318.3153@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<_r1U1.919$ce.1135750@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
In message <<_r1U1.919$ce.1135750@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
> 
> Separating code into multiple programs is usually for one of these reasons,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> usually simpler to keep it one program.

I am sure that he has a fairly good idea that those are the 
reasons to or not to.  What he really wants to know is
whether these apply to him or not.  

Obviously if a program gets so big it won't compile or the
editor karks then it needs to be split, but how does one
know ahead of time that this may happen ?

In general with interactive programs I like to have one
program per screen and a clear interface (linkage) into
it so that it can be called from other programs as 
required.

For example another screen being added may be 'list of
existing quotes'.  This may be called from either the
'estimate' screen or the 'customer' screen and may give
different sequences or selections depending which it
was called from.

This will allow for facilities such as: 'make a new quote
that is a copy of <select from list>' or 'find the quote
for customer ,select from list>'.

Obviously for the 'find quote' you need to able to bring
up the customer screen _without_ going through the estimate
one so that, for example, search parameters can be input:
name is smith, or phone is 276something; so that the list
can be usefully shortened and manageable.

Then while scanning the list the user may say 'what is
that one' and want to show more details which would then
call another program.

It may be that the user would want to start with the list
(in say descnding order of date - latest first) and then
pick one to change - the phone number on one yesterday
was entered wrongly.

However, re-entrancy is usually too difficult to design for
and so the number of possible permutations may need to be
constrained - I usually pass a flag through the call
structure to prevent re-entering an active program when
this may seem to be a possibility.
```

#### ↳ Re: couple of questions

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com>`

```
On Sat, 10 Oct 1998 23:28:36 -0400, SkidMike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
wrote:

>right now, it's under 500 lines so i would say it is
>no nightmare.  A00000 and B00000 are contiguous (i.e. one leads to the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>estimate i/o and customer i/o be in separate programs?

hmm. without seeing any of the code itself, you may want to
do the following:

put a0000 into one copybook
put b0000 into one copybook

then at

procedure division.

*insert
copy "a0000.cbl"
copy "b0000.cbl"

*this allows your code to be seperated BUT does not require much of a
*rewrite of code.

then lets pretend a0000.cbl uses certain data items.

*you might want to insert

working-storage section.
copy "a0000.dat"

*with the a0000 data items. this allows the data to be maintained in
*a seperate module, for later moving to linkage sections, if needed.
*then if the extra memory is needed, you make the
*a000.dat items the new working storage of the a000.cbl module,
*and the working storage items from the main procedure called
*by reference

*making

perform some-a00-module

*into

call some-a00-module by reference current-ws-section





gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3624BFE1.244EF00B@home.com>`

```
One trouble with copybooks is that you can't trust your editor to find a
field in your source code.  You end up compiling so that you can do
searches through the program listing.
```

```
On Wed, 14 Oct 1998 09:14:41 -0600, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>One trouble with copybooks is that you can't trust your editor to find a
>field in your source code.  You end up compiling so that you can do
>searches through the program listing.

That's really a problem with your editor and not with copy books. Not
being able to 'trust' such an important part of your development
environment is a serious flaw.

Suggest you either look up how to use grep if your editor doesn't
search copy files or 'all files in a project/directory' (or change
your editor)
```

```
First, I am not using Unix.   If I edit a program, I like searching for
a string in that program.  If part of that program resides elsewhere, I
need to use a tool to look elsewhere.  If all of that program is in one
file, it is much easier to do maintenance.  I can do everything with one
edit session.
```

```
On Wed, 14 Oct 1998 10:15:55 -0600, Howard Brazee
<NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>First, I am not using Unix.

Neither am I. Grep or similar tools are available on most OS's.

>   If I edit a program, I like searching for
>a string in that program.  If part of that program resides elsewhere, I
>need to use a tool to look elsewhere.  If all of that program is in one
>file, it is much easier to do maintenance.

Personally I use editors with source and project intelligence if
available. Ie. they understand the languages syntax and can locate
data-items, sections, procedures, functions or whatever because they
keep infomation about the program not because of a text search.

They also keep information about which files belong to the project I'm
working on so multiple locations isn't a problem.

>  I can do everything with one
>edit session.

So can I but it doesn't matter if I'm working with one file or 400.
```

```
> 
> On Mon, 12 Oct 1998 18:31:21, gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore) wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> hate it.

A practical consideration, if it's a CICS program and the Procedure
Division copied code contains commands, it won't work. The CICS
translator won't see the commands, so they won't get translated. I'm
with you, Thane, GAK!

Bill Lynch
```

```
On Tue, 13 Oct 1998 21:49:20, "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com> 
wrote:

> In our non-CICS shop we've been putting all kinds of stuff into
> procedure copies for YEARS.  Things like standard print routines, stock
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

That kind of stuff should be in a FILE - what happens when you make a 
change and someone "forgets" to recompile ALL of the programs with the
copybook?

For DATA formats it does not matter, but now you have divergent 
procedure code.

What if someone BREAKS it?

Do you work from the compile listing?  One thing I HATE is trying to 
follow alreadly "Evolved" logic and hitting a perform that is not IN 
the source - which of the 7 procedure division copy books is that 
paragraph in?  By the time I find it I've forgotten why I am looking 
for it.

GAK!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3625ded7.223740551@news.freeserve.net>`

```
On 15 Oct 1998 10:44:52 GMT, jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347) wrote:

>> run a grep on the libraries to find the paragraph on disk.
>
>Now there's a command that should be standard equipment on
>any OS.  grep.   When we moved our system off a unix box
>I cried a million tears when I found out dos has nothing comparable.

Not as standard no but there are plenty of ports of UNIX tools to DOS,
Windows and OS2. 

Then there are the commercial offerings like Norton Utils' TS or FF
commands. Lastly (and this is probably a last resort) the Find menu
option in Win95.

The best one I've come across is a Delphi IDE hack called GExperts.
Delphi's IDE is pretty poor in comparison to Micro Focus COBOL's IMHO
but GExperts makes up for it (mostly). The GExpert GREP utuility
became a seperate program in the last few releases and you can now use
it with other IDE's or standalone. I've used it with COBOL as I like
it so much.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298290.10534.18211@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36291f66.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In message <<36291f66.0@news1.ibm.net>> "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> writes:
> JCJ0347 wrote in message <19981015064452.08267.00000941@ng139.aol.com>...
> >> run a grep on the libraries to find the paragraph on disk.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> often can be used as a reasonable substitute.

'grep' is 'global regular expression print' (actually it
derives from the ed/vi command g/re/p where 're' is
'regular expression').

As Find does not understand regular expressions, or even
wildcards, then it may struggle to meet a single digit
percent of what grep does.

Free greps are widely available, or are included with
programmer's products.  I also find sed to be useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7029ra$5d5$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2ddiHcQfd4xY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>Do you work from the compile listing?  One thing I HATE is trying to
>follow alreadly "Evolved" logic and hitting a perform that is not IN
>the source - which of the 7 procedure division copy books is that
>paragraph in?  By the time I find it I've forgotten why I am looking
>for it.


I use the same thing constantly.  Mind you, I do not put stuff into
a copy unless it is fairly simple and standard code. But for example,
every program in a system might have a common start up that
puts the copyright message and the time and date at the top
of a screen.  Typing that sort of thing into 70-80 separate source
modules rather than just using a copy seems silly to me.  The code
would look like:

        perform standard-system-start.
        perform local-startup.

in each of 70 programs.  I cannot see that it makes the code
anything but easier to maintain.  I tend to do the same with
file read modules for consistent locking procedures.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** SkidMike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00

```
I have often worked with standard error routines in copy books.  And of
course, IDMS binds in the data dictionary.  Nowadays, there are standard
y2k conversion routines in copybooks.

But in the mainframe environments which I have worked with, my editors
have no links to the copybooks or data dictionaries.  If I want to know
whether a field is updated in a program, I can't exclude all, find
fieldname all, and see every place it is referenced.  Not if it is
referenced in a copy member.

SkidMike wrote:
> 
> so far, i've avoided putting ANY procedure division into a copy file.
> i'd lose my mind trying to keep up with that.  the only copy statements
> i use are for the data division.  comes in real handy for me.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> But in the mainframe environments which I have worked with, my editors
> have no links to the copybooks or data dictionaries.  If I want to know
> whether a field is updated in a program, I can't exclude all, find
> fieldname all, and see every place it is referenced.  Not if it is
> referenced in a copy member.

I agree - I hate having to compile a program just to be able to view the
SYSPRINT listing and see the copybooks expanded.  If I'm working in a
Panvalet or Endevor environment, I prefer to use ++includes, and I'll
retrieve the program to my own PDS with includes expanded, so that I can
use TSO/ISPF edit, set bookmarks in the code while I'm doing my
analysis. Sure beats browsing a SYSPRINT listing, or trying to work in
split screen with 20 copybooks in the program.

Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nZ5V1.827$9R2.1369072@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2ddiHcQfd4xY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7029ra$5d5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>file read modules for consistent locking procedures.

I agree, Donald.  But the kind of code I would put into COPY files is
what I call 'black box' routines like date edit and manipulate routines,
dictionary based field edit code, general or system specific utility
routines, stuff like that.  While it is true that you get an occasional
problem that you have to search out, these routines tend to be well
tested and documented, and limited in scope.  Used in this way, I have
found them to be a great boon.  The COBOL routines found on my web page
are examples.  Here is a list of some of them.

  DATE.ZIP     A variety of date routines
  EASTER.ZIP   Calculate date of Easter
  GETNUM.ZIP   Get free-form number
  MONEY.ZIP    Convert numeric dollars to text (e.g. 25 to 'TWENTY FIVE')
  NAME.ZIP     Edit/reverse name (e.g. Smith, John, Mr., Jr.)

It is hard for me to see how inserting any of those into 1000 different
programs would make the code more maintainable than using COPY.  Believe
me, the routines in DATE.ZIP and GETNUM.ZIP are in many, many thousands
of programs belonging to my clients.  Recently I have been remediating
a number of my client's systems for Y2K.  The programs in which the date
routines had been inserted directly into the source were much more time
consuming to fix than the ones simply using the COPY files, often because
the code had been modified (usually for no apparent reason).  With the
COPY files, it was largely a simple replacement with a few tweaks of the
reference code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70358q$5kf$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>I agree, Donald.  But the kind of code I would put into COPY files is
>what I call 'black box' routines like date edit and manipulate routines,


Ditto.  In fact one of the things I dislike most about Fujitsu is their
insistence on a ".CBL" extension for copies, as I would tend to have
a DATE.WRK, and a DATE.COD.  Or for files, a FILENAME.SEL,
FILENAME.FD, FILENAME.WRK (file status etc.) and a
FILENAME.COD.  That has been a huge problem in conversion,
as I have a choice of going though and renaming every file, then
changing the source, or of not being able to trust the MAKE, as
I cannot structure the make files completely.  When a complete
rebuild takes 35 minutes, that is a first class pain.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8BA9FD76F62BB3E5.BED8A14BA5689B4E.B58F1288DDDFE1BB@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2ddiHcQfd4xY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 13 Oct 1998 21:49:20, "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That's why we have extensive source/copy cross-references.  When I make
a change to a procedure copy (which is rare--that's why it's been canned
into a copy) I don't have to remember anything, I just have our utility
recompile all programs containing the copy.  Done.

As to keeping that kind of stuff in a file...  It's not *data* it's
*logic*.  Using retail price calculation as an example:  Items can be
priced in any number of ways which vary by the item itself (is it a
serialized individual item, a general stock-number, a "by weight" item)
and by the store to which it is being sent (our retail stores, our
"wholesale" outlet, etc.).  Additionally, if the item is in a current
advertisement with a special rate, unique pricing may apply, and so on. 

The data describing the item, its classification, store location, etc.
are all in data files.  The rules determining what price to use based on
all other criteria are in the procedure copy.  How would one store that
in a file?

> For DATA formats it does not matter, but now you have divergent
> procedure code.
> 
> What if someone BREAKS it?

What if someone broke (or "fixed" :) ) a hard-coded version in one of
the 25 programs using the rules.  Is there a difference?  To my mind the
risk of divergent code is greater by not using the copy.


> 
> Do you work from the compile listing?  One thing I HATE is trying to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> GAK!

Yes, it can be frustrating if one is unaware of the copy book
"library."  However, once that learning curve is passed the copy books
become second nature.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<702so2$g@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2ddiHcQfd4xY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <8BA9FD76F62BB3E5.BED8A14BA5689B4E.B58F1288DDDFE1BB@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3624bcea.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```

Chris Osborne wrote in message <3624bcea.0@news2.uswest.net>...
  <miuch snippage>
>
>I get the same thing from using dynamically loaded sub-programs, and I
don't
>need any other utility or program.
>
>

Although I tend to agree that placing "common code" in called subroutines is
a good idea, I would like to point out that in some (many?) operating
systems/compilers, "dynamic calls" have significant overhead and can have
serious performance problems - especially in routines that need to be called
frequently.  It also (obviously) raises the entire question of "last used
state" versus "initial state" which can be controlled - but must be
understood when writing and using such routines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362585A4.CF2D7111@att.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2ddiHcQfd4xY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <8BA9FD76F62BB3E5.BED8A14BA5689B4E.B58F1288DDDFE1BB@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3624bcea.0@news2.uswest.net> <702so2$g@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>
(snip)
> Although I tend to agree that placing "common code" in called subroutines is
> a good idea, I would like to point out that in some (many?) operating
> systems/compilers, "dynamic calls" have significant overhead and can have
> serious performance problems - especially in routines that need to be called

Bill,

I've seen this mentioned many times off and on, even saw a reference to
a CICS white paper concerning the additional overhead of dynamic calls
(which I couldn't find). Can you post any URLs, IBM or otherwise, where
this is discussed. As I've posted before, my application uses CICS/COBOL
II dynamic calls pretty heavily (the new date routine is the most
heavily used example, 500,000 - 600,000 times a day (18 hours, or so)),
and we haven't had a hint of any performance problems (unlike
implementing LE/370 <g>). I'd really like to et a better handle on this,
if possible. Maybe 500,000/day isn't that much?

Bill Lynch
```

```
called

>Bill Lynch

After some searching, I found it.  What you want (and it isn't all that
current, but is pretty reasonable) is:

PUBLICATION NUMBER
GG09-1001                  SUFFIX: 00

TITLE
VS COBOL II IMPROVEMENTS FOR CICS AND MVS/XA

ABSTRACT
This publication compares VS COBOL II using EXEC CICS LINKs
and COBOL CALLs executing above and below the MVS/XA 16Mb
line to OS COBOL using EXEC CICS LINKs executing below the
MVS/XA 16Mb LINE. Performance for the various runs is
reported showing average transaction response time, average
CPU time per transaction and the average CPU delta time
consumed due to the varying number of LINKs or CALLs per
transaction. Virtual storage consumption is shown by the
average and maximum CICS DSA used below the 16Mb line for
selected runs. Both virtual storage constraint (VSC) and
virtual storage constraint relief (VSCR) runs are reported
in this publication.

This publication is a component of GBOF-2208.

ORIGIN DATE
AVAILABLE DATE:    01/88   LATEST DASH LEVEL  01/88
```

- **Message-ID:** `<36260D4B.F453D02C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2ddiHcQfd4xY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <8BA9FD76F62BB3E5.BED8A14BA5689B4E.B58F1288DDDFE1BB@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3624bcea.0@news2.uswest.net> <702so2$g@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <362585A4.CF2D7111@att.net>`

```
When I started programming, the CPU cost was the most important cost,
then storage cost,  with programmer and opportunity costs at the bottom.

This has changed.  Programmer and opportunity costs are much more
expensive than CPU and storage costs.  Dynamic calls save save
programmer and opportunity costs.  (and a good virtual OS on a machine
with lots of memory can make these pretty efficient).

We no longer segment our COBOL procedure division memory.  We no longer
code everytinging linearly, we no longer use 66 level.  We no longer use
alter statements.  We optimize that which is more expensive NOW. 
Maintenance needs to be optimized more than CPU cycles.

And quite often, the systems people solve the CPU efficiencies.


> I've seen this mentioned many times off and on, even saw a reference to
> a CICS white paper concerning the additional overhead of dynamic calls
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch
```

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> David,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
(snip)

Chris,

They are all home-grown utilities.  We index every copy member of every
source program in an indexed file.  Along with the copy books, we index
every direct CALL statement, and every literal (i.e., program name) that
is MOVEd into one of our standard WS fields called
"CALLED-PROGRAM-NAME".  

An inquiry tool can produce "instant" lists of any program with a
particular copy, or all those that CALL a specific subroutine.  The
lists are displayed on-screen, or can be passed to another home-grown
program that invokes the compiles.

Incidentally, in another indexed file we cross-reference every
data-name, paragraph-name, or section-name that is referenced in the
procedure divisions of every program or by any "PL" copy book.  The same
type of screen inquiry can pop up a list of everything referencing a
particular field, etc.  Comes in quite handy--especially for y2k work! 
:)
```

```
"Canned" logic is for sub-programs, preferably dynamically loaded
sub-programs.  This way, each change is picked up throughout all
parent-programs without having to lift a finger.
```

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>

- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3624b884.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
> 
> "Canned" logic is for sub-programs, preferably dynamically loaded
> sub-programs.  This way, each change is picked up throughout all
> parent-programs without having to lift a finger.
> 
(snip)

I agree and disagree.  Dynamically loaded sub-programs are appropriate
in many situations.  We tend to call a sub-program for "whole"
operations (for lack of a better word) such as "here is a bunch of
stuff  I've finished with, now do a bunch more stuff to it, but don't
clutter up this program..."  We use procedure copy books for the
repetitive incidental stuff that is performed a million times within a
program, but isn't worth the overhead of a dynamic call. 
```

```
Chris Osborne wrote:
> 
> "Canned" logic is for sub-programs, preferably dynamically loaded
> sub-programs.  This way, each change is picked up throughout all
> parent-programs without having to lift a finger.

Notwithstanding Bill Klein's comment above, I'm with Chris on this.
Package these guys up, publish the calling sequence and we're off to the
races, without recompiling any of the callers if the subroutine changes
(in general). Another aspect of this approach is that these smaller,
single function programs *tend* to be easy to maintain.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<707qoj$6ik$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3624b884.0@news2.uswest.net>`

```

Chris Osborne wrote in message <3624b884.0@news2.uswest.net>...
>"Canned" logic is for sub-programs, preferably dynamically loaded
>sub-programs.  This way, each change is picked up throughout all
>parent-programs without having to lift a finger.


I have been following this thread and thinking about it for a couple
of days now, and would like to make a few points ... the above is
not the entire issue that I am responding to, but indicative of the
problems.

I think it a huge mistake to simply "make a rule" and assume that
a standard approach is correct, or even preferable for all platforms
and for all shops ... even if the language is always Cobol.  The
dynamically loaded subroutine, for example, may indeed be the
best solution to canned logic --- when running on a virtual memory
machine in a professional shop with production runs.

For my installations, it would be an unmitigated disaster.  When
memory is a constraint, it is hugely dangerous.  All it would take
on a DOS machine, for example, would be for someone to install
some software that increased the environment, or lessened memory
in some other way ... like changing networks, and you have inexplicable
crashes three weeks later as certain modules cannot load.

In addition to that, I there is the very practical consideration that
nesting of subroutines more than about three levels deep broke
the DOS the linker.  Since most large systems consist of some
sort of mainline that reads the configuration and proceeds to
call the main modules in response to user actions, one level
is already gone.

Last but not least, subroutine calls use more memory than in-line
code, at least as a general rule.

The bottom line is that memory constraints affect the choice of
methodology in a very real way, as do all the other "wrinkles"
imposed by any compiler/linker/OS.  "Best" has to start with
"it's gotta work".

The other point mentioned above is the run circumstances. Very
different methodologies are required for a system that is installed
once by professionals, then tuned, then maintained, as compared
to a system that is delivered on a CD, installed by an amateur, and
expected to work with no maintenance for several years, often
through staff changes and by users that have never seen a computer
before.

There is another aspect of this "copy" Vs "subroutine" debate, as well.
That is the type of shop that produces the code.  There is a saying
among authors that the most important aspect of writing is to know
your audience.  I think the same thing applies to code.

If a shop is large, and uses outside consultants a lot, then I
could certainly see that copies of procedures would be a real
pain in the butt.  I notice,though, that the screams of anguish come
from the contractors, while the proponents of the method
work on their own code in their own shop.  The real
key here is knowledge.

When I see a procedural copy in my own code (I write code
that I sell through companies in which I am a major shareholder),
that indicates to me that it is a section of code I need not
concern myself with. I know what it does, since I wrote it,
and I also know that it is a routine that has almost nothing
to do with the local problem.  It will be system wide standardized
procedure that may have to change once per century (like
date routines).  It will also be well tested and proven, as those
procedural copy routines are usually done as post-mortems
at the end of a project for use on the next one.  It will also have
a standalone test routine somewhere, if I want to check it out.

I think the think to remember is that "what is best" has to be
taken in context, and changes.

Just to put that in perspective, I am currently converting a
huge number of programs (at least for me<G>) from one
compiler running on DOS to another running under
Windows95.  As part of that, I am changing a large number
of copy routines to subroutine calls.  If they had not originally
been made as copies, the code would have been unmaintainable
in the past, and the changes I am making would be near
impossible.  Both methods have their place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@getridofspam.worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<709bmn$c42@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <3624122e.7111524@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Qdhg9ZvRetzb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36229F93.A4ECF782@att.net> <437F701BD2DD0080.2F5461A1A6EBFE1B.E442EE4E70691A53@library-proxy.airnews.net> <3624b884.0@news2.uswest.net> <707qoj$6ik$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <707qoj$6ik$1@news.igs.net>...
>
<snip>
>
>It will be system wide standardized procedure that may have
>to change once per century (like date routines).

I am a great proponent of long range systems planning,
both for its own sake and as a tool for validating
short-range plans where appropriate.

If you are under the impression that date routines will only need
to be changed once per century, you are mistaken, and have an
awakening coming, should you not be retired by the time the
recycled bovine byproducts collide with the electrically-powered
revolving air redistribution system.

If all of your databases and programs and input/output streams,
including data received from Electronic Data Interchange
partners (fat chance) are year-2000 compliant (I avoid the Y2K
acronym; we had to remove all trace from our web sites for
liability reasons - it is a registered trademark, but that's another
thread), then you have nothing to be concerned about.

But if you have employed windowing, as many companies have,
then those windowing routines will have to be restructured a lot
sooner than 100 years from now.

Pity the poor unfortunates who chose 20 as the breakpoint
(IF YR < 20 MOVE 20 TO CC ELSE MOVE 19 TO CC).
If they are a large sales-oriented organization booking orders
up to 5 years in advance, they will run into trouble around
2015, when they try to book an order for 2020 and guess what.....

So now they have to start remediation when, 2010?

Pop the champagne when 2000 rolls around, by all means.
The party won't be over for a while.  It's perpetual, you see.

Mainframes used to have 2K of 48-bit word memory,
and a word would hold either 12 4-bit numeric digits
or 8 6-bit alphanumeric characters.  For that reason,
and because tape was SLOW and programs were
large relative to available memory and disk space
(if you were advanced enough to have any), and
 the millenium was 30-40 years away
"and COBOL won't be around then",
the Century field was usually dispensed with.

I am beginning to hear the same tune again,
as if all of the large enterprises will decide to
put their business at risk by junking a multi-billion
(your currency may vary) investment to switch to....
to....SAP?   J D Edwards?   WIN NT 8.5?

All the students who have been posting their concern
about missing the remediation gravy train have nothing
to fear, not even the freshmen.  There will be COBOL
jobs waiting when they get out of school. They might
even get one, if they are smart enough to intern while
in school to get the priceless experience and exposure.

THINK.  Old IBM motto.  Good advice, as long as we
have a devil's advocate handy to nudge us back to
reality because we didn't do enough of it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: couple of questions

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70j9fd$a0l$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<362025E3.B2CB45A9@mindspring.com> <707qoj$6ik$1@news.igs.net> <709bmn$c42@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <3636102e.7160462@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <70fibd$bal$1@callisto.clark.net> <362ff353.4353355@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```

G Moore wrote in message <362ff353.4353355@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
>employees doing what?


Grunt work like changing tax tables, or modifying two lines on
a thousand page report.  Programs in the real world tend to
evolve rather than get written ... small functions get added
to them one junior programmer at a time.

Consultants usually only get called in when things get so
screwed up that everyone has pulled all their hair out, the
project is six months behind, and the IS department head
is looking for a scapegoat. You may get called in as a junior,
but more likely as a sacrificial lamb.

The real problem as a junior is that most juniors have no idea
of what a real program looks like.  Anything done as a student
would probably be considered a fifteen minute job by a pro. In
emergency situations, people do not have time to babysit or to
train.
```

<a id="t-de0021125f"></a>
## Thread: inspect v's unstring — 40 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### inspect v's unstring

- **From:** "ben" <robertshaweb@dnb.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vo3fh$63j$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```
My program can be written to use INSPECT or UNSTRING for the manipulation of
a variable string of data (containg logical  segments delimited by linefeed
chars).

Which verb is more preferable ?

(i have used the UNSTRING as the code looks neater)
```

#### ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VcOT1.676$ce.813849@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6vo3fh$63j$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```
ben wrote:
>My program can be written to use INSPECT or UNSTRING for the manipulation of
>a variable string of data (containg logical  segments delimited by linefeed
>chars).
>
>Which verb is more preferable ?

That's like asking if a wrench or screwdriver is more preferable.  Use the
tool which best suits what you are doing.

>(i have used the UNSTRING as the code looks neater)

This is a valid reason.
```

#### ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6vo3fh$63j$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```

ben wrote in message <6vo3fh$63j$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>...
>My program can be written to use INSPECT or UNSTRING for the manipulation
of
>a variable string of data (containg logical  segments delimited by linefeed
>chars).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you are using this section of code in a "tight loop" *and* performance is
important, then I suggest that you look at the generated code.  In some
compilers one of these verbs may be much more efficient than the other (and
which is better may well vary from compiler to compiler).

On the other hand, if this is not a section of code that is performed many
times, the difference in performance is unlikely to be significant and the
"readability" (and maintainability) is a good deciding factor.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298283.16202.23943@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In message <<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
> 
> If you are using this section of code in a "tight loop" *and* performance is
> important, then I suggest that you look at the generated code.  In some
> compilers one of these verbs may be much more efficient than the other (and
> which is better may well vary from compiler to compiler).

In many cases the 'generated code' may give no indication of
the relative performance at all because it may be 'byte code'
output used by the run-time virtual machine (such as MF .INT
or Acucobol or RM byte codes) where the relative performance
is not laid out by the vendor and is, in any case, dependant
entirely on the characteristics of whatever target machines
are likely to be used, and/or the code may simply set up
paramaters and call a utility routine of unspecified
performance.

<gasp  ;-)>

The only way to determine performance is to set up a 
realistic test and execute it on the appropriate target
machine.  Even then you have to allow for such distortions as
instruction caching and .DLL load times which may give very
wrong indications as to relative performance.

Grubbing around in the specific output code of compilers
is not an appropriate way (IMHO) of formulating coding
styles.  It is (in my view) similar to determing how an
animal should be fed and treated by studying its excrement   ;-).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362333b1.21972999@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <3298283.16202.23943@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

In message <<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>> "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
> 
> If you are using this section of code in a "tight loop" *and* performance is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

From what I remember of this the 'generated' code told us nothing back
then.  Both STRING and INSPECT were implemented by a call to a COBOL
service subroutine. The only way we were able to track this was by
measuring the time spent in the subroutine, recompiling using the
other verb and running it again. I don't ever remember seeing much
difference between the two. 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3620C76B.4369@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <3298283.16202.23943@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> In message <<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> output 

Perhaps you misunderstand... I believe the intention is to look as the
ASM listings, the equivalent of your compiler's PMAP or LIST options.

[snippage]

> 
> Grubbing around in the specific output code of compilers
> is not an appropriate way (IMHO) of formulating coding
> styles.  It is (in my view) similar to determing how an
> animal should be fed and treated by studying its excrement   ;-).

I hate to disagree but Assembley output is the Temple of Truth, where
one finds out *exactly* what is going on... and faecal analysis or
urinalysis are very useful tools, as well.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298283.82808.7501@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3620C76B.4369@erols.com>`

```
In message <<3620C76B.4369@erols.com>> The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com> writes:
> > 
> > In many cases the 'generated code' may give no indication of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ASM listings, the equivalent of your compiler's PMAP or LIST options.

Perhaps you misunderstand: some compiler do not have ASM
output, even as an option, they do not output 'object code'.
But even if they do generate assembly there may be no way to
determine from the _listing_ what the relative performance
may be.  Perhaps if you have worked with the same CPU for
many decades then you can just count the instructions, but
this is completely _inappropriate_ and unusable for modern
machines, especailly when the compiler may output code
that calls run-time routines.

> I hate to disagree but Assembley output is the Temple of Truth, where
> one finds out *exactly* what is going on... and faecal analysis or
> urinalysis are very useful tools, as well.

Absolutely, when used _appropriately_ for diagnosing problems,
ususally those in the compiler itself.

What I was referring to was using faecal analysis as being
the basis for writing _style_.  This is like using urinanalysis
for deciding which shirt to wear with your suit.

Now I have done much compiler output inspection, 25 years ago.
It was extremely useful at that time, both in diagnosing
compiler problems and optimising Cobol for use on 16Kword
machines.

But I never saw it as a useful determinent of style, nor
does _any_ of the work I did with it apply those decades
later.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3621DFB7.4477@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<3620C76B.4369@erols.com> <3298283.82808.7501@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> In message <<3620C76B.4369@erols.com>> The Goobers <docdwarf@erols.com> writes:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> output, even as an option, they do not output 'object code'.

How very interesting; I will admit my ignorance and say that I am
unaware of such compilers.  The three I am familiar with (IKFCBL00,
IGYCRCTL and MicroFocus) all have the ability to generate ASM output;
might you be so kind as to mention which ones you know of that don't?

> But even if they do generate assembly there may be no way to
> determine from the _listing_ what the relative performance
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that calls run-time routines.

If you have generated the listing then you have the ability to know what
machine you are working on; if you know what machine you are working on
then there is little excuse for unfamiliarity with the instruction-set. 
Granted, such familiarity is not mandatory, true... but there is nothing
which precludes one's learning.

> 
> > I hate to disagree but Assembley output is the Temple of Truth, where
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ususally those in the compiler itself.

Good of you to grant this... 'propriety' is *such* a slippery subject,
though.

> 
> What I was referring to was using faecal analysis as being
> the basis for writing _style_.

Was that your reference now?  I must have missed it, *do* be so kind as
to point out the obvious association between the two which slipped past
my beady eyes.

> This is like using urinanalysis
> for deciding which shirt to wear with your suit.

Urinalysis can be used to determine genetic composition, genetic
composition can be used to ascertain colorblindness... a rather
roundabout way, but possible.

> 
> Now I have done much compiler output inspection, 25 years ago.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> later.

'Style' is tricky... it is a balance between machine efficiency and
programmer efficiency (inversely related, of course).

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3621e9e7.222923296@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<3620C76B.4369@erols.com> <3298283.82808.7501@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On 11 Oct 98 23:00:08 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
wrote:

>  Perhaps if you have worked with the same CPU for
>many decades then you can just count the instructions, but
>this is completely _inappropriate_ and unusable for modern
>machines,

I've met a few programmers like that, even with ASM code. Someone went
through some code I wrote replacing a group of 5 instructions with 2
on the basis that it was more efficient. The fact that those two
instructions took 3 cycles each where mine only took one each
obviously was unkown by the programmer in question.

Ok, 1 clock cycle I hear you cry. This was in a serial port device
handler where performace was extremely important.

> especailly when the compiler may output code
>that calls run-time routines.

Ah yes. The fun I've had with slow runtime calls.

>
>> I hate to disagree but Assembley output is the Temple of Truth, where
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>ususally those in the compiler itself.

Even though compiler technology is getting better every day, sometimes
they just throw out daft code for particular COBOL verbs. Usually
because the compiler programmer hasn't got around to investigating
optimizing that feature/verb. It's always worthwhile knowing which
ones and in which cases.

>
>What I was referring to was using faecal analysis as being
>the basis for writing _style_.

I'll have to disagree there.

>  This is like using urinanalysis
>for deciding which shirt to wear with your suit.


Don't have a suit - T shirt every time.

>
>Now I have done much compiler output inspection, 25 years ago.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>later.  

As I said in another post, you have to keep revising as the compiler
changes though without sounding to Microsoft-y, it's less important
today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3621e758.222268755@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <3298283.16202.23943@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On 11 Oct 98 04:30:02 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
wrote:

>In message <<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>or Acucobol or RM byte codes)


This is probably why Bill said 'generated code'. May be a bit close to
Micro Focus terminology where .INT is intermediate code which runs on
the runtime's virtual machine. .GNT is commonly referred to as
'generated code' and is native to the processor usually unless using a
cross compiler.

> where the relative performance
>is not laid out by the vendor and is, in any case, dependant
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>performance.

Sure but if you for example, know i86 instructions well, it can be
useful.

>
><gasp  ;-)>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>machine.

That implies you don't know what you (not you personally) are doing.
Profiling performance through tests is perfectly valid but there comes
a time where looking at the compiler output can yield further results.
Sometimes in fact you really have to. I had a problem a while back
with extra i86 IRQ slowing network traffic and ASYNC comms down. I
modified my code so that so many IRQ's weren't generated after looking
at the GNT code.
 
>  Even then you have to allow for such distortions as
>instruction caching and .DLL load times which may give very
>wrong indications as to relative performance.

Sure. Instruction caching is an important one to grasp in such things
as handling loops. 

>
>Grubbing around in the specific output code of compilers
>is not an appropriate way (IMHO) of formulating coding
>styles.  It is (in my view) similar to determing how an
>animal should be fed and treated by studying its excrement   ;-).

It's a bad thing if you change compilers often but a good thing to
document which instructions are fast and which are slow in a
particular compiler. You should of course revise this often as
compiler technology improves (or gets worse in some cases)
```

```
>On 11 Oct 98 04:30:02 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
>>In message <<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>> "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> writes:
>It's a bad thing if you change compilers often but a good thing to
>document which instructions are fast and which are slow in a
>particular compiler. You should of course revise this often as
>compiler technology improves (or gets worse in some cases)


Why is it that the compiler vendors do not provide this kind of
information? A page or two at the end of the user's guide
detailing the timing of various Cobol verbs would be very
useful, but no: this information is not available. I even know
compiler vendors that object to performance comparisons
of their products with other vendor's. (sigh)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vt3as$a6n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <3298283.16202.23943@kcbbs.gen.nz> <3621e758.222268755@news.freeserve.net> <36220d8c.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <36220d8c.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>
>Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <3621e758.222268755@news.freeserve.net>...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think that both Shaun and I are used to Micro Focus that very definitely
does (or at least when we worked there DID) publish this type of
information.  Also, the IBM mainframe COBOL programming guide gives at least
some guidance on this.  It is, however, real hard for vendors to keep
correct and current.  Often "performance tuning" is the last thing to be
done during alpha and beta testing - when the dox have already gone to
print.

(and of course, there is always the competitive issue of NOT telling your
competitors about your "weak" spots)
```

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36222df6.240346109@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<6vogek$of@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <3298283.16202.23943@kcbbs.gen.nz> <3621e758.222268755@news.freeserve.net> <36220d8c.0@news1.ibm.net> <6vt3as$a6n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 12 Oct 1998 09:19:43 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>I think that both Shaun and I are used to Micro Focus that very definitely
>does (or at least when we worked there DID) publish this type of
>information. 

Probably. It's (or at least was when we worked there) very much part
of the internal culture though I guess. Something I foster where I am
now. Pretty easy to do really as most of the company are programmers
and clued up.

> Also, the IBM mainframe COBOL programming guide gives at least
>some guidance on this.  It is, however, real hard for vendors to keep
>correct and current.  Often "performance tuning" is the last thing to be
>done during alpha and beta testing - when the dox have already gone to
>print.

Yes though in these days of online docs, I guess it could be more
automated and current.

>(and of course, there is always the competitive issue of NOT telling your
>competitors about your "weak" spots)

At least in the UK you can't do comparitive advertising based on
competitors products. You have to say 'Brand X' and 'Brand Y' and
often you get raked over hot coals when your claims turn out to be
false so I don't think Micro Focus ever published any adverts in the
UK atleast with performance figures. I do recall some other vendors
advertising and it was way of the mark when I actually ran the same
tests.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298285.13592.15632@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36222df6.240346109@news.freeserve.net>`

```
In message <<36222df6.240346109@news.freeserve.net>> scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk  writes:
> 
> Yes though in these days of online docs, I guess it could be more
> automated and current.

The real problem is that many Cobol vendors target dozens or
hundreds of different machines.  These will have a vast range
of variation in the characteristics depending on endiannes,
alignment issues, preferred sizes, and so on.

It is also very different in interactive environments from
batch because much data is displayed almost as often as 
calculated.  For example if in order entry the quantity and
price fields are displayed and accepted then the internal
holding of these may overall be better as display numeric
even though COMP or COMP-3 may be indicated because there
is a multiply (or COMPUTE).

MVS programmers may have a vastly different view on this 
because they have used the same CPU for several decades
(in PC land there is a new one every few months) and
because their batch processing only needs to worry about
a rather restricted environment.

> 
> >(and of course, there is always the competitive issue of NOT telling your
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> tests.

Also, if any Cobol vendor put up a performance issue there
would ten thousand C programmers doing 'the same thing'
several hundred times faster and getting this written up
in all the trade press.

I recall Byte having a language benchmark many years ago.
I think the best hand coded assembler did 10,000 loops
in 3 seconds while one Cobol version did 1000 loops in
3/4 of an hour.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fCNU1.980$q3.7025203@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<36222df6.240346109@news.freeserve.net> <3298285.13592.15632@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298285.13592.15632@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>
>Also, if any Cobol vendor put up a performance issue there
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I seem to remember that MicroFocus addressed COBOL
vs C performance, several years ago, in a issue of
Compilations. As I remember, they suggested there was no
performance penalty associated with using COBOL. I do
not remember there being any specific benchmarks quoted!

It is a common misconception that C programs are faster
than COBOL programs.

The primary determinent of magnitudes of difference in
performance is the algorithm, not the langauges. Where the
algorithm is the same, a significant difference in performance
may reflect the naive use of one of the languages. Where the
programs have been optimised for both the language and the
platform, the performance will be nearly identical. (Excluding
assembler versus other languages!)

A few years ago, I did such a comparison with one non-trivial
program, expecting the C program to be significantly faster
than COBOL. It wasn't! After optimising for the languages and
the platform, the performance of the two programs was nearly
identical with the COBOL program being slightly faster.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3624BE8F.9CC7AD83@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36222df6.240346109@news.freeserve.net> <3298285.13592.15632@kcbbs.gen.nz> <fCNU1.980$q3.7025203@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
One would think that it would be difficult to design an optimizing
compiler for C.  Optimizing compilers need to know what you want done. 
Cobol is more like a RISC computer, with larger functions which can each
be optimized fully.  C is CISC functions, which cannot be optimized
individually.

I know that some compilers have looked at benchmark programs and figured
out how to eliminate their loops, but nobody has been very successful in
optimizing complete programs by figuring out what the complete programs
are trying to do.  It works better to look for loops and Cobol sized
instructions.

Now large cataloged procedures can be optimized greatly.  (I bet Java
will soon be near C++ speeds).  

There is a danger in programmer optimization.  I remember using an Atari
basic program which was optimized by having the first line a go-to to
the main program past all of the loops which are listed in order of use
at the start.  I plugged in a third party Basic cartridge and that whole
optimization was lost.  The new Basic took a moment when it started a
program, storing where all GOTOs pointed to, so that it didn't have to
search each time.   C is supposed to be portable.  Some of your
optimizations for one platform won't be efficient on another platform
(or compiler).

At one time, people were taught to use indices instead of subscripts in
COBOL.  (not to mention ALTERed GO-TOs).  These were designed to speed
up execution time.  Nowadays, compilers have changed, making subscripts
just as fast as indices.  PERFORMs have very little overhead, etc.

Sure, there are some obvious efficiencies we can make, but sometimes
even obvious ones can be contra-productive given the right optimizing
compiler!

> A few years ago, I did such a comparison with one non-trivial
> program, expecting the C program to be significantly faster
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362505AA.823687EF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36222df6.240346109@news.freeserve.net> <3298285.13592.15632@kcbbs.gen.nz> <fCNU1.980$q3.7025203@news1.atlantic.net> <3624BE8F.9CC7AD83@home.com> <Af7V1.45$SQ4.592903@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
I bet it was real efficient!  Are you familiar with the IBM utility
IEFBR14?  All it does is go to register 14, which returns you to the
JCL.  It actually gets used quite a bit as sort of a dummy program to
allow the rest of the JCL (file allocation, etc) to run.

Rick Smith wrote:

> I read about a FORTRAN compiler that implemented global optimization.
> In one program, it optimized to nothing because there were no output
> statements!
> -------------------------------
> Rick Smith
```

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** scottp4.removethis@ibm.net (Scott Peterson)

- **Message-ID:** `<36276199.15095691@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:

>I bet it was real efficient!  Are you familiar with the IBM utility
>IEFBR14?  All it does is go to register 14, which returns you to the
>JCL.  It actually gets used quite a bit as sort of a dummy program to
>allow the rest of the JCL (file allocation, etc) to run.
>

And the classic junior programmer mistake is catalog a file using
IEFBR14 and then wonder why the data set is unreadable unreadable. 

But it's a real oldie. Goes back to the original OS/360. 

It consists of two instructions 

	SR 15 15   	Zero the return code
	BR 14  		Return to calling program

In spite if this simplicity, there have been three or four APARS
accepted against this module that I know of. I had one accepted
against it, long, long ago. <g> 

			Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn729s3k.jt.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<36222df6.240346109@news.freeserve.net> <3298285.13592.15632@kcbbs.gen.nz> <fCNU1.980$q3.7025203@news1.atlantic.net> <3624BE8F.9CC7AD83@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 Oct 1998 09:09:03 -0600, Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:
>One would think that it would be difficult to design an optimizing
>compiler for C.  Optimizing compilers need to know what you want done. 
>Cobol is more like a RISC computer, with larger functions which can each
>be optimized fully.  C is CISC functions, which cannot be optimized
>individually.

I thought it was the opposite - COBOL is more CISC-like because it has a number
of verbs which perform various complex behaviors - consider how many actual
machine-code instructions a single READ or UNSTRING would translate into. C, on
the other hand, is RISCier since it has simpler lower-level functions. 

This is one reason why Acucobol runs well even though it doesn't compile to
machine-code. Most COBOL verbs involve so much processing that the overhead of
a runtime is minimal compared to the actual work.
```

```
One of the significant advantages of byte code is that the
programs can be distributed to target machines of very
different types froma single compiled set.  Not only is
this not usefully inspectable (it is not i86 code) but
the performance charateristics will vary enormously from
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36234536.53339878@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<3621e758.222268755@news.freeserve.net> <3298285.11487.12700@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
On 13 Oct 98 03:11:27 GMT, riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
wrote:


>
>Please explain how knowing i86 instructions well can be
>useful if:
>
>        The compiler targets byte code only

Not that useful but I didn't say it was in that case.

>   or   The generated code simply calls utility routines

Depends. It could be useful to see where utility routines are being
used instead of inline code I guess. Depends on the compiler a lot.
Does it use the stack? Registers? Is the processor good with
registers, stack etc

>
>
>One of the significant advantages of byte code is that the
>programs can be distributed to target machines of very
>different types froma single compiled set.

Yes though you pay for it in speed. If speed is not a concern then you
aren't likely going to be concerned about check the compiler output
are you!


>  Not only is
>this not usefully inspectable (it is not i86 code) but
>the performance charateristics will vary enormously from
>one run-time to another.

Depends on the virtual machine implementation. For example, MS Word
isn't native. It runs on a virtual machine in p-code. MS's virtual
machine is fairly close to the i86 though. Stick the same p-code on
the Mac and the difference in architecture makes it run slower. 

>
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>not knowing what one is doing ?

ie. you don't know what code the compiler will generate. Writing
performance tests to find out what the compiler generates is perhaps a
good idea if that is the case.


>
>Especially when the conditions indicated above apply ?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>These 'further results', do they invalidate the timings ?

Possibly. It could be that you look at the code and spot where you can
optimise better yourself than the compiler should that be of enough
importance.

>
>> Sometimes in fact you really have to. I had a problem a while back
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>What was causing the additional IRQs ?

In that case a runtime call that displayed an activity indicator in
the last position on the screen. DOS wants to scroll the screen if you
write to the last position on the screen. I moved the indicator to the
second last position and the code ran much quicker. 

I was also getting too many DOS protect mode/real mode switches. 
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)

```
> >
> >The only way to determine performance is to set up a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

That is true of any machine, in general terms.  What it may
not be possible to adequately determine in any simple way
is how fast it will do it.

I also suspect that MVS programmers are unfamiliar with the
idea of 'byte-code' (I think there is a mainframe AcuCOBOL
though, isn't there?) or 'run-times'.

It seems, from the conversation that the Assembler listing
is also expected to be the _complete_ record, is this so ?

Wheras in many PC based compilers anything tricky and the
assembler is 'load a few parameters and Call ...'.

This produces nice compact code but is indeterminate from
a performance viewpoint, _except_ with test programs to
time it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** "ben" <robertshaweb@dnb.com>

```
I guess I didn't give enough information.

The UNSTRING command I use,  results in only one "segment" of the string
being passed to a WRITEQ command ... then the loop progresses with the
POINTER repositioning the string for the next "segment" to be written ...
until there is no more data in the string

The string can be 4000 bytes and the segment may be as small as 8 bytes ...

The code looks small but is performing up to 500 UNSTRINGS a waste of
resources for every string thats read ?

The INSPECT would result in an extra MOVE statement ( I guess the UNSTRING
is doing the move too)

William Lynch wrote in message <361FEF8E.D90AD039@att.net>...
>ben wrote:
>>
>> My program can be written to use INSPECT or UNSTRING for the manipulation
of
>> a variable string of data (containg logical  segments delimited by
linefeed
>> chars).
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298283.83258.7806@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<6vqn5n$rfp$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>`

```
In message <<6vqn5n$rfp$1@news.mel.aone.net.au>> "ben" <robertshaweb@dnb.com> writes:
> 
> The UNSTRING command I use,  results in only one "segment" of the string
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> is doing the move too)

The INSPECT is presumably giving enough information for you
to formulate a MOVE with reference notation, eg:
MOVE buffer(start:end).  This may well be more efficient
than an UNSTRING WITH POINTER, but it may be even better to
just use a PERFORM loop to update the start and end values
for the reference MOVE.

The only way to determine which is most efficient is to
code all 3 and test them with _your_ compiler and _your_
environment.  You may also find some guidelines on which
usages are going to be more efficient for your machine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: inspect v's unstring

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)

