```
ok, so i interfere again in the thread.  (somewhere cobol will resurface
i'm sure)	
i like the points i see.  so far i can summarize this:  centralized
federal taxation is out as an option.  take for example "prohibition". 
coming from an ancestry of moonshiners, it ain't worth a damn.  crack
and heroin and meth are not moonshine, they are far worse.  kids didn't
kill kids over moonshine during prohibition.  people did not rob and
kill for a shot of whiskey during prohibition.  now we have criminal
gangs running the supply of crack.  in my town (macon, ga pop 150,000)
there are over 1000 gang members in 15 gangs.  want to know their
biggest fear?  a CITIZEN with a .45 in "condition one".  no gov't law
will win the drug war...just you and me.  want to stop the drug
problem?  move the sonsabitches out of town!  one town at a timeDonald
Tees wrote:
> 
> Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is the *price* of the drugs that cause the violence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2k Death March Begins.

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70p8dd$h3n$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i336$lc8$1@news.igs.net> <slrn72sfao.di.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <362ECD69.4BBB971A@att.net> <70n8qh$240$1@news.igs.net> <UkIX1.1026$Se2.1886209@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70nqeo$et9$1@news.igs.net> <boLX1.1980$xx4.1981762@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70odmi$qa$1@news.igs.net> <362FEFF0.217CAD18@mindspring.com>`

```
SkidMike wrote in message <362FEFF0.217CAD18@mindspring.com>...
>ok, so i interfere again in the thread.  (somewhere cobol will resurface
>i'm sure)

>kill kids over moonshine during prohibition.  people did not rob and
>kill for a shot of whiskey during prohibition.  now we have criminal


Mike, did you ever hear of Al Capone? Or the St. Valentines Day
massacre?  That was over prohibition, and over alcohol. Gang
warfare in the states was INVENTED to control illegal booze. The
violence continues because there are people that think the best way
to solve problems is to grab a ".45" and go out and shoot somebody.

>gangs running the supply of crack.  in my town (macon, ga pop 150,000)
>there are over 1000 gang members in 15 gangs.  want to know their
>biggest fear?  a CITIZEN with a .45 in "condition one".  no gov't law
>will win the drug war...just you and me.  want to stop the drug


I live in a city of 1.5 million.  Here, we put citizens that grab .45's and
use them
to shoot people in jail.  We put drug addicts in drug rehabilitation
clinics. In the
twenty years that I have lived here, there have been two murders.  The
deaths
due to crack have been OD's.  I just came back from a walk through the
downtown area.  It is 2:30 in the morning. I walked for an hour, and stopped
and chatted with several street people.  It was quite a pleasant walk, and I
enjoyed it.  Could you do that?

You say "you and me" can solve the drug problem.  Although I have been clean
for four years, I am a crack cocaine addict.  I spent roughly $10,000 last
year
putting two street addicts from my neighbourhood through rehab, and at the
moment have a third staying at my house while waiting for her first
apartment
in fifteen years.  The rent is paid for the first and last month (I
co-signed) and
she moves in on the first.  I enjoy my life, and can go anywhere in this
town,
including covert crack houses, unarmed, with no fear, and be treated with
respect by both the crack addicts and the local narcotics squad.  It sounds
to me like your way does not work as well as mine.

You are quite right in one thing, though, this is getting seriously off
topic, so
this will be my last post in this thread.  If anybody would like to continue
it,
we can do so in private mail, or switch over to one of the empty NG's that
have not been used for a few years.  There are a few thousand of those
that can be used as private channels.

Donald@willmack.com
Donald Tees, Kitchener, Ontario, Canada.

P.S.  With regard to your screen problem.  In the morning, I will package up
a version 4.0 program that uses a 10 line*45 column screen, and send it
off to you as an example.  It is a program that keeps track of the number
of days, hours and minutes that an addict has been clean. It will handle
five
addicts.  I wrote it several years back, when I was getting clean myself.
For
now, I am going to bed.

Donald.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Drugs

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2k Death March Begins.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70l5pm$8vn$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i336$lc8$1@news.igs.net> <70kdek$1vg_002@news.netdirect.net>`

```
Doug Miller wrote in message <70kdek$1vg_002@news.netdirect.net>...

>Since we're going off-topic already...can anyone think of a single reason
>why the production, distribution, possession, and use of marijuana should
>be regulated differently in any fashion from the production, distribution,
>possession, and use of ethyl alcohol?
>
Sure.  As someone that has spent every Sunday for the last two years
in either a jail or a rehab centre giving counceling, I would suggest that
alcohol should be more tightly controlled because it is more addictive
and more damaging to both the body and the psyche.

As far as danger goes, I would rate alcohol as the most dangerous drug,
heroin second, with crack cocaine in a close tie, regular cocaine third
nicotine fourth, and pot  a rather mild fifth.

I know personally of several cases where pot served as a treatment
for addiction to other drugs, though the conquering of *all* dependencies
is the only really satisfactory end goal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2k Death March Begins.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70n0cu$3m8_002@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i336$lc8$1@news.igs.net> <70kdek$1vg_002@news.netdirect.net> <70l5pm$8vn$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <70l5pm$8vn$1@news.igs.net>,
   "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
+Doug Miller wrote in message <70kdek$1vg_002@news.netdirect.net>...
+
+>Since we're going off-topic already...can anyone think of a single reason
+>why the production, distribution, possession, and use of marijuana should
+>be regulated differently in any fashion from the production, distribution,
+>possession, and use of ethyl alcohol?
+>
+Sure.  As someone that has spent every Sunday for the last two years
+in either a jail or a rehab centre giving counceling, I would suggest that
+alcohol should be more tightly controlled because it is more addictive
+and more damaging to both the body and the psyche.
+
+As far as danger goes, I would rate alcohol as the most dangerous drug,
+heroin second, with crack cocaine in a close tie, regular cocaine third
+nicotine fourth, and pot  a rather mild fifth.
+
I'm not sure I agree with your assessment of alcohol's dangers relative
to the other drugs you named, but I certainly agree that it's far more
dangerous than commonly supposed. The major impediment to any successful
stringent regulation of alcohol production and consumption, as the U.S.
discovered earlier in this century, is that any idiot can make it easily,
from commonly available ingredients.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2k Death March Begins.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Mk2X1.466$Se2.714049@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i5qn$ogj$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jeff Farkas wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>in Great Britain it was 17.5%!!! That hurts...

The total tax burden here in the U.S. a bit above 45%, and that is on
total income.  Sales tax in my part of the country is 8%-9%.  I don't
expect to pay less money.  I would be willing to pay more money, if only
the IRS was out of our affairs.  I was audited in 1996 for 1992-1995,
and it was not a nice experience.  I pay taxes honestly, and there was no
problem with that, they never questioned my returns as far as accuracy.
But the process was extremely disruptive and intrusive.  For example,
they went through all my check books, line by line.  If you ever get an
IRS auditor who decides to give you a hard time, they can practically
destroy your life on a whim.  To me it is unthinkable that in a 'free'
society an honest citizen would be subjected to such risk of harm and
violation of their privacy by the government during the normal course of
business.  An honest person should have no reason to fear the government.
But there is hardly a person in the U.S. whose heart wouldn't skip a beat
on learning they were going to be audited by the IRS.  That is tyranny.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i5qn$ogj$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <Mk2X1.466$Se2.714049@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70id9s$5r1$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362D781C.86A44389@att.net>`

```
On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 05:58:18, William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> wrote:

> If it makes you feel better, anyone who works in NY State pays a higher
> state income tax than CT, plus the poor buggers in NYC & Yonkers (that I
> know of, might be others) pay 8.25 % sales tax, including clothing,
> which is tax free here in NJ.
> 

I used to live in Tennessee - No state income tax, 8.25% sales tax, 
INCLUDING FOOD.

When I was looking for a job, I wanted either enough of a  salary bump
to cover any extra taxes (Amazing how many recruiters can't figure out
why I needed such a large percentage increase to BREAK EVEN), or to 
move to a state with an equal or lower tax burden.

I ended up in Texas.  Slightly higher cost of living, but still NO 
income taxes, sales tax a little over 8%, but does NOT include food. 
(Except snack food).
```

```
why not a state tax on income or sales or both and no fed tax? 
originally that was the way it was with each state sending money to the
federal gov.  consider what you pay in fed tax as far as your
withholding.  go ahead and pull out your latest pay stub now and read
the fed tax line.  wouldn't you rather you have those several thousand
dollars for yourself?  want to know where your fed tax dollars go?  come
to my shop and i'll show you the welfare neighborhood behind my shop. 
cut the bums off, cut foreign aid off and see what's left!  sorry i got
wound up a little
```

```
On Thu, 22 Oct 1998 19:28:07 GMT, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
>>First it is in the city of Chicago. It is caffeinated water!! No flavor, not
>>fizz just pure buzz. And think of it, you can make coffee with this stuff!!

>I think that I read about this somewhere.....hmmmmm....instead of paying for
>caffeinated water, I would just mix up a bottle of No-Doz in a quart of spring
>water.

When the subject came up in ASR, the consensus was that this is an entirely
worthless product unless you are unable to make your own caffeinated water or
have abnormally weak coffee. Given that most true coffe-addicts have no trouble
brewing things strong enough that worries about heart failure aren't entirely
academic, buying caffeinated water makes about as much sense as paying $3.00
for distilled water in a bottle with naivE on the side. The guys hawking this
are probably laughing all the way to the bank...
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn731dh9.1hc.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i5qn$ogj$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <Mk2X1.466$Se2.714049@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70id9s$5r1$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362D781C.86A44389@att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YztOvX1cHhIf@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <70lvid$k55$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362E8854.2A0BC767@mindspring.com> <70m6mu$1sv$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362f4c7c.11508520@news.enter.net> <70nmhi$luv$3@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362f8600.26235347@news.enter.net> <slrn72v2ep.12f.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <70ofn4$rde$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <slrn72vi4u.157.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <70oqhf$gp3$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Thu, 22 Oct 1998 22:42:24 -0400, Jeff Farkas
<JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net> wrote:
>> >You got that right. All I know is that is sounded very funny to me, sort of
>> >like the "Pet Rock" of the late 90's. :-)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am just guessing. What in the world is a Tamagotchi???

It was the first of the "virtual pets". Basically you have a simulated creature
that you must feed by pressing a button, play with by pressing other buttons
and so forth. If you don't feed it, it will die. If you don't play with it, it
will die. For inexplicable reasons, some people get very attached to them (It
sounds way too much like admining an NT farm to get me interested). Some people
have driven off the road or into things because their tamagotchi started to
beep about needing food or attention. They start playing with it and forget
what they're doing...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Taxes was.. Y2k Death March Begins.

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** stevewie@apk.net (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3632a2a6.6979665@news.apk.net>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YztOvX1cHhIf@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <70lvid$k55$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362E8854.2A0BC767@mindspring.com> <70m6mu$1sv$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362f4c7c.11508520@news.enter.net> <70nmhi$luv$3@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362f8600.26235347@news.enter.net> <slrn72v2ep.12f.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <70ofn4$rde$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <slrn72vi4u.157.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com> <70oqhf$gp3$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Thu, 22 Oct 1998 22:42:24 -0400, Jeff Farkas
<JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net> enlightened us:

>
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Tamagotchi is a small computer game that simulates caring for a pet or
a baby.  I believe the first one was a chicken.  What happens is, the
pet will require feeding, nurturing and sleeping.  It will make
obnoxious sounds if these things are not done.  The user provides
feeding etc. by pressing buttons on the device which is just a little
larger than a digital watch.  If you don't take care of your pet, it
will die!

These things were a big fad with the kids last year and became quite a
nusiance for teachers (sounds going off in the middle of class etc.)
that many schools band them.

They started in Japan, but have spread to just about everywhere.  I
haven't heard or seen much about them lately, so maybe the fad is
dying out.

Cheers,


>
>Jeff
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Cats know how we feel. They don't give a damn, but they know.
 Steve
```

```
In article <362f8600.26235347@news.enter.net>,
Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>I think that I read about this somewhere.....hmmmmm....instead of
>paying for caffeinated water, I would just mix up a bottle of No-Doz
>in a quart of spring water.

Mmmmmmmmmm, *boy*, that shore is some *goooood* eatin'!  Hey, I got an
idea... how about you brew up a jug a' that stuff, set 'er out on the
winder-sill on a cold winter's night an' let 'er freeze right up... then
shave down the ice, add some syrup an' hey, presto!  Caffeinated
Yummy-Freez!

Bleargh... why is it that the only flavor for the syrup which comes to
mind as an appropriate choice for Caffeinated Yummy-Freez is... cigarette?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Taxes was.. Y2k Death March Begins.

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3630c762.21892277@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <362E8854.2A0BC767@mindspring.com> <362f8600.26235347@news.enter.net> <70q7i2$hj7$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:

>In article <362f8600.26235347@news.enter.net>,
>Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yummy-Freez!

Doc:

You *must* be joking!  Everyone knows that it's extremely important to
first pour the No-Doz Water into small molds and shove little wooden
sticks in the center of each mold to make....No-Dozer Ice Pops!

Nobody would ever shave the ice to make a Yummy-Freez...except, of
course, the local 7-11 convenience store.  They don't call these
caffeinated wonders "Yummy-Freezes" or "Slurpies" either...they call
them "Jumpies."

>Bleargh... why is it that the only flavor for the syrup which comes to
>mind as an appropriate choice for Caffeinated Yummy-Freez is... cigarette?

No...there's also Doughnut flavor for the Dunkin Donuts crowd as well
as Macanudo flavor for those trendy folks who spend time in the cigar
bars.

Mmmmmmm...doesn't this make you thirsty for a tall, cool glass of
No-Doz water?

....It's Friday again!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
I seem to recall a couple of Dave Barry columns, one about the military buying
$400K *combat* Fax Machines[1] and another about how some government agency was
storing dead radioactive beagles in a freezer. 

Of course, I always enjoyed the way the city council handled a lean year in a
place I used to live: while closing libraries and cutting back office hours and
services everywhere, they paid an "artist" a couple hundred grand to build a
statue by the pier. Said statue was a piece of pipe with a bend in it, placed
in the surf so water could shoot out the top. Of course, the council was
apparently unaware that water in the ocean contains salt, which promptly
started corroding said work of "art". 


[1] Designed to handle the rigors of combat conditions. Left unanswered, of
course, are the questions such as: what would you want to fax in combat or why
night buy 20 $200 civilian fax machines and replace as needed...
```

```
>On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 22:53:04 -0400, Jeff Farkas
><JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I seem to recall a couple of Dave Barry columns, one about the military
buying


It is a fact of life that the best selling pot in the world (Northern
Lights) was
developed by the University of British Columbia, under a federal grant. That
particular strain may have fallen from grace a bit by now, as the program
in an on-going one.

I tried their latest a couple of weeks back, as produced by an old friend
with a PhD in agriculture and thirty years growing experience.  It was
produced under 55 watts power per square foot, and the bud dried to about
15% moisture by weight. He then mixed back about 20% shake to give
good burning characteristics (that by request, as I like the flavour of
a bit of shake mixed in).  He grew the original plants from seed out of
Amsterdam, then selected the five best females and cloned from them.

By the time you get to my advanced years, booze is far too hard on the
body.
;<)
```

- **From:** jeff@jakfield.xu-netx.com (Jeff York)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363a2ae6.57972339@news.u-net.com>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i5qn$ogj$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net> wrote:

>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>in Great Britain it was 17.5%!!! That hurts...

and we still pay income tax too..

>To our friends in the UK please correct me if I am wrong.
>Also, what is used to determine when to apply VAT??

The rules are arcane in the extreme,  and somewhat arbitrary..  A
couple of examples..

Plain biscuits are considered "food" and thus have a VAT rate of zero
percent.  Chocolate biscuits are "luxuries" and are thus taxed at the
standard rate of 17.5%.  A box of mixed biscuits,  should in theory,
always be open by the retailer who should then count the number of
each type of biscuit and pro-rata the tax accordingly..  :-)

There is a type of "cattle nut" which is normally fed to cattle,  and
as such carries no VAT as it is "food for cattle" and is covered by
the "food" rules..  These nuts can also be fed to horses,  which are
considered a "luxury" sporting item,  and thus subject to VAT..  Thus
one item can be either zero rated or standard rated depending upon its
final use..

Other areas are somewhat more complex..  :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT:Re: Y2k Death March Begins.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36375D99.19F51FE6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<70g28j$4ue$1@news.igs.net> <19981019192106.27116.00002964@ng63.aol.com> <362c7af8.85223003@news21.bellglobal.com> <70i0a4$jqm$1@news.igs.net> <RB%W1.425$Se2.633786@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <70i5qn$ogj$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <363a2ae6.57972339@news.u-net.com>`

```
> >To our friends in the UK please correct me if I am wrong.
> >Also, what is used to determine when to apply VAT??
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> final use..

VATs scare me.

In the states though we can sometimes be taxed differently for the same
object.  In some states you may be asked whether you are going to use
some liquid as a mouthwash or a gargol.  The first is a cosmetic, and
the latter is a medicine...

But I really don't want to approach finding which taxes are fair - a
better appoach is to limit government spending.  Say, we prohibit them
from increasing spending in any department (without 2/3 war or emergency
type vote) if the previous year the government spent more than 15% of
the GDG.  With this approach, there is little incentive to finding more
ways to sneak our money from us (hurting our economy).  They may choose
to tweak where the money comes from - but not with the goal of having
more money to spend.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to display ascii char in pic x clause?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36235D2F.FEE228B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<19981012205237.06447.00000346@ng40.aol.com>`

```
Quiteman25 wrote:
> 
> I want to use the ascii chart to display special characters.
> 
> Is it possible to assign a hex value (I assume it'd be hex?) to an alphanumeric
> pic clause?

Yes
 
> Is there another way?

Yes.  I suspect you want to use a loop, in which case you may choose
something like this  (adjust the picture sizes to fit your machine and
compiler):

 05  chars.
     10  char1     pic x(02).
     10  char2     pic x(02).
 05  nums          pic 9(05)   comp.  redefines chars.

You can fill up a table of these and then display char2.   If you start
using signed nums, you can have trouble.

But when I did this, I just edited a file using a hex editor.  (I have
also done so with ebcdic).  It was easier, especially since I needed to
discover which characters did funny things to my display or print. 
Remember some characters (LF) won't display correctly.  On my charts, I
had to edit those characters out.

Also, different printers will display the same characters differently.
```

```

>something like this  (adjust the picture sizes to fit your machine and
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to display ascii char in pic x clause?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SiKU1.363$9R2.702088@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<19981012205237.06447.00000346@ng40.aol.com> <36235D2F.FEE228B@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>Yes.  I suspect you want to use a loop, in which case you may choose
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 05  nums          pic 9(05)   comp.  redefines chars.

Be ready to adjust not only the PIC, but the USAGE as well.  COMP means
different things on different compilers/platforms.  What you want here
is a binary representation.  In COBOL 85, BINARY might do the trick.
Also note that you may be able to use either CHAR1 and/or CHAR2 above,
but not necessarily both, because of the way the binary representation
and signs work on the compiler/platform.  And leave out the '.' between
COMP and REDEFINES. :-)
```

## Thread: C program call from COBOL — 11 msgs

### C program call from COBOL

- **From:** "Gerhard Bouma" <pmi@nl.aegon.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net>`

```
Hi,

I have made a C program which calls a DLL (all on MVS), but now I want to
call this main C-program from a COBOL program. Everything goes well, except
the passing of parameters to the C-program.
Does anyone have some tips?

Gerhard
```

#### ↳ Re: C program call from COBOL

- **From:** Stuart Gaunt <stuartg@sequent.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com>`
- **References:** `<6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net>`

```
I believe that COBOL passes all its parameters by reference, so your 'by
value' parameters will appear as addresses, not exactly what you want.

I've tried this on UNIX, and it works OK.  If you need a code fragment,
I should be able to dig one out.

Regards

Stuart
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C program call from COBOL

- **From:** aflinsch@njeb.att.com (Alex Flinsch)
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vvfuo$n0q@newsb.netnews.att.com>`
- **References:** `<6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net> <36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com>`

```
In article <36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com>, stuartg@sequent.com wrote:
>I believe that COBOL passes all its parameters by reference, so your 'by
>value' parameters will appear as addresses, not exactly what you want.
>

Unless you have coded your C proggie to accept a pointer to your 
parameters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C program call from COBOL

- **From:** bgwillia@vcu.edu (Boyce G. Williams, Jr.)
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36235107.3991841@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`
- **References:** `<6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net> <36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com> <6vvfuo$n0q@newsb.netnews.att.com>`

```
aflinsch@njeb.att.com (Alex Flinsch) wrote:

>In article <36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com>, stuartg@sequent.com wrote:
>>I believe that COBOL passes all its parameters by reference, so your 'by
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>parameters.

I wrote a reply to a simular question regarding passing data between C
and COBOL programs.  Here it is from Deja News:

I'm not sure what platform you wish to perform this feat, so I'm
assuming you're doing this on a mainframe (MVS).  Linking
multi-language object files together can be done provided the venders
allowed means to this end via linkage cross-referencing (That's why
AFAIK Pascal can't be used as a subprogram to a COBOL call- one can't
pass data to it because one can't resolve cross-referencing issues).
I've written FORTRAN subprograms called from COBOL on MVS.

Examples used here are from James G. Janossy book: 'Advanced MVS JCL.'

The COBOL program fields to pass to C must be as follows:

C          COBOL             Size      Alignment
-----      ---------         -------   ---------
char       PIC X             1 byte    byte
signed int PIC S9(9) COMP    4 bytes   fullword
double     COMP-2            8 bytes   doubleword
struct     group item entry
array      OCCURS clause

BTW- C cannot directly handle packed decimal (COMP-3) type COBOL
fields.

COBOL uses the following to perform the call:

01  WS-VALUES.
    05  VALUE-1 PIC S9(09) COMP.
    05  VALUE-2 PIC S9(09) COMP.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000-CALL.
    CALL 'CSUB' USING WS-VALUES.

C uses the following to receive the call:

#pragma linkage( csub, COBOL )

struct bucket
{
    int int1;
    int int2;
};
void csub( struct bucket *parm )
{
  (*parm ).int2 = ( *parm ).int1 + 1;
}

One then compile the two programs seperately:

//STEP1 EXEC EDCCL,
//  CPARM='SOURCE,XREF',
//  INFILE='USER.SOURCE.LIB(CSUB'
//  OUTFILE='USER.LOAD.LIB(CSUB),DISP=SHR'
//STEP2 EXEC IGYWCL
//COBOL.SYSIN DD DSN=USER.SOURCE.LIB(COBMAIN),DISP=SHR
//LKED.SYSLMOD DD DSN=USER.LOAD.LIB(COBMAIN),DISP=SHR

The remainder of the programs and JCL are left as an excersize.

This will create (assuming compiler options are defaulted correct)
dynamically linked calls between the COBOL main and C subprogram.

I haven't done calls between COBOL and C, just between COBOL and
FORTRAN.  But the process is reasonable.

As always, just my two cents worth adjusted for inflation,

Boyce G. Williams, Jr.

 .--------------------------------------------------------------------.
 | "People should have two virtues:  purpose- the courage to envisage |
 | and pursue valued goals uninhibited by the defeat of infantile     |
 | fantasies, by guilt and the failing fear punishment;  and wisdom- a|
 | detached concern with life itself, in the face of death itself."   |
 |                                                     Norman F. Dixon|
 '--------------------------------------------------------------------'
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C program call from COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36242006.2EC9@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net> <36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com> <6vvfuo$n0q@newsb.netnews.att.com> <36235107.3991841@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`

```
Boyce G. Williams, Jr. wrote, amid snippage:
> 
> aflinsch@njeb.att.com (Alex Flinsch) wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>

Depending on the dialect you may be able to pass a parameter "BY
VALUE," possibly with some restrictions on the size or type of
parameter so passed.  The C program receives the value rather than
a pointer to a value.  I've done this with MicroFocus under Unix; I
haven't tried it under MVS.

> I'm not sure what platform you wish to perform this feat, 

The original post specified MVS.

> so I'm
> assuming you're doing this on a mainframe (MVS).  Linking
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I've written FORTRAN subprograms called from COBOL on MVS.

[snip -- examples from Janossy's book]

> BTW- C cannot directly handle packed decimal (COMP-3) type COBOL
> fields.

Standard C can't, at least not without a lot of bothersome bit
twiddling.  However, through an IBM extension, C for MVS can handle
packed decimal as a native datatype.

> COBOL uses the following to perform the call:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> #pragma linkage( csub, COBOL )

Note for the unwary: this pragma, like all pragmas in C, is
inherently compiler-specific.  Its presence here hints that COBOL
and C don't really get along very well under MVS without special 
tweaks from the compiler.

I don't know if this pragma is still necessary in the LE environment.

> struct bucket
> {
>     int int1;
>     int int2;
> };

Note that the C Standard permits the compiler to insert padding
bytes between structure members in order to maintain byte alignment.
Your options are:

   1. Pass elementary items rather than group items.  It's safest
      for the elementary items to be 01-levels; otherwise, depending
      on the platform, the byte alignment may not work right for
      the C program.

   2. Pass a group item which the C program expresses not as a
      struct but as an array of char, from which it plucks the
      members according to specified byte offsets.

   3. Pass a group item expressed by the C program as a struct;
      carefully make sure the byte alignment is correct for your
      environment and (where applicable) the set of compiler 
      options in use; remember to check again if you ever port to
      another environment or to another release of the C compiler.

[snip -- more sample code and JCL]

> I haven't done calls between COBOL and C, just between COBOL and
> FORTRAN.  But the process is reasonable.

I haven't done calls between COBOL and C under MVS, so I can't give
you the details for this platform, but I can suggest what to worry 
about:

1. COBOL passes parameters by reference, while C passes them by 
value.  Arrays are a special case which I won't go into here, but
any competent C programmer will know what I mean.

2. Compatible definitions of datatypes.

3. Byte alignment, especially in group items as noted above.

4. The order in which parameters are passed.  Due to differences in
calling conventions, in some environments you may have to reverse
the order.

5. How to handle a value which is returned by a C program.  MicroFocus
provides syntax for COBOL to receive the return value; I don't know
about MVS.

6. By convention, C usually uses nul-terminated character strings,
whereas COBOL defines fixed length fields and pads with blanks.

7. Possible interference between signal handlers in the C code and
whatever the COBOL runtime system does with exceptional conditions.


The ways to resolve these issues are highly platform-specific, so read
the manuals carefully.  Very likely, either your COBOL or your C will
have to be written differently in order to accommodate the quirks of
the other language.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C program call from COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36249800.3EBB@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net> <36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com> <6vvfuo$n0q@newsb.netnews.att.com> <36235107.3991841@usenet.acw.vcu.edu> <36242006.2EC9@swbell.net>`

```
Michael C. Kasten wrote:

[much snippage -- in particular, my list of things to consider when
 calling C modules from COBOL]

I just remembered another one, specific to the MVS environment:

8. In COBOL, the names of programs and entry points (other than
nested programs) must be no longer than eight characters.  Object
modules in normal libraries use eight-digit names for external
references.  However, names of functions and globals in C may be longer.
As a result, unless you confine your C external references to short 
names, you have to store your C object modules in a special funky 
library format which mangles the names to make them fit.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C program call from COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gerhard Bouma" <pmi@nl.aegon.nl>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7026uh$r8v$1@newnews.nl.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net> <36233F32.5A841378@sequent.com> <6vvfuo$n0q@newsb.netnews.att.com> <36235107.3991841@usenet.acw.vcu.edu> <36242006.2EC9@swbell.net> <36249800.3EBB@swbell.net>`

```
[snippage
>In COBOL, the names of programs and entry points (other than
>nested programs) must be no longer than eight characters.  Object
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>library format which mangles the names to make them fit.


Well yes, but to be complete, I use a development tool (Natstar by Nat
Systems) on my client to build (windows) applications, which generates C
coding. Also I can generate C for MVS and FTP it to the host to compile
there. So now I can build a function to be called on any platform. It also
takes care of the long names for me, so I can still just use them.

The final coding (for my experiment) now looks like this (sorry for some
dutch words):
COBOL:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01  PRPI                 PIC  9(2),9(7).
01  AANTAL-TERM          PIC  9(3).

01  WS-VALUES.
    05  VALUE-1          PIC S9(9)          VALUE ZERO.
    05  VALUE-2                     COMP-2  VALUE ZERO.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000-CALL.
    ACCEPT AANTAL-TERM.
    MOVE AANTAL-TERM TO VALUE-1.
    CALL "NSBMAIN" USING WS-VALUES.
    MOVE VALUE-2 TO PRPI.
    DISPLAY "AANTAL-TERM: " VALUE-1.
    DISPLAY "PI         : " PRPI.
    GOBACK.

If I don't want to use the #pragma pack in my following C, then I have to
use:
01  WS-VALUES SYNC.
in the COBOL source.

In C:  (NSBMAIN)

#pragma linkage( csub, COBOL )
#pragma pack(1)
#include "nslib.h"
#include "nsthform.h"
#include "nsthings.h"

_Packed struct bucket
{                                                      
    signed int   aant_term;                            
    double       pi;                                   
};                                                     
                                                       
void csub( struct bucket *parm )                       
{                                                      
    printf("NS-IN : %d\n",(*parm).aant_term);          
                                                       
    I_BEREKEN_PI ( (*parm).aant_term, &(*parm).pi);    
                                                       
    printf("NS-IN : %d\n",(*parm).aant_term);          
    printf("NS-UIT: %f\n",(*parm).pi);                 
}                                                      
                                         
This then calculates Pi. Notice the long instruction name! Of course I now have to make things more generic. 
My next problem (little project or maybe even opportunity) is that we also use CSP 4.1. (it generates COBOL to be compiled) CSP doesn't allow for very much data-types and alignment. The #pragma pack comes in handy, but as of now I don't see a way to pass double's. 

Thank you all for your reactions, I didn't expect this many.
Gerhard.
```

```
MVS (or Windows or AIX or OS/2) - check out the PGMNAME compiler option in
```

```
Gerhard Bouma schrieb in Nachricht <6vva63$fst$1@newnews.nl.uu.net>...

use #pragma-option or

LINKAGE SECTION.
01 P1 PIC S9(9) USAGE IS BINARY
01 P2 PIC S9(9) USAGE IS BINARY
CALL 'CFUNC' USING BY VALUE P1
     RETURNING P2.

int CFUNC(int
{ 
  int p2;          
  p2=p1;           
  return p2;       
}                       
    
RTFM OS/390 V2R4.0 LANG ENV WRITING ILC APPLICATION
```

<a id="t-1640b6737b"></a>
## Thread: PICTURE VALUE "P" — 3 msgs

### PICTURE VALUE "P"

- **From:** cesaralt@blockbuster.com.pe (Cesar Altamirano)
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362374e3.5579609@news.unired.net.pe>`

```
Hi all,
I have a program with the following definition:
.
11 FIELD04   PIC SVPP9(15).
.
What happens if I move the value 0.15 to FIELD04?
What is the usefulnes of this clause?
The documentation says that the P's are replace by zeros.

Thanks for any help.
Best Regards,
Cesar Altamirano.

E-mailto: cesaralt@blockbuster.com.pe
```

#### ↳ Re: PICTURE VALUE "P"

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R0MU1.409$9R2.756519@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<362374e3.5579609@news.unired.net.pe>`

```
Cesar Altamirano wrote:
>I have a program with the following definition:
>
>  11 FIELD04   PIC SVPP9(15).
>
>What happens if I move the value 0.15 to FIELD04?

The result is that FIELD04 will have a value of zero.

>What is the usefulnes of this clause?

'P' is called the 'scaling symbol'.  It is used to store values
which will never have digits within a certain range of the decimal
point (e.g. two in the above).  For example, if you were storing a
number which will always be in whole millions, you can save storage
space by using a PIC like this

   03  MILLIONS         PIC S9(4)P(6) COMP.

Only two bytes are used to store the above number (assuming COMP
is binary).  Other than the fact that you cannot store any values
in fractional millions there (and it takes less space), it acts in
all other respects as if it had been declared as

   03  MILLIONS         PIC S9(10) COMP.

One other use is that you can truncate to a certain range of digits
other than the decimal point by moving a value to a field with the
'P' character where you want to truncate the digits (e.g. millions
in the example above).  This is why in your example FIELD04 is set
to zero.

>The documentation says that the P's are replace by zeros.

That is not strictly correct in a general sense.  The more general
statement is to say that the 'P' characters are 'treated as if' they
were zeros in calculations, editing, compares, etc.  There's nothing
actually 'there' at all.  'P' is similar to the 'V' assumed decimal
point in that respect.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PICTURE VALUE "P"

- **From:** stevewie@apk.net (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3629c778.7952652@news.apk.net>`
- **References:** `<362374e3.5579609@news.unired.net.pe> <R0MU1.409$9R2.756519@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Tue, 13 Oct 1998 17:24:01 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> enlightened us:

>Cesar Altamirano wrote:
>>I have a program with the following definition:
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>point in that respect.

Good explanation, Jusdon.  For the benefit of the original poster my
old college text explains 'P' thusly:

The character P indicates the presence of an assumed zero.  The Ps are
used to position the assumed decimal point away from the actual
number.  For example, the actual value in storage is 15.  It would be
treated as 15000 if the PICTURE clause is 99PPP, or as .00015 if the
PICTURE clause is VPPP99.

The scaling position character P is not counted in the size of the
data item.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Todays tip to annoy people:  Practice making fax and modem noises.
 Steve
```

<a id="t-569e27c1b9"></a>
## Thread: Win95 API Help NetExpress — 3 msgs

### Win95 API Help NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981014112637.11792.00000566@ng73.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

I am new to Win95 API programming and have been working with a few samples that
people have been kind to send.

I am trying to use a new API and am unsure how to properly code this.  I would
like to use the SetSysColors API to change the COLOR_BACKGROUND (color of
windows desktop) to black.

I am not sure how to setup the three parameters that go with this API.  They
are int nDspElements, CONST INT * lpnDspElements and CONST COLORREF *
lpdwRgbValues.

Could anyone offer some help ?

Thanks in advance,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Win95 API Help NetExpress

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<707mh9$2bp@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19981014112637.11792.00000566@ng73.aol.com>`

```
Bob,

NetExpress contains a copy file called WINDOWS.CPY which contains the
definitions of the Windows data types and constants needed for API
programming. To code the example you mentioned you would need something like
:

SPECIAL-NAMES.
    CALL-CONVENTION 74 IS WINAPI.

01 nDspElements int VALUE 1.
01 DspElements.
    03 FILLER         int VALUE COLOR-BACKGROUND.
01 RgbValues.
    03 FILLER         COLORREF VALUE h"00bbggrr".

* replace the 'bbggrr' with the hex values you want. Black will be zeroes.

CALL WINAPI 'SetSysColors' USING BY VALUE nDspElements
                                                                 BY
REFERENCE DspElements
                                                                 BY
REFERENCE RgbValues

Hopefully that should give you a start point. Note that I put this together
from looking briefly at the Win32 SDK, so I can't guarantee that it is
accurate.



Bob7536 wrote in message <19981014112637.11792.00000566@ng73.aol.com>...
>Good Morning,
>
>I am new to Win95 API programming and have been working with a few samples
that
>people have been kind to send.
>
>I am trying to use a new API and am unsure how to properly code this.  I
would
>like to use the SetSysColors API to change the COLOR_BACKGROUND (color of
>windows desktop) to black.
>
>I am not sure how to setup the three parameters that go with this API.
They
>are int nDspElements, CONST INT * lpnDspElements and CONST COLORREF *
>lpdwRgbValues.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Hennessey
```

### COM and COBOL

- **Message-ID:** `<702ias$esp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Can someone please tell me if COBOL is or can be COM enabled?
Please reply to this address: mitchell_l_land@uhc.com

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: COM and COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NB5V1.823$9R2.1355295@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<702ias$esp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
mitchell@inlink.com wrote:
>Can someone please tell me if COBOL is or can be COM enabled?

That would depend on the COBOL compiler.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COM and COBOL

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<704dln$tcu@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<702ias$esp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <NB5V1.823$9R2.1355295@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>mitchell@inlink.com wrote:
>>Can someone please tell me if COBOL is or can be COM enabled?
>
>That would depend on the COBOL compiler.

Micro Focus NetExpress provides high-level run-time/syntactical support 
for both COM automation and ActiveX controls. This will include dual 
interface support in automation servers from NetExpress 3.0.

COM itself is largely language-independant, although heavily based on the 
design of C++. Any language that can export a pointer to a table of 
virtual functions (a 'vtable'), or can dereference such a table when given 
a pointer in order to call the functions, and can supply the appropriate 
calling conventions and parameter types should be able to use COM. It 
might help if the language is OO-based, as this makes handling of multiple 
objects/vtables easier.

Micro Focus COBOL is able to do all of that, but you need a reasonable 
understanding of COM (and COBOL) to be able to do this. We provide a class 
library, to enable the calling of COM interfaces, in the NetExpress 
product in addition to the high-level support I mentioned above. Both 
require some knowledge of OO (Object) COBOL, for which we provide several 
tools within the product.

NB: This discussion doesn't include binding to COM type libraries, which 
*is* largely a compiler/linker issue.

Steve.
Micro Focus Development.
```

## Thread: Home Cobol — 4 msgs

### Home Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3624D6A1.26B0FECC@home.com>`

```
I am thinking about getting a version of Cobol for home use.

I have had over 20 years experience with Cobol and databases on main
frame computers, but will finish my masters in object-oriented
methodologies in a couple of weeks. 

I plan on converting one of my peer-to-peer computers at home into a
server (haven't decided on OS yet) this winter, but would like to play
around with a PC based COBOL (and put it on my resume).

I once played with a beta version of Visual Age, but didn't "get" it. 
Maybe the Cobol version would be easier.  I have used Java with Visual
Cafe, and a couple of versions of C++ during my masters.

I could pick a non-gui based COBOL which is easy (for me) to use, or get
one which can do more tasks on my PC and LAN, or get OO experience. 
Please advise.
```

#### ↳ Re: Home Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<702so3$g@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3624D6A1.26B0FECC@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3624D6A1.26B0FECC@home.com>...
>I am thinking about getting a version of Cobol for home use.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Please advise.

I am certain that others will comment on this, but I think your 3 best bets
are:

 -  IBM VisualAge COBOL (most similar to CURRENT IBM mainframe COBOL - with
and without OO - fairly pricey)
 -  Micro Focus COBOL (either NetExpress or MainframeExpress if you want to
spend a lot of money - or Personal COBOL if you don't need to product
"production" code)
 -  Fujitsu COBOL (V3.0 can be downloaded for free, V4 is available for
purchase)
 - (I tend to "ignore" AcuCOBOL for this type of use - but others may
recommend it instead)

Several of these are available on CD's with some of the current COBOL books.
I would strongly suggest that you get one of the cheap/stripped-down
versions first - to see if it meets your desires/needs.
```

<a id="t-3ad0c1f76a"></a>
## Thread: Logical vs. Physical Cancel in MF Cobol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Logical vs. Physical Cancel in MF Cobol

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981014091916.23889.00000514@ng25.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<70260t$squ$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

I am not familliar with the specific compiler you are using, yet I have an idea
of what the problem is.

CANCEL only guarantees that data memory will be reset to the initial values. 
So if you had a subroutine B and had called it once or many times, iterating
through various loops.  You could reset all loop controls and all of data
memory with the CANCEL command in the higher level program which called it.

However, CANCEL does not reload the executable procedure division. There may be
an IDE command or debugger command that can do that. But CANCEL does not, and I
do not know of a COBOL verb that would.

Incremental compile and link may be built into your tools, but it does not
surface at the syntax level of the procedure division.

So you may wish to check that your recompile of program B actually entails a
relink.  Some development tools hace a Rebuild menu option. I don't know your
compiler, but you must rebuild the .EXE file not just recompile the submodule;
and then coerce a reload of the executables.


Hope that Helps,
 


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Logical vs. Physical Cancel in MF Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-bUVMnncfSdLN@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<70260t$squ$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981014091916.23889.00000514@ng25.aol.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 Oct 1998 13:19:16, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:

> CANCEL only guarantees that data memory will be reset to the initial values. 
> So if you had a subroutine B and had called it once or many times, iterating
> through various loops.  You could reset all loop controls and all of data
> memory with the CANCEL command in the higher level program which called it.
> 

In actuality, you are not even guarenteed that - though most compilers
do, and many actually unload from memory - all you are guarenteed is 
that the items in WS with a VALUE clause are reset to that value.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Logical vs. Physical Cancel in MF Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<702so4$g@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70260t$squ$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <19981014091916.23889.00000514@ng25.aol.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-bUVMnncfSdLN@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Wed, 14 Oct 1998 13:19:16, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:
>
>> CANCEL only guarantees that data memory will be reset to the initial
values.
>> So if you had a subroutine B and had called it once or many times,
iterating
>> through various loops.  You could reset all loop controls and all of data
>> memory with the CANCEL command in the higher level program which called
it.
>>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A difference between the '74 and '85 Standard is that in the '85 Standard,
you ARE guaranteed that all PERFORM ranges will be "reset" by a CANCEL.
(The times that this can cause a difference are pretty obscure - but happen
when you GO TO an EXIT PROGRAM within a PERFORMED paragraph of a subroutine
and then recall it and some how hit the exit of the PERFORM range without it
being currently PERFORMED.  As I say, this is pretty obscure, but is a
documented difference between the two Standards.)

P.S.  Release of storage is definitely NOT required by the Standard.
```

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Wed, 14 Oct 1998 15:19:59, jim hurd <jhurd@adelphia.net> wrote:

> Are there any alternatives to Micro Focus?
> 

Fujitsu
RM
AccuCobol
CA-Realia

However, if you want a CURRENTLY supported DOS compiler I think the 
only games in town are RM (Liant) and AccuCOBOL.  I personally think 
that the best available conversion from MF is to Fujitsu, but it's 
Windows or Unix only.  If you need to support some other platform, 
AccuCobol looks like it has the most currently supported platforms - 
if you can stomach the runtime fees.  I think the recent MicroFocus 
decisions will be the virtual end to their COBOL "dominance" but they 
may not care.  They now have other fish to fry.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need Cobol w/installable file handler

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8p6V1.832$9R2.1383443@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3624C11F.C1BD903D@adelphia.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-5F3QvCHoZOpg@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>...
>I think the recent MicroFocus
>decisions will be the virtual end to their COBOL "dominance" but they
>may not care.  They now have other fish to fry.

Around 1980 I was part owner of a local computer store when Apple started
playing hard-ball with their dealers and customers.  At the time, Apple
was the Big Cheese of the Microcomputer industry, and had the most loyal
customer base of just about any company.  I said then "Apple, it may take
a while, but you are going to pay for this Big Time."  Recently Apple was
bailed out by arch nemesis Bill Gates.  Can you imagine the chagrin this
caused among Apple employees?  I see the same thing coming for Micro Focus.
There will come a day when they would dearly love to see us independent
developers buying their products, but nobody is going to be interested.
```

## Thread: A question about Assembler and COOL GEN — 6 msgs

### A question about Assembler and COOL GEN

- **From:** "Bruce Engel" <brucee@bestnet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdf88e$cd8175e0$403740ce@lexus.bestnet.com>`

```
Hi My name is Jill Matthews with a company called BEST Consulting.  I am
supposed to find COBOL people who have Assembler and COOL GEN but I am not
sure what those skills are and what they are used for.
Can anyone help me.
Thanks.

I can be reached at jillm@bestnet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: A question about Assembler and COOL GEN

- **From:** "rmch" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3626b291.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<01bdf88e$cd8175e0$403740ce@lexus.bestnet.com>`

```
If you are a recruiter, you better find out what those skills are... fast!!!


Bruce Engel wrote in message
<01bdf88e$cd8175e0$403740ce@lexus.bestnet.com>...
>Hi My name is Jill Matthews with a company called BEST Consulting.  I am
>supposed to find COBOL people who have Assembler and COOL GEN but I am not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I can be reached at jillm@bestnet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: A question about Assembler and COOL GEN

- **From:** "Noel P.C. Ng" <noelng@mail.cibc.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362741F6.B50C069C@mail.cibc.com>`
- **References:** `<01bdf88e$cd8175e0$403740ce@lexus.bestnet.com>`

```
COBOL is a high level business programming language.

Assembler is a low-level everything programming language. If you know
assembler, you can probably program in just about any language.

COOLGEN is a case/code generation tool (formerly IEF by Texas Instruments)
that generates gibberish COBOL.

Noel Ng
Consultant, Toronto.

Bruce Engel wrote:

> Hi My name is Jill Matthews with a company called BEST Consulting.  I am
> supposed to find COBOL people who have Assembler and COOL GEN but I am not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I can be reached at jillm@bestnet.com
```

```
> 
> Assembler is a low-level everything programming language. If you know

'Assembler' is hundreds of low-level languages.

> assembler, you can probably program in just about any language.

If you know every assembler you are already programming in
a fair proportion of 'any language'.

But to extrapolate to 'just about any language' is rather
misleading, just like 'real programmers can write FORTRAN
programs in any language'.
```

<a id="t-7bb005a505"></a>
## Thread: OT - Cherry Tree Hearings Transcript — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Cherry Tree Hearings Transcript

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.lang.basic,comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<lanV1.1559$lp3.1832280@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Released this morning for the first time is the complete transcript
of the Cherry Tree Hearings. Due to the nature of the content, reader
discretion is advised. Parental discretion is also advised.  In fact,
discretion of all kinds is generally a good idea for everyone.

"George?"

"Yes, father."

"George, I have a very serious question to ask you and I want you to
promise you'll answer truthfully.  Will you?"

"Yes, father."

"Good.  Now here is the question.  Did you cut down my cherry tree?"

"No, father"

"You're quite sure?"

"Yes, father."

"Well, I'm afraid I'm very disappointed in you, George."

"Why, father?"

"Because 12 people saw you cut down the cherry tree with your little
hatchet."

"Oh."

"In view of that, would you like to change your previous answer,
George?"

"No, father.  I believe the answer I gave you was legally accurate."

"You still insist you were telling me the truth?"

"In my own mind I was telling you the truth, yes father."

"What the [deleted] is that supposed to mean?"

"Well, you asked me if I had 'cut' down the tree.  In my own mind, it
seemed to me that 'cutting' is something one does with a knife or a
sickle.  In my In my own mind it seemed that, since I used my little
hatchet, the relationship I had with the tree, while perhaps
inappropriate, was not a 'cutting' relationship. I would call it a
'chopping' relationship."

"Very well. I'll give you another chance, George. Listen very
carefully.  Did you chop down my cherry tree?"

"No, father."

"No?  No?  Why do you still say no?"

"Because, father, I cannot tell a lie.  And in my own mind I did not
chop down' your cherry tree."

"Well, what did you do, then?"

"I chopped it into two pieces and one piece fell to the ground."

"So you chopped it down."

"No, father, I merely chopped it. I did not cause that piece to fall
down. The force of gravity caused it to fall down. Were it not for
the force of gravity, over which I have absolutely no control, the
tree, though segmented, would presumably still be up, not down."

"George, I'm losing patience with you. But I'm going to give you one
last chance to tell the truth. Did you take your little hatchet and
chop my cherry tree, which action on your part, combined with the
force of gravity, caused the tree to fall down?"

"No, father."

"NO?  NO? IT'S STILL NO?  HOW CAN YOU STILL SAY NO?"

"I still say no because of my legendary regard for the truth,
father.  What is that object at which I am pointing with my childish
little finger?"

"It's the stump of the cherry tree you cut down."

"And isn't the stump part of the tree, father?"

"It sure is."

"In fact, isn't the stump the most important part of the tree,
father, since, without a stump there would be no tree?"

"I guess so."

"Yet the stump is still standing. So when you asked me if I had
chopped down the tree, my own mind said to me, 'George, you must tell
the truth.  And the truthful answer is no. You chopped, gravity
caused part of the tree to fall down yet the most important part of
the tree is still standing.'"

"I see."

"All I can suppose, father, is that those 12 people whose exaggerated
claims allege they saw me 'cut down' the entire 'tree' were motivated
not by a search for truth but by some personal vendetta against me,
perhaps because I am from Virginia."

"George, you're a real crafty [expletive deleted.]"

"Thank you father."

"Have you thought about what you want to be when you grow up?"

"Yes, father. If they ever build a White House I would like to occupy
it as the first White House lawyer."

(P.S. - I don't know who wrote this.)
```

- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3626DAA1.CFF8C531@att.net>`
- **References:** `<lanV1.1559$lp3.1832280@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

Bill Lynch
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3626F76F.5717@bellsouth.net>`

```
   (snippage of Slick George's testimony)

At least I understood what George said.  When I watched Bubba's
```

## Thread: URGENT HELP: Monthly salary report — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### URGENT HELP: Monthly salary report

- **From:** BMAN6 <BMAN6@student.monash.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<703sv5$b05$1@towncrier.cc.monash.edu.au>`

```
I am a junior programmer and currently my company is trying to compare
the use of COBOL to other languages.  I have already done a few sample 
programs, however I am having trouble doing a Monthly Salary report.

Currently we use a database for this and it works fine, however to prove 
that COBOl is capable of performing such functions I would like to 
produce a simple program to prove that COBOL has features to match other 
languages such as DBASE, Borland DELPHI, C, VB etc.

If you can spend a bit of your time to help me produce such a simple 
program or have something similar please reply to me ASAP!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: URGENT HELP: Monthly salary report

- **From:** Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36416600.136653682@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<703sv5$b05$1@towncrier.cc.monash.edu.au>`

```
On 15 Oct 1998 04:16:37 GMT BMAN6 <BMAN6@student.monash.edu.au> wrote:

:>the use of COBOL to other languages.  I have already done a few sample 
:>programs, however I am having trouble doing a Monthly Salary report.

Why?

If your company is happy with their languages and is satisfied without using
COBOL why would you want to introduce a new language that you are not even
familiar with?

I assume the company is paying you to do something. Why not do your job?

:>If you can spend a bit of your time to help me produce such a simple 
:>program or have something similar please reply to me ASAP!!!

If you are willing to enter a contract and pay a consulting fee, sure.
```

### report generator

- **From:** schelhaas@tip.nl
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<352bdefb.2540426@news.tip.nl>`

```
Is there anybody who knows a (freeware) report generator for MF COBOL
```

#### ↳ Re: report generator

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pQ2W1.88$IF6.949169@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<352bdefb.2540426@news.tip.nl>`

```
>Is there anybody who knows a (freeware) report generator for MF COBOL

Do you have acess to Microsoft ACCESS? You could generate a sequential file
from COBOL and inport it into ACCESS as a table and use its report
generating feature to make yourself a report.

Not sure if its the answer you are looking for, but I try to give an obvious
answer first.
```

<a id="t-42f6f7d696"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus COBOL in Unix Environment — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus COBOL in Unix Environment

- **From:** andyho99@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<705700$4jt$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi,

We just converted MF Cobol from Mainframe to Unix environment. We found that
we have to recordize(add new line character) or unrecordize (remove new line
character) files before or after Cobol programs. For example, one Cobol
program need to read a sorted file. I had to recordize that file and use Unix
sort command to sort, then unrecordize the file and feed to Cobol program to
process.

Is there a way to avoid this recordize and unrecordize processing?

Thanks, Andy

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL in Unix Environment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<705jtm$413@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<705700$4jt$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I don't have the exact information handy, but I think that there is a
FILETYPE directive that you can use to avoid this problem. (I assume you are
already using ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL)

andyho99@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<705700$4jt$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Hi,
>
>We just converted MF Cobol from Mainframe to Unix environment. We found
that
>we have to recordize(add new line character) or unrecordize (remove new
line
>character) files before or after Cobol programs. For example, one Cobol
>program need to read a sorted file. I had to recordize that file and use
Unix
>sort command to sort, then unrecordize the file and feed to Cobol program
to
>process.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL in Unix Environment

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<706uma$oo0@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<705700$4jt$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

Hello Andy,

There is a directive SEQUENTIAL that specifies the default file type for
sequential access files.

I think it may be SEQUENTIAL"LINE" or SEQUENTIAL"RECORD" that you need to
use.

Regards
David.


andyho99@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<705700$4jt$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...

Unix
```

```
Hi,

I am searching for a old IBM System/360.        
Who can help me ???     

I am mainly interested in a model at the lower end of 
the S/360 range, a model 30 would match my wishes perfectly.

and with all hardware documentation. Location is no problem.

hardware.

THANKS
```

<a id="t-94fb8b7419"></a>
## Thread: Tuxedo, Fujitsu Cobol & SP2 on a Unisys Clearpath — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Tuxedo, Fujitsu Cobol & SP2 on a Unisys Clearpath

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)
- **Date:** 1998-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36258edb.17160578@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca>`

```
I posted an article about our problems few days ago.  I just would
like to mention that the problem is now fixed thru the help of Andy
from Flexus International (makers of SP2).  Tuxedo is not even there
product yet they took time to give us a hand.  There are companies out
there that won't even talk to you if it's not there product.  Thank
you very much Andy and Bob Wolfe for the help.

The problem was the way we coded the C DLL tuxedo routine.  Tuxedo,
Fujitsu Cobol and SP2 are now working together to communicate with the
Unisys Clearpath mainframe (Unisys 2200).  Our next move is to use the
SP2 thin client and last web technology.

Are there other people out there doing the same thing or similar?  If
so, please email me or post to this group.  It would be nice if we
could exchange ideas.

Tony M. Mina
jets@nbnet.nb.ca
```

---

<a id="t-dbd5027eaf"></a>
## Thread: Implied decimal in amount field — 12 msgs

### Implied decimal in amount field

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3626F132.286F@bellsouth.net>`

```
My program reads a file with an amount field of PIC 9(8)V99 in the
record.  I list any records with amounts over $9,999.99.  

I changed the input field to PIC 9(10) and if the input amount was
greater than 999999, I moved it to the edited report line, which was
WRONG.  Then I set up a working-storge field, WS-AMT  PIC 9(8)V99, moved
the INPUT-AMT  PIC 9(10) to WS-AMT, and moved that to the edited field,
which was WRONG.  (Is it even legal to move to a field with an implied
decimal??)

I'm wondering about a redefines on the input amount field,

05  INPUT-AMT      PIC 9(10).
05  INPUT-AMT-R REDEFINES INPUT-AMT
                   PIC 9(8)V99.

05  EDITED-AMT     PIC ZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.99.

IF INPUT-AMT GREATER THAN 999999
    MOVE INPUT-AMT-R TO  EDITED-AMT.

- OR - Could I do this:

05  INPUT-AMT      PIC 9(8)V99.
05  INPUT-AMT-X REDEFINES INPUT-AMT
                   PIC X(10).

IF INPUT-AMT-X GREATER THAN '999999'
    MOVE INPUT-AMT TO EDITED-AMT.

- OR - what??

kitty
```

#### ↳ Re: Implied decimal in amount field

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gGV1.26087$lp3.2402368@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3626F132.286F@bellsouth.net>`

```
Kitty Carr wrote:
>My program reads a file with an amount field of PIC 9(8)V99 in the
>record.  I list any records with amounts over $9,999.99.

Kitty, you answered your own question right there.  If the input data
is formatted as 9(8)V99, then use that for the PICTURE.  Then compare
it to numeric literal 9999.99.

   05  INPUT-AMT      PIC 9(8)V99.

   05  EDITED-AMT     PIC ZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.99.

   IF INPUT-AMT GREATER THAN 9999.99
       MOVE INPUT-AMT TO  EDITED-AMT.

Sure, you can move to fields with assumed decimal points.  If you
couldn't, we would all be a lot of trouble. :-)

P.S. -I know you have ordered it already, but for your info, the
      Books-A-Million at Eastwood Mall has "COBOL Unleashed".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Implied decimal in amount field

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36276CB7.68F5@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<3626F132.286F@bellsouth.net> <8gGV1.26087$lp3.2402368@news2.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Kitty Carr wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>        MOVE INPUT-AMT TO  EDITED-AMT.

This is the one way I DIDN'T try.  I said greater than 999999 (without
the decimal point); I thought if I wrote greater than 9999.99, I'd get
an error when it hit the period, thinking (thinking?) it was the end of
the statement.  I mean, unless it was in quotes.  And that's what I get
for thinking . . .

Thanks.
```

```
> My program reads a file with an amount field of PIC 9(8)V99 in the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> decimal??)
```

#### ↳ Re: Implied decimal in amount field

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00

```
On Fri, 16 Oct 1998 05:15:16, Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> My program reads a file with an amount field of PIC 9(8)V99 in the
> record.  I list any records with amounts over $9,999.99.  
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Let's look at this a second.  If the actual value of input-amt is 
888,888.99

It is NOT greater than 999999 but you will still move it because 
Input-Amt will equal 88888899.  Instead:

If Input-Amt-R > 999999 
   Move Input-Amt-R to Edited-Amt.

Should work fine.
```

```
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>There is nothing wrong with what you have here.


The original post is mostly gone by now, but the problem is because
Kitty needed to test for > 9999.99, not > 999999.  I believe she was
concerned that the actual decimal point in the literal would not compare
properly with the assumed decimal point in the PIC, and that is why
she was trying those other forms.
```

#### ↳ Re: Implied decimal in amount field

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1998-10-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<709v0o$fg_001@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<3626F132.286F@bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <3626F132.286F@bellsouth.net>,
   Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net> wrote:
+My program reads a file with an amount field of PIC 9(8)V99 in the
+record.  I list any records with amounts over $9,999.99.  
+
There have already been several very good responses to your question,
so I'll omit duplicating the effort here. But there was something else
that I felt should be pointed out.
[snip]
+
+- OR - Could I do this:
+
+05  INPUT-AMT      PIC 9(8)V99.
+05  INPUT-AMT-X REDEFINES INPUT-AMT
+                   PIC X(10).
+
+IF INPUT-AMT-X GREATER THAN '999999'
+    MOVE INPUT-AMT TO EDITED-AMT.
+
This will certainly _not_ work. An alphanumeric comparison between two 
operands of different lengths proceeds as if the shorter one is padded on the 
right with spaces to make it the same length as the longer one. Hence, the IF 
statement you propose is equivalent to
	IF INPUT-AMT-X GREATER THAN '999999~~~~' (~ = space)
If the numeric value of INPUT-AMT is, say, 10000.00, this will result in
comparing "0001000000" with "999999~~~~" and the program will conclude that
the second operand is greater than the first -- clearly not what you want.

IF INPUT-AMT-X GREATER THAN '0000999999' would produce the desired result.
But IF INPUT-AMT GREATER THAN 9999.99 is more clear and hence preferable.
```

### What happened to Column 8???

- **From:** skyraven@home.com (Sue L)

```
Ok... here's the situation.. I attempted to open up a source code file
in Fujitsu and yes, it opened however, it opened up in column 9, which
it was not set for. When I tried pulling it back to Column 8 it
wouldn't let me as if Column 8 didn't exist.  Everything is totally
thrown out of whack. Has anyone ever experienced this before and if so
what was done to fix it? I'm very curious about this.  Any ideas??

Sue
```

#### ↳ Re: What happened to Column 8???

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-3hn6fzu9J5Dw@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<36278425.2033727@news>`

```
On Fri, 16 Oct 1998 17:35:20, skyraven@home.com (Sue L) wrote:

> Ok... here's the situation.. I attempted to open up a source code file
> in Fujitsu and yes, it opened however, it opened up in column 9, which
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> what was done to fix it? I'm very curious about this.  Any ideas??


Let's see....

Open the file in question.
Click on Options
Click on Setup Environment for POWERFRAMEVIEW
Click the SET button for the second selection - File Information
Click the PowerFRAMEVIEW editor tab
Change the file extension to match the one you are having trouble 
with.
(ie COB or CBL)
Change the line number length to 6 - bet it got changed to 8 or 
something like that on you.

(Just reduce it by at least one)

Save all the settings, close and reopen the file - see if that fixes 
it up.  Let me know - this is guesswork at this point.
```

```
On Sun, 18 Oct 1998 13:11:43 -0500 ""Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>" posted to
"comp.lang.cobol" about: "Re: What happened to Column 8???" :

-->Adrian Boldan wrote in message ...
-->>Sue L (skyraven@home.com) wrote:
-->>: wouldn't let me as if Column 8 didn't exist.  Everything is totally
-->>: thrown out of whack. Has anyone ever experienced this before and if so
-->>: what was done to fix it? I'm very curious about this.  Any ideas??
-->>
-->>Use a DECENT DOS editor and shift the whole file.
-->
-->
-->A simple and straightforward DOS Cobol editor can be found at
-->http://www.etk.com/download/index.htm

A powerful simple text editor I use is UltraEdit (www.ultraedit.com).  It has
too many useful features to list and is good for a number of languages in
addition to Cobol.  I started with it in .html, but it has syntax highliting
already set up for C and C++, etc.  I find this to be a very useful feature.
Cobol syntax hightliting was not yet setup by default but there is a simple text
file to edit it in (now supporting up to 10 or 15 langs total) and even I was
able to accomplish this easily, esp. due to the adequate help file.

It has bookmark features which higlite one or more lines and you change to them
with F-2.  I put thin vertical column markers on 8 and 71.  Newer versions have
autoindenting on CR.  Also a column editing feature that is not quite as
powerful as I would like:  wish it allowed multiple inserts (sounds like what
you are looking for too) as well as multiple deletes.....  Etc.

UEdit is not free ware, but a time limited shareware.  Not too  expensive.


"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
++++++*******Dontcha*just*hate*this*antispamming*stuff??********++++++
*********No generalization is wholly true, not even this one**********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```
