```
On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 12:45:18 -0400, Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>>> Take a look at the inspect converting instead.  Much easier.
>>>      inspect full-name converting "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz" to "ABCD
>...
>>
>>Thanks Donald. I was trying to remember that command and for some reason I

>Funny, I have been using an assembler routine for years ... did not even
>know the "converting" option existed.  Read about it in this echo about
>six months back, and was quite delighted with it.

Wouldn't it be nice if companies would give their programmers a week or two to
read through the new manuals for each release?

As a side question, I've noticed a number of people using a hardcoded alphabet.
How many people actually have that in their code? I've been using two WS items
for alphabets (e.g. INSPECT CONVERTING LowerCase TO UpperCase) and was
wondering how common this was. I figure it's easier to change two variables in
a copybook if I ever need to support accents or some weird spec than searching
through all my programs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Lower Case To Upper Case in COBOLII

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362e6f16.39982371@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-2010981903230001@u29.dialup.seanet.com>`

```
On Tue, 20 Oct 1998 19:03:22 -0700, akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent) wrote:

>I have, I believe, a rather simple question that (hopefully) someone can
>help me with.  We have an application in which the names of customers were
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Arthur M. Kent

COBOL II doesn't have an UPPERCASE-TRANSLATION function. You can :

a) write a small assembler program that will do this for you, just OC the field in
question with SPACES. (possibly the fastest way)

b) INSPECT name-field CONVERTING 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
                                                       TO 'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'

c) INSPECT name-field REPLACING 'a'  WITH 'A'  'b' WITH 'B' ......
                                                            

d) migrate to COBOL for MVS & VM, or COBOL/390, which has FUNCTION UPPER-CASE

HTH

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Lower Case To Upper Case in COBOLII

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70js2p$n7d$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-2010981903230001@u29.dialup.seanet.com>`

```

Arthur M. Kent wrote in message ...

>I would appreciate some assistance as to how one can "convert" lower case
>to upper case using COBOL II.


There are two ways.  There is and intrinsic function (TOUPPER). (There
is also a TOLOWER, I believe, though I have never used it).  The inspect
verb can also do it.

   Inspect dataname converting "abcdedfghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz"
                                               to
"ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ".
```

## Thread: microfocus 88-level validation probl — 36 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com>`

```
Again, suppose you have (in working storage)

       01  Data-Record      OCCURS 20.
            03  Alpha-Item          PIC X.
            03  Num-Item            Pic 9.

and you want to initialize it (spaces for alpha, and zeroes for numeric).
How can you do it?  There is no way to INITIALIZE the entire record, because
it doesn't have any name.  Similarly, you can't even MOVE SPACES to the
entire record - or do anything else where the COBOL verb requires a
data-name (or identifier) as an operand.


Howard Brazee wrote in message <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com>...
>There are environments which allow OCCURS on 01 levels in Working
>Storage.  The results there are not confusing at all.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>> or WRITE Data-Record(n) is done ?
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com>`

```


William M. Klein wrote:

> Again, suppose you have (in working storage)
>
>        01  Data-Record      OCCURS 20.
>             03  Alpha-Item          PIC X.
>             03  Num-Item            Pic 9.

That is true Mr Klein.
How about:

         01 Data-Record.
              02 Data-Item occurs 20 times.
                    03 alpha-item         pic x.
                    03 num-item           pic 9.

You can INITIALIZE this with: initialize data-record.


>
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> or WRITE Data-Record(n) is done ?

Jeff Farkas wrote:
>
>William M. Klein wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>You can INITIALIZE this with: initialize data-record.

That illustrates a couple of points, Jeff.  One is why some of us are
opposed to permitting OCCURS at the 01 level.  The other is why we
use non-consecutive numbers for group levels; it lets you add a level
when necessary. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

- **From:** "BR-549" <dsrush@webzone.net>

- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362d5ce8.0@wznews.webzone.net>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70jeat$ci2$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Also in Cobol 85 you can assign values  to the table variables IN working-storage    

01  SOME-TABLE.
      05  SOME-TABLE-RECORD            OCCURS 17 TIMES.
            10     STR-NAME                       PIC X(30)     VALUE SPACES.
             10    STR-AGE                          PIC 9(3) COMP-3 VALUE 0.

This initializes the table to spaces and zeroes immediately.  Of course if you want to reinitialize it again then - INITIALIZE SOME-TABLE-RECORD does the same thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362DFCD4.97BE4A98@home.com>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70jeat$ci2$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362d5ce8.0@wznews.webzone.net>`

```
> Also in Cobol 85 you can assign values  to the table variables IN
> working-storage
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> SOME-TABLE-RECORD does the same thing.

Are you sure you don't mean INITIALIZE SOME-TABLE?  
Anyway, if your only initializing is at compile time, you shouldn't need
the third group level (except for a compiler limitation).

Of course many compilers also allow REDEFINES at the 01 level:

01  MONTH-TABLE  OCCURS 12.
    05  MONTH-NAME  PIC X(3).
    05  MONTH-NUM   PIC 9(02).
01  MONTH-REDEFINES  REDEFINES MONTH-TABLE PIC X(60) VALUE
     'JAN01FEB02MAR03APR04MAY05JUN06JUL07AUG08SEP09OCT10NOV11DEC12'.

I'm not saying which code is best.  I'm saying it is irritating when I
switch compilers or do conversion, and a technique which worked fine
before no longer works.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fqoX1.737$Se2.1338485@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70jeat$ci2$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362d5ce8.0@wznews.webzone.net> <362DFCD4.97BE4A98@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>> Also in Cobol 85 you can assign values  to the table variables IN
>> working-storage
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>     'JAN01FEB02MAR03APR04MAY05JUN06JUL07AUG08SEP09OCT10NOV11DEC12'.

The VALUE clause must be on the first 01, and you cannot redefine an
item containing an OCCURS clause. :-)  I prefer this form:

01  MONTH-TABLE        VALUE
     'JAN01FEB02MAR03APR04MAY05JUN06JUL07AUG08SEP09OCT10NOV11DEC12'.
    03  MONTH-ENTRY    OCCURS 12 TIMES.
        05  MONTH-NAME    PIC X(3).
        05  MONTH-NUM     PIC 9(02).

But I do not use an 01 for tables like yours, for a very good reason
which you may agree with.  After correcting your example:

01  MONTH-VALUES      PIC X(60) VALUE
     'JAN01FEB02MAR03APR04MAY05JUN06JUL07AUG08SEP09OCT10NOV11DEC12'.
01  MONTH-TABLE       REDEFINES MONTH-VALUES.
    03  MONTH-ENTRY   OCCURS 12.
        05  MONTH-NAME    PIC X(3).
        05  MONTH-NUM     PIC 9(02).

Please note that if you make a mistake and the two 01 items are not
declared the same size, the compiler won't complain, because it is
legal to do so at the 01 level.  I never do it myself, but have been
zapped by this in other people's code.  But if you declare it at a
subordinate level as in the following, the compiler should flag it
if the redefining item is larger than the redefined item.  I would
prefer that the compiler flag it any time the size is different.

01  MONTH-TABLE.
    03  MONTH-VALUES  PIC X(60) VALUE
       'JAN01FEB02MAR03APR04MAY05JUN06JUL07AUG08SEP09OCT10NOV11DEC12'.
    03  MONTH-TABLE   REDEFINES MONTH-VALUES  OCCURS 12.
        05  MONTH-NAME  PIC X(3).
        05  MONTH-NUM   PIC 9(02).
```

>Working-Storage)  - with some minor restrictions on things like not

- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70jeat$ci2$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <362d5ce8.0@wznews.webzone.net>`

```
On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 03:55:22, "BR-549" <dsrush@webzone.net> wrote:

> This is a multi-part message in MIME format.
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I want to clarify something here.  I *think* you are jumping to a 
conclusion, that the VALUE clause has something to do with what goes 
in the field when you use INITIALIZE.  Let's examine a clearer 
example.

01 Group-Item.
      03  Elementary-Item Pic X(5) Value "ABCDE".

If you execute this statement:

INITIALIZE GROUP-ITEM

What do you think is in Elementary-Item?

Not ABCDE -  it will be SPACES.
```

>On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 03:55:22, "BR-549" <dsrush@webzone.net> wrote:
>
>> This is a multi-part message in MIME format.
>>
>> Also in Cobol 85 you can assign values  to the table variables IN
working-storage
>>
>> 01  SOME-TABLE.
>>       05  SOME-TABLE-RECORD            OCCURS 17 TIMES.
>>             10     STR-NAME                       PIC X(30)     VALUE
SPACES.
>>              10    STR-AGE                          PIC 9(3) COMP-3 VALUE
0.
>>
>> This initializes the table to spaces and zeroes immediately.  Of course
if you want to reinitialize it again then - INITIALIZE SOME-TABLE-RECORD
does the same thing.
>>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Usual "plug for the new Standard".

   INITIALIZE TO VALUE

INITIALIZE - I am not certain, but I think some compilers already allow this
as an extension.)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362DF939.F5415A4B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
> That illustrates a couple of points, Jeff.  One is why some of us are
> opposed to permitting OCCURS at the 01 level.  The other is why we
> use non-consecutive numbers for group levels; it lets you add a level
> when necessary. :-)

Except one is a mandate, and the other is a recommendation.  Try
converting from a liberal cobol compiler to a conservative one, and you
will wish that the new one allowed what the old one does.

When I write code, I always have 01, 05, 10, 15 levels.  But when I am
converting code, I don't translate someone else's 03 to my 05.

Although I remember once when I was converting from small Burroughs to
large Burroughs, and I had to translate PC to PIC !!!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70k1u6$92$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>That illustrates a couple of points, Jeff.  One is why some of us are
>opposed to permitting OCCURS at the 01 level.  The other is why we
>use non-consecutive numbers for group levels; it lets you add a level
>when necessary. :-)


I can understand why you would avoid it, but I with the exception
of the file section, I cannot see why it should be a compiler error.
I had code that I recently converted, and I was forced to change

01 this-is-an-array-i-need        picture 99 occurs 10 times.
to
01 this-is-a-data-name-i-do-not-need.
     02 this-is-the-data-name-i-really-need    picture 99 occurs 10 times.

I really cannot see that it makes the code "better" in any way. In fact,
it obscures it.  I'll bet you are against 01 fillers as well, though that
alleviate the same problem.  I find it annoying that I cannot do

01 line-for-printing.
     05 layout1-etc                picture (repeated for columns).
01 filler  redefines line-for-printing.
     05 layout2-etc ...
01 filler redefines line-for-printing

Instead, I have to make up names for the 01 levels that serve no usefull
purpose beyond taking up symbol table space, and making the code less
clear.  There are cases where you DO NOT WANT an 01 level name
used for initialization, or anything else.  By not giving extra names that
are not needed, you avoid some latter person fiddling about.

You also avoid triggering checks by CREF listings that flag unused
names, etc.  Mind you, you have a valid point with

fd file
data record is what-the-heck(?).
01 what-the-heck    picture 9 occurs 10 times.
and
write figure-this-out.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GYjX1.676$Se2.1210192@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70k1u6$92$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>write figure-this-out.

I disagree with you on the not needing an 01 name.  I think the 01 name
is very important, and I use it in my overall data naming methods.  I
name all subordinate data names with a prefix derived from the 01 name,
often an acronym.  This permits me to immediately recognize where a field
is defined when I see it.  And because I always use the exact name for a
given field across the entire system, except for the prefix, it also
allows me to unambiguously name fields on the fly without having to look
them up.  To assist in this I create an abbreviation dictionary as I
design the system, permitting consistent data field naming by other
programmers working on the same system, even later.

Also, since you agree with me on OCCURS for 01s in the File Section, do
you think it is good to have a conflicting standard?  I believe that
would cause confusion.  Yes, you cannot use REDEFINES for an 01 in the
File Section, but that is because the REDEFINES is implicit, no feature
loss is involved.  And though you cannot use VALUE in the file section
either, neither can you use VALUE in any item with or subordinate to a
redefines anywhere else.  And there is very good reason not to use VALUE
in the File Section.  Languages have an implicit philosophy, and it is a
very good idea to make all enhancements to the language within that
philosophy.  In the philosophy of COBOL, the 01 record has a very special
place, and I believe that permitting OCCURS at the 01 level would be a
violation of that philosophy.  And don't forget, doing so would add *no*
capability to the language, it would only permit a slightly abbreviated
form at the expense of other important capabilities like INITIALIZE and
REDEFINES, and consistency with the File Section.  At least, that's my
opinion. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70l4hd$7r7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70k1u6$92$1@news.igs.net> <GYjX1.676$Se2.1210192@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Donald Tees wrote:
>>
>>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>>

>I disagree with you on the not needing an 01 name.  I think the 01 name
>is very important, and I use it in my overall data naming methods.  I
>name all subordinate data names with a prefix derived from the 01 name,
>often an acronym.  This permits me to immediately recognize where a field


My mother used to say "you cannot legislate good taste", and I tend to
agree with her.  Personally, I choose my names to make the code as
english-like as possible.  I dislike prefixes, and use the *same* name
for the same field in various places, then use references, so the code
reads
MOVE CUSTOMER-NUMBER of CUSTOMER-RECORD to
CUSTOMER-NUMBER of TEMPORARY-CUSTOMER-RECORD.

It makes for a lot of typing, but I like the way it reads, and I find it
easier
to read code that I may not have looked at for five or ten years.

I think it a matter of personal taste, really.

>is defined when I see it.  And because I always use the exact name for a
>given field across the entire system, except for the prefix, it also
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers working on the same system, even later.


Ditto, as far as standardizing names go.  Actually, I put all file layouts
into copies as a matter of course.

>Also, since you agree with me on OCCURS for 01s in the File Section, do
>you think it is good to have a conflicting standard?  I believe that
>would cause confusion.  Yes, you cannot use REDEFINES for an 01 in the


I do not think it does any harm.  The value clause and editing picture
clauses,
as you mention, are already illegal in file section record clauses.  There
are
special rules (redefinition being another) wherein a file record differs
from a
working storage record.

I think we are a bit philosopically opposed here.  My tendancy is to have
a loose standard, and expect individual programmers to develope a writing
style that is easy to read and even individualistic.  I sense that you
prefer
a tighter standard as an enforcement method.  I tend to be philosophically
opposed to having my software dictate my style.  I think it stiffles
creativety,
and imposes limits that ultimately make systems poorer.

As mom said, "you cannot legislate good taste", and mothers are always
right<s>.

>in the File Section.  Languages have an implicit philosophy, and it is a


As do programmers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70o7eh$r8f@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<362b6b73.0@news1.ibm.net> <3298292.11803.17704@kcbbs.gen.nz> <362C9540.9A7BF528@home.com> <70j6rn$cum@sjx-ixn3.ix.netcom.com> <70j999$3sr$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <aMaX1.930$xx4.980402@news3.mia.bellsouth.net> <70k1u6$92$1@news.igs.net> <GYjX1.676$Se2.1210192@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

  <much snippage>

>  And though you cannot use VALUE in the file section


  <more snippage>

And coming in the next Standard ...

VALUE clause *is* allowed in both FILE SECTION and LINKAGE SECTION.  In both
cases it is ignored (treated as documentation) during "program
initialization" - but has meaning with new INITIALIZE TO VALUE and VALIDATE
syntax.

Note: Many compilers allow it today as an extension (and treat it as
documentation) - which is quite useful when using the same COPY book member
for a file layout in File Section, Working-Storage Section, and Linkage
Section.
```

Said
>>
Usual "plug for the new Standard".

   INITIALIZE TO VALUE

If the bearer of bad tidings gets a terrible reception does not the bearer of
good tidings deserve great reward?

But I can not think of good enough reward for news this nice. So meager though
it be, lets make William M. Klein king of COBOL for this weekend.

And maybe when the standard is cast, the verb will not be so verbose as 
INTIALIZE TO VALUE but perhaps something elegant like ENKLEIN the-item. So as
to honor the great king and all those who contribute at his level to these
masterful missives.

Thanks!

And please do steamroll anyone who delays this standard rectification.











Robert Rayhawk

  I think this thread started going off in a strange tangent.

My example was SOLELY for showing the problems with allowing an OCCURS at
the 01-level (in Working-Storage Section).  I am well aware of how all these
problems go away if you do use (current) Standard COBOL and move the OCCURS
to a subordinate level.  I am also aware of other methods of initializing
data (such as VALUE clauses and REDEFINES) - but was simply trying to point
out the problems with OCCURS at the 01-level.

All right, time out, Hommie has a question!

Please review the following code and tell me what is wrong
with it.
       identification division.
       program-id. "labrat1".

       data division.
       working-storage section.

       01 data-record.
            05 data-item occurs 20 times.
                 10  alpha-stuff    pic x value 'z'.
                 10  num-stuff      pic 9 value 9.

       procedure division.

              display 'num-stuff 1 = ' num-stuff (1)
                           '  alhpa-stuff 1 = ' alpha-stuff (1).

              initialize data-record.

              display 'num-stuff 1 = ' num-stuff (1)
                           '  alpha-stuff 1 = ' alpha-stuff (1).
              stop run.

So the question was can this table be initialized with one statement
or did I mis understand the question???

If I understood the question then why, based on what has been said,
is it not possible to initialize data-record with one statement??

Just one more thing I have done this using various IBM Cobol
compilers and I tried it out using Fuji's 3.0. And guess what
it works.

Please tell me what am I clearly missing!! I must know!!!!

In message <<70jj02$fjk$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

Not 'regardless of level', no.  If there was a level above
the array you could use it to initialise.

> value statements, then redefined it as an array.  That is the same
> problem and the same solution.

And you cannot use an occurs on an 01 level when redefining
a record, so it is _not_ the 'same solution'.

You are simply raising issues where it would be impossible
to use OCCURS on the 01 level as support for allowing it,
this highlights the fact that OCCURS on 01 is pointless
and meaningless.

- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981021131223.19974.00003667@ng02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<362DF47F.384F0A8E@home.com>`

```

The discussion so far has covered most of the subject, with many good comments.
One of the few aspects that remains are performance and the robustness of
initialization

The discussion so far has dealt with syntax of VALUE clauses and the INITIALIZE
verb, and also useful interaction about style issues.

Another way to initialize the DATA-RECORD is to set up separate definition that
looks exactly the same, and has detailed value clauses, either the repeating
values mentioned or the vatiations, say of months of the year.

This new record would be static. Infact, every field except the 01 level could
be a FILLER (utterly unnamed in some compilers, by the way).

When you are ready to re-init just move DATA-RECORD-STATIC to DATA-RECORD. 
This would generate something like an MVC on mainframes or a REP STOSB on PCs.
(It is not inconcevable that some optimizing compilers could generate REP STOSW
or REP STOSD in some cases, but I don't know that personally).

This relates to the original posters question, perhaps, by virtue of the fact
that re-initialization is considered at all. For re-inits inside of inner
loops, it would make a difference if you could reduce it to a move that
executes at the speed of an individual instruction.  

The COBOL INITIALIZE verb is a syntactic short cut for a long list of moves.
The list of moves gets coded for you by the compiler, as it were. And in the
case of numerics, sundry extra instructions to patch signs can actually be
emitted.

Programmer time is the most precious resource, usually. But where speed of the
application counts, a group move can help.

An occurs 20 by two byte work area, is going to initialize quickly no matter
what you do, unless there is a truely large number of executions. Yet sometimes
posters do us the service of reducing examples for our focused analysis.  In
the real world awesome resources are used to init 01s with the INITIALIZE verb.

One other consideration here, and I know this glides away from the main focus
of the syntactical interaction between INITIALIZE and data definitions.  But
semantically INITIALIZE does not mean anything at all for the FILLER and
unnamed data items.

INITIALIZE leaves FILLER and unnamed data items un-re-initialized.
If such items have no original VALUE clause and were not effected by group
moves, then troublesome garbage remains in the data area.

This is a bit of a problem because practical applications have oodles and
oddles of filler to allow for 'expansion' with out increasing the size of data
records.

To get around this problem some apps will initialize by first moving spaces, or
low values to either the entire 01, or numerous subsection group levels.
AND THEN INITIALIZE THE 01, Definitely engaging some duplicate effort by the
faithful CPU.  It is not that the programmers are lame, INITIALIZE actually
misses the target partially.

Moving  something like DATA-RECORD-STATIC to DATA-RECORD can address the
portion of the data record not accessible via INITIALIZE. This is a solution in
a limitted set of conditions.

Also, not to pick on any one comment, but it seemed implied in some of the
discussion that INITIALIZE picks up VALUE clause values.  This seemd to be the
notion in the discussion of the original values if there were not repeating.

For example, if all the PIC 9s items were not to be zero, but say values
ranging from 0 to 9 (cycling), then a redefined area with such values could be
the target of an INITIALIZE verb, and this perhaps to some seems like a way to
get the table to snap back.

I don't think so.  (and perhaps I draw too much of a conclusion about implied
meaning).  The point is INITIALIZE (AFAIK) does things by class
alphabetic/numeric and assigns a single value to each item per class.
You are not stuck with merely zeros and spaces.  But what ever you pick goes
into all the items of that class.

The original value clauses are not referenced by the INITIALIZE verb. And that,
I understand as the language standard.  Let me know if anyone sees it
differently, or has a compiler with extensions that go beyond this.

So, I emphasize, that move of a DATA-RECORD-STATIC to DATA-RECORD has limitted
utility.
But in addition to performance advantages, it permits initialization in a
NON-NEANDERTHAL manner, and it reaches portions of a group item that are
utterly transparent to the INITIALIZE verb.

So I advocate a new verb for the language: NON-NEANDERTHALIZE. And whatever way
we definte it, it would be a prerequisite that it happen quickly, even on
Sierra Class machines.








Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362E2AB4.EABCAAD8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<362DF47F.384F0A8E@home.com> <19981021131223.19974.00003667@ng02.aol.com>`

```
One advantage of using the value clause is that it is a compile-time
move.

I have seen people say that it would be better to have constants in
COBOL, but I wonder if that is true.  Would the compiler make it any
faster for being stored in a constant area?  Or would it recognize that
it doesn't change and treat it as a constant anyway?

Any move is a run-time move, and thus slower than a compile-time move.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus 88-level validation probl

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362e2e23.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<362DF47F.384F0A8E@home.com> <19981021131223.19974.00003667@ng02.aol.com> <362E2AB4.EABCAAD8@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <362E2AB4.EABCAAD8@home.com>...
>I have seen people say that it would be better to have constants in
>COBOL, but I wonder if that is true.  Would the compiler make it any
>faster for being stored in a constant area?  Or would it recognize that
>it doesn't change and treat it as a constant anyway?


The advantage is that you cannot change a constant.
Since it is constant it would be OK to use it in picture
clauses and avoid the error-prone contraction:

01  My-table.
      02  My-table-size   pic 9(3) value 100.
      02  My-table-entry  pic x(10) occurs 100 times.

where the "100" is repeated.
```

We both picked up on this.
 
> For example, if all the PIC 9s items were not to be zero, but say values
> ranging from 0 to 9 (cycling), then a redefined area with such values could be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You can also use INITIALIZE to set a field to a value other than 
spaces or zeros.  For example, if you had a bunch of signed fields you
could code:

INITIALIZE GROUP-LEVEL REPLACING NUMERIC DATA BY -1.

That would set all numeric fields in the group to -1. You can use a 
similar replacing for alphabetic or alphanumeric data.
```

A new option has been added to INITIALIZE to let you ALSO initialize FILLER
items (obviously, the default is upwardly compatible and still will NOT
initialize such items).

## Thread: another variable question — 11 msgs

### another variable question

- **From:** savggerbil@aol.com (SavgGerbil)
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981020000040.28784.00000279@ng24.aol.com>`

```
is there a way to move a number which was inputed from the keyboard to a pic
clause?

i'm trying to have this program ask how many characters are in a data feild on
disk, read said feilds to be processed, and then written to disk 
with spaces in between each feild. 

like so:

01  var                           pic 999

01 data-rec-in
01 invar-in                     pic x(var)

where var is the number inputted by the user and invar-in would be the variable
which read from the .dat file.

i got alot of help with the first question i posted i'm not sure if i'm asking
the same question twice so bare with me.

Thanks to everyone who helped me the first time.
```

#### ↳ Re: another variable question

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981020180224.26954.00003440@ng07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19981020000040.28784.00000279@ng24.aol.com>`

```

On 20 Oct 1998 04:00:40 GMT
From: savggerbil@aol.com (SavgGerbil)
asked a very interesting question

>>
is there a way to move a number which was inputed from the keyboard to a pic
clause?

i'm trying to have this program ask how many characters are in a data feild on
disk, read said feilds to be processed, and then written to disk 
with spaces in between each feild. 

like so:

01  var                           pic 999

01 data-rec-in
01 invar-in                     pic x(var)

where var is the number inputted by the user and invar-in would be the variable
which read from the .dat file.

<<

Your objective is probably easily achievable, but the coding strategy your
considering will not work with COBOL. Thesyntax of the language is highly
influenced by its extemely early genesis in DP history.  

COBOL syntax is essentially a compiled syntax. The details of the
WORKING-STORAGE section, for example, become entirely hardened as of
compile-time.  Even the ability to manipulate at run-time the location of
information mapped with working storage layout via an ADDRESS OF phrase is a
comparatively new language feature.

The coding technique you envision is more like BASIC or even LISP, where you
can interpret and re-interpret (or EVALuate and re-evaluate) source code.

COBOL is rather more rigid syntactically, even under the mediation of modern
interpreters or incremental compilers. A PIC cluase is essentially fixed. And
by execution time is actually gone and only its effects remain.

You may do better to consider one of two basic paths. Both use a PROCEDURE
DIVISION verb to respond to dynamic data conditions.

The first approach is to UNSTRING the input data area into one or more
subfields. This requires you to discover reliable delimitters within the input
data. Data which has been so input usually requires fixups after you isolate
the pieces (leading zeros, left/right justification within the targeted
subfields).  

From the subfield(s) you can do simple moves to the output, or construct an
outbound image with STRING.  It is not at all necessary to use STRING to
output, just because UNSTRING was used on part or all of your input operation.

(Be warned that you must init target fields for both UNSTRING and STRING verbs,
these are not MOVEs and will not blank fill!)

The second strategy is to INSPECT your input field TALLYING various LEADING
classes of text type data.
With the tallies you can do exact moves with reference modification.

This can be tough for beginners. But it is achievable for you if your input is
not tremendously complex.

Anyway, the layout of things in the DATA DIVISION gets set something like
concrete.  It is not unusual for folks to use numerous REDEFINES of the same
area to compensate for this.  But if your input is highly variable, you need to
scan it directly yourself with INSPECT, or more automatically with UNSTRING ...
DELIMITTED BY.

Keep posting and let us know how you do.


 


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: another variable question

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70jrml$mnv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981020000040.28784.00000279@ng24.aol.com> <19981020180224.26954.00003440@ng07.aol.com> <362D54BE.6538625B@inficad.com>`

```

Warren G. Simmons wrote in message <362D54BE.6538625B@inficad.com>...

File names can be data names in every Cobol I have ever worked with.
About the only difference is where you name them. (either in the select
or in the value of id clause).

The following should work just fine.

        Select file-out assign to my-file.
        fd file-out
            data record is blank-line.
        01 blank-line        picture x.
        working-storage section.
        01 my-file            picture 999.
        procedure division.
        perform create-100-files varying my-file
                from 1 by 1 until my-file is greater than 100.
        stop run.
 create-100-files.
        open output file-out.
        write blank-line.
        close file-out.

>I had been wondering how one would program "lets say it's a name and
>address" application where one of the menu options was to create a new
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: another variable question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70o7e5$r8f@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981020000040.28784.00000279@ng24.aol.com> <19981020180224.26954.00003440@ng07.aol.com> <362D54BE.6538625B@inficad.com>`

```
The next Standard does provide a method of "dynamically" changing what file
an FD points to.  (As mentioned in another post in this thread, this is
already a common feature with PC compilers - but is not allowed with IBM
mainframe compilers)  The new syntax is INcompatible with all existing
implementations (that I know of).  It has

   Select ABC USING xyz ....

where XYZ is a (working-storage) data item including the "physical file
name".  For IBM mainframers, I think this will still end up being the DDName
and not the DSName - but that is up to IBM to decide  upon.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: another variable question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** savggerbil@aol.com (SavgGerbil)
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<19981022233800.21850.00000943@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
>The next Standard does provide a method of "dynamically" changing what file
>an FD points to.  (As mentioned in another post in this thread, this is
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>and not the DSName - but that is up to IBM to decide  upon.


This is what i use....

       File-control.
       Select data-file assign to name-of-inp
           organization is line sequential.

       Select out-file assign to name-of-outp

       Data Division.

       File Section.

       FD  data-file.
       01  Input-Rec               pic x(120).

       FD  out-file.
       01  data-line               pic x occurs 0 to 120 times depending on
feild-in.

       working-storage section.

       01  name-of-outp            pic x(15).
       01  name-of-inp             pic x(15).

       Procedure Division.

       000-main.
           Display ' Please enter the file name. i.e. "A:test.dat" '.
           accept name-of-inp.
           Display ' Please enter a filename for your output file.'.
           accept name-of-outp. display.


the only bad part with this is you have to type in the drive letter also.
most of the stuff i got from people in this newsgroup. thanks to all that
helped me.
```

- **Date:** 1998-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70ismc$813$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```

II?
Tks in advance,
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL74 to COBOLII

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70o7e7$r8f@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70ismc$813$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
Look at the IBM Migration Guide under the discussion of "WRITE AFTER/BEFORE
ADVANCING and/or WRITE AFTER/BEFORE POSITIONING".  If you don't have a copy
of the IBM Migration Guide, then get one - you will need it for any
migration from OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II.

P.S. Are you aware that you are migrating to almost-obsolete technology?
Find out who decided to go to VS COBOL II - when IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
(or if you are under VSE, COBOL/VSE) is the current IBM product.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

<a id="t-d99030700e"></a>
## Thread: DB/2 binds - main vs. subroutine — 2 msgs

### DB/2 binds - main vs. subroutine

- **From:** "Doug McKibbin" <dmckib@goodnet.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.db2-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qzpxvotbbqargpbz.f17h1z0.pminews@enews.newsguy.com>`

```
A new COBOL subroutine (batch) is written and accesses MVS DB/2 tables. 
The calling program has no SQL and calls the subroutine dynamically.  The
subroutine compiles and binds ok, but now the calling program must be
executed via IKJEFT01 with the RUN PROGRAM/PLAN statement.  If the calling
program is compiled as a DB/2 program, the compile issues errors because
there are no SQL statements and no package is created.  So the question is:
how can the main program be compiled to create a DBRM to bind?  A simple
SQL statement can be added, but that seems hokey.
Suggestions?

Regards,
Doug McKibbin
(remove NOSPAM from address when emailing)
```

#### ↳ Re: DB/2 binds - main vs. subroutine

- **From:** stephengoss@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.db2-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70qe9l$otn$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<qzpxvotbbqargpbz.f17h1z0.pminews@enews.newsguy.com>`

```
Doug, yes, adding an SQL stmt to get your compile through is hokey.
Precompiling a program with no SQL stmts is only a level 4 (a warning). But
binding an empty dbrm into a package is an error. If your bind step was at
the end of your compile jcl then it wouldn't matter. This is the cheap and
cheerful approach.

However, you could just have separate jcl for compiling a program with no SQL
in it. Then you wouldn't be doing a bind which fails.

At run time, it is no big deal if your main (calling program), say MAIN has no
SQL in it and the called module, say SUB, has SQL.

You just BIND the package for SUB and do a once off BIND PLAN (MAIN) PKLIST
(<collid>.SUB).

Then RUN PLAN (MAIN) PROGRAM (MAIN)

Note that I have called the plan MAIN just because that is the name of the
loadmodule we're running, which distinguishes this execution of SUB from
executions of other loadmodules which may call SUB. This is useful for
performance monitoring, when looking at accounting traces from DB2PM etc.

Hope this is of some help
Stephen Goss


In article <qzpxvotbbqargpbz.f17h1z0.pminews@enews.newsguy.com>,
  "Doug McKibbin" <dmckib@goodnet.com> wrote:
> A new COBOL subroutine (batch) is written and accesses MVS DB/2 tables.
> The calling program has no SQL and calls the subroutine dynamically.  The
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-0c8c90c46c"></a>
## Thread: X"F4" & X"F5" — 17 msgs

### X"F4" & X"F5"

- **From:** "Dennis Komeshak" <komeshak@fgi.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com>`

```
In converting to Fujitsu COBOL from MicroFocus, I use the x"f4" (pack data
into a byte) and x"f5" (unpack data into a byte) routines. Can anyone
suggest a work around or has anyone come up with replacement routines?
Thanks,
Dennis Komeshak
```

#### ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com>`

```
Dennis Komeshak wrote:
>In converting to Fujitsu COBOL from MicroFocus, I use the x"f4" (pack data
>into a byte) and x"f5" (unpack data into a byte) routines. Can anyone
>suggest a work around or has anyone come up with replacement routines?

Below is a solution I posted to a similar problem several days ago.

Here is a general approach which will usually work, though you may need
to adapt it for your specific compiler.  Basically, you define a PIC X
field and redefine it as a binary numeric type, or vice-versa, then you
reference the binary numeric type as a numeric value, using add, subtract,
etc. to do the work.  The following Micro Focus program contains a routine
which takes a byte and splits each bit into a separate PIC 9 field, and
another routine which merges the separate fields into one byte.  Once the
bits are separated, you can easily test them individually.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  WS-CHAR             PIC  X(01).
       77  WS-VALUE            PIC  9(03).
       01  BINARY-WORK.
           03  BW-BINARY       PIC S9(04)  BINARY  VALUE 0.
           03  BW-ALPHA        REDEFINES BW-BINARY.
               05  FILLER          PIC  X(01).
               05  BW-CHAR         PIC  X(01).
           03  BW-BITS.
               05  BW-BIT128   PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT64    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT32    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT16    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT8     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT4     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT2     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT1     PIC  9(01).
           03  BW-BIT          REDEFINES BW-BITS
                               OCCURS 8 TIMES
                               INDEXED BY BW-X
                                   PIC  9(01).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       TEST-IT.
           MOVE "I" TO BW-CHAR.
           MOVE BW-CHAR   TO WS-CHAR.
           MOVE BW-BINARY TO WS-VALUE.
           PERFORM SPLIT-BYTE.
           DISPLAY WS-CHAR " "
                   BW-BIT128 BW-BIT64 BW-BIT32 BW-BIT16
                   BW-BIT8   BW-BIT4  BW-BIT2  BW-BIT1
                   " " WS-VALUE.

           MOVE 1 TO BW-BIT16.
           MOVE 0 TO BW-BIT1.

           PERFORM MERGE-BYTE.
           MOVE BW-CHAR   TO WS-CHAR.
           MOVE BW-BINARY TO WS-VALUE.
           DISPLAY WS-CHAR " "
                   BW-BIT128 BW-BIT64 BW-BIT32 BW-BIT16
                   BW-BIT8   BW-BIT4  BW-BIT2  BW-BIT1
                   " " WS-VALUE.
           STOP RUN.

       SPLIT-BYTE.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 8 BY -1
                   UNTIL BW-X < 1
               DIVIDE 2 INTO BW-BINARY
                   GIVING BW-BINARY
                   REMAINDER BW-BIT(BW-X)
           END-PERFORM.

       MERGE-BYTE.
           MOVE 0 TO BW-BINARY.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL BW-X > 8
               COMPUTE BW-BINARY = BW-BINARY * 2 + BW-BIT(BW-X)
           END-PERFORM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 19:52:27, "Judson McClendon" 
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> Here is a general approach which will usually work, though you may need
> to adapt it for your specific compiler.  Basically, you define a PIC X
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I like your approach.  But - like my Right Justify question I'd like 
to see a "Cleaner" "Simpler" "More Elegent" solution posted.  

Are the netizens of c.l.c up to the challenge?

Take a byte and separate it into bits - using whatever indicators you 
like for the bits - the ultimate goal is to be able to query a 
particular bit for  a 1 or zero value, and to SET particular bits to 1
or zero.

Standard COBOL - should work across compilers - assume Ansi 85 with 89
extensions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362e5391.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
see below

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 19:52:27, "Judson McClendon"
><judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> reference the binary numeric type as a numeric value, using add,
subtract,
>> etc. to do the work.  The following Micro Focus program contains a
routine
>> which takes a byte and splits each bit into a separate PIC 9 field, and
>> another routine which merges the separate fields into one byte.  Once the
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The BITPK program from ETKPAK at http://www.etk.com/download does this:

Summary: Provides bit manipulations of up to 32 bits. Can get, set, clear,
test, and pack bits.

How to use: Instead of non-portable redefinitions to access bits packed in
numeric variables, use BITPK's get operation to convert a number into a
string of 32 characters each with values "0" or "1". You can then easily
test or otherwise manipulate these character values. The pack operation
packs the character values back into a number.

Limitations: At most 32 bits can be handled at a time.

To call BITPK, define BITPK-CONTROL as follows:

              01  BITPK-CONTROL.
                  02  BITPK-TASK-ID           PIC X(4) .
                  02  BITPK-OPERATION         PIC X(1) .
                  02  BITPK-FEEDBACK          PIC X(1) .
                  02  BITPK-NUMBER            PIC 9(10).
                  02  BITPK-BITS.
                      03  BITPK-BIT           PIC X(1)  OCCURS 32 TIMES.

Define CALL-BITPK as follows:
              CALL-BITPK.
                  CALL "BITPK"
                        USING BITPK-CONTROL
              .
BITPK supports the following operations:

G (get)
Get the bits of BITPK-NUMBER interpreted as an unsigned 32-bit number. If
the number is larger than 32 bits a (R)ANGE feedback is returned and the bit
string is all zeroes.

P (pack)
The reverse operation of get. Builds the 32-bit number having the bit string
given in BITPK-BITS.

S (set)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, set the corresponding
bit in BITPK-NUMBER. This is the binary OR operation.

A (and)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, set the corresponding
bit in BITPK-NUMBER if that bit is also a one, otherwise clear the bit. This
is the binary AND operation.

X (xor)
For each bit of the bit string given that is different from the
corresponding bit in BITPK-NUMBER, set that bit in BITPK-NUMBER, otherwise
clear the bit. This is the binary XOR operation.

C (clear)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, clear the corresponding
bit in BITPK-NUMBER.

1 (test for 1)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, test if the
corresponding bit in BITPK-NUMBER is a one. Return an (A)LL feedback if all
bits tested were ones, a (N)ONE feedback if none of the bits were ones, or a
(S)OME feedback if only some of them were.

0 (test for 0)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, test if the
corresponding bit in BITPK-NUMBER is a zero. Return an (A)LL feedback if all
bits tested were zeroes, a (N)ONE feedback if none of the bits were zeroes,
or a (S)OME feedback if only some of them were.
BITPK returns these feedbacks: a space means there were no errors. An "O"
means that the specified operation was invalid. An "R" means that the number
given was too large. An "A", "N", or "S" means that either All, None, or
Some bits met the condition.


000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    BITPK.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  96/12/06
000600     -REVISED:  97/01/22.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  BIT-HANDLING.
001900     02  BIT-VALUE               PIC 9(10)  COMP.
002000     02  BIT-TOP                 PIC 9(10)  COMP  VALUE ZEROES.
002100     02  THE-WEIGHT              PIC 9(10)  COMP.
002200     02  BIT-WEIGHTS.
002300         03  BIT-WEIGHT          PIC 9(10)  COMP  OCCURS 32 TIMES.
002400
002500     02  BIT-COUNT               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002600     02  BIT-MATCH               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002700     02  BIT-NBR                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002800
002900     02  BIT-WANTED              PIC X.
003000     02  BIT-STRING.
003100         03  THE-BIT             PIC X      OCCURS 32 TIMES.
003200
003300 LINKAGE SECTION.
003400
003500 01  BITPK-CONTROL.
003600     02  SESSION-TASK-ID         PIC X(4).
003700     02  BITPK-OPERATION         PIC X(1).
003800     02  BITPK-FEEDBACK          PIC X(1).
003900
004000     02  BITPK-PATTERN           PIC 9(10)  COMP.
004100     02  BITPK-BITS.
004200         03  BITPK-BIT           PIC X      OCCURS 32 TIMES.
004300
004400 PROCEDURE DIVISION
004500     USING BITPK-CONTROL.
004600 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
004700     IF BIT-TOP = ZEROES
004800         PERFORM INITIALIZE-BIT-WEIGHTS
004900     .
005000     PERFORM GET-AND-CHECK-THE-BITS
005100     MOVE SPACE TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
005200     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "G"
005300         MOVE BIT-STRING TO BITPK-BITS
005400     ELSE
005500     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "S" OR "O"
005600         PERFORM SET-THE-BITS
005700     ELSE
005800     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "C"
005900         PERFORM CLEAR-THE-BITS
006000     ELSE
006100     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "A"
006200         PERFORM AND-THE-BITS
006300     ELSE
006400     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "X"
006500         PERFORM XOR-THE-BITS
006600     ELSE
006700     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "1"
006800         PERFORM TEST-IF-ONES
006900     ELSE
007000     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "0"
007100         PERFORM TEST-IF-ZEROES
007200     ELSE
007300     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "P"
007400         PERFORM PACK-THE-BITS
007500     ELSE
007600         MOVE "OPERR" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
007700     .
007800 RETURN-TO-CALLER.
007900     EXIT PROGRAM
008000     .
008100
008200 INITIALIZE-BIT-WEIGHTS.
008300     MOVE 1 TO THE-WEIGHT
008400     PERFORM SET-BIT-WEIGHT
008500       VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 32 BY -1
008600         UNTIL BIT-NBR < 1
008700
008800     MOVE THE-WEIGHT TO BIT-TOP
008900     .
009000
009100 SET-BIT-WEIGHT.
009200     MOVE THE-WEIGHT TO BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR)
009300     ADD  THE-WEIGHT TO THE-WEIGHT
009400     .
009500
009600 GET-AND-CHECK-THE-BITS.
009700     MOVE BITPK-PATTERN TO BIT-VALUE
009800     IF BIT-VALUE < BIT-TOP
009900         MOVE ZEROES TO BIT-STRING
010000         PERFORM GET-THE-BIT
010100           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
010200             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
010300
010400         PERFORM CHECK-THE-BIT
010500           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
010600             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
010700     ELSE
010800         MOVE "RANGE" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
010900         MOVE  ZEROES TO BIT-STRING
011000     .
011100
011200 GET-THE-BIT.
011300     IF BIT-VALUE NOT < BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR)
011400         SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BIT-VALUE
011500         MOVE "1" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
011600     .
011700
011800 CHECK-THE-BIT.
011900     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = ZERO
012000         NEXT SENTENCE
012100     ELSE
012200     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = SPACE
012300         MOVE ZERO TO BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR)
012400     ELSE
012500     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) NOT = "1"
012600         MOVE "1"  TO BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR)
012700     .
012800
012900 SET-THE-BITS.
013000     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
013100         PERFORM SET-THE-BIT
013200           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
013300             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
013400     .
013500
013600 SET-THE-BIT.
013700     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
013800         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "0"
013900             MOVE "1" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
014000             ADD BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) TO BITPK-PATTERN
014100     .
014200
014300 CLEAR-THE-BITS.
014400     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
014500         PERFORM CLEAR-THE-BIT
014600           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
014700             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
014800     .
014900
015000 CLEAR-THE-BIT.
015100     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
015200         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
015300             MOVE "0" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
015400             SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BITPK-PATTERN
015500     .
015600
015700 AND-THE-BITS.
015800     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
015900         PERFORM AND-THE-BIT
016000           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
016100             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
016200     .
016300
016400 AND-THE-BIT.
016500     IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
016600         IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) NOT = "1"
016700             MOVE "0" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
016800             SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BITPK-PATTERN
016900     .
017000
017100 XOR-THE-BITS.
017200     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
017300         PERFORM XOR-THE-BIT
017400           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
017500             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
017600     .
017700
017800 XOR-THE-BIT.
017900     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
018000         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
018100             MOVE "0" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
018200             SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BITPK-PATTERN
018300         ELSE
018400             NEXT SENTENCE
018500     ELSE
018600         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "0"
018700             MOVE "1" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
018800             ADD      BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR)  TO  BITPK-PATTERN
018900         ELSE
019000             NEXT SENTENCE
019100     .
019200
019300 TEST-IF-ONES.
019400     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
019500         MOVE "1" TO BIT-WANTED
019600         PERFORM TEST-THE-BITS
019700     .
019800
019900 TEST-THE-BITS.
020000     MOVE ZEROES TO BIT-COUNT, BIT-MATCH
020100     PERFORM TEST-IF-BIT-IS-WANTED
020200       VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
020300         UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
020400
020500     IF BIT-COUNT = BIT-MATCH
020600         MOVE "ALL"  TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
020700     ELSE
020800     IF BIT-MATCH = ZEROES
020900         MOVE "NONE" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
021000     ELSE
021100         MOVE "SOME" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
021200     .
021300
021400 TEST-IF-BIT-IS-WANTED.
021500     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
021600         ADD 1 TO BIT-COUNT
021700         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = BIT-WANTED
021800             ADD 1 TO BIT-MATCH
021900     .
022000
022100 TEST-IF-ZEROES.
022200     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
022300         MOVE "0" TO BIT-WANTED
022400         PERFORM TEST-THE-BITS
022500     .
022600
022700 PACK-THE-BITS.
022800     MOVE ZEROES TO BITPK-PATTERN
022900     PERFORM PACK-THE-BIT
023000       VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
023100         UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
023200     .
023300
023400 PACK-THE-BIT.
023500     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
023600         ADD BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) TO BITPK-PATTERN
023700     .


It could be streamlined for 8 bit bytes, but it is simpler to retain its
full
capability.


Thane, maybe I'll work on a "smaller" solution, but often you really
need the other capabilities as well.

The main problem is to convert a byte to a number in a portable
way, i.e. without redefinition.


>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VvsX1.778$Se2.1479298@news4.mia.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>extensions.


Though it wasn't 'standard COBOL', I thought my first solution was
reasonably 'clean' 'simple' and 'elegant'. :-)  But if you insist,
back to the old drawing board.  <mumble> <mumble>

This is 'standard COBOL'.  However, be careful of how the ALPHABET
clause in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph of the CONFIGURATION SECTION
may affect FUNCTION ORD and FUNCTION CHAR.

       01  BIT-WORK.
           03  BW-BYTE        PIC X(01)      VALUE SPACE.
           03  BW-ORD         PIC 9(04) COMP VALUE 0.
           03  BW-BITS.
               05  BW-BIT128  PIC 9(01).
               05  BW-BIT64   PIC 9(01).
               05  BW-BIT32   PIC 9(01).
               05  BW-BIT16   PIC 9(01).
               05  BW-BIT8    PIC 9(01).
               05  BW-BIT4    PIC 9(01).
               05  BW-BIT2    PIC 9(01).
               05  BW-BIT1    PIC 9(01).
           03  BW-BIT         REDEFINES BW-BITS
                              PIC 9(01) OCCURS 8 TIMES INDEXED BY BW-X.

       SPLIT-BYTE-INTO-BITS.
           COMPUTE BW-ORD = FUNCTION ORD(BW-BYTE) - 1.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 8 BY -1 UNTIL BW-X < 1
               DIVIDE 2 INTO BW-ORD
                   GIVING BW-ORD REMAINDER BW-BIT(BW-X).

       MERGE-BITS-INTO-BYTE.
           MOVE 0 TO BW-ORD.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL BW-X > 8
               COMPUTE BW-ORD = BW-ORD + BW-ORD + BW-BIT(BW-X).
           MOVE FUNCTION CHAR(BW-ORD + 1) TO BW-BYTE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IpuX1.514$Kp5.3758254@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>
>I like your approach.  But - like my Right Justify question I'd like
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Would you settle for different?

I thought I would post code which uses called subprograms since this
more closely matches the "F4" and "F5". The differences are that
neither byte nor array are comp-x.

MF:  byte pic x comp-x
        array pic x comp-x occurs 8
becomes: byte pic x.
       array pic 9 occurs 8

The calls are are to "b-pack" and "b-unpack".

       program-id. b-test.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 ws.
         03 ws-text.
           05 ws-char pic x.
           05 ws-position pic 9.
       01 ws-bit-value pic x value space.
       01 ws-byte pic x.
       01 ws-array.
         03 ws-bits pic 9 occurs 8.
       procedure division.
       b-test-start.
           display "Enter an action and a bit position."
           display "For example, 's1' to set bit 1"
           display "or 'c5' to clear bit 5."
           display "Enter ?4 to get the value of bit 4."
           display "Bits are numbered 1 through 8."
           display "Enter q to quit."
           move low-values to ws-byte
           perform display-bit-pattern
           perform with test after
             until ws-char = "Q"
               accept ws-text
               if ws-text ( 2 : 1 ) not numeric
                   move "0" to ws-text ( 2 : 1 )
               end-if
               move space to ws-bit-value
               move function upper-case ( ws-char ) to ws-char
               evaluate ws-char
                 when "?"
                   call "b-what" using ws-byte, ws-position,
                       ws-bit-value
                 when "S"
                   call "b-set" using ws-byte, ws-position
                 when "C"
                   call "b-clear" using ws-byte, ws-position
               end-evaluate
               perform display-bit-pattern
           end-perform
           stop run
           .

       display-bit-pattern.
           call "b-unpack" using ws-byte, ws-array
           display ws-bits(1) ws-bits(2) ws-bits(3) ws-bits(4)
               ws-bits(5) ws-bits(6) ws-bits(7) ws-bits(8)
               space quote ws-byte quote space ws-bit-value
           .
       end program b-test.

       program-id. b-what.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 ws-array.
         03 ws-bits pic 9 occurs 8.
       linkage section.
       01 ls-byte pic x.
       01 ls-position pic 9.
       01 ls-bit-value pic x.
       procedure division using ls-byte, ls-position,
           ls-bit-value.
       b-what-start.
           if ls-position > 0 and < 9
               call "b-unpack" using ls-byte, ws-array
               move ws-bits ( ls-position ) to ls-bit-value
           end-if
           exit program
           .
       end program b-what.

       program-id. b-set.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 ws-array.
         03 ws-bits pic 9 occurs 8.
       linkage section.
       01 ls-byte pic x.
       01 ls-position pic 9.
       procedure division using ls-byte, ls-position.
       b-set-start.
           if ls-position > 0 and < 9
               call "b-unpack" using ls-byte, ws-array
               move 1 to ws-bits ( ls-position )
               call "b-pack" using ls-byte, ws-array
           end-if
           exit program
           .
       end program b-set.

       program-id. b-clear.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 ws-array.
         03 ws-bits pic 9 occurs 8.
       linkage section.
       01 ls-byte pic x.
       01 ls-position pic 9.
       procedure division using ls-byte, ls-position.
       b-clear-start.
           if ls-position > 0 and < 9
               call "b-unpack" using ls-byte, ws-array
               move 0 to ws-bits ( ls-position )
               call "b-pack" using ls-byte, ws-array
           end-if
           exit program
           .
       end program b-clear.

       program-id. b-unpack.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 ws.
         03 ws-b-value pic s9(4) binary.
         03 ws-x pic s9(4) binary.
       linkage section.
       01 ls-byte pic x.
       01 ls-array.
         03 ls-array-bit pic 9 occurs 8.
       procedure division using ls-byte, ls-array.
       b-unpack-start.
           compute ws-b-value = function ord ( ls-byte ) - 1
           move all "0" to ls-array
           perform varying ws-x from 1 by 1 until ws-b-value = 0
               if ws-b-value >= 128
                   move 1 to ls-array-bit ( ws-x )
                   subtract 128 from ws-b-value
               end-if
               multiply 2 by ws-b-value
           end-perform
           exit program
           .
       end program b-unpack.

       program-id. b-pack.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 ws.
         03 ws-b-value pic s9(4) binary.
         03 ws-x pic s9(4) binary.
       linkage section.
       01 ls-byte pic x.
       01 ls-array.
         03 ls-array-bit pic 9 occurs 8.
       procedure division using ls-byte, ls-array.
       b-pack-start.
           move 0 to ws-b-value
           perform varying ws-x from 1 by 1 until ws-x > 8
               multiply 2 by ws-b-value
               add ls-array-bit ( ws-x ) to ws-b-value
           end-perform
           move function char ( ws-b-value + 1 ) to ls-byte
           exit program
           .
       end program b-pack.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362f3bc1.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <IpuX1.514$Kp5.3758254@news1.atlantic.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-FKeWfbX9ZX7d@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 23:47:50, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

>Now, which is more efficient <G> (Using the functions, as you and
>Judson did, or not using them as Leif did?)


Thane,
I did not use the functions because my stuff has to run with ANY
compiler, even old ones without functions.
In fact, I do not know of a portable solution to  the "ORD" problem
(getting the PIC X(n)  into a PIC 9(m)).
So, even with BITPK, you must use a redefinition (in the calling program)
to do that. My solution is to have another package CONVPK that
effectively does the ORD function (and other such likes). CONVPK
itself is not portable, but at least the developer can concentrate
all the non-portable stuff in one place.

If you care about efficiency (and I have seen few places where the
bit diddling *really* needs to be efficient), the fastest way I can think
of is to have a table of 256  8-bit textstrings indexed by the byte
value (+1 of course).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lGJX1.647$Kp5.4184786@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <IpuX1.514$Kp5.3758254@news1.atlantic.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-FKeWfbX9ZX7d@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>
>It's amazing how similar your solution and Judsons are at the base
>level.
>
There is not much that can be done differently and remain portable!

>Now, which is more efficient <G> (Using the functions, as you and
>Judson did, or not using them as Leif did?)
>
The intrinsic functions ORD and CHAR are NOT efficient in MF3.2.
They use calls to library subprograms.

I will agree with Leif that a table lookup is probably the fastest
means for unpacking.

Unrolling the perform with a large compute would be about the
fastest way to pack the bits.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70nf07$5lh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

Wrote the following in response, then came back and read your
message again ... did not notice the SET requirement the first time.
Nevertheless, I will post it.  I extended it to work for all binary, not
just a byte (*that* at least was deliberate, not a bug<S>)

>Take a byte and separate it into bits - using whatever indicators you
>like for the bits - the ultimate goal is to be able to query a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. "Setbits".
       AUTHOR. D. L. Tees.
       COMMENTS.
                Convert binary number to Display format.
                Input 9(18) usage Binary.
                Output X(64), all positions "1" or "0".
                Call "Setbits" using Input Output.
       SECURITY.
                Public domain, 1998, Donald L. Tees.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. Up to 64 bit.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. Up to 64 bit.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77 LEFTMOST-BITS                PICTURE 9(18) USAGE BINARY.
       77 CURRENT-BIT                  PICTURE 9 USAGE BINARY.
       77 BIT-TEST-NUMBER              PICTURE 99.
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01 BINARY-NUMBER-IN             PICTURE 9(18) USAGE BINARY.
       01 BIT-ARRAY-OUT.
           05 BIT-NUMBER               PICTURE X OCCURS 64 TIMES.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING BINARY-NUMBER-IN BIT-ARRAY-OUT.
       MAIN-LINE.
           MOVE BINARY-NUMBER-IN TO LEFTMOST-BITS.
           PERFORM TEST-EACH-BIT VARYING BIT-TEST-NUMBER
                                 FROM 64 BY -1
                                 UNTIL
                   BIT-TEST-NUMBER IS LESS THAN 1.
           EXIT PROGRAM.

       TEST-EACH-BIT.
           DIVIDE LEFTMOST-BITS BY 2 GIVING LEFTMOST-BITS
                                     REMAINDER CURRENT-BIT.
           MOVE CURRENT-BIT TO BIT-NUMBER (BIT-TEST-NUMBER).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362f9a8f.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <70nf07$5lh$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Here is an optimization of Donald's code that stops when there
are no more one-bits left.  Very few numbers will use the full 64 bits.

Donald Tees wrote in message <70nf07$5lh$1@news.igs.net>...
>Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>>Standard COBOL - should work across compilers - assume Ansi 85 with 89
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>           MOVE BINARY-NUMBER-IN TO LEFTMOST-BITS.
====>  MOVE ZEROES TO BIT-ARRAY-OUT
>           PERFORM TEST-EACH-BIT VARYING BIT-TEST-NUMBER
>                                 FROM 64 BY -1
>                                 UNTIL
>******           BIT-TEST-NUMBER IS LESS THAN 1.
====>          LEFTMOST-BITS = ZERO.


>           EXIT PROGRAM.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70oc2r$ste$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <70nf07$5lh$1@news.igs.net> <362f9a8f.0@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <362f9a8f.0@news1.ibm.net>...
>Here is an optimization of Donald's code that stops when there
>are no more one-bits left.  Very few numbers will use the full 64 bits.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: X"F4" & X"F5"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362fbf22.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<70lddo$bh3$1@supernews.com> <%XqX1.760$Se2.1426437@news4.mia.bellsouth.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-65hqB1xjV2ld@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <70nf07$5lh$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I've modified Donald's program (shown at bottom) to work on
a byte value up to value 255.
Since division is slow, I show how it can all be done by subtraction
and addition.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID. SETBITS.
000300 AUTHOR.       TEES.
000400
000500 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000600 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000700 SOURCE-COMPUTER. UP-TO-8-BIT.
000800 OBJECT-COMPUTER. UP-TO-8-BIT.
000900
001000 DATA DIVISION.
001100
001200 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001300 77  LEFTMOST-BITS                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
001400 77  BIT-TEST-NUMBER              PIC S9(3)  COMP.
001500
001600 LINKAGE SECTION.
001700 01  BINARY-NUMBER-IN             PIC S9(3)  COMP.
001800 01  BIT-ARRAY-OUT.
001900     02 BIT-NUMBER                PIC X OCCURS 8 TIMES.
002000
002100 PROCEDURE DIVISION
002200     USING BINARY-NUMBER-IN
002300           BIT-ARRAY-OUT.
002400 MAIN-LINE.
002500     MOVE BINARY-NUMBER-IN TO LEFTMOST-BITS
002600     PERFORM TEST-EACH-BIT
002700       VARYING BIT-TEST-NUMBER FROM 1 BY +1
002800         UNTIL BIT-TEST-NUMBER > 8
002900     .
003000 RETURN-TO-CALLER.
003100     EXIT PROGRAM
003200     .
003300
003400 TEST-EACH-BIT.
003500     IF LEFTMOST-BITS > 127
003600         SUBTRACT 128 FROM LEFTMOST-BITS
003700         MOVE "1" TO BIT-NUMBER (BIT-TEST-NUMBER)
003800     ELSE
003900         MOVE "0" TO BIT-NUMBER (BIT-TEST-NUMBER)
004000     .
004100     ADD LEFTMOST-BITS TO LEFTMOST-BITS
004200     .

Donald Tees wrote in message <70nf07$5lh$1@news.igs.net>...
>

…[56 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>

## Thread: IBM Cobol for MVS: untangle different versions?!? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM Cobol for MVS: untangle different versions?!?

- **From:** dreich@docscience.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70lk8l$tim$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
We're using IBM Cobol for MVS v1.2.1 on our new p390 running IBM MVS OS/390
v2.4 which I believe is less than a year old.

Our customer down under in Australia reports they have IBM VS Cobol II v1.5
which at first glance I thought was a newer version since the # is higher,
duh.

Searching the Internet I find IBM Visual Age Cobol.

We're also using AcuCobol v3.2.1 for Windows and Unix.

Can anyone shed some light on the history of the different versions of IBM MVS
Cobol?
Which came first?
What is the latest?
How much does the upgrade cost?
I'm trying to endorse our code with a specific compiler and need to be more
specific on the vocabulary "IBM MVS Cobol compiler required" is getting me in
trouble for being too vague. Thanks.

Our StrSize function uses REVERSE to get around TALLY's lack of a TRAILING
option like AcuCobol's version. REVERSE is missing from the other version of
Cobol.

To be fair, IBM's Cobol compiler gives a lot more warning messages that help
clean up the code over AcuCobol's but it would be nice if the error messages
made any sense...

Darrell Reich
Human Interface Design Engineer
Document Sciences Corporation
San Diego, CA, USA
dreich@docscience.com
http://www.docscience.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Cobol for MVS: untangle different versions?!?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70ln31$kbt$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70lk8l$tim$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
