```
>To be fair, IBM's Cobol compiler gives a lot more warning messages that
help
>clean up the code over AcuCobol's but it would be nice if the error
messages
>made any sense...


How come every Cobol on the market provides full Docs in machine
form, usually complete with a search engine, yet still persists in giving
4 character codes as error messages, and expects you to have memorised
all 8 thousand of them?
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Cobol for MVS: untangle different versions?!?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981021193050.12918.00004090@ng117.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<70lk8l$tim$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Darrell Reich writes ...

>We're using IBM Cobol for MVS v1.2.1 on our new p390 running IBM MVS OS/390
>v2.4 which I believe is less than a year old.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>trouble for being too vague. 

You know, if you're a consultant or software supplier (and it sounds like you
are from the context of your message), it seems to me it would be a good idea
to be current with what's going on in the market. There are a lot of ways to
find out and places to keep up. It's always embarrassing when you're not sure
what you're talking about and the customer is paying such a nice price.

For the MVS, OS/390 world, the most recent COBOL compilers from IBM are:

VS COBOL II - all versions and releases support both the ANSI 68 and ANSI 74
standards, using appropriate compiler settings
 Version 3 and later support the ANSI 85 standard (but not the '89 amendment to
that standard, which was mainly intrinsic functions)

COBOL/370 - version 1 release 1 of a new compiler; this and all subsequent
releases require LE (Language Environment) to run; this is the first
IBM-supplied mainframe compiler to support intrinsic functions

COBOL for MVS & VM - version 1 release 2 of the new compiler; added a ton of
new features, many of them anticipated in the next ANSI standard;

COBOL for OS/390 & VM - version 2 release 1 of the new compiler; only some
minor enhancements, from an applications programmer perspective.

There is a PTF that can update both COBOL for MVS & VM and COBOL for OS/390 &
VM to support two new formats of ACCEPT and three new intrinsic functions, all
of which enhance Y2K support in the language.

Training is available, check the web site listed below.

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Cobol for MVS: untangle different versions?!?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70o7eb$r8f@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70lk8l$tim$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
See comments at bottom of post.


dreich@docscience.com wrote in message <70lk8l$tim$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>We're using IBM Cobol for MVS v1.2.1 on our new p390 running IBM MVS OS/390
>v2.4 which I believe is less than a year old.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Can anyone shed some light on the history of the different versions of IBM
MVS
>Cobol?
>Which came first?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>specific on the vocabulary "IBM MVS Cobol compiler required" is getting me
in
>trouble for being too vague. Thanks.
>


There is no such thing as VS COBOL II R1.5.  The last version of VS COBOL II
was V1.4.

The order of IBM MVS compilers is:

ANS COBOL (V4 was the last version in the early '80s)
OS/VS COBOL (R2.4 was the last version dropped in the mid-90's)
VS COBOL II (r1.0, R1.1, R2.0, R3.0 R3.1, R3.2, R4.0 - support for R4.0 will
end in March 2001)
AD/Cycle COBOL/370 (1st compiler with Intrinsic Functions - support already
dropped)
IBM COBOL for MVS & VM (still supported, 1st compiler with OO support)
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (most current compiler)

The last 3 listed require LE (Language Environment as their run-time)
The last 2 listed have an available add-on called MLE (Millenium Language
Extensions) for additional Y2K date handling.
All products listed from VS COBOL II on have an available add-on product for
Report Writer support.

For the PC (not originally, but currently for both OS/2 and Windows), IBM
VisualAge for COBOL is available which use the identical "front-end" to the
current IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM product  - so syntax support is identical
(except when it isn't - for example LINE SEQUENTIAL support is only
available on the PC).  The PC product does NOT support the mainframe CMPR2
compiler option so no ANS'74 support is available on the PC.

ANSI support:
   ANS V4, OS/VS COBOL (with LANGLVL(1)) support ANS'68
   ANS V4, OS/VS COBOL (with LANGLVL(2)), VS COBOL II, and IBM COBOL for
this-and-that (with CMPR2) support ANS'74
   VS COBOL II and higher all support ANS'85
   COBOL/370 and higher support ANS'85 *plus* Intrinsic Functions
   IBM COBOL for this-and-that support ANS'85 *plus* Intrinsic Functions
*plus* Corrections Amendment.

    ****

Hopefully, this answers your question (in more detail than you really
wanted).

For AIX, there is a comparable product - but I haven't seen it being
upgraded as frequently as the PC product.
```

## Thread: Question on JCL DD statement for EXPDT. — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Question on JCL DD statement for EXPDT.

- **From:** lcy <lcy@interlog.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362E9F6D.8E7EBB09@interlog.com>`

```
Folks,

When we need to create a dataset, we can use the parameter EXPDT to set
the expiry date for the Dataset in DD statement. According to the manual
if EXPDT can be YY/DDD or YYYY/DDD, DDD is 000 to 366, if it is equal to
99365 or 99366, the dataset will never be expired. However, is there any
special meaning for 99000? Thanks.

Regards,
lcy
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on JCL DD statement for EXPDT.

- **From:** "Vincent DiGioia" <vmdigioia@digioia.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iVHX1.170$K4.3474767@news.abs.net>`
- **References:** `<362E9F6D.8E7EBB09@interlog.com>`

```
The expiration date defaults to the system default (30, 60, 90 or whatever
your system folks set the parameter at).

Vincent
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on JCL DD statement for EXPDT.

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362f550c.84207343@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<362E9F6D.8E7EBB09@interlog.com>`

```
On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 22:58:53 -0400, lcy <lcy@interlog.com> wrote:

>Folks,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>lcy


Yes, there is.  The UCC-1 (oops, my age is showing) with CA-1 the EXPDT=98000 and 99000
had special meaning for tape datasets, like EXPDT=98000 means tape comes from outside the
datacenter's tape library


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question on JCL DD statement for EXPDT.

- **From:** Clifton Ivy <clif@purdue.edu>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362F9F73.55427D23@purdue.edu>`
- **References:** `<362E9F6D.8E7EBB09@interlog.com> <362f550c.84207343@news3.ibm.net>`

```
I expect any special meaning would depend on the system you are using to
keep track of datasets -- in Volker's example below, these are tape
datasets.

We also have UCC-1 with CA-1, and the 99000 means keep the dataset as
long as it is cataloged.  Daily a job runs that checks to see what tape
datasets are past their expiration date; if the expiration date is 99000
and the dataset is still cataloged, then it is kept; if the dataset is
not cataloged, the tape (in the tape management system) is marked as
"scratch", available to be written on.

There are other special values, all of which are probably going to be
problems between now and 12/31/1999.  Ah, well, it probably seemed such
a good idea at the time!  For instance, 99365 means the tape is to be
kept forever (or maybe just until 1/1/2000!!!).

Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 21 Oct 1998 22:58:53 -0400, lcy <lcy@interlog.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> (BSP GmbH)
```

## Thread: JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset

- **From:** stephen_knapp@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Sorry if this is somewhat off-topic but there is no good newsgroup (that I
know of) for JCL discussion.

 We have several jobs that run every business and append their output to an
accumulated month to date dataset. Some of these produce so much output that
by the end of the month the dataset spans 5 cartridges.

Recently we've been getting a lot of tape errors. Looking at the job, I can
see that although all we really need to do is write some records to the 5th
cratridge (the end of the dataset), all five cartridges are monted one after
the other until the end is reached. This happens even though the DISP=MOD

Given that I can't code a particular Volume (because on the first of the month
I don't have one), any ideas how to get to last volume of a tape dataset
without going thru all of the other volumes?

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362e63b8.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

stephen_knapp@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Sorry if this is somewhat off-topic but there is no good newsgroup (that I
>know of) for JCL discussion.
>
> We have several jobs that run every business and append their output to an
>accumulated month to date dataset. Some of these produce so much output
that
>by the end of the month the dataset spans 5 cartridges.
>
>Recently we've been getting a lot of tape errors. Looking at the job, I can

What sort of tape errors?  Can you please post the exact MVS message or
system error?

>see that although all we really need to do is write some records to the 5th
>cratridge (the end of the dataset), all five cartridges are monted one
after
>the other until the end is reached. This happens even though the DISP=MOD
>
>Given that I can't code a particular Volume (because on the first of the
month
>I don't have one), any ideas how to get to last volume of a tape dataset
>without going thru all of the other volumes?


Now as to this, in order to read any record on a tape dataset (in MVS), you
must first read through every other record; so yes, if you had a 47 volume
tape dataset, and only wanted to add records to the end, the tape device
would have to read all the way through all 47 tapes to the last record
before whatever program you're using could write new records.  I'm not sure
if MAGSTAR or the other new virtual tape devices get by this, but on old
devices where your dataset is on actual physical tapes, the devices need to
go through every tape in a multi-volume dataset.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70lrc8$cvv$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


stephen_knapp@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> Sorry if this is somewhat off-topic but there is no good newsgroup (that I
> know of) for JCL discussion.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the other until the end is reached. This happens even though the DISP=MOD

The DISP=MOD has no effect on that because the DST is comprised of
multiple VOL. SER numbers. With out identifying a specific volume you will
always get all of the tapes, operators can't guess which one to mount so
they start from 1 until all have been mounted. And if your shop has an
automated mounting device machines can't guess either.

>
>
> Given that I can't code a particular Volume (because on the first of the month
> I don't have one), any ideas how to get to last volume of a tape dataset
> without going thru all of the other volumes?

You might have an option if you set up a separate job for the first work day,
first day of the month. But you still will need to keep track of all of the volume

numbers to know which one to mount. That is not very nice or very easy to
accomplish. In fact it is very messy. I have seen something like this and it was
not pretty.

I will see what I can find on this but I don't think you really want to do
this, IMO.

>
>
> -----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
> http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70lrph$cvv$2@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70lrc8$cvv$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Here is an interesting thought:

   Assuming that you can actually accomplish this and you just
update the last tape of a group of tapes until the end of the month.
What would you do if you found out that say tape 3 out of 5 is
bad. How would you know what was on that tape and even more
important how would you recreate that tape??

This sounds like a lot of potential problems, IMO.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362E8C69.3C162711@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I can't comment on the tape errors you received since you did not identify what
they were. As for as the job itself, you may want to create daily tapes, and have
a weekly merge job to combine these tapes,and a monthly job to combine the weekly
tapes. In general, I use SORT  with OPTION COPY for these kinds of jobs. It's much
faster, gives you record counts, and will abend on I/O errors. Also, check that
the JCL has BLKSIZE=0.

stephen_knapp@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> Sorry if this is somewhat off-topic but there is no good newsgroup (that I
> know of) for JCL discussion.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@getridofspam.worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70muit$14e@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <362E8C69.3C162711@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Mr Triton's advice is spot on. Your current JCL setup is amateurish. Ditch
it.
Set up three sets of JCL, giving you a daily, a weekly and a monthly
procedure.
Also, set up a 5-entry daily GDG, a 5-entry weekly GDG, and a 12 entry
monthly GDG.
The rest of it should be easy enough to figure out from Mr Triton's post.

John Trifon wrote in message <362E8C69.3C162711@ix.netcom.com>...
>I can't comment on the tape errors you received since you did not identify
what
>they were. As for as the job itself, you may want to create daily tapes,
and have
>a weekly merge job to combine these tapes,and a monthly job to combine the
weekly
>tapes. In general, I use SORT  with OPTION COPY for these kinds of jobs.
It's much
>faster, gives you record counts, and will abend on I/O errors. Also, check
that
>the JCL has BLKSIZE=0.
>
>stephen_knapp@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>
(clipped)
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL for MODing to MTD Multi-volume Tape dataset

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362E6136.2ACE9427@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<70la89$fd6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Are you using  UNIT=AFF=prev-ddname  and the VOL=REF=*.prev-ddname ?

try this and your job may run smoothly.
```

## Thread: OT warning — 2 msgs

```
No, the poster is an IT recruiter with a vast array of skills, and DB@ is
the new database from IBM specifically for IT recruiting firms.

SOme of the features:

1. H-1 table dynamic table build by country and rate;
2. the "margin" data field that calculates the optimal processing margin so
that the recruiter can bill higher and offer you a lower rate;
3. Computer skills tables that "speak" the correct way of saying a funny
acronym (like CICS) to the prospect when recruiting;

oh, this isn't as funny as i thought i could make it . . .

Vincent
```

<a id="t-e9d20db74e"></a>
## Thread: Query — 3 msgs

### Query

- **From:** dcrow10250@aol.com (DCrow10250)
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981021005507.24010.00002451@ng58.aol.com>`

```
Hello all,
I am a "one-year" COBOL programmer who's been working in a fairly large
division of one of the top three IT services companies in the U.S.  I do not
have a degree in CS, but took, rather, a 6-week COBOL training class at the
local University, and was hired by these folks, for which I am very
appreciative.  I've been doing batch mainframe conversions this past year, and
been learning what I can on the fly.  
     I may have an opportunity to work for a different large company that is
developing an intranet using COOLGEN.  They say it's cutting edge technology,
and will provide training in this, and DB2, in order to get up to speed with
the rest of the team.
     My question is this...should I take this opportunity, if offered, and run
the risk of losing the basic COBOL O.T.J. training that I need, including the
COBOL IMS project I've been assigned to, or go for this COOLGEN stuff, with
DB2, and who knows what else?  
     I would appreciate any feedback from some of the long-timers out there
regarding this silly newbie predicament.  Thanks.
    /)
~~~~~
sail on!
```

#### ↳ Re: Query

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70kgn5$llv$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<19981021005507.24010.00002451@ng58.aol.com>`

```


DCrow10250 wrote:

> Hello all,

Hello guy...

>
> I am a "one-year" COBOL programmer who's been working in a fairly large
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> been learning what I can on the fly.

A degree CS is very desirable but not necessary.

>
>      I may have an opportunity to work for a different large company that is
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> sail on!

So it's Cobol/IMS vs COOLGEN/DB2. Since you have about a years worth of
Cobol experience I would suggest that you get some DB2 experience
under you belt and not concentrate on just Cobol/IMS. Once you get
labeled an IMS programmer it seems harder to switch over to DB2.
At least what I have seen at several large Insurance companies.

Good Luck.:-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Query

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981021120820.19974.00003643@ng02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<70kgn5$llv$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
You're lucky. You are choosing between two good options.

IMS experience makes you valuable. DB2 experience makes you valuable.
Additional IMS experience makes you more valuable then initial DB2 experience,
IMHO. But its a close call, because a mix of experience makes you more valuable
then anything else. (It's just that you really need to get solid in each area,
and the second choice does not solidify your current forte).

Longevity of service at a single company also makes you more valuable. So don't
jump unless there is a quantitative basis.

Something is missing here though. You have said nothing about an increase in
pay. That does not make sense. Programmers increase in value very rapidly
during the first several years of experience.

Almost all companies are entirely unable to match this value increase with pay
increase. So the way to get your fair share early is to move.

It is not unusual for companies to loose folks at the 12-month or 18-month
point (newbies eventually wake-up). 

But you have not mentioned the pay factor. So there is perhaps a problem here.
This is a guess, but I am thinking you are not shopping well enough.

You should move when the new pay matches the skill level you have acquired
already. And if it is a concern to you that you have IMS and DB2, then make
that your goal. Move only to a situation where you can continue to build the
IMS experience and add DB2.

In other words, just to play devil's advocate, a weakness here is that you are
making a choice with OR logic. You should use AND logic. You are the one with
the talent.  You can call the tune.  You just have to believe in strength of
your position.

But I do not mean to be presumptuous, nor do I invite you to comment on the pay
issue, since you have not elected to do so.  Infact, if the pay is better at
the new spot, then that plus the additional DB2 skills would be heavy
indicators.

Early in your DP career the only way to get fair increases is to jump. But you
do not want to jump too often (that will hurt you).  So jump when the
remuneration warrants it, and jump to skill combinations (not just to new
skills).

Best Wishes for a good decision.


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

---

<a id="t-3d4ec11e5e"></a>
## Thread: Length of Dynamic SQL string in COBOL pgm — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Length of Dynamic SQL string in COBOL pgm

- **From:** "Blair McDonald" <bmcdonald@qquest.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.db2-l,comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70ktp6$4l4$1@news0-alterdial.uu.net>`

```
I'm working on developing a COBOL program for OS/390 on DB2 version 5 that
uses dynamic SQL. On the PREPARE statement, I'm getting an SQL error -134,
which implies that my host variable containing the built SQL string is too
long. Messages and Codes says that this error means that the host variable
can't be over 254 characters long.

I can't believe that there's no way for me to build an SQL statement over
254 characters and dynamically execute it. Can anyone give me a hint about
what I might be doing wrong? Could this be some "installation"-set limit
that I'm running into? Has anyone built dynamic SQL that was, say, 1000
characters long?


Please reply here or email me at:

bmcdonald@qquest.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Length of Dynamic SQL string in COBOL pgm

- **From:** "Ed Zell" <ewzell@imonline.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.db2-l,comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70l5nr$ii6$1@sol.pdnt.net>`
- **References:** `<70ktp6$4l4$1@news0-alterdial.uu.net>`

```
>I can't believe that there's no way for me to build an SQL statement over
>254 characters and dynamically execute it. Can anyone give me a hint about
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Hi Blair,

  I can't speak for DB/2 OS/390, but on DB/2 for VM and VSE, we do this
  all the time.  Here is a bit of code from one of our COBOL programs:


 01  DYNAMICSQL.
     49  DYNAMIC-SELECT-LENGTH   PIC S9(4)  COMP VALUE +300.
     49  DYNAMIC-SELECT-DATA        PIC X(300).


............................................................................
.......................


        MOVE 'PREPARE   ' TO SQL-EYECATCH.
        EXEC SQL PREPARE ALPHASELECT
                 FROM :DYNAMICSQL
                 END-EXEC.

        IF SQLCODE NOT = ZERO
           PERFORM 9999-UNEXPECTED-SQL-ERROR.


  I also check several other programs, and in one of them we use 1500
characters
  for the host variable used in the prepare.  Must be something else that is
wrong
  because my manuals say 8k is the max size of the SQL statement to be
prepped.


Ed Zell
Illinois Mutual Life
ewzell@imonline.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Length of Dynamic SQL string in COBOL pgm

- **From:** "Svein Gr���e" <graee@online.no>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.db2-l,comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70l5v5$bct$1@readme.online.no>`
- **References:** `<70ktp6$4l4$1@news0-alterdial.uu.net>`

```
Try this definition of your variable contaning the statement.

       01 selstmt.
           49 selstmt-length     pic s9(4) comp-5 value 0.
           49 stmt                     pic x(2000).
```

<a id="t-5dd00cdc9c"></a>
## Thread: Length of Dynamic SQL in COBOL pgm — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Length of Dynamic SQL in COBOL pgm

- **From:** "Blair McDonald" <bmcdonald@qquest.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.db2-l,comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70ku42$527$1@news0-alterdial.uu.net>`

```
Apologies if I've posted twice...

I'm working on developing a COBOL program for OS/390 on DB2 version 5 that
uses dynamic SQL. On the PREPARE statement, I'm getting an SQL error -134,
which implies that my host variable containing the built SQL string is too
long. Messages and Codes says that this error means that the host variable
can't be over 254 characters long.

I can't believe that there's no way for me to build an SQL statement over
254 characters and dynamically execute it. Can anyone give me a hint about
what I might be doing wrong? Could this be some "installation"-set limit
that I'm running into? Has anyone built dynamic SQL that was, say, 1000
characters long?

Please reply here or email me at:
bmcdonald@qquest.com
```

#### ↳ Re:Length of Dynamic SQL in COBOL pgm

- **From:** Duane Lee - ATCX <DLee@mail.maricopa.gov>
- **Date:** 1998-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<44C2247EAFF4D011B0BD00805F85B09C0CBD85CF@news.maricopa.gov>`
- **References:** `<70ku42$527$1@news0-alterdial.uu.net>`

```
It appears you have some other problem.  If you look at Appendix A of
the SQL Reference manual you will see that the limit on an SQL statement
is 32765 bytes (page 467). You need to check your code for some other
possible problem.  A CHAR defined string has a limit of 254 (page 465)
so check your variables.

Good luck.
Duane

> -----Original Message-----
> From:	Blair McDonald [SMTP:bmcdonald@qquest.com]
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: entry-level COBOL Programmers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<715o5i$ng7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981022060548.09408.00004263@ng19.aol.com> <3632773e.510089@nntp.urban.ne.jp> <36328082.0@news1.ibm.net> <36346cde.928397@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3635b62f.2417458@nntp.urban.ne.jp>`

```
Shat T Cat wrote in message <3635b62f.2417458@nntp.urban.ne.jp>...

>     This is also something I have been worried about.  I have done
>some coding myself over the years (nothing more than a few hundred
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>documented experience how do I break into the field?


The only documented experience worth a damn is code.  If you
show an employer a best selling program that you wrote, you will
get a job.  Of course, then you will also have one ... you can write
programs for yourself.  I notice Skidmike in here who has never
even taken a course, to my knowledge.  I would bet money that
if he asked for an entry level job in here, he would get one.  He
writes code.  You can tell.  He can show it to me. I can talk code
with him.  It is documented. Forget about talking about getting a
job.  Talk about code.  'course Mike already has a job.  He has
code that he is writing ...

It sounds to me like you do not really want to be a Cobol programmer.
What you want is to have a job as a Cobol programmer.  It is not
the same thing.
```

```
G Moore writes ...

>
>i already do that. i tell them i can give them all the source code i
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

>plenty of ideas for cobol projects, but i'd prefer entry level work

<snip>

Well, not intending to be contentious, perhaps it is your communication style.
I know that COBOL is case insensitive, but English is not. Similarly constructs
such as "u r seeking ..." may be cool to you but they may strike people in
business as lazy.

There are companies hiring entry level COBOL programmers. Maybe you're hurting
your case by how you present yourself.

Good luck,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: entry-level COBOL Programmers

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36375E8B.96584657@home.com>`
- **References:** `<36369d0b.448682@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <19981028103614.16069.00000506@ng127.aol.com> <363751ef.759557@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> >There are companies hiring entry level COBOL programmers. Maybe you're hurting
> >your case by how you present yourself.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that way. at least, thats why i think posts were written that way.

In the old days we did quite a few things differently.  We used ALTERs
in our COBOL for efficiency sake.  We had in-line code for efficiency
sake.  But times change, CPU time isn't the most expensive item.  Disk
space isn't the 2nd most expensive thing (we forget whey we left out
centuries out of our dates).  Programmer time, and business opportunity
costs are what are important now.

If you show your prospective employer that you are concerned with
yesterday's technologies and short-cuts, how will he think?  Clarity of
prose is much more highly rated now.
```

```
barely 2,000 years old.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: entry-level COBOL Programmers

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71a2fp$h8o$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981022060548.09408.00004263@ng19.aol.com> <3632773e.510089@nntp.urban.ne.jp> <36328082.0@news1.ibm.net> <36346cde.928397@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3635b62f.2417458@nntp.urban.ne.jp> <715o5i$ng7$1@news.igs.net> <363f607e.4389140@nntp.urban.ne.jp>`

```

Shat T Cat wrote in message <363f607e.4389140@nntp.urban.ne.jp>...

>     Donald, I understand what you are saying, and in some ways you
>may be right.  At the moment, I am fully employed as a Lead Operator
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>(whichever comes first).


I do not know of *any* profession that you get the job first, and then
get the qualifications afterwards.  For a brief while, about thirty years
back, you might have been able to do that with programming, but
that was because there were no "qualifications" at the time.  Even
then, the people that became prominent at it usually had some
qualifications, even if only a degree in a related field.

I am not saying that you have to have a degree in programming to break
into the field, but in fact, that is the easiest way.  The only other way is
to prove yourself through work you have done.

As to the preaching, if you do not want to hear the truth, why ask?
Even a truck driver would tell you the same thing. You learn to drive,
THEN you get a job as a driver, not the other way around. Programming
is at least as skilled as truck driving, and a company does not want
an amateur learning on productions systems for the same reasons.
The potential for damage is simply too great.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: entry-level COBOL Programmers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-u1Wdj7rskvQ5@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<19981022060548.09408.00004263@ng19.aol.com> <3632773e.510089@nntp.urban.ne.jp> <36328082.0@news1.ibm.net> <36346cde.928397@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <3635b62f.2417458@nntp.urban.ne.jp>`

```

>      This is also something I have been worried about.  I have done
> some coding myself over the years (nothing more than a few hundred
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> documented experience how do I break into the field?

Write a structured COBOL program, print the listing and take it with 
you to the interview.  Be ready to explain it -- why and how you did 
things.

I started in operations.  You have some necessary experience - you 
understand the operation - and this is a STRENGTH.

Good Luck.
```

### Sockets with NetExpress

- **From:** dcantua@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70nnnl$5pp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I need a way to get the COBOL program to communicate with another system using
a Berkley Socket Connection.

COBOL Program.
    Operating System:   WindowNT 4.0
    COBOL Software:     MicroFocus NetExpress 2.0

Any Help will be appreciated.

Daniel G. Cantua

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: Sockets with NetExpress

- **From:** notrleech@sprynet.com (Raymond W. Leech, III)
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.109ba192e23582e7989681@news.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<70nnnl$5pp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <70nnnl$5pp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, dcantua@my-dejanews.com 
says...
> I need a way to get the COBOL program to communicate with another system using
> a Berkley Socket Connection.
[snip]

I have had success using a free library Tcp4u by Philippe Jounin. Try 
http://perso.magic.fr/jounin-ph/man/tcp4u.htm (very slow to load but 
always latest version). Search AltaVista on tcp4u for the mirror sites. 
He also offers a free FTP library.

Gene Webb suggested I look at Smart Sockets from Talarian but I can't 
seem to find the URl just now. I chose tcp4u/ftp4w because it was free 
(I'm cheap).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to C conversion tools

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362F77F5.2E12D226@home.com>`
- **References:** `<70nrmk$a0r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
> I am looking for tools to assist in the conversion of COBOL to C.  Any help in
> locating them will be appreciated.

I've never been a fan of such converted code.  The only place I have
seen it work is where you have a macro-assembler which looked like Basic
- so that the maintenance people were, for all effects, maintaining the
same code.

I have seen RPG converted to COBOL.  Atrocious code!

It is better to re-spec the program and write in C using C's strengths.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to C conversion tools

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3632aecf.1139803@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70nrmk$a0r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
i would not under *any* circumstances convert COBOL to C.
C lacks alot of standards COBOL has.also, your COBOL may be system
dependant, so any conversions would probably hang on those
dependancies.

on the other hand, converting from asm to C is a good thing, if you
have the people to do it.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

- **References:** `<70nrmk$a0r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3632aecf.1139803@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
>i would not under *any* circumstances convert COBOL to C.

I tend to agree.  Any system well suited to COBOL is not likely to be
improved in any way by conversion to C.
```

```
Hi,
I am a college student that doesn't have much experience with languages
except a shaky background in C.  I am now in a COBOL class and I am
having major problems learning the language.  I have bought  additional
books to supplement my text, but I just can't seem to get the concepts
to sink in.  Can anyone offer me some study tips and/or advice?  Right
now I am having trouble coding a multi-page report with totals.

Thank you,
Allison Mahady
arm@sgi.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Please Help--having trouble learning COBOL

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981024112712.12529.00000523@ng140.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<362FA031.60CA5563@pisces.bv.sgi.net>`

```

Alison,

You are not the only person who has stumbled in learning computer languages. 
The answer to your situation is to CODE.

It is like a bicycle.  Just keep trying until you get it.  It does take time.
Actually it takes lots of time. But as you improve, you do not sense it as
taking time, you sense it as fun.

The extra books you have bought are a good idea. In COBOL, examples are a very
good idea.  But you need to code.  You need machine time, or as an aviator
friend of mine says, you are after stick time.

Looking at the enigne is not enough, you need to feel what it is like to be
propelled by an engine that you can control. It changes the gestalt utterly.

CODE, CODE, CODE!

Programming can be a surprising source of emotional engagement. For example,
code can be beautiful and code can be ugly. You can write code that your are
proud of, and though no one out here in cyberspace will ever admit it, it is
possible to write code that is postively shiftless.

And being as how it is close to holloween, I should mention to you that
programming can also be very,  very scary.  Especially when you put you code
into production.

Welcome to your new world. Whatever your fate at school, I guaran-damn-tee-ya
that when you leave school you will enter a realm categorically dominated by
these devices. You would be well advised to master them. There is no road back.


Face it. It is at the keyboard. And since everything you are facing there is a
projection, you are facing yourself. The way to deal with that aspect of self 
is to CODE. Ya just gotta be active, it is a biderectional thingy, 'tain't like
TV.

CODE!

PRODUCE!

GENERATE!

BE A SOURCE!


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Please Help--having trouble learning COBOL

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3631fe66.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<362FA031.60CA5563@pisces.bv.sgi.net> <19981024112712.12529.00000523@ng140.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981024112712.12529.00000523@ng140.aol.com>...
>Alison,
>The answer to your situation is to CODE.
>Face it. It is at the keyboard. And since everything you are facing there
is a
>projection, you are facing yourself. The way to deal with that aspect of
self
>is to CODE. Ya just gotta be active, it is a biderectional thingy, 'tain't
like
>TV.


Very good words Robert! very true.
It also helps to READ others code to see how
it is done, to be exposed to various styles, etc.

There are several places on the net, where you
(Allison) can download code examples to learn
from. One such is http://www.etk.com especially
the download section (ETKPAK).

Good luck Allison, and, as Robert said: keep at it!
```

## Thread: Technical Support Manager — 2 msgs

### Technical Support Manager

- **From:** avalon <avalon@avalonjobs.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362F4FB9.130@avalonjobs.com>`

```
Position: Technical Support Manager

Experience: Responsible for support of production activities, ensuring
that hardware, applications and communications are optimally configured
for the use of outside customers.  Will manage all aspects of the AS/400
environment and the areas it supports.  Also will be responsible for
staffing, evaluations, and coordination of the computer operations
staff.  Must have a strong background in AS/400 operations in a
distribution environment, strong technical skills (COBOL, CLP,
Query/400, PC/LAN, TCP/IP, FTP, and IPX), and a proven record of
successful management abilities. BS in CS or equivalent degree is
mandatory along with at least 3 yrs. Of technical management experience.

Location: Western Burbs

Salary:   To $85,000

Contact: Jennifer Weisman
	Avalon Human Resources, Inc.
	Phone: 630-836-1255
	Fax: 630-836-1266
	E-Mail: avalon@avalonjobs.com
```

<a id="t-85c2f79a62"></a>
## Thread: COBOL to CICS — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL to CICS

- **From:** "Jason" <superj@door.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70o190$agh$1@supernews.com>`

```
I am working on a program that displays a map through cics.  The map sends
correctly, but when the program is run with it, the field where the user is
to make a selection always comes up with trash in it.  The field also
doesn't allow any entry into it (it is coded as unprotected in cics), been
stumped for weeks, need help, no-one else has been able to help.
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to CICS

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XSDS61CNBVQ8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<70o190$agh$1@supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 22 Oct 1998 05:04:56, "Jason" <superj@door.net> wrote:

> I am working on a program that displays a map through cics.  The map sends
> correctly, but when the program is run with it, the field where the user is
> to make a selection always comes up with trash in it.  The field also
> doesn't allow any entry into it (it is coded as unprotected in cics), been
> stumped for weeks, need help, no-one else has been able to help.


Make sure that you are pulling in the correct/updated version of the 
map copybook as generate by the BMS assembly.  If you are using some 
other tool for screen generation ensure you have the correct map 
copybook.

Maybe someone included the map copybook in working storage without 
using the copy statement, or you are pointing to a copy library that 
is not current.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to CICS

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36300AD1.646417D3@att.net>`
- **References:** `<70o190$agh$1@supernews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-XSDS61CNBVQ8@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 22 Oct 1998 05:04:56, "Jason" <superj@door.net> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> is not current.

I think Thane's suggestion is the best place to start. If possible, get
the correct source for the map and reassemble it (I'm assuming your BMS
proc does both DSECT and physical map in one run). If you have multiple
map libraries, i.e., the DSECT output from the map assembly, make sure
it's in the SYSLIB concatenation for the COBOL step and recompile your
program.

Something else I'd look at is to browse the loadlib where your physical
map is and turn HEX ON. The date the map was link edited is in (IBM)
Julian format at the end of the 3rd or 4th record in the load module.

Also, make sure you're compiling your program into a loadlib which is
available to the CICS region where you're testing, and insure that you
don't have a stale copy of your program or map in another loadlib in the
DFHRPL concatenation before the loadlib into which you're linking your
program in 1998.

Bill Lynch

PS: If your BMS proc doesn't do both MAP & DSECT passes in one job,
either update it, if you can, or make some noise. This is a maintenance
nightmare.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to CICS

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981023015601.23934.00001917@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<70o190$agh$1@supernews.com>`

```

In article <70o190$agh$1@supernews.com>, "Jason" <superj@door.net> writes:

>The map sends
>correctly, but when the program is run with it, the field where the user is
>to make a selection always comes up with trash in it.  The field also
>doesn't allow any entry into it (it is coded as unprotected in cics), been
>stumped for weeks, need help, no-one else has been able to help.

The types of things that can cause this problem are:

1. Uninitialized logical map (record layout) with garbage sitting in it and the
map is being sent without the MAPONLY. Fix by either doing ERASE MAPONLY (which
means what is on the screen before the SEND MAP is ignored and the record
layout is not used), or moving low values into the record layout for the map
before you start populating the record layout before the SEND MAP.

2. If there is a different map on the screen, you may (probably will) have to
put ERASE as one of the directives on the SEND MAP.

3. Discrepancies between the logical map (DSECT) and the physical map (MAP).
Make sure that you are pointing to the correct library to pull in the current
record layout. If the record layout is hand-coded, you may have to do a BMS
assembly for DSECT and then compare field sizes and atrributes between the two.

Good luck!
Mark A. Young
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to CICS

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3630424b.2121991@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<70o190$agh$1@supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 22 Oct 1998 00:04:56 -0500, "Jason" <superj@door.net> wrote:

>I am working on a program that displays a map through cics.  The map sends
>correctly, but when the program is run with it, the field where the user is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Jason,

assume your map is called MYMAP.  Then, as a first statement in your PROCEDURE DIVISION,
code:

	MOVE LOW-VALUES to mymapO

(note the letter O at the end of the name)

This should cure the problem

HTH



with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to CICS

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981023182746.12189.00000226@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3630424b.2121991@news3.ibm.net>`

```

This is a student context. Probably there is a lack of full initialization. 
The suggestion of moving LOW-VALUES is an excellent start.  But more generally,
the logical screen (that is the WS area in your program from which you are
going to SEND the screen) needs to be initialized properly before you send.

Very often this can be done by doing a RECEIVE first. That lucky situation
fills up the buffer with something you can immediately SEND back out. But this
engages a chicken and egg issue.

How can you receive a well formatted image of that mapped data if you have not
yet sent it?

Anyway, initialize it before you send it. Learn how to initialize it.

The stated problem includes the phrase, "... The map sends correctly ...". That
is not completely true.  If unprotected fields are protected (that is you can
not enter data into them), then you did not send it correctly. Probably because
you have not initialized it and there is garbage in the attribute fields.

The basic approach for the initial creation of a screen is to 1) allocate the
area for the screen (if that is not done implicitly by inclusion of the
copybook in WS, some programs use GETMAINs for this), 2) initialize it, then 3)
send it.

Once you are cycling through repeated use of a screen layout, you will be
reading the image into an allocated area, and do not need full
re-initialization.  But it can be necessary to do partial re-init (to
de-highlight previous temporary error fields etc., and to get control of the
cursor position).  

However, this cycling does not work too well on the data fields unless the user
has touched each field, or you get the Modified Data Tags (MDTs) under control.
 From your description, you are not at that stage yet.  But you may be.

You have an initial non trash value that you asked CICS for in the user
selection field, and that is contradicted by your description of trash in the
field.  CICS probably did not put trash there, it must be coming from your
program.  If you do not set the modified datatag automatically (by CICS
definition), AND you do not touch that field (and you say you can't), it will
not map back into your program. This is a crucial lesson.  

Even if data starts right and/or is established successfully by your program or
by user entry, upon some subsequent iteration if that field is not touched and
you have an absence of preset MDTs, it does not come into the next scheduling
of your program. If you send that screen back out, the good data on the screen
is replaced with the unmanaged data in the buffer from where you do your SEND.

So on the first iteration it is not a bad idea to initialize the whole buffer
with binary zeroes, but then put some character data into the text fields (such
as SPACES). Generally it is best to preset the MDTs to get the good data that
either you or CICS placed INITially upon the screen. Otherwise you need lots of
extra logic to retrieve from some convoluted place all the data that you are
going to 'remember' that you had put onto the screen.  In other words, use the
screen as your short-term memory.


Also whenever garbage comes into the program,clear it. That is different from
echoing user errors back to them. Real garbage you must clear. User errors are
policty decisions, echo back the error with diagnostics and highlighting, or
clear, and prompt all overagain.  But definitely clear actual garbage.

You are lucky that the screen is coming up.  You will no doubt encouter this
uninitialized situation again.  But as your screens get larger, the
uninitialized buffers become more likely sources of trash that will make the
screen PROG; becasue they have a binary value that looks like a plausible 3270
data stream command, but are illegal.  

If you are going to send a screen, every datafield should have reasonable data
in it before you SEND. The control attributes must have intentionally populated
values.

So initialize upon first construction.

Upon receipt and prep for re-sending, make certain every field has legitimate
values. That is edit everything, no matter what small subset you choose to
diagnose.  Edit every user field. Edit every user field, and make sure you edit
every user field BEFORE you send the screen back out. 

Take-over each user data field BEFORE ever sending out a screen. Every field
must have a value you establish or have reviewed.

Learn what MDTs are. Exploit them. Use the buffer inside of the 3270 (emulated
or not, or any other device) as your memory.  The way to remember is to map it
back in with MDTs.

You have had good suggestions from other posters about sending initially
MAPONLY.  That is very frequently perfect. But, also sometimes you want to send
that first screen from a buffer (initialized and controlled) to get default
values that very per context (such as operator ID, or some previous process
factor), which can not be coded as single initial values in the CICS
definitions.  It is for that reason good to learn how to set up the entire
image, user data and all, from scratch within your program.

Best Wishes
  





Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```
As far as I remember you can't set a catch-all env-var
for your DATA files. You have to do that for each file,
see the manual on that.

Wendy Cliffe wrote in message <70ngv9$75v$1@news.tbcnet.com>...
>I am trying to configure MF Cobol Toolset 3.2 .  I am having trouble
>figuring out how to direct the compiler to my data files.  I have the

0
>0-s\po;r:\rw2000-s\rw
>SET COBDEMO=R:\COBOL\DEMO
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: Help w/ MF Cobol Configuration

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70np5k$dmu$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70ngv9$75v$1@news.tbcnet.com>`

```

Wendy Cliffe wrote in message <70ngv9$75v$1@news.tbcnet.com>...
>I am trying to configure MF Cobol Toolset 3.2 .  I am having trouble
>figuring out how to direct the compiler to my data files.  I have the
>following set in my autoexec


With most PC Cobol's you do it in your code.  The usual is in
the SELECT clause, though with MF and several others, you can
also do it in the VALUE OF ID clause of the FD.  That is an older
standard, though, and normally only done for compatibility with
older systems.
Typical:

    SELECT MY-FILE ASSIGN TO "C:\myarea\myfile.dat"
            rest of select clause.

Note that if you want to vary the filename, or keep it in a setup file,
you can use a data name in working storage instead of the literal
of the example above.
IE:
    SELECT MY-FILE ASSIGN TO FILE-NAME-VARIABLE
            etc.
    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01 FILE-NAME-VARIABLE        PICTURE X(45) VALUE "c:\myarea\myfile.seq".

You can then change FILE-NAME-VARIABLE in your program
before your open, under program control.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help w/ MF Cobol Configuration

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70o7e9$r8f@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70ngv9$75v$1@news.tbcnet.com> <70np5k$dmu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
There is a 3rd way (see below)

Donald Tees wrote in message <70np5k$dmu$1@news.igs.net>...
>
>Wendy Cliffe wrote in message <70ngv9$75v$1@news.tbcnet.com>...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>    01 FILE-NAME-VARIABLE        PICTURE X(45) VALUE
"c:\myarea\myfile.seq".
>
>You can then change FILE-NAME-VARIABLE in your program
>before your open, under program control.
>
>


You can also have

      Select MY-FILE ASSIGN TO ENVIVAR
              rest of select clause.

and then have

SET ENVIVAR=C:/this/that.dat

in a BAT or CMD file that you execute outside your program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help w/ MF Cobol Configuration

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363087BC.15BBC3F0@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<70ngv9$75v$1@news.tbcnet.com>`

```
Wendy Cliffe wrote:
> 
> I am trying to configure MF Cobol Toolset 3.2 .  I am having trouble
> figuring out how to direct the compiler to my data files.
[snip]
> My data files are in various subdirectories of r:\rw2000\ (similar to the
> cobcpy line various subdirectories)

Hi Wendy.

I don't want to get your hopes up, but some products support the
COBDATA env. variable. This is a bit like COBDIR, but for data files.
However, I'm not sure if Toolset 3.2 is such a product (in fact, I
suspect it isn't).

Cheers, Kev.
```

## Thread: How transfer from recorded data in Cobol to another application. — 2 msgs

### How transfer from recorded data in Cobol to another application.

- **From:** "JAHF" <dcsa@cma.ulpgc.es>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362f9f2c.0@noticias.ulpgc.es>`

```
Hello to all. I need your help.



I am doing a new application for my enterprise to substitute the previous
one, done in cobol. And we need to transfer the recorded data of the old
application (cobol) to the new one (dbase).

I have the description of the fields but it is impossible  to read well the
data because the length of every field doesn't correspond with the length
specified in the definition.

Please I would like to receive veritable information about how doing a
transfer of information from one application to another one.

Thank you at all.

With regards,



C. If you know Spanish, it would be better but not a problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: How transfer from recorded data in Cobol to another application.

- **From:** Scott McKellar <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36306644.55A4@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<362f9f2c.0@noticias.ulpgc.es>`

```
JAHF wrote:
> 
> Hello to all. I need your help.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> transfer of information from one application to another one.

We need to know more about the kind of COBOL which produced the files,
and perhaps how the application was coded.  For example:

When MicroFocus COBOL writes line sequential files which contain binary
data, it adds nul bytes (binary zeros) in front of bytes with values
less than hex 20.  It also removes trailing blanks, and gives special
treatment to tab characters.  There are also ways to suppress these
kinds of data mangling.

(One reason for these quirks is that line sequential files are text
files, whose records are delimited by line feeds or carriage return/
line feed pairs.  The presence of binary data can potentially cause
confusion because a line feed or carriage return can occur as part of
the data.  The extra nul characters enable the reading program to
determine which line feeds are data and which are delimiters.)

Please post again with more detailed information.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

<a id="t-78c1f4d197"></a>
## Thread: Testing Computer Software (TCS'99) -- CALL FOR PAPERS (Advanced Topics) — 1 msgs

### Testing Computer Software (TCS'99) -- CALL FOR PAPERS (Advanced Topics)

- **From:** uspdi@earthlink.net (USPDI)
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<uspdi-2210981629330001@1cust80.tnt5.tco2.da.uu.net>`

```
 
                  C A L L   F O R   P A P E R S

                    ADVANCED TOPICS TRACK

       16th International Conference and Exposition on
 
               TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS'99)

                 Theme: Future Trends in Testing

            June 14-18, 1999 * Washington D.C. * USA
    (Conference: June 16-18; Workshops/Tutorials: June 14-15)

        Conference Web Site: www.uspdi.org/conference
 

                           OVERVIEW

Software development is rapidly moving toward the component-design
paradigm. And because the manner by which systems are built is changing,
it will be necessary to have the appropriate testing methodologies and
tools that complement these changes. Because of this, the theme of the
TCS'99 is "Future Trends in Testing." The goal of the 1999 Conference will
be to explore which testing technologies we will need to take into the new
millennium and which ones we will not. Further, we will explore the
testing problems that accompany component-based design and what research
is needed to address these deficiencies.

TCS'99 is changing the submission procedures for those professionals who
wish to publish papers that are presented in the Advanced Topics Track.
Beginning with the 1999 Conference, TCS will employ a formal review
process by committee for full and short length paper submissions. Previous
TCS Conferences have not required paper submissions for inclusion in this
track. However, to continue to increase the quality of the information
provided to the attendees, TCS'99 has decided to employ a standard peer
review committee to encourage state-of-the-art submissions. 

Topics of particular interest to the Program Committee for TCS'99 include: 

* Empirical results from testing large-scale systems 
* Java testing methodologies
* Software assertions
* Joint testing/formal methods approaches
* Analysis of situations where testing failed to provide
  expected benefits 
* Object-oriented testing
* Empirical results gained during Y2K testing 
* Distributed system testing
* Real-time system testing
* Embedded system testing
* Slicing Object-Oriented programs
* Dataflow algorithms
* Software Metrics and Measurement
* Software testing standards
* Software Reliability Measurement
* Software Safety Assessment
* Software Security Assessment
* Testing Mobile Code and Agents
* Testing Web-based systems
* Testing COTS-based systems

However, any other topics related to the Conference theme or to advances
in software testing are always encouraged. Note also that there are other
tracks at TCS'99 that do not employ this submission procedure and which do
encourage presentations on other topics in software testing. Information
on submission can be found on the conference website at
www.uspdi.org/conference/tcs99call.html.

                  INSTRUCTIONS TO AUTHORS

Submissions to be included in the Advanced Topics Track of the TCS'99
program are now being accepted by the TCS'99 Program Committee. Send six
copies of your Paper to Jeffrey Voas, Advanced Topics Track Chair, at the
address given below. Papers must be received by November 1, 1998.
Full-length papers, which should include an abstract, must not exceed
6,000 words. Full-length papers should provide state-of-the-art
information addressing the topics listed above or related topics. Short
papers can also be submitted. Short papers are desirable for "work in
progress" or preliminary ideas with early results. Short papers should not
exceed 2,500 words. Both full-length papers and short papers will be
published in the Conference Proceedings.

In addition to a paper submission, an ASCII copy of the paper title and
abstract should be sent to Jeff Voas (jmvoas@rstcorp.com) by electronic
mail. Abstracts will be distributed electronically to the entire program
committee to aid in the appropriate assignment of referees. Authors must
certify in their cover letter prior to November 1, 1998 that any and all
necessary clearances for publication have been obtained. Authors will be
notified of paper acceptance by February 1, 1999. Camera-ready copies of
papers and viewgraph slides are due no later than April 1, 1999. Papers
and viewgraphs slides (black and white, 2 per page) should be submitted IN
HARD-COPY FORMAT to Jeffrey Voas at: 
Reliable Software Technologies
21515 Ridgetop Circle, Suite 250
Sterling, VA 20166 USA
http://www.rstcorp.com
Tel: 703.404.9293
Fax: 703.404.9295

                     IMPORTANT DATES

November 1, 1998  Paper submissions due
February 1, 1999  Notification of selection 
   April 1, 1999  Camera ready copies of papers

+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

         One complimentary Conference registration is provided
                   for each accepted presentation.

+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
```

## Thread: Peoplsoft Survey — 1 msgs

### Peoplsoft Survey

- **From:** Survey <survey@reliantdata.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,bit.listserv.db2-1,comp.databases.oracle.misc,comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,alt.comp.baan
- **Message-ID:** `<362FAE8D.36536313@dcle.com>`

```
We are conducting a brief survey to attempt to ascertain the detailed
requirements of how people are currently building their legacy system
interfaces and conversion programs into their production PeopleSoft
application systems.  Our target audience consists of Project managers,
Independent consultants, third-party system integrators, or any
technical
representitive of a PeopleSoft implementation project.

Thanks in advance for helping us with this endeavor!  All information
will, of course, be kept confidential.

In the interest of not wasting too much bandwidth, please reply to this
survey by email to:

survey@dcle.com

All respondents will be emailed the results of the survey (with names
and
organization names, of course, kept anonymous), and a summary of the
survey will be posted to this newsgroup.

Thanks for your time.


                                    PeopleSoft Survey

Company :                       http     :
Name :                          email  :
Title :                         phone :
Manager's Name :

[SECTION 1]
Please mark any of the following PeopleSoft modules as already in use
(U),
currently being implemented (I), planned (P), or not planned (N) within
your organization (if planned, please give anticipated date) :

PeopleSoft modules
------------------

Financials
Accts Payable
Accts Rec
Gen Ledger
Asset Mgmt
Projects
Budgets
Expenses
Treasury
HRMS
Human Resources
Benefits
PSA Admin
Payroll
Payroll Interface
Time & Labor
Pension
Materials Management
Inventory
Purchasing
Distribution
Order Mgmt
Product Configuration
Billing
Enterprise Planning
Manufacturing
ERO Manuf. Solution
Production Planning
Engineering
Billings & Routings
Production Mgmt
Cost Mgmt
Supply Chain

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

[SECTION 2]

My Responsibilities within the PeopleSoft deployment include :

Who is/was ultimately responsible for the PeopleSoft deployment project
as
a whole?

[SECTION 3]
Survey Questions

1. Did you move, or are you planning to move historical data into the
new
application modules?

2. If no data is to be moved (or if only some modules are to be
implemented) are you planning on interfacing these modules to your
other,
existing systems?

3. Do you currently have a plan for moving (converting) this data from
the
old application into the new?  Is so, please detail.

4. Will the conversion be done only once into the PeopleSoft system, or
will additional data be periodically updated?  If so, how frequently?

5. What is the time window for the initial conversion?  For subsequent
conversions?  (i.e. 12 hours, or over a weekend, etc?)  How much data
(in
number of records and in gigs) will be converted initially? Recurring?

6. What is the make-up of your PeopleSoft conversion team? (please
include
skill sets and titles)  How will the conversion/interfacing work be
divided?

7. Would it be preferable to offload the conversion/interfacing work to
a
business analysts, rather than a dba or systems consultant?

8. If multiple people will be working on the same
conversions/interfaces,
how important is it for them to share work?  How would this sharing
ideally take place? (i.e. a check-in/check-out procedure?  A shared
multi-user metadata repository?)

9. Will (or was) a system integrator used to assist in the deployment or

conversion?  If so, who?

10. Where do you typically go for PeopleSoft information? (i.e.
magazines,
web sites, user groups, peoplesoft direct, third-party consultants,
etc.)

11. Will (or did) your implementation also include technology for 1)
moving PeopleSoft data in/our of data warehouses or data marts   2)
interfacing (EDI, EAI) to other ERP or legacy apps (and if so, which
ones)
3) data replication to ERPs at other sites (if so, please list) or  4)
creating enterprise metadata repositories?

12. Please describe any tools (internal or third-party) you might have
used to facilitate building a conversion or interface.  What are the
three
things you like most about these tools?  What are the three things you
like least?

13. For a given PeopleSoft module implementation, how much time (in
man-weeks) is typically spent (or budgeted) on the initial data
conversion?

14. For a given PeopleSoft module implementation, how much time (in
man-weeks) is spent (or budgeted) building interfaces to other existing
systems?

15. How are your conversion processes typically built? (i.e.
programming,
tools, etc.)  Please describe.

16. How are your interfaces typically built? (i.e. programming, tools,
etc.) Please describe.

17. Are these interfaces usually real time, near-real time, or batch?
If
you were to describe the "typical" interface now, what would it look
like?
If you were to describe the "ideal" interface now, what would it look
like?

18. Do your current interfaces have a mechanism for restarting  in mid
process when errors occur (i.e. checkpoints, etc)?  How important is a
restart mechanism for interfacing?

19. How do you interfaces currently handle change data capture (i.e.
selecting out and transferring only records that have changed.)?  How
important is change-data capture to interfacing?

20. What are typically the hardest obstacles in building your conversion

processes/programs or your system interfaces?

21. What are the most common systems (database, operating systems,
applications) you convert data from (into PeopleSoft)?

22. What are the most common systems (database, operating systems,
applications) you interface to PeopleSoft?

23. Does your organization currently use a systems management framework
(such as CA's Unicenter TNG, BMC's Patrol or Tivoli's TME/Enterprise)?

24. Does your organization currently use a common scheduler (such as
CA's
Unicenter TNG, BMC's Patrol, or Tivoli's TME/Enterprise)?

25. Does your organization currently use a common performance monitor
for
your PeopleSoft application? (such as CA's Unicenter TNG, BMC's Patrol,
or
Tivoli's TME/Enterprise)?

26. If any of the above system management utilities in use, are any of
them capable of tracking the performance of your current interfaces?
If
not, would this be of value?  How would such an interface monitor look,
ideally?

27. If a single physical machine were to run multiple different
databases
(or instantiations of the same database), would it preferable to have a
single remote agent or process running for each interface, or to have a
single process controlling all interfaces for that machine?

28. At what phase in your PeopleSoft deployment timeline do you
typically
create your conversion programs/processes?

29. At what phase in your PeopleSoft deployment timeline do you
typically
create your interfaces to other systems?

30. Would you be interested in looking at a software product that
minimized the process of build conversion processes and/or interfaces?

31. Assuming a software package could reduce the time and effort
necessary
to build and manage these conversions/interfaces dramatically (i.e. by
more than 50%), how valuable would this be? (Please rank numerically,
0=No
Value, 10=Exceptional Value)


Thank-you for taking the time to complete this survey.
```

<a id="t-eec6e65052"></a>
## Thread: snow report writer 4.1 question — 2 msgs

### snow report writer 4.1 question

- **From:** Ren Gonzalez <isanyone@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<362EBF3C.1E4F42F0@hotmail.com>`

```
I'm no COBOL guru.
But I do have a question if you're familiar with Snow Report Writer 4.1.

I need to link more data files.  As it is right now, only three are
linked and
there are close to 100 data files.  I don't know how to link data files
in Snow.
But what I do know is that there are two versions of Snow Report Writer.

One version for users and another version for companies that create
systems for customers.  I think maybe I have the version that can't link

data files.  We pay them a yearly fee for support.  Do you think they
owe
it to us to link the files for us if we can't?  If not, why did we get
the
user addition and not the ultimate edition?  Anyway, if you're familiar
with
Snow, I'd appreciate any comments or suggestions towards this
predicament.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: snow report writer 4.1 question

- **From:** maxomtech@wiscasset.net
- **Date:** 1998-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70n9c7$nv3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<362EBF3C.1E4F42F0@hotmail.com>`

```


Snow does have several product lines, the only line that I have worked with is
what is called "Snow Report Writer and Query Facility". In "my" version of 4.1
(and copies of it that I have installed at other user sites in the past,
includes the ability to "Define File Relationships" under "Special Menu
Selections". To do the actual linking does require knowledge of how the
relationship between the two files are established (key structures and type of
relationship).

As far as your support issue and whether or not they should do this for you
as part of the support is up to them.  For our customers, we would define the
typical lookups in advance.  If the user required something different, we
would either "walk" them thru it or do it for them.  Since these requests
were occasional and usually only one or two, we would do it as part of our
support contract with them.

My suggestion would be to talk to your support people, maybe they didn't give
you the correct Relationship file (RELFIL.DAT), or they may have an updated
version of it, or maybe they will do what you need.  Worst case, you could go
back to Snow and "Upgrade" your product to the full blown version that gives
you the capabilities that you need.

Good Luck!

Chris Loy
Maxom Holdings, Inc.
www.maxom.com


In article <362EBF3C.1E4F42F0@hotmail.com>,
  Ren Gonzalez <isanyone@hotmail.com> wrote:
> I'm no COBOL guru.
> But I do have a question if you're familiar with Snow Report Writer 4.1.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

```
