It's finally the weekend and I've noticed a couple of reponses posted.
Let me address them in detail.


Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Tue, 27 Oct 1998 00:31:36, <kenmullins@mindspring.com> wrote:
>
>> tried to beta V3 for them, but could not compile any of my production
>> projects...have not had time to get back to it yet...How does V3 look?
>> Improved over V2 I hope....

I'm going to address this toward the bottom.

>>
>> kenMullins
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>He's not a happy camper.

I'm sorry to hear this.

>
>(Yes he has/had an open incident on it, it may be closed now - I have
>not corresponded with him in a few weeks).
>
>


I work for a major insurance processing firm here in Oklahoma City.
We do both life and health insurance processing for several companies around
America.

Basically the way our operation works is our customers are tied into our
Unisys machines via
a FEP or Front End Processor via dedicated lines to our customer sites.

And the code we are working with is COBOL running on a Unisys V series
computer.
In-fact we have banks of these machines, each performing processing for
multiple companies.

The Unisys V series uses StoQues and Core to Core memory transfers as it's
major communication
system.  It uses this process to pass data to and from programs as well as
to and from the FEP.

And we are re-writing this software on a test NT server, specially equipped
to talk to the FEP,
with STOQ and CRCR access, and our COBOL development platform is Net
Express.

We started out using OS/2 as our platform and Micro Focus OS/2 based Cobol
compilers.
The system was about 1/3 the way developed when the decision was made to
drop OS/2
in favor of NT.  I noticed that Micro Focus has also given notice of their
desire to leave the
OS/2 market in the comming months.  So I'm glad we made this decision back
in January.

So the platform to use was to be Net Express Version 2.0.

And we had some problems with Net Express Version 2.0 which virtually
stopped our project.
We were calling the support department in Micro Focus multiple times a week
with new bugs
we'd found.

And because of this fact, they elected to offer us the chance to be BETA
testers for Version 3.0.

I'm glad they did.  It saved our butts.

Let me discuss what we were up against here so you can have a better feeling
for our situation.

In our system there can be 300 programs in the MIX at any given time.  Some
take only seconds to
run, while others may take as long as 20 minutes yet others just reside in
there all day long.
We are 3/4 of the way through with 900 some odd programs left to convert.
By PHYSICAL COUNT, around 95% of the functionality of the Micro Focus Net
Express
product is in use.  It took that to emulate the V series which is a very
complicated and demanding
environment.   The PC we are running on is a very BEEF'ED up server running
NT.

Unisys COMP is like Micro Focus COMP-6.  We used this everywhere.  COMP-6 is
something
I'll bet nobody else uses.  But's it's a near perfect emulation of Unisys
Digits based COMP.
We had to interface with highly customized, NEW software and hardware to
communicate with
our FEP.  Our system not only has the potential of having 300 programs in
the MIX but behind
these .exe's there typically are multiple .dll programs being called and
dropped.
And entirely brand new sub OS was written under NT using Net Express to
simulate
several tasks our firm needed to accomplish our goals.  Custom drivers
galore!

We are NOT a lan based networking system.  Our Server does all the
processing and passes
data buffers to and from terminals and printers located all over the nation.
Our system is incredibly
fast.  It's faster than any LAN based system I've ever seen.

We have automatic LOGS on our system which record run times of software,
failures, ect...
We can detect failure in the operation of software with the flick of a wrist
on a console.
I can tell you the run times of every one of those packages and how they
related from
Version 2.0 to Version 3.0.

We have communicated extensively with the Beta Development people at Micro
Focus
in the last few months.

So it should be obvious to the causal reader that we do 'NO' Internet stuff.
We do 'NO' GUI stuff.  We just do the COBOL .exe/.dll stuff,,, the meat of
what COBOL has
to offer.  File I-O, processing, multi company data communication insurance
processing.

We have beat the hell out of this product.

This product is a much improved product over Version 2.0.
A much improved product.

A much improved product for an industry which demands a perfect cobol
product.
A mission critical product.  Net Express is both these for us.

I can not testify to it's internet capabilities, nor it's GUI capablilites,
nor alot of it's third party
data base capabilities.

But what I can tell you is the MEAT of it's COBOL services has been
thoroughly tested here.
That engine behind all those interfaces, the one which is the heart of your
product.
And in all that software, all working as advertised, the product has a very
consistent heartbeat.

Lately we have not been submitting FATAL errors to Micro Focus.  We haven't
had any for quite
a while.   We have been submitting wish list items which we feel make the
product easier to use and
more efficient.

I'm very glad they picked us as a beta site.  We had to be a BETA site.

COBOL is a very powerful language for which it is difficult to write a
decent compiler for.
It's not like C.  C has very little built in power.  C coders write their
own proceedures
and library them in via OOP.

COBOL has natural power built right into the language.  And because of that,
it's much
more difficult to maintain than C when refering to compilers.  But you will
never find more
maintainable source code than COBOL source code.  The more complex the
compiler,
the more work it does for the programmer, the more the programmer can
accomplish
in an 8 hour day.

I've been using Micro Focus products for around 10 years.  My background is
in insurance
processing.

The people I work with all have in excess of 20 years working in COBOL in
the insurance
industry.  A couple of whom have 30+ years.

COBOL standards in such a shop must be maintained.  And I think for the most
part
our crowd is satisfied.

I've worked for another major U.S. insurance processing company in the past.
They chose to drop COBOL in favor or Visual Basic for their next platform.

Standards just don't exist in other worlds.  Going from version 2.1 to 2.3
can be
a collossal catastophy in other platforms, C included.

But in Micro Focus's world of COBOL development I will tell you from
experience that
moving from one version to the next represented little challenge to us in
the past.
Occassionally you might have to re-code something, but it was rare.

I've recompiled hundreds and hundreds of cobol .exe's and .dll's using Net
express.
I've done it multiple times, each time we go to a new version in the BETA
since we
started the program.  Not one time was I forced to RE-code anything.  Not
one time.

I have a pentium II 450 mhz NT workstation.  I recompiled everything in our
shop, installed
the new runtime and was back up in just 6 hours.  That's a pile of running
software folks.

So I feel confident when I say that Net Express 3.0 is a good running
system.
I feel confident stating that Net Express is a platform which has MISSION
CRITICAL written
all over it.  How many of you have tried converting 35 years of Unisys V
code????
I mean we are using everything Net Express BASIC COBOL has to offer.
Virtually all
of their function calls.  Call's to strange third party pc software.  Call's
to data bases.
Math using every number system they support.  File I-O like you wouldn't
believe, locked or not.

But after all of this, I won't lie to you.  I too have a handfull of issues
they are still working on.
They are not fatal.  And I'm confident that Micro Focus will prevail in them
soon.

You must consider we have sent them dozens and dozens of issues, many fatal
in nature for
our situation.  Micro Focus has fixed them all.  We have NO fatals.

Net Express Version 3.0 is very solid.  I've very happy with it over all.
And the beta team in the U.K. has worked hard to address all the concerns of
all their beta clients from
around the world.

Now as you can see, most cobol programmers would probably have never used
the functionality I've
described in this message in their programs.  They would have no need to.
But I did and I'll say it all works.
And I want to point out that Net Express Version 2.0 worked well for most
"programmers".  It was their
comments I read when we opted to buy Net Express over Workbench; when we
moved from OS/2 to NT.
I am not implying that Net Express 2.0 is unusable to the general public in
any fashion what so ever.
What I am saying is in MY situation, Net Express 2.0 had some problems I
simply could not code around.
We tried but we simply couldn't do it.  And Micro Focus saw to it we were
included in their beta program
because they cared about our situation.  They realized we were an ideal beta
site to test the core of
the compiler and the run time.  And my heart pours out to them for their
months of support to me on this
project.

I have to give credit where credit is due.  Their support has been ""THERE""
with me since I started this
conversion some 2 years ago.  I've made alot of freinds along the way in
Micro Focus.  Support is critical
for developers.

I'm not trying to convince you to NOT buy Net Express version 2.0.  I
believe that 2.0 offeres
everything most shops would need.  If you take away the conversion
proprietary considerations
I'm mentioning, 2.0 was stable for use.  I seriously doubt most shops would
do the things we had to do.

I bought Net Express Version 2.0.  I also made sure we bought the
maintenance contract which
is offered with it.

Rather, I wanted to put something public to see if I can clear the air over
mainframe conversions
of COBOL code and their migrations to server platforms.  For us, 3.0 is
everything 2.0 was not.

Gentlemen!  It really CAN be done.
You really can take 35 year old COBOL code which has been running on a host
of mainframes for decades
and bring it to a NT server platform.

Net Express has the tools to make this possible.  Net Express is in my
opinion a 5 star compiler/run time system.

Their beta team has been responsive to my whinning!  After we got over the
fatals, the whinning from
the group started.  They have been making changes which are more framiliar
and acceptable to
older generations of COBOL developers and these suddle suggestions are
making my life brighter also.

If you NEED a very powerful and capable cobol compiler and run time then you
need Net Express.
I know I've never seen this much code running under a server at one time
sucessfully ever....

It's really very impressive.

It gives you a great respect for those who worked on Mainframes in the past.
And it gives you some hope for the future when you realize that you don't
have to throw thousands
of dollars of COBOL code away in favor of another language just to move to a
P.C.

I wanted to make this message a strong one in support of Net Express.  I
wanted to make this message
in such a fashion that people would believe in what I say.  I didn't want to
make it sound like a marketing
rep wrote it.  I hate marketing reps.

So many people put out messages which are negative based on their negative
experiences.
I felt it necessary to put out a positive message.  And in order to do that
you have to break a few eggs.

I think other's have been here with me in the past and know what I'm
refering to here.

My firm has made a positive impact on Net Express by filing their beta
reports and support reports thru
Micro Focus.  Micro Focus has molded these comments into working code.

And everybody will benefit in this.

I'm proud to be involved with the Net Express Version 3.0 beta and the Micro
Focus team.
And I will also bet you that if you read the comments of other BETA testers
working on various
beta's from around the country, no one else would have written as much about
their beta as I have.

And I will be happy when Version 3.0 is finally released.

I'm very pleased with the progress which has been made in this product over
the last few months.
We've come so far.  And the future for COBOL based systems looks very bright
from here.

Thank you Beta Team and by all means, buy the Net Express product.
If you need a mission critical product which is capable enough to handle the
tasks of mainframe compilers
then you really need this product.  If you intent to write sub OS systems,
do FEP communications,
talk to various strange sub products, do massive file I-O, and do it all at
a speed which is 50 times faster
than our old V, then buy this product.  If your organization has over 3000
packages which are 30 years
old in nature, spanning several ANSI COBOL generations in nature, HA HA
HA,,,
Do I need to ramble on here!!!!

Get your butt out there and try this thing.

I think they still offer a free 30 day test drive CD.
That's how I got hooked.

CU guys.

Charlie
```

## Thread: COBOL II get current date and time — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### COBOL II get current date and time

- **From:** txuxlxixcx@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70q1l3$ca7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I have (fortunately) not had to do any COBOL Y2K conversion work yet, but now
I have to do some work on an IBM mainframe using COBOL II.  I seem to recall
there are Y2K-acceptable and unacceptable ways to get the current date and
time from COBOL. Could someone please enlighten me on the difference so I
don't create any problems?

-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL II get current date and time

- **From:** John Calahan <jcalahan@magpage.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70qarn$ov6$0@204.179.92.50>`
- **References:** `<70q1l3$ca7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
The best way for you to go in COBOL II (not COBOL MVS, right?) would be to
use the intrinsic function CURRENT-DATE. If you have the COBOL Language
Reference manual from IBM (I forget the number), check Part 7 - Intrinsic
Functions. There's lots of useful date stuff in there.

HTH,

John 

On Fri, 23 Oct 1998 txuxlxixcx@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> I have (fortunately) not had to do any COBOL Y2K conversion work yet, but now
> I have to do some work on an IBM mainframe using COBOL II.  I seem to recall
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL II get current date and time

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3630B480.8AE5C12F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<70q1l3$ca7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70qarn$ov6$0@204.179.92.50>`

```
John Calahan wrote:
> 
> The best way for you to go in COBOL II (not COBOL MVS, right?) would be to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
COBOL II doesn't have functions, COBOL 370 has the CURRENT-DATE
function.

CURRENT-DATE was a field before COBOL II in IBM environments which had a
format of mm/dd/yy.  IBM COBOL II had DATE, which unlike DATE on a VAX,
did not have a century, it had yymmdd.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL II get current date and time

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70qjd7$99t@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70q1l3$ca7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70qarn$ov6$0@204.179.92.50>`

```

John Calahan wrote in message <70qarn$ov6$0@204.179.92.50>...
>The best way for you to go in COBOL II (not COBOL MVS, right?) would be to
>use the intrinsic function CURRENT-DATE. If you have the COBOL Language
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I have (fortunately) not had to do any COBOL Y2K conversion work yet, but
now
>> I have to do some work on an IBM mainframe using COBOL II.  I seem to
recall
>> there are Y2K-acceptable and unacceptable ways to get the current date
and
>> time from COBOL. Could someone please enlighten me on the difference so I
>> don't create any problems?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>


The one AND ONLY way to get the current date with a century in (batch or
IMS) VS COBOL II is to call an IBM supplied sub-routine called IGZEDT4.  To
do so, you need to make certain that current maintenance has been applied to
your VS COBOL II software. (Check with your systems people that they have
applied the fix for APAR PN76666.)

If you are running under CICS, then do NOT call IGZEDT4, but instead use the
CICS ASKTIME function (or use the EIBDATE field - but be careful - as it has
a funny way to handle the century).

In the unusual case that you are using the VS COBOL II compiler, but are
running under the LE (Language Environment) run-time (and your programs are
compiled with the RES compiler option) then you can make dynamic calls (use
the CALL identifier format) and call the LE date/time routines.  (This
should work under both batch and CICS)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL II get current date and time

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3630d3ef.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<70q1l3$ca7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70qarn$ov6$0@204.179.92.50> <70qjd7$99t@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
But the current-date is eminently suited for windowing:
ACCEPT YYMMDD FROM DATE
IF YY < 98
   MOVE 20 TO CC
ELSE
  MOVE 19 TO CC
.
where:
01  DATE-NOW    PIC 9(8).
01 FILLER REDEFINES DATE-NOW.
    02  CC               PIC 99.
    02  YYMMDD    PIC 9(6).
    02  FILLER      REDEFINES YYMMDD.
         03  YY        PIC 99.
         03  MM      PIC 99.
         03  DD       PIC 99.


Why complicate matters with obscure and "funny" stuff?

William M. Klein wrote in message <70qjd7$99t@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>John Calahan wrote in message <70qarn$ov6$0@204.179.92.50>...

but

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> time from COBOL. Could someone please enlighten me on the difference so
I
>>> don't create any problems?
>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>do so, you need to make certain that current maintenance has been applied
to
>your VS COBOL II software. (Check with your systems people that they have

>
>If you are running under CICS, then do NOT call IGZEDT4, but instead use
the
>CICS ASKTIME function (or use the EIBDATE field - but be careful - as it
has
>a funny way to handle the century).
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL II get current date and time

- **From:** d.s.kirk@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3631204e.1454927@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<70q1l3$ca7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Fri, 23 Oct 1998 13:51:30 GMT, txuxlxixcx@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>I have (fortunately) not had to do any COBOL Y2K conversion work yet, but now
>I have to do some work on an IBM mainframe using COBOL II.  I seem to recall
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum

I've seen lots of good advice, so won't repeat it. However, just a
reminder ---  IBM has stated that COBOL II is not and never will be
Y2K-compliant... so, my recommendation is to alert your management
that you're saddling a dead horse.  COBOL/MVS has many powerful
features for Y2K projects, none of which are in COBOL II. If you must
spend the effort to convert a system for Y2K compliance, might as well
get the current compiler.  Also, support for cobol ii expires
somewhere around march, 2000 (or is it march, 00?   <g>)
david
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL II get current date and time

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-2810981404010001@138.202.137.39>`
- **References:** `<70q1l3$ca7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
ACCEPT DEST-STR FROM DATE YYYYMMDD

DEC COBOL
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to CICS

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363182A2.CA118EBC@att.net>`
- **References:** `<70o190$agh$1@supernews.com> <70ovsk$fml$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70p4kn$7fv$1@supernews.com>`

```
DSECT is an Assembler term (Dummy Section, if you're interested) for the
data structure, i.e., what's copied into your program to describe the
mapped data. 

Sorry for any confusion,
Bill Lynch

Jason wrote:
> 
>  I do believe that I am using the most recently compiled versions of both
> the program and the mapset, and the loadlib is accessible by the CICS.  As
> far as DSECT is, I have no clue, this is only my second semester on the
> system.

Every BMS map must be Assembled twice, once to produce the physical map
(this is a load module) and once to produce the data structure (many of
us still call this a DSECT, even though it usually isn't anymore). The
output from the second pass is usually stored as a member in a PDS and
is, usually, copied into your Working Storage.

> This is the field with problems.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>  00287                INITIAL='_'

>  00288          DFHMDF POS=(17,31),     x
>  00289                LENGTH=1,         x
>  00290                ATTRB=ASKIP

Jason, what's the purpose of this field? If it's intended as a "field
stopper" it's redundant, just start the next DFHMDF in (17,31). The
attribute byte of this field will limit the ENTRYF(I) field (all
attribute bytes are hidden and ASKIP). If you want some space between
the "_" and "THEN ..." insert some spaces in the INITIAL parameter just
before "THEN PRESS ENTER".

>  00291          DFHMDF POS=(17,34),       x
>  00292                LENGTH=22,          x
>  00293                ATTRB=(NORM,PROT),  x
>  00294                INITIAL='THEN PRESS ENTER.'

BTW, this is three fields - in which one do you get "trash"?

If you want to discuss mapping in some more detail, feel free to email
me offline.
```

As far as the "trash" goes, you might want to check a run-time option.

If you are using VS COBOL II - check that you are running with WSCLEAR

If you are using the LE run-time - check that your STORAGE run-time option
as "00" for the initial values.

This is a Cobol Problem using NetExpress, not an HTML problem.

What you must do is go to Project Build Settings (ALT F7).
Select Link tab, and make sure that under Runtime Library you have select
static. I have tried this to create a standalone single program that does not
need the runtime libraries.

Otherwise, you must find the Runtime files that it need and copy them to the
server, but try to change the Runtime Libraries to static.

j> Is there a way to connect to the data files stored by a MicroFocus
j> Cobol application from Microsoft Access?  How would I accomplish this?
j> Any help or insight into this problem would be greatly appreciated.

Do you have access to the file descriptions? If so, you can access the  
files via ODBC with Transoft's (www.transoft.com) U/SQL or Liant's  
(www.liant.com) Relational Data Bridge.

Best regards

Sven

#### ↳ Re: MicroFoucus Cobol and Microsoft Access

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Mv0Y1.6524$s_4.1825748@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
jnickles@home.com wrote in message <70q2jm$d51$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Is there a way to connect to the data files stored by a MicroFocus Cobol
>application from Microsoft Access?  How would I accomplish this?  Any help
or
>insight into this problem would be greatly appreciated.
>
>-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

Micro Focus has a package called OpenESQL which provides ODBC access to anMS
Access database, or via Microsoft's SQL Server product. It ships with
NetExpress and is optional extra with Workbench.

What doesn't ship with NetExpress (at least I haven't been able to find it)
is an XDB precompiler, which is strange given that Micro Focus owns XDB.
```

## Thread: Record keys as group items: completely filled? — 18 msgs

### Record keys as group items: completely filled?

- **From:** BuyukYarak@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70ss03$b9r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


This should be a walk in the park for the keepers of the COBOL standard:

I am MOVEing in data into Patient-Code of DB2-Key1 and Test-Code of DB2-Key2,
then READing the ORGANIZATION INDEXED file, but get a return error of key not
found.  The partial FD is below and shows the two record keys, which are
obviously group items.  I am only completing part of the elementary items in
each.  I am slowly being persuaded of the opinion that I must complete ALL
elementary items in a group item if the group item is a record key (or its
alternate).  Would someone care to set me straight?

I have a tougher follow-up question below, if you are really game.

----------
FD  DB2-File LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01  DB2-Patient-Record.
    05 DB2-Key1.
        10 Patient-Code       PIC 9(07).
        10 Order-Number       PIC 9(02).
    05 DB2-Key2.
        10 Test-Code          PIC 9(04).
        10 Visit-Date.
            15 DB2-VD-Year    PIC 9(02).
            15 DB2-VD-Month   PIC 9(02).
            15 DB2-VD-Day     PIC 9(02).
    05 DB2-Status             PIC 9.
      .
      .
      .
----------

A tougher question: The FD above is someone else's coding here.  I am
learning COBOL on the fly because I need to write short COBOL programs that
set up a "daemon" program between the data base (an ORGANIZATION INDEXED
file) and my user interface coded in the language I am more proficient at.

The OS is Unix.  The user interface I wrote starts the COBOL "daemon" as a
child process and the two processes set up a communication channel (two
unidirectional named pipes for I/O).  The READ statement COBOL standard
appears not to account for all of the nuances of Unix.	I have learned that
the implementation I am using (to my surprise) does a "blocking" read, and
this is not configurable within the COBOL language.  Moreover, I am
challenging the COBOL implemtation to READ something not a "regular" file,
that is, I/O involved in batchwise processing.	Has anyone here had
experience with doing READs and WRITEs on "special files" in a filesystem?
```

> 	record key is patient-code

You would think so.  I am only using a database with a file structure already
defined 10 years or more ago.  This database stores clinical lab patient
data: test orders and results, and it uses several different files...at least
one to store the initial data when a patient orders tests (I call it "DB1"),
and then another to store each result as a record as it comes in (in "DB2"). 
It is not a marvel of optimized coding, with all names in a language other
than English and giving no indication of the name's purpose, and virtually
without comment in 160,000 lines of source code distributed in 25+ source
files.	This is all to ensure the job security of the person who figured into
a lot of its coding, and he doesn't like the idea I am writing a support
program that interacts with his. So he imposes the file structure on me.  It
is not my job to tell him what is more intelligent or logical.	At any rate,
I have figured out on my own how to fill ALL of the elementary items in the
key group item so that the record is found.  My tests of the code have been
completely successful, and it seems the missing elementary items were the
problem for my unsuccessful record searches.

I appreciate the comments this subject has generated about how to be careful
when defining a key as a group item and considering the ordering of the
elementary items.
```

#### ↳ Re: Record keys as group items: completely filled?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70tun3$g48$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70ss03$b9r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
BuyukYarak@yahoo.com wrote in message <70ss03$b9r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>I am MOVEing in data into Patient-Code of DB2-Key1 and Test-Code of
DB2-Key2,
>then READing the ORGANIZATION INDEXED file, but get a return error of key
not


Since the key is the entire item, you have to be careful what is actually
in the low order position.  Consider the following.

01 this-is-the-key.
    02 this-is-high-order        picture 9.
    02 this-is-low-order          picture 9.

Lets say that you have a key on file that is actually "55".  That is,
the both the low order and the high order are "5".  If you move a five
into this-is-high-order, and read with "key greater than", you might get
that record, and you might not.  If this-is-low-order happens to be a "6"
you will not.  If it happens to be a "0", then you will get the record,
(provided that the record "50" through "54" do not exist; if one does,
you will get the first).  If you read with a "key equal to" then you will
not;  equal to means just that -- there is no "almost equal to".

Certainly you do NOT have to initialize the entire key.  However you
do have to be aware of the ramifications. If your intention is to get
all records that contain the high order portion, then you should move
low-values to the low order, read with key greater than, then read
sequentially until you hit the next value in the high order portion. If you
want an exact match, then you need the entire key.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record keys as group items: completely filled?

- **From:** SkidMike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3632934F.965333A3@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<70ss03$b9r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70tun3$g48$1@news.igs.net>`

i've re-read and re-read the code.  can i just make the observation that
naming the fields as such will be very confusing to the future
programmers who have to maintain the app.
at least there should be documentation directly above the entry in
question to specify which field is the actual key and how the program
will use it to find a specific record.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record keys as group items: completely filled?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70u8rv$n6b$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70ss03$b9r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70tun3$g48$1@news.igs.net> <3632934F.965333A3@mindspring.com>`

```

SkidMike wrote in message <3632934F.965333A3@mindspring.com>...
>i've re-read and re-read the code.  can i just make the observation that
>naming the fields as such will be very confusing to the future
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>will use it to find a specific record.

No, you are missing the point.  If I pick up that program in the future,
all I have to remember is that there are two coinciding tables, one
in a file copied to working storage, and one in the COBOL.KBD file.
To add a new key in the future, I do not even have to read the code
to see what goes on.  I simply add a new element in both tables using
the existing portion of the table as an example.  I will know that no
program will be affected by that change.

That done, I then add a single new line to the point where I want to add
a function, with a perform.  In effect, my entire system becomes tables.

You build it keystroke by keystroke.
```

With steam coming out my ears::

You said:
1 If I pick up....
2 (all) I have to remember...
3 I do not even ....
4 I simply add...

6 I then add...
7 (where) I want to add...

but for the benefit of those new to programming......

You will know what to do, but someone else will probably not.
Whether you are in Uzbekistan on vacation,
or have bolted for West Upper Snowshoe, Wisconsin to do
remediation for another company for obscene amounts of lucre ,
you won't be around, so someone else will have to pick up where
you left off, and that someone will almost assuredly not have your
guidance, because you neglected to embed those essential facts
into data names or comments in the program.

Skidmike didn't miss the point.  He nailed it, dead solid perfect.

In no way, shape, manner or form should you be interposed in a
production jobstream, or essential to its' maintenance.

Let's agree to disagree here.  Emphatically.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70v72r$hmj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<70ss03$b9r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <70tun3$g48$1@news.igs.net> <3632934F.965333A3@mindspring.com> <70u8rv$n6b$1@news.igs.net> <70ui1s$2qt@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hugh Candlin wrote in message <70ui1s$2qt@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>...
>With steam coming out my ears::

Since you do not have the code in front of you, and both Skidmike
and I do, perhaps I should outline the entire thing so that the steam
can settle.  What I proposed to Mike was designed to alleviate the
exact things you are talking about, in spite of the fact I used "I".  That
was simply because I was pointing out sloppy-ness in *my* own code.

The keystroke trap routine in the code Mike has calls a subroutine
called "getkeys" that waits for a keystroke, and returns it as a four
digit number.  The actual numbers returned are heritage code, but
are set using the file COBOL.KBD.  A fragment is below.

[COBOL.KBD]
;CRT_STATUS(KEY1='1')
ESC=11027
F01=12059
F02=12060
F03=12061
<SNIP>
ALT+ESC=01090
ALT+F01=12104
<SNIP>
;CRT_STATUS(KEY1='2')
LEFTARROW=02001
RIGHTARROW=02002
UPARROW=02003
DOWNARROW=02004
HOME=12071
TAB=02006
END=12079
BACKSPACE=02008
DELETE=12083
INSERT=12082
PAGEUP=12072
PAGEDN=12081

The code, after calling the subroutine, has inline statements as follows:

    if keycode is equal to 2059
        perform help-routine
    else
    if keycode is equal to 2060
       perform ...
etc.

Those numbers are directly related to the values in the COBOL.KBD
file.  I am saying that it should not be written that way.  I had to choose
the actual numbers in the KBD file to return those specific values
because the code is heritage and I did not want to have to change
it.  I made up the keyboard file to match the code. Instead. it should
be written as follows.

In a file that is copied into the program, there should be a table
that looks like:

      * note, values correspond to COBOL.KBD values
        01 KEYSTROKES-IN.
            02 F1-KEY         PICTURE 9(4) VALUE 2059.
            02 F2-KEY         PICTURE 9(4) VALUE 2060.
            02 F3-KEY         PICTURE 9(4) VALUE 2061.

       or
      * note values correspond to COBOL.KBD values
        01 MY-KEY-IN            PICTURE 9(4).
            88 F1-KEY-READ      VALUE 2059.
            88 F2-KEY-READ      VALUE 2060.
            88 F2-KEY-READ      VALUE 2061.

The code should be written as:

    if keycode is equal to F1-KEY
        perform help-routine
    else
    if keycode is equal to F2-KEY
       perform ...
etc.
 or
   IF F1-KEY-READ
        perform help-routine
   else
   if F2-KEY-READ
etc.

Which of the two is a stylistic difference that is rather trivial, though
I am sure there will be comment both ways.  The crucial fact is the
second table replacing the in-line literals.

Now.  If the code is written that way, you are NOT forced to go
in and change code to introduce new keystrokes. The problem
becomes one of adding the new keystroke to both tables.

All the information is in two files that are external to all code. One
no those is a run time data file, and the other is a copy file
that is copied to all programs as they are compiled.  Adapting the
routines to a new keyboard layout, or to a new operating system
is entirely captured in two files and one subroutine.

If the original code had been written that way, I would not have had
to write the current subroutine to return values like "2059" for the
F1 key.  I could have chosen new values that were more easily
remembered, and set up the COBOL.KBD file in a more intuitively
remembered manner, then set up a matching table.  No code would
have changed.

However, since thousands of lines of code currently contain literals
I do not have that option without causing myself a great deal of work
scanning all that code and updating it.  So I am stuck with the current
values for keystrokes.  If I want to adapt to a completely different
keyboard, I have to make it mimic the current one.

Skidmike, however, is in an entirely different position.  I just gave him
that example out of my library.  He is now going to use it in
brand-spanking-new-code, never touched by human hands before he
writes it.  I am suggesting to him that before he starts, he add that
simple table mechanism, and use it throughout, thereby avoiding the
problem that I now have, left over from sometime back in the mid 70's.

I will stand by more suggestion.  It does make it easier to maintain,
and not just for me.  For anybody, forever.

Perhaps you could elucidate your objections based on the programming
practices, rather than on the fact that I used the first person singular
in my post ...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record keys as group items: complete

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298297.15559.15260@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<70tun3$g48$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<70tun3$g48$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> 
> Certainly you do NOT have to initialize the entire key.  However you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> want an exact match, then you need the entire key.

I think your message may be more confusing than clarifying.
There is no such thing as "read with key greater than",
you are probably using this as a shortcut for:

           MOVE "5"     TO High-Order
           MOVE LOW-VALUE  TO Low-Order
           START file
               KEY > file-key
           READ file
               NEXT RECORD

To clarify: To read an exact record by primary key (RECORD KEY)
the key must be set exactly and then a random READ (ie ACCESS
MODE RANDOM OR DYNAMIC) will get the record that matches the
key specifiied.

If it is required to read by partial match then the ACCESS
MODE must be DYNAMIC or SEQUENTIAL, the partial key set in
the record area and a START done to set that point in the
file.  A READ NEXT RECORD will then read whatever follows
in the file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Record keys as group items: completely filled?

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0004379d@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<70ss03$b9r$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Dear Buyuk,

B> I am MOVEing in data into Patient-Code of DB2-Key1 and Test-Code of
B> DB2-Key2, then READing the ORGANIZATION INDEXED file, but get a return
B> error of key not found.
B> FD  DB2-File LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
B> 01  DB2-Patient-Record.
B>     05 DB2-Key1.
B>         10 Patient-Code       PIC 9(07).
B>         10 Order-Number       PIC 9(02).
B>     05 DB2-Key2.
B>         10 Test-Code          PIC 9(04).
B>         10 Visit-Date.
B>             15 DB2-VD-Year    PIC 9(02).
B>             15 DB2-VD-Month   PIC 9(02).
B>             15 DB2-VD-Day     PIC 9(02).
B>     05 DB2-Status             PIC 9.

It would have been easier if you included the file's SELECT statement and  
said, what you want to do exactly.

I assume DB2-Key1 is the primary key, DB-Key2 the alternate one. If so,  
you have to fill one of the keys completely before doing a random read.

If you have the Patient-Code and want to read all records related to that  
patient, you can set Order-Number to ZEROES and fill Patient-Code. Then

START DB2-File KEY IS NOT < DB2-Key1
   INVALID {EOF-Handling}.

A READ DB2-File NEXT RECORD will then retrieve the first record for that  
patient (or of the following, if the patient is not in the file).

BTW: You should consider making your files Y2K-compliant first (DB2-VD- 
Year).

Best regards

Sven
```

### OT: (sort-of) Selecting a server

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70tg5d$59o@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<006501bdfd33$1f570d00$0300000a@atomsmasher.netin.com>`

```
I thought I might start an interesting "flame war" by passing on this post
from the IBM-MAIN listserver to my COBOL friends.

"Hi all,

this is not really an MVS question, but somebody might give me a hint.

If i compare OS/390, various UNIX (like the SUN E 10000) and Windows NT
Enterprise Edition (on big SMP machines)  for doing server type of
operation, what are the BIG pro's and con's of OS/390 ? What comes to
mind ?

Any ideas are welcome - please don't shoot me for the question; but i
need some high level answers to this from a big group of mainframers.

Ciao, B. Kunrath (calling from the wild south of germany)

Chris Craddock wrote in message
<006501bdfd33$1f570d00$0300000a@atomsmasher.netin.com>...
>This is a good question, but the answers will be mystifying to proponents
of
>Unix and/or NT for two reasons;
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>However, a big OS/390 box could be a 10 way IBM 9672, or a 12 way Amdahl,
or an
> 8 way HDS Skyline. Any of those puppies is (ballpark) 1000 mips, but then
you
> could couple up to 32 of them in a data sharing parallel sysplex without
> changing a line of application code.
>
>So, in comparison with SMP systems from other vendors, the main advantages
of
> OS/390 as a server platform are...
>
>1. Scalability. You can run exactly the same applications (and system
> software!!) on anything from a tiny 4 mips P/390 to a monster parallel
sysplex
> with roughly 30 thousand mips. In many cases you can add capacity without
> disrupting service.
>
>2. Reliability. In common usage it just doesn't break - at least not in the
ways
> that you are familiar with. There's no such thing as a kernel panic or the
> "blue screen of death".
>
>3. Security. Unlike any other platform. There are no security holes. There
are
> no "superusers". There are no viruses. Period.
>
>4. Availability. You can leave it up indefinitely. You don't have to take
it
> down to do housekeeping functions such as data backups. You never have to
take
> it down at all unless you are upgrading the operating system itself and
even
> then you can do it in a sysplex without disrupting service.
>
>5. Serviceability. The most robust diagnostics and corrective service
mechanisms
> on the planet. You can selectively apply program maintenance. The hardware
has
> its own builtin diagnostics and automatically calls the service center
> when its feeling ill. Often the first time you will know you have a
problem
>is when the CE walks in the door and tells you about it.
>
>6. Manageability. Much of the system's operation is completely automatic
and
>there are rich tools from IBM and other vendors to cover the areas that are
>not already covered by the operating system.
>
>7. Software. This is the platform that has the best database and
transaction
>processing software. In addition, it supports all of the communication
>protocols, it includes Unix functionality, Java, Web servers etc. Nothing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>their promotional literature. They ALWAYS compare themselves with
"MAINFRAME
>CLASS" capabilities. These days, the prices are so competitive, the
>reasonable question to ask is "Why not buy the real thing?".
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

#### ↳ Re: ATTN:>donald tees

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

SkidMike wrote in message <36324841.E2657356@mindspring.com>...

>explain the phrase CONSOLE IS CRT

Well, to tell the truth, I do not have a clue what it does in Fujitsu.  That
bit of code was converted from DOS, different compiler, and that is
a leftover.  The norm is that display is directed to the screen for the
screen section, but statements like "display test" go to the console,
which is a totally different window.  It might just put everything onto
the console, and it might be a no-op.  Once I get the other 50,000 lines
of code on my plate converted, maybe I will have time to play and find out.

That program made a good test, and I wanted to use it, so once it worked
I just put it on my desktop and left it.  I sent it because is is small and
stand alone so it makes a good example.  It is probably also a good
example of use of the copy.  Those date routines are in use in dozens
of programs, and the program you have is the test routine. It should
probably be converted to a subroutine for Fujitsu code under Windows.

>and how come i still can't get MORE than 80x24.  i followed what you
>did, but still have the same size.
>can i use only certain values for row and column?  do they have to be a
>certain value when multiplied?

The docs only say that the total screen area cannot be more than 16K
characters.  I am converting DOS code, so have not tested screens
larger than 80*25, but have used that with no problem.  It may have to
do with your screen resolution. I am using a 19" screen cranked up pretty
high, and can get 4  80*24 screens on it. I'll test to-morrow on a different
screen/screen card, and get back to you.
```

#### ↳ Re: ATTN:>donald tees

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70tvj7$go6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36324841.E2657356@mindspring.com>`

```

SkidMike wrote in message <36324841.E2657356@mindspring.com>...
>thanks for the code you sent.  i have the .kbd file printed and taped to


As a PS to that last message.  That particular .KBD file was set up
to duplicate the scan codes picked up in assembler for a Microsoft
Cobol program running under DOS.  I did not want to change the programs
so I figured out how to get Fujitsu to give me the same numeric results
for the same keystrokes.

While the methodology is neat, personally I would choose different values
if I was going to set up a new system from scratch.  Probably the best
way to do it is to set up a new KBD file that is easy to remember, then
the values as a set of 88 level names that you copy from a file.  That will
keep your code logic separate from the keyboard keys that trigger it, and
make it easy to change if you ever have to.  If that does not make sense,
say so and I will type in an example.
```

## Thread: Win95 API NetExpress — 11 msgs

### Win95 API NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981025182957.29669.00001337@ng73.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I am trying to accomplish the following with Win95 API's.  In the Windows
Explorer I can select a folder that is out on my network and then click and
select "Map as a Network Drive"  Then I can tell the computer that I would like
to refer to this as the "F:" drive.  This allow me to go to a DOS prompt and
type in F: and return and I am accessing that folder as the "F:" drive.

I would like to be able to accompish this with Win95 API's in the background so
the user can not see what is going on.  I have looked in the Win95 SDK
documentation and have found the WNetAddConnection2 API.  I believe that this
is the correct API.  Can anyone confirm this ?  Also, I am still a novice at
converting these "C" data type to COBOL.  Any advice on the lpNetResource
element that is listed ?  It refers to a pointer to a structure.  Also the
dwFlags refers to a set of bit flags.  Is this just a working storage item
defined as DWORD ?

As allways I am very grateful for any help that can be offered.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

at
>converting these "C" data type to COBOL.  Any advice on the lpNetResource
>element that is listed ?

Bob, to pass a pointer to a structure you define the structure as normal eg

01 NetResource.
    03 dwScope         DWORD.
    ...

then pass the item BY REFERENCE when making the API call. Note that BY
REFERENCE is the default anyway.

>It refers to a pointer to a structure.  Also the
>dwFlags refers to a set of bit flags.  Is this just a working storage item
>defined as DWORD ?

Yes, dwFlags is defined as DWORD. You then simply set the appropriate bit by
moving the value to the data item.

>
>As allways I am very grateful for any help that can be offered.
>
>Thanks,
>Bob Hennessey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Win95 API NetExpress

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36349daa.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<711gsa$446@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <19981026105613.27402.00001448@ng150.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 wrote in message <19981026105613.27402.00001448@ng150.aol.com>...
>Thanks for the reply.  I have made the entries that you suggest, but i am
>running into a different problem when i issue either one of the following
API
>calls:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Since both deal with names you need to specify is the name
is ASCII or Unicode (16-bit).  This is done by adding either
A or W to the API name, e.g. "WNetAddConnectionA".
The documentation tends to not mention that because many
IDEs add the A or W themselves, but with Cobol YOU have
to do it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Win95 API NetExpress

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** dlmax@netcom.com (David L Maxwell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dlmaxF1J3Bx.6pH@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36349daa.0@news1.ibm.net> <19981026154055.18133.00001534@ng105.aol.com>`

```
In article <19981026154055.18133.00001534@ng105.aol.com>, bob7536@aol.com 
says...
>
>Hi Leif,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Hennessey

Bob,

Here is a quick a dirty sample application using NetExpress and
the win32api "WNetAddConnection".  My version of NetExpress did
not contain a LIB file containing WNetAddConnection, but my copy
of VC 5.0 did so it was used instead.

To compile:
        cbllink netcon.cbl wsock32.lib mpr.lib

I hope this helps.

Dave Maxwell


*----------------------------------------------------------------
      $set ans85 case
       identification division.
           program-id. netcon.

       environment division.

       configuration section.

       special-names.
           call-convention 74 is win32api.

      * -------------------------------------------------------------- *
       working-storage section.
       78  ERROR-ACCESS-DENIED             value 5.
       78  ERROR-BAD-DEV-TYPE              value 66.
       78  ERROR-BAD-NET-NAME              value 67.
       78  ERROR-ALREADY-ASSIGNED          value 85.
       78  ERROR-INVALID-PASSWORD          value 86.
       78  ERROR-BAD-DEVICE                value 1200.
       78  ERROR-DEVICE-ALREADY-REMEMBERED value 1202.
       78  ERROR-NO-NET-OR-BAD-PATH        value 1203.
       78  ERROR-CANNOT-OPEN-PROFILE       value 1205.
       78  ERROR-BAD-PROFILE               value 1206.
       78  ERROR-EXTENDED-ERROR            value 1208.
       78  ERROR-NO-NETWORK                value 1222.
      *
       01  WS-Return-Code                  pic x(4) comp-5.
       01  ErrMsg                          pic x(30).
      *
       01  Connection-Stuff.
           03  lpRemoteName     pic x(20) value
                                 "\\bigcat\windows 95" & x"00".
           03  lpPassword       pic x(01) value x"00".
           03  lpLocalName      pic x(03) value "r:" & x"00".

      * -------------------------------------------------------------- *
       procedure division.
       MyMain section.
      *
      * -- Clear screen
      *
           display "COBOL NetAddConnection Application " at 0101
                 with blank screen
                 background-color 0 foreground-color 7
      *
      * -- test the WNetAddConnection win32api
      *
           call win32api "WNetAddConnectionA" using
                   by reference lpRemoteName
                   by reference lpPassword
                   by reference lpLocalName
                   returning WS-Return-Code
           end-call

           if WS-Return-Code <> zero
              perform Get-API-Error
              display "WNetAddConnection Error: " at 0201
              display ErrMsg
              stop run
            else
              display "WNetAddConnection was Successful " at 0201
              stop run
            end-if
            .

      * -------------------------------------------------------------- *
       Get-API-Error SECTION.
           call win32api "WSAGetLastError"
                   returning WS-Return-Code
           end-call
           perform Get-Error-Msg
           .

      * -------------------------------------------------------------- *
       Get-Error-Msg SECTION.
           evaluate TRUE
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-ACCESS-DENIED
                  move "Access Denied"                   to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-BAD-DEV-TYPE
                  move "Bad Device Type"                 to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-BAD-NET-NAME
                  move "Bad Net Name"                    to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-ALREADY-ASSIGNED
                  move "Already Assigned"                to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-INVALID-PASSWORD
                  move "Invalid Password"                to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-BAD-DEVICE
                  move "Bad Device"                      to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-DEVICE-ALREADY-REMEMBERED
                  move "Device already Remembered"       to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-NO-NET-OR-BAD-PATH
                  move "No Net or Bad Path"              to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-CANNOT-OPEN-PROFILE
                  move "Cannot Open Profile"             to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-BAD-PROFILE
                  move "Bad Profile"                     to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-EXTENDED-ERROR
                  move "Extended Error"                  to ErrMsg
             when WS-Return-Code = ERROR-NO-NETWORK
                  move "No Network"                      to ErrMsg
           end-evaluate
           .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Win95 API NetExpress

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<712rqv$pe0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<711gsa$446@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <19981026105613.27402.00001448@ng150.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 wrote in message <19981026105613.27402.00001448@ng150.aol.com>...

>I get the following error message:
>
>Error 173 Called program file not found in drive / directory.
>


I am not a net express user, but I notice that no one else has been
able to help so maybe a couple of suggestions ...

A 173 error is a dynamic load error, I think you need to force
those to static links.  I am not sure how to do that with a given
name, the norm is that an underscore as first character forces
it.  But try it with a litlink option and see if you get a linker error.

The other thing that I have found with Cobol and API's is that
Cobol is not case sensitive, whereas the API names are. With
Fujitsu you also need a special compiler directive to force the
names to retain the case, otherwise it converts them to capitals
and you cannot find the API.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)

```
Hi,

Just wanted to thank everyone for all the help that they have been on this
question.

If anyone would like a copy of the API copy books ( working storage and
procedure division ) that i am building for API calls just give me an e-mail
and i will send them to you.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey

<a id="t-be8c70731a"></a>
## Thread: final thought for tonight — 8 msgs

### final thought for tonight

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3633D41B.454FE991@mindspring.com>`

```
thought that as programmers, you guys may be interested in this.  in my
shop, we have an "on-line" parts catalog to look up exhaust parts.  what
do we use?  the old-fashioned book.  at the warehouse i call for brake
parts, what does the manager use?  same thing.  i have a complete set of
raybestos and wagner catalogs to find part numbers.  the computers (how
much money spent on on-line catalogs?) are relegated to looking up
prices, invoicing, and bookkeeping.  i was taught this method by those
who have been in the business for 30+ years.  they and i can locate the
correct part in the book in almost the same amount of time it takes
anyone else to find it in the computer's on-line catalog.  i'm not
trying to insult anyone, but in the automotive business all the on-line
parts catalogs i've ever seen totally suck.  and i'm sure that this
phenomena is not unique to the auto repair industry.  maybe y2k will not
be as catastrophic as some say.
have a good night, y'all.  see you tomorrow.
```

```

>shop, we have an "on-line" parts catalog to look up exhaust parts.  what


Unfortunately, a lot of stuff is being converted to CD not because
it is better, but because it is cheaper to produce and distribute.
```

- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn739s9g.i4.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`

```
On Sun, 25 Oct 1998 23:30:57 -0500, Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>skidmike wrote in message <3633D41B.454FE991@mindspring.com>...
>>thought that as programmers, you guys may be interested in this.  in my
>>shop, we have an "on-line" parts catalog to look up exhaust parts.  what

>Unfortunately, a lot of stuff is being converted to CD not because
>it is better, but because it is cheaper to produce and distribute.

It's a shame, too, because this would be a really easy way for one vendor to
set themselves apart from the market. It's *really* bad when you find yourself
wishing that a company had provided an SQL interface to the database behind a
catalog or website because it would be easier to manually enter SQL queries
than use the provided "interface".
```

#### ↳ Re: final thought for tonight

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981026070048.11057.00001374@ng-fd2.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3633D41B.454FE991@mindspring.com>`

```
> thought that as programmers, you guys may be interested in this.  in my
> shop, we have an "on-line" parts catalog to look up exhaust parts.  what
> do we use?  the old-fashioned book. 

We are wholesale distributors of commercial lawn maintenance equipment.

Ninety percent of our illustrated parts lists have been put on CD by a
third-party vendor in Wisconsin.  The software that displays the pages
(quite clearly, I might add) also includes the ability to print pick lists,
cross-referenced parts, and other inventory functions.  It also has hooks
to tie in with various retail point-of-sale systems available to the retail
dealer.  

These catalogs on CD are quite useful at both the wholesale and retail
level.  While paper manuals are still in widespread use, even our 67 year
old service manager still goes to the computer first to look up a part. 

Perhaps the difference being the number of parts catalogs we have to
deal with.  Literally dozens and dozens.  Just the Kohler IPLs take up
6 feet of shelf space.

Regards,
JC Jones.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: final thought for tonight

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363515F7.277B24D3@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<3633D41B.454FE991@mindspring.com> <19981026070048.11057.00001374@ng-fd2.aol.com>`

```
illustrated parts lists?  sounds like an idea i've had floating around.
the Bosal exhaust parts catalog we use has excellent representations of
all of their exhaust systems (never mind that the book shows them as if
looking from above, i know them better from below but still a very good
representation nonetheless).  if the suppliers (mainly maremont) would
license me to, i would consider spending the time scanning each page
into the pc.  then have each part number be "clickable" to add to an
estimate or invoice.  but considering that i work 10 hours a day before
i get to this computer, most of the cars in the 1983-present volume
would be classics by the time the project was done.  still, may be a
good suggestion for any programmers reading this.
```

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn739s4g.i4.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<3633D41B.454FE991@mindspring.com>`

```
On Sun, 25 Oct 1998 20:44:59 -0500, skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com> wrote:
>shop, we have an "on-line" parts catalog to look up exhaust parts.  what
>do we use?  the old-fashioned book.  at the warehouse i call for brake
[...]
>trying to insult anyone, but in the automotive business all the on-line
>parts catalogs i've ever seen totally suck.  and i'm sure that this

This is actually a fairly common complaint. Entirely too many vendors create a
catalog on CD or the web because it's trendy or cheaper to produce and never
end up consulting the people who will actually be using it. There's also the
problem that many programs were designed for attractive first impressions
instead of being efficient for heavy usage. I seem to recall this being
discussed back when we were beating the "GUI vs CUI" horse to death awhile
back.
```

## Thread: need a cobol compiler — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### need a cobol compiler

- **From:** Ellis Cipriano <zeerookool@rocketmail.com>

<a id="t-9db41866a0"></a>
## Thread: RM-COBOL 85 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### RM-COBOL 85

- **From:** "Steve Jewkes" <jewkes@iconz.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70tfcn$pml$1@news.iconz.co.nz>`

```
I have a small but vexing problem.

In learning Cobol through university I have a problem with
the supplied text not compiling with the supplied compiler.

Some I have managed to rectify myself but the statement
WITH BLANK SCREEN will not compile.

BLANK being a reserved word will also not compile and
none of my texts are able to help.

Of interest the statement WITH REVERSE-VIDEO also
did not compile but REVERSE did.

Both WITH BLANK SCREEN and WITH REVERSE-VIDEO
were used in the supplied sample coding.

My tutor was unable to assist in this matter saying that it
was not an examinable detail.

Thanks in advance.

Steve Jewkes.
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-COBOL 85

- **From:** "Ron McGregor" <R.Mcgregor@uq.net.au>
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bdffc1$5a422fe0$0fe365cb@ron>`
- **References:** `<70tfcn$pml$1@news.iconz.co.nz>`

```
I have also used RM-COBOL-85, but my text shows the syntax for BLANK SCREEN
without the WITH statement. This is used in the SCREEN SECTION as opposed
to the WORKING STORAGE SECTION.
Similarly, my text shows a syntax of REVERSE as opposed to WITH
REVERSE-VIDEO.
In case it matters, my text is Modern Cobol Programming 2nd edn by Wilson
Price & Jack Olson.
```

MicroFocus Cobol and some with Visual Basic.  In the few hours I've been
exploring PowerCobol I've gotten excited about the possibilities.

<a id="t-e418012733"></a>
## Thread: Getting the server time from Relia COBOL — 1 msgs

### Getting the server time from Relia COBOL

- **From:** sstocker@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71042i$66m$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Is anyone out the a Realia COBOL MLI guru?

I have an interface which requires host system updates to be date/time
stamped. In order to eliminate variances in the workstation clocks I want to
have my Realia COBOL 4.29 application get the time from the NetWare 3.12
servers that host the system.

I have established that I need to do the following

1) populate the AX, CX, & DX registers as follows
MOVE 7                       TO DX.
MOVE X'14F2'                 TO AX.
MOVE 0                       TO CX.

2) Generate int 21

3) Check the AX register for error and get the results from a seven byte
server reply packet

I have ran various tests and have been able to successfully generate
interrupts using the MLI_INT function. The problem is when I generate an
interrupt that requires a reply packet to be populated, I always GPF. I have
tried various combinations of defining the server packet then using SET
ADDRESS and using DOS_ALLOC to try to allocate a specifc pointer. I have
spent a lot of time trying to get this to work but I have not had much luck.

If anyone has any code examples from Realia 4.29 that set a pointer for a
reply packet, or a way to get the time from a NetWare server without shelling
to DOS I would greatly appreciate it.




-----== Posted via Deja News, The Leader in Internet Discussion ==-----
http://www.dejanews.com/rg_mkgrp.xp   Create Your Own Free Member Forum
```

The ETK toolset includes a screen painter that you use to define a map.
A runtime module (SCRNIO) then generates the 3270 datastream on the
fly. No BMS map is needed. From the map, SCRNIO can also generate
HTML instead of the 3270 datastream. Go to http://www.etk.com for more.
```

<a id="t-f1f7f79945"></a>
## Thread: Passing an open file handle — 11 msgs

### Passing an open file handle

- **From:** GregL <gregl@csi.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
Hi,

I have a MFCobol program that prints a report.  I need to be able to
call a sub-program to print additional information on some occasions. 
When I try to open the printer in the sub-program I get a 'file already
opened' message.  If I close the printer in the main program before
calling the sub-program, the printer ejects the page and the sub-program
prints on a new page.

So I'm thinking I need to be able to pass a handle (or something) of the
open printer in the main program and let the sub-program use it for
printing.  I can put the file name in the call 'name' using fd-printer,
but I can't figure out how to define the file in the linkage section of
the sub-program.

Has anybody done this?  Is there a better way to skin this cat?

Greg L.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** skidmike <skidmike@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3635181D.599B22AA@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
imho, go with the flow.  if your main pgm opens the print file, and your
error message says "already open", skip the open and close statements in
the sub-program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36351a90.0@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com> <3635181D.599B22AA@mindspring.com>`

```
skidmike wrote in message <3635181D.599B22AA@mindspring.com>...
>imho, go with the flow.  if your main pgm opens the print file, and your
>error message says "already open", skip the open and close statements in
>the sub-program.

Mike, it is also important to know why.

Programming is in danger to degenerate to the
point where people say: "I tried this and that,
and it seems to work...". Maybe we are already
past that point? (sigh).

As Thane advised: make the file EXTERNAL
in both programs and you know why only one
open is required.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ojgqMGm8YPpG@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
On Mon, 26 Oct 1998 17:26:48, GregL <gregl@csi.com> wrote:

> Hi,
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Leave the select and FD in both programs.

Don't close the file in the calling program before making the call and
don't open it , or close it, in the called program.  In the FD for 
BOTH programs add:

IS EXTERNAL

That'll fix you up.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3634bd33.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
Hi

I am not sure that this will work, but it sounds the same as sharing files
with the Fujitsu compiler.

When you define the file in the FD section, if I remember correctly, you
define it as SHARE (or SHARED).  Then define everything the same in the
subprogram. The subprogram READ/WRITE will now work irrespective of whether
the verbs are in the MAIN or SUB program - but only issue OPEN/CLOSE in MAIN
program. This is standard ansi '85 syntax (I think), so check your manual.

Please tell me if it works.

Simon Cordingley
www.casegen.co.uk
simonc@casegen.co.uk

GregL wrote in message <3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Greg L.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36367158.69C@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
GregL wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the sub-program.

Others have quite properly suggested making the file EXTERNAL.  There
is also a useful alternative: confine all the IO to a single subprogram
which can be called from whatever module needs to access the file.
Instead of coding WRITE statements, code CALL statements, passing
the records to be written.  You also need a separate entry point, or
a separate action code of some kind, to trigger a CLOSE; the
subprogram has no other way of knowing when you're finished.

This approach has several advantages:


1. It works in older dialects that do not support EXTERNAL.

2. The FD is confined to a single place.  If you need to change the
FD you can change it in a single place.  If your flat file turns
into an indexed file, or a database, or a network socket, you can
confine the changes to a single place.

3. It enables you to encapsulate error handling and any tricky or
tedious processing that you'd rather not code over and over.  For 
example, if you're writing to a log file, the subprogram can add a
timestamp and/or sequence number to each record.


Circumstances alter cases.  In the case originally cited, where the 
program was simply writing a report, a separate access module like this 
would probably be overkill.  In other cases an access module makes
better sense, especially when item 3 (above) applies.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7160e8$se6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com> <36367158.69C@swbell.net>`

```
Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <36367158.69C@swbell.net>...

>Others have quite properly suggested making the file EXTERNAL.  There
>is also a useful alternative: confine all the IO to a single subprogram
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>subprogram has no other way of knowing when you're finished.


Michael has given you excellent advice in this post.  He mentioned
several advantages to this basic approach but there are even more
than he mentioned.

By getting all your file I/O into one place, it makes it simple to do
things like retrofit file locking if you ever decide to execute it on
a network.

If you also add file function codes as one of the arguments
of the subroutine call, then you are well on the way to creating
a fully object oriented file object that can be dropped into source
code at one level, and integrated into the program logic at
another.  IE: you now have an object with methods and data.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298299.14096.2263@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
In message <<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>> GregL <gregl@csi.com> writes:
> 
> So I'm thinking I need to be able to pass a handle (or something) of the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Has anybody done this?  Is there a better way to skin this cat?

What you require is the EXTERNAL clause on the FD (look it
up).

This should be specified on both the main and the sub program
FDs and it makes the two specifications into one file so
that either program can operate it.  Open and close it in
the main program only, the sub-program can then just
write to it without any further requirements.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** GregL <gregl@csi.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36363493.C6D30BC2@csi.com>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
Thank you all.  Making the file EXTERNAL was the thing to do.  Works
just like you said it would...

Greg L.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-kdQe2LPoud4f@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com> <36363493.C6D30BC2@csi.com>`

```
On Tue, 27 Oct 1998 21:01:07, GregL <gregl@csi.com> wrote:

> Thank you all.  Making the file EXTERNAL was the thing to do.  Works
> just like you said it would...
> 
> Greg L.


You are welcome, and thank you for posting the results - it's not 
often we find out if we ultimately helped or not.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing an open file handle

- **From:** dcantua@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<717e5d$dm9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>`

```
In article <3634B0D8.D18C25B7@csi.com>,
  GregL <gregl@csi.com> wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just pass a flag letting the sub-program know that the file is still open
and do not reopen or close the file in the sub-program.  The flag will let the
sub-program know if the file is still open or not.

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 1998/04/22

                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Wednesday, April 22, 1998
(For  details of what technical items have changed so far, please
see Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x.)
This page accessed ___ times

Version: 2.09
Additional  information  and corrections are  encouraged.  Please
send comments to
     wmklein@ix.netcom.com

CONTENTS
     NOTE: As this FAQ is still under "reconstruction," there may
     be items out of their logical order (for example incorrectly
     alphabetized.  Similarly, there may  still  be  missing  and
     inserted "odd" section numbers. All of these items  will  be
     fixed eventually, but not necessarily soon.
 1. Copying this FAQ
2. Where can I get this FAQ?

3.1 for DOS, Windows or OS/2?
3.2 for Windows/NT or Windows/95?
3.3 for UNIX?
3.4 for Linux?
3.5 for the Macintosh?
3.6 for other environments?
4. Is there a free COBOL compiler?
4.1 for DOS, Windows or OS/2?
4.2 for Windows/NT or Windows/95?
4.3 for UNIX?
4.4 for the Macintosh?

6. Where can I contact...

and what is in it?
