and in English once.  Nothing is really off topic in some ways.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Standards??? Since when? Ha.

- **From:** "Charlie Ebert" <kd5ob@theshop.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iK%_1.182$CM.2086@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<FvoZ1.220$Ak.79@news1.atlantic.net> <715oqi$nrj$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I don't know how we got Morality into it, but I dont' think we've
had a set of Cobol Standards observed for at least a decade.
And that's a dangerous thing...

Donald Tees wrote in message <715oqi$nrj$1@news.igs.net>...
>Rick Smith wrote in message ...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>existence.  Laws are standards, after all.

I'll give you a for instance.  In regards to OOP, I don't believe it
has been standardized in C yet.  So why would we be thinking about
standardizing it in COBOL?

>
>It is funny.  I have been accused of lack of morals twice in this
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


>I said the same thing back in both cases, but said it in Cobol once
>and in English once.  Nothing is really off topic in some ways.
>


And nothing is really off topic or dis-allowed in COBOL either.
If one just reviews the 7 or 8 different types of PC cobol systems
available, then one looks back at the various mainframe cobol
systems which have been offered over the last 35 years,
one can see there is a STANDARD amongst all that time which
is observed.

But when they started putting statements which are proprietary to
the OS you are using such as "PC_SET_DRIVE" or perhaps
the multitude of variations they have incorporated for seeking
a Julian Date or how about how the launching of programs in
new windows with a GUI, there is damn little standard or going
to be standard in that.

And because of this, the whole standards issue is thrown in the
toilet.

And I think that's a shame.

I remember about a year ago starting a large debate on the
interserv news group about what is a julian date.
There were 37 responses to my new question, in E-mails,
there were about 12 posted, and out of all of these only
2 seemed to agree on what it was.

In order to adopt a standard you have to presume there is to
be a NON-moving target to put a fence up around which we
can validate will not change.

OOP is still evolving.  I don't know if you know this or not but
there are 3 types of OOP out there right now.

And virtually everybody has their own answer to a GUI.

Rather than put our faith in standards, PC compiler manufacturers
are using MF compatibility as the issue as the consideration
of which compiler to use has gone beyond the old STANDARDS.

And what of people like me who use no OOP and use no GUI;
who are engaged in converting Unisys V COBOL code to a
server???  Well, we can have another STANDARDS discussion
there.

In the Unisys world you can have things like a 9(1200) comp.
Their HEX representation of their number is like this
@01FFFC@ and not X"01FFFC".  Where is the standard in that?

Or how about blocking factors, sectoring, and all that old time
file I-O stuff used on mainframes and not used on PC's.

Even 25 years ago, when standards were rigidly enforced,
you still couldn't sucessfully take a cobol package from
and IBM system and port it to another mainframe without
some re-coding.

You can't take something written in MF and move it to Fujitsu without
some re-coding.

Here's another one for you to laugh at.
In the Unisys digits math world, a 9(5) comp value 12345 looks
in hex like this @12345@.  They don't do bytes.

The MF equivalent of this is 9(5) comp-6 value 12345 which looks
like this @012345@ as PC's work only in bytes.  Henceforth you
end up rounding all your numbers up one digit to a 9(6) for example.

And let's not forget that wonderful set of phrases,
Entering Symbolic here.

Entering Cobol here.


I think standards are important when it comes to upward compatibiltiy
with a COBOL vendor.  I think it would be even grander if they all
used an adopted standard for everything encompassing a PC.
But they do not.

They do not for several reasons.
   #1.  PC hardware is changing the scope of what you can do almost daily.
   #2.  Major software manufacturers I.E. Microsoft are changing their day
to day
          business coding philosophy with every version; they are changing
old
         commands and at the same time adding more commands to do different
         things which eventually spill out into COBOL source code.
   #3. Competing COBOL manufacturers have a tendency to put special things
         in their compilables which make it hard to port COBOL code to other
machines thus
         forcing a major re-write.

So therefore,
I have no objections to them adding OOP as a part of the standard.
Why not.

Charlie Ebert
```

---

<a id="t-22dba01f76"></a>
## Thread: S: Tools to compare generated sources — 14 this month / 18 total

### S: Tools to compare generated sources

- **From:** Burkhard Perkens-Golomb <perkens@sdm.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rvq1znue0zc.fsf@gadenste.muc.sdm.de>`

```

We're using generators to produce cobol source code and are now in a 
process of switching from an old to a new generator.

Because the generators will work very different internally it will be
nearly impossible to produce literally the same cobol code with the
two generators. That's why we need methods or even better tools to compare 
the cobol sources produced by the old and new generator and to prove that
the generated code is not literally but functionally equivalent.

Any good tips and hints how to do it?

Thanks in advance!

        Burkhard
```

#### ↳ Re: S: Tools to compare generated sources

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3636828F.57111D75@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<rvq1znue0zc.fsf@gadenste.muc.sdm.de>`

```
Burkhard Perkens-Golomb wrote:
> 
> We're using generators to produce cobol source code and are now in a
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Burkhard

We will have such a tool soon. We already have all the components.

What you need to do is to parse both programs and then
compare their Program Trees. You can parse programs using 
CobolTransformer and then tree comparison is what remains
to be written.

So you have two choices here:
1. License CobolTransformer and write tree comparison routine.
2. Contract us to write such a utility.

Besr regards,
Vadim Maslov

http://www.siber.com/sct/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: S: Tools to compare generated sources

- **From:** Burkhard Perkens-Golomb <perkens@sdm.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rvqiuh56rdc.fsf@gadenste.muc.sdm.de>`
- **References:** `<rvq1znue0zc.fsf@gadenste.muc.sdm.de> <3635f16e.8381368@news.enter.net>`

```
We're programming for a big car company a large batch
system running on OS/390 V1R3. Some time ago the people
of this company have written a generator to expand some
common so called "macros". When our project started our 
customer (this big car company) has forced us to use their
generator.

But now we've convinced them that this generator is
terrible (it *is* terrible) and to rewrite some of *our*
in other projects existing generators for them.

Our Generators are running on Linux (later on NT) and
generate from a "higher level" language with type
concept, subroutines etc. (the old generator has nothing
like this) Cobol code. This Cobol code is sent to the mainframe 
for compilation.

Because our higher level language is quite different from
the old macro language used before we can't generate 
literally the same code. That's why we need a tool to
compare the generated code and to prove that the sources
are functionally equivalent. The tools must compare a lot
of modules so they have to run in batch modus (platform is
not important).

Some things that will be different in generated sources:
whitespace in sources, order of sections, order of
variables declarations, partly level of variables. The
hierarchy of variables must be preserved of course.

Does it help to understand the problem?

        Burkhard



rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe) writes:

> Burkhard Perkens-Golomb <perkens@sdm.de> wrote:
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: S: Tools to compare generated sources

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7181q3$6h2@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Burkhard,
   There is still something that confuses me about your posts.  What is it
that you REALLY care about?

A) Do you want to verify that the COBOL source code is the same (excluding
data names, white space etc)?

B) Do you want to verify that the output of the program logic is the same
(doesn't matter about how the program is structured or even what it does) -
as long as the application FUNCTIONS the same?

C) Do you want to verify that the machine object code (generated code) is
identical - or at least very similar?

It would seem to me that "B" is what you should be trying to verify,
however, depending on your contract, I could imagine that "A" is what you
are after.  If you try for "C" - I doubt very strongly that this is going to
be possible in any cost-effective way.

Burkhard Perkens-Golomb wrote in message ...
>We're programming for a big car company a large batch
>system running on OS/390 V1R3. Some time ago the people
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >two generators. That's why we need methods or even better tools to
compare
>> >the cobol sources produced by the old and new generator and to prove
that
>> >the generated code is not literally but functionally equivalent.
>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>www.sdm.de
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: S: Tools to compare generated sources

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00

```
What you describe will be almost impossible to do.
A better way to proceed would be to convince the
customer that the "high-level" description is an accurate
reflection of what he wants at the *functional level*.
The rationale for using the generator is presumably that
the high-level description is easier to understand than
the Cobol (which is a bit questionable in itself), so it
should be possible to convince the customer of the
functional "correctness" of the code.
-----------------------

#### ↳ Re: S: Tools to compare generated sources

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <JeffreyFarkas@~nospam~earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<717m02$3i4$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<rvq1znue0zc.fsf@gadenste.muc.sdm.de>`

```


Burkhard Perkens-Golomb wrote:

> We're using generators to produce cobol source code and are now in a
> process of switching from an old to a new generator.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Burkhard

Hi Burkhard,

    While I do not have any tool that will help you out with your none to
small
problem I did work with a tool that is similar to what you are describing.

   The product was IEF and it is a PC based tool that would take
pseudo code "macro" instructions and generate either PL/I or Cobol
source code to be used on a mainframe environment. Most of the time
we used this tool the way new versions were tested was by taking the
PC code transfer it to the Mainframe and re-generate the source,
compile, link and bind the programs. At this point we would test the
load modules and determine if they were performing the same
function(s).

  We seldom used or compared the source from one version to
another. First because the code was nasty to use and second
because we never really cared what the source looked like we
just focused in on the results the load module created.

 I would suggest that comparing the results created by the
new load modules will be a better test then attempting to
compare the source code.

My best wishes.
```

On 27 Oct 1998 11:46:31 +0100, Burkhard Perkens-Golomb
<perkens@sdm.de> wrote:

>
>We're using generators to produce cobol source code and are now in a 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

hmmm.... what you're asking for is a product that compares the logic
of two different cobol programs and documents the differences....
whoever writes such a software package can compete with bill gates for
richest person on the planet.... <vbg>  my first thought is to rethink
why you're switching generators.... and...  if the reason is sound,
then the solution is to have a well-documented test plan. even then,
the problem you may face is, if the test fails, what do you do next?
It is this uncertainty on 'what do you do next' that causes me to feel
that we are not on firm ground in this dialogue. we need lots more
info to help you.   good luck.
david
```

reports (and files - I think) to see if they were "functionally" identical -
even if details were different (such as one using two blank lines while the
other used a carriage control character to skip 2 lines).  I don't know what
happened to such software, but if you think this would be helpful, let me
know and I can research it for you.

### US-MN-job to$50K's-Sr.AS/400 Programmer/Analyst-Manufacturing

- **From:** NYCOR <paulaf@nycor.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3635EA38.C1956AB3@nycor.com>`

```
US-MN  Hi $50K's    Sr. AS/400 Programmer/Analyst - AS400/Manufacturing

<a id="t-39cfbfa437"></a>
## Thread: Draft of the next COBOL Standard - *not* Available on-line — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Draft of the next COBOL Standard - *not* Available on-line

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7158ft$67e@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(Sent to comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol - with CC to J4 and WG4 - from Bill
Klein)

FYI -
  The draft of the next COBOL Standard which was previously available
on-line (in either ASCII or PDF format) is currently NOT available.  The
reason being - that the version that was most recently posted was CD 1.2
which was wildly out-of-date.

The J4 (ANSI-ish) and WG4 (ISO-ish) Standards groups are currently doing an
"internal quality review" of CD 1.4.  For various processing reasons, they
have decided NOT to open this up to the public and, hence, have not made an
electronic version of this draft generally available.

Their schedule for processing this review has been extended at least once
and I do not now know when they hope to have all comments processed and the
next publicly available version of the draft Standard available for viewing
(much less comments).  However, as CD 1.2 was so out-of-date and will be
even more out-of-date by the time all outstanding comments are processed, it
was felt (I think) that having no version available was better than having
this misleading one out there.

If you are interested in what the current draft looks like - or in what is
happening or when you will be able to see a more current copy, please
contact

  For J4 - Don Schricker (J4 Chair) at
      doncobol@mediaone.net
            or
  For WG4 - Ann Wallace (WG4 convenor) at
       nwallace@us.ibm.com

NOTE: If you are working with any Vendor's product that claims to "match"
the next COBOL Standard (for anything - most commonly for OO), please
remember that each and every draft of the next Standard IS a draft and is
very much a "moving target".  There are many features that have not changed
for many drafts while there are other features that seem to change with each
draft.  Just don't believe a vendor when they tell you that they KNOW what
the next draft will look like (or when the final Standard will be
official/approved).
```

<a id="t-9a2fb0495f"></a>
## Thread: Object oriented Cobol — 33 this month / 39 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>`

```
I was just reading the users guide for IBM COBOL for MVS.  It appears
that NEXT SENTENCE is replaced by CONTINUE.  No big deal.

But then I got to the section on object oriented COBOL.  Now in my
search for my masters, I have had 2 C++, 2 JAVA, and 2 theory of OO
courses.  The professors talk about "obsolete" databases such as
relational (I use IDMS).  So I am familiar with OO.

But I don't get it.  Who uses mainframe (or other) object oriented
Cobol, and why?

I am a big defender of structured cobol and hierarchical databases.  I
see problems with fully integrated environments (but like Integrated
data dictionaries).  I like the extract, sort, report means of giving
users what they want.  So I am biased.

Is Object Oriented Cobol a marketing gimmick? Why should someone use
it?  I don't think Cobol programmers can be trained to write good OO
Cobol code significantly quicker than it would take them to learn to
write good OO Java code.
```

#### ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-5CxrCdqd8Ukb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 17:52:32, Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> 
wrote:

 
> Is Object Oriented Cobol a marketing gimmick? Why should someone use
> it?  I don't think Cobol programmers can be trained to write good OO
> Cobol code significantly quicker than it would take them to learn to
> write good OO Java code.


I think it's a gimmick.  You assume there is such a thing as GOOD OO 
Code.  My exposure leads me to beleive that for handling EVENT driven 
processing OO might have a place, but the overhead, science, and 
philosophy of OO coding gets in the way of any real work being done.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GONZ1.455$Ak.114@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-5CxrCdqd8Ukb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 17:52:32, Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
>wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
IMHO there is no relationship between event-driven and OO. Consider
that VB programs can be event-driven but they can not be OO. What
is required for event-driven programming is a means to recognize the
event and to execute instructions to respond to that event.

What do you think of

    USE AFTER EVENT event-name.

as in

    DECLARATIVES.
    MouseDownEvent SECTION.
    USE AFTER EVENT Mouse-Down.
        *> Mouse-Down code
    MouseUpEvent SECTION.
    USE AFTER EVENT Mouse-Up.
        *> Mouse-Up code
    KeyPressEvent SECTION.
    USE AFTER EVENT Key-Press.
        *> Key-Press code
    TimerEvent SECTION.
    USE AFTER EVENT Timer.
        *> Timer code
    ...
    END DECLARATIVES.

If it were available, event-driven programming could be done in
COBOL whether the program used OO features or not. Of course,
the platform must be capable of providing the events.

Just a thought!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-xRHh0uSrgz7S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-5CxrCdqd8Ukb@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <GONZ1.455$Ak.114@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 23:31:52, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> 
wrote:

> IMHO there is no relationship between event-driven and OO. Consider
> that VB programs can be event-driven but they can not be OO. What
> is required for event-driven programming is a means to recognize the
> event and to execute instructions to respond to that event.
> 

Sorry, didn't mean to imply that.  It's just that the "isolation" 
provided by OO design has a good fit with event driven programming.  
Events being isolated items that can be handled as objects.  This 
doesn't mean, as you point out, that you have to use OO to do event 
driven programming.  Events don't have to be user interface events - 
they can be other processing events.

As far as the rest of your  post goes, it was very interesting.  Not 
sure what I really think of it right now.  Declaratives have always 
been a pain, IMHO -  so I don't use them much, but they do have their 
place and using them for event driven programming is really a good 
fit.
```

#### ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com (Chris Adams)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn73ersu.i4.cadams@cadams-ntw.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 10:52:32 -0700, Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com> wrote:
>Is Object Oriented Cobol a marketing gimmick? Why should someone use
>it?  I don't think Cobol programmers can be trained to write good OO
>Cobol code significantly quicker than it would take them to learn to
>write good OO Java code.

One thing to remember is that OO Cobol allows you to do a partial conversion to
an existing system. Use of Java would require a far more extensive rewrite.

I think it's perceived that OO COBOL is easier to retrain and there is (or was
before the Y2k scare) a perception that Java programmers are in shorter supply.
```

#### ↳ NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE (was: Re: Object oriented Cobol)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joKZ1.448$Ak.227@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>...
>I was just reading the users guide for IBM COBOL for MVS.  It appears
>that NEXT SENTENCE is replaced by CONTINUE.  No big deal.
>
This is not quite true. CONTINUE behaves like a no-op.
NEXT SENTENCE like a go to where the destination is the statement
or action following the next period.

Notice, in the following code, how NEXT SENTENCE may be used
with a period to implement "exit perform" and "exit paragraph".
CONTINUE cannot be used in this manner.

------------------
       program-id. nextsent.
       data division.
       01 x pic s9(4).
       01 char pic x.
       procedure division.
           display "start"
           perform varying x from 1 by 1 until x > 100
               if x > 50
                   next sentence   *> same result as exit perform
               end-if
           end-perform
           .
           display "end next sentence " x
           perform varying x from 1 by 1 until x > 100
               if x > 50
                   continue    *> does not exit
               end-if
           end-perform
           .
           display "end continue " x
           perform paragraph-test-next
           display "end paragrpah next sentence " x
           perform paragraph-test-continue
           display "end paragraph continue " x
           accept char
           stop run
           .

       paragraph-test-next.
           move 1 to x
           if x < 50
               move 50 to x
               next sentence   *> same result as exit paragraph
           end-if
           move 100 to x
           .

       paragraph-test-continue.
           move 1 to x
           if x < 50
               move 50 to x
               continue    *> not the same as exit paragraph
           end-if
           move 100 to x
           .
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE (was: Re: Object oriented Cobol)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3637753C.F6AFE524@home.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <joKZ1.448$Ak.227@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or action following the next period.

Thank-you.  I probably wouldn't have discovered that for some time.  Old
code without imbedded if statements and with sentences terminated with
periods wouldn't have showed up the difference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE (was: Re: Object oriented Cobol)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C8LZ1.452$Ak.29@news1.atlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <joKZ1.448$Ak.227@news1.atlantic.net> <3637753C.F6AFE524@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3637753C.F6AFE524@home.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>periods wouldn't have showed up the difference.

I should have included a warning that the code presented was for
demonstration purposes only and that the use of the technique
presented would likely create a serious maintanence problem.
This is because the location of the period is paramount. For example,
in the "exit paragraph" example, adding any code with periods before
the end of the paragraph would cause the NEXT SENTENCE to go to
that period, rather than the period at the end of the paragraph.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

```

Rick Smith wrote in message ...
>
The previously posted code should have included a warning that
the code presented was for demonstration purposes only and that
the use of the technique presented would likely create a serious
maintanence problem. This is because the location of the period
is paramount. For example, in the "exit paragraph" example,
adding any code with periods before the end of the paragraph
would cause the NEXT SENTENCE to go to that period, rather
than the period at the end of the paragraph.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE (was: Re: Obje

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298301.66730.5811@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<joKZ1.448$Ak.227@news1.atlantic.net>`

```
In message <<joKZ1.448$Ak.227@news1.atlantic.net>> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> writes:
> >
> This is not quite true. CONTINUE behaves like a no-op.

Exactly, this is often misunderstood and deserves emphasizing.

> NEXT SENTENCE like a go to where the destination is the statement
> or action following the next period.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>            .

Actually in COBOL, NEXT SENTENCE cannot be used this way.
NEXT SENTENCE is part of the IF statement and the standard
specifies:

     "If the END-IF phrase is specified, the NEXT SENTENCE
     phrase must not be specified."

Without the END-IF a "." would be required to terminate the
IF and thus the construct would be uncodable.

Microfocus has an extension where NEXT SENTENCE is a stand-alone
statement and not just a phrase of the IF statement.  I have
no idea what the next standard will have, but if you are 
using extensions then you should stick to a meaningful one
such as MF's EXIT PERFORM which _will_ become standard.

The other problem with using NEXT SENTENCE in this way
is that the behaviour will chenge if the full stop is
removed.  This has all the disadvantages of a GO TO 
without any of the advantages.  At least with a GO TO the
destination is a label (paragraph name) and this can be
searched for to see if it is being used.  With this
misuse of NEXT SENTENCE there is nothing to signal that
the "." is actually being used as the target of a GO TO
disguised as NEXT SENTENCE.

In this respect the 85 standard is correct in bannishing
its use in this way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE (was: Re: Obje

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71akum$i8@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<joKZ1.448$Ak.227@news1.atlantic.net> <3298301.66730.5811@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

..
>
>Actually in COBOL, NEXT SENTENCE cannot be used this way.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


A couple of bits of information:

A) The next Standard (as currently drafted) STILL prohibits a NEXT SENTENCE
before an END-IF (or END-SEARCH)

B) Micro Focus has an extension that allows NEXT SENTENCE as a "verb" so it
can be used within other verbs besides IF and SEARCH

C) The extension to allow a NEXT SENTENCE before an END-IF (or END-SEARCH)
is really quite common (and is much more common today than the EXIT
PARAGRAPH/SECTION) extension.  Therefore, using code (like the original
example in this thread) is reasonably portable - but is still NOT standard.

D) NEXT SENTENCE is really incredibly error prone wherever it is used and
(IMHO) should be avoided whenever possible.  Although the use of CONTINUE
cannot be automatically inserted to replace NEXT SENTENCE, I would strongly
suggest that no new code be done with NEXT SENTENCE and existing code be
examined for modification to use CONTINUE.  (I think that NEXT SENTENCE was
put in the "archaic" list in the draft Standard - but I may be wrong about
this - it was fairly controversial.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE (was: Re: Obje

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

#### ↳ NEXT SENTENCE vs CONTINUE (was : Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7180ul$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>...
>I was just reading the users guide for IBM COBOL for MVS.  It appears
>that NEXT SENTENCE is replaced by CONTINUE.  No big deal.
>


A) can you give me a documentation reference that says this?  As it is not
really true, I want to follow up on it with IBM dox people.

B) If you have an ANS'74 conforming program with NEXT SENTENCE, it is true
that you can (but need not) replace any NEXT SENTENCE with CONTINUE.
HOWEVER, the two are by no means identical when coding new ANS'85 conforming
code.

NEXT SENTENCE *always* goes to the next period.

CONTINUE *always* acts as a "no-op" which lets the program flow continue
(which may or may not go to a period)

In ANS'74 (before END-verb scope terminators and NOT exception phrases, the
only place you could go after a NEXT SENTENCE in a conditional statement was
to a period.  However, with the new structures, it is possible to CONTINUE
on and not get to the period for several statements.
```

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

I am used to placing periods after each statement.  This new CONTINUE
statement allows greater flexibility, especially within in-line
performs.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <NOSPAMbrazee@home.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363881A3.E2599DF6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com> <36379e34.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```
> I'd like to hear what "the professors" said about relational databases being
> obselete (I don't mean worse or better than any other DBMS, just how
> obsolesence has been detected/defined).  RDBMSes and SQL seem to be very
> useful to me.

Well, they come from two places - shops programming in C++ and JAVA, and
the academia.

The former find that OO databases work better with their languages,
although the JAVA class was able to teach enough (in the same amount of
time), to get into mapping OO onto relational databases.   We tend to
think the whole industry works the way we do.

The academic people read papers.  A well-developed, stable technology
doesn't produce as many papers as a new, changing, and unstable
technology.

I am making my capstone project into a defense of COBOL, flat files &
HIERARCHAL databases.  Not a popular POV in schools, but at least it is
original!!

Any good arguments and especially source documents would be wonderful!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363839e4.0@scooby.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>`

```
Firstly, with regard to OO Cobol.

We use C++ for building the Casegen product and fully adopt OO methodology,
so I am not a dynasoar! However, from a commercial viewpoint I do not
believe there is any value that OO Cobol adds over a well-structured
(preferably generated) Cobol program that is worth the cost of training and
implementing. Component-based development, I believe is different - a
component with an agreed stanfdard interface as in the case of CORBA is
worth the effort commercially.

With regard to the CONTINUE - we use this heaviliy in our generated Cobol,
as well as EXIT to avoid using full stops in generated logic. Here is an
example...

A100-OUTPUT SECTION.
    MOVE A-VARIABLE        TO ANOTHER-VARIABLE
    PERFORM D100-CURSOR

    CONTINUE.
A100-OUTPUT-EXIT.
    EXIT.

B100-INPUT SECTION.
......

The CONTINUE is used when the logic "continues" and EXIT is used when the
logic "EXITS" if you understand! This stops the compiler objecting to the
missing sentances and means never having to code a fullstop (period) unless
you build your own sections (Casegen allows you to add your own code to
sections generated by the product). This may not work on all compilers - it
is designed for IBM mainframe and MicroFocus compilers.

Of course CONTINUE and EXIT do nothing procedurally, its just that the above
sections will be performed from elsewhere.

We also use -EXIT after every section as a common exit point for the
section. We do not generate or advise using a GO TO anywhere else! Notice
also that the TO in MOVE statements is always on column 40 (FROM in col 38).

If you are interested in this coding style, I will be pleased to send you a
mini-program.

Simon Cordingley
Development Manager
Casegen Systems Ltd www.casegen.co.uk
simonc@casegen.co.uk


Howard Brazee wrote in message <363759E0.AC2AF2E5@home.com>...
>I was just reading the users guide for IBM COBOL for MVS.  It appears
>that NEXT SENTENCE is replaced by CONTINUE.  No big deal.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>write good OO Java code.
```

>Is Object Oriented Cobol a marketing gimmick? Why should someone use
>it?  I don't think Cobol programmers can be trained to write good OO
>Cobol code significantly quicker than it would take them to learn to
>write good OO Java code.
>
I agree.

And speaking of OO COBOL, I had dinner last Saturday night with a big
hitter in the CS department at East Tennessee State U.   He wanted to know
if I'd be interested in getting back into teaching COBOL again.

Seems that have two sections of COBOL this semester and had to turn
students away bacause they filled up the first 2 days of registration.  All 
this Y2K hubbub, don't you know.

Then he told me they're teaching OBJECT ORIENTED COBOL.
<Chuckle>.  That will sure be a big help when these folks start
searching for ACCEPT WS-DATE FROM DATE in 10 year old
code.  

I suspect there's still a little bit of misconception regarding the
difficulty in remediating code for Y2K.  And also some misconception
regarding the tedious, boring, incredibly huge job it's turned out to be.

JC Jones.
Tell me one more time why this is necessary......
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981029010351.24005.00000765@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19981028211535.23334.00000744@ng-fd2.aol.com>`

```

Ask a single PHM to define the phrase 'object oriented' two or more times, and
you get two or more different answers.

Ask the entity one level up from PHM (lets call that PHM++) "what does
encapsulate mean?", and it is possible you will get no answer at all.

Ask a technical person about the meaning of CONTINUE and NEXT SENTENCE and you
learn that they kind of know, sort of; they even post opinions about it,
approximately. Ask them if they have enough manuals, ask them if their managers
even think about manuals.

The Y2K problem is not about dates. It is about the fact that people do not
read. The movement away from procedure oriented management of computers is a
necessary consequence of the gradual and tragic loss of the tradition of
reading.

It is not that people can not read, it is that they will not read.

You really can't do  much about trends, accept be swept along. 

Object Oriented Code is definitely vacuous in a context devoid of object
oriented design strategies. And the horendous truth is that overwhelming
majorities of business management personnel are simply incapable of such
abstractions.

I have encountered managers who are real excited that some of the systems they
will interface with have objects inside of them. You would think they are toy
trucks from the glema in their eyes.

Most managers I have met in COBOL shops will point blank punish abstraction.

I think in truth the movement away from traditional design is not a movement
towards object oriented nor towards any other design.  It is simply a movement
away from design. Design requires reading.

Writing and reading are behaviors which clearly distinguish individuals. 
Distinctions are bad. Sameness is good.

"'Equality', I spoke the word as if a wedding vow.
Ah, but I was so much older then, 
I'm younger than that now"
-from "My Back Pages" 
 by Robert Zimmerman.




Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

Add a culture of re-use and asset management. There is no point in
designing 'objects' unless you are going to manage them so that they

It's only when you grasp that and build a big enough repository of
objects that it's payback time.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298301.69354.10878@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<19981029010351.24005.00000765@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
In message <<19981029010351.24005.00000765@ng-fd1.aol.com>> rkrayhawk@aol.com  writes:
> 
> Ask a technical person about the meaning of CONTINUE and NEXT SENTENCE and you
> learn that they kind of know, sort of; they even post opinions about it,
> approximately. Ask them if they have enough manuals, ask them if their managers
> even think about manuals.

I just posted a response on CONTINUE and NEXT SENTENCE, please
do comment on it.  You are tending to over generalise.  My
computer related bookshelf space (not counting magazines is
around 60 feet, about 8 foot is specifically Cobol (much of
which is two sets of MF manuals).

> 
> The Y2K problem is not about dates. It is about the fact that people do not
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You really can't do  much about trends, accept be swept along. 

That would only be true if people stopped writing.  Your
message has several unwarranted and unsupported generalisations
that do have a small basis of truth.  

> 
> Object Oriented Code is definitely vacuous in a context devoid of object
> oriented design strategies. And the horendous truth is that overwhelming
> majorities of business management personnel are simply incapable of such
> abstractions.

"Business Management personnel" do not have to make such
abstractions, they use Systems Analysts to do the abstractions
for them.

> 
> I have encountered managers who are real excited that some of the systems they
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Most managers I have met in COBOL shops will point blank punish abstraction.

Computer programs are abstractions.  Elements within the program
are abstractions.  You appear to be wanting to use the term
'abstraction' in a particularly abstract way.

> 
> I think in truth the movement away from traditional design is not a movement
> towards object oriented nor towards any other design.  It is simply a movement
> away from design. Design requires reading.

Certainly many appear to think that using RAD techniques
somehow is Object Orientated and thus a 'good thing' and
use this as an excuse for skipping the design.  However,
OO requires a more stringent approach to design than 
traditional methodologies.  But it is a different way of
designing.  In fact the only real benefits of OO arise
out of the heavier reliance on the design stage.

Where you may be seeing a 'move away from design' is where
prototyping is being used as part of RAD techniques.  This
may be using tools that are claimed to be OO, but it is
not itself OO.

In many ways prototyping is a useful technique where new
tools are being used.  With a screen interfcae tool, for
example, it is necessary to know all the ways that the tool
can be used, and what is difficult or impossible to do
with it _before_ the design is finalised.  Otherwise the
design may require something that cannot be implemented.

In these cases the prototype may in fact pre-empt the
design work in this area.

> 
> Writing and reading are behaviors which clearly distinguish individuals. 
> Distinctions are bad. Sameness is good.

And why do you think that ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71apiu$if0$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19981028211535.23334.00000744@ng-fd2.aol.com> <19981029010351.24005.00000765@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote in message <19981029010351.24005.00000765@ng-fd1.aol.com>...

>Object Oriented Code is definitely vacuous in a context devoid of object
>oriented design strategies. And the horendous truth is that overwhelming
>majorities of business management personnel are simply incapable of such
>abstractions.


I think that one of the real problems here is that OO is essentially a
bottom
up mechanism, whereas most Cobol code (and most business programming
for that matter) is traditionally a top-down problem.  That is why 95% of
all
OO is being used for *very* basic building blocks, like a single screen
position.  It is going to take quite some time to build to the stage that I
can drop a ledger sub-system into an accounting application.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Object oriented Cobol

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363dea6f.6156433@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19981028211535.23334.00000744@ng-fd2.aol.com> <19981029010351.24005.00000765@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
On 29 Oct 1998 06:03:51 GMT, rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:

>I think in truth the movement away from traditional design is not a movement
>towards object oriented nor towards any other design.  It is simply a movement
>away from design. Design requires reading.

here's the thing. i think design is rarely a good thing, except for
specific user applications. and the reason why is, most software has
already been written. its connecting that is more important these
days. connecting and customizing.

I don't think i have ever written a line of OO code. I have written
function calls, and seperated code into modules, but as for writing
specific *atomic* objects, nope. The reason is, as an experienced
programmer, I have always known that there is no such thing as an
object which is totally seperate from the rest of the machine.

You abstract the objects as much as possible when coding, but after a
certain point, you realize the person has to have some idea of the
architecture they are working with. Even Java has native methods.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

## Thread: help — 17 this month / 31 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help

- **From:** Kelly Wakeley <kwake@cnetech.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com>`

```
If anyone can answer these please let me know.
              1.  If you move a I to a subscript, what will it contain?
              2.  If you set an index to 1, what will it contain?
              3.  A binary search is more efficient and saves time by?
              4.  What causes a SOC7 abend code?
              5.   What is used to relate files within the program to
JCL file descriptions?

I am trying to learn COBOL on my own and have come across these
questions that I can't seem to find the answers to.  They are probably
quite simple but i have yet to understand them.  Please feel free to
e-mail any suggestions.  Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: help

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363b8caf.4344531@news-server>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 21:25:23 -0800, Kelly Wakeley <kwake@cnetech.com>
enlightened us:

>If anyone can answer these please let me know.
>              1.  If you move a I to a subscript, what will it contain?
                                       1
>              2.  If you set an index to 1, what will it contain?
                                       1
>              3.  A binary search is more efficient and saves time by?
                     It divides the array in half and either goes up
or down depending on the comparison.  It is efficient for very large
table/array searches where you might program the search to start at
the first entry and keep looking at the next until you get a match or
hit end of table.  If ,eg, your table contained 500,000 entries this
could take quite a lot of time.  Using a binary search you would find
the match or end much faster.  The caveat here is that the table is in
some logical order either ascending or descending.

>              4.  What causes a SOC7 abend code?
                      This is an MVS error and says you have a data
exception.  The primary cause of such an error, at least in the
MVS/VSE IBM mainframe world, is trying to perform arithemetic on a
field that does not contain numeric data, e.g., it contain alpha
characters or spaces or hex zeroes or other "garbage".  Note the data
must also be in packed format.


>              5.   What is used to relate files within the program to
>JCL file descriptions?
                      Select statements and FD entries.


>
>I am trying to learn COBOL on my own and have come across these
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Good luck.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
Cats know how we feel. They don't give a damn, but they know.
 Steve
```

In message <<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>> redsky@ibm.net  writes:
> 
> > On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 21:25:23 -0800, Kelly Wakeley <kwake@cnetech.com>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Answer 2 is incorrect.

Actually answer 2 may be correct or incorrect or irrelevant
depending on far too many thing to identify here, but
including:
       Compiler vendor
       machine environment
       compiler options settings
       compiler version
       
It may be incorrect for your particular combination of
these, but may be correct for other's.  

A compiler that uses INDEXes as a numeric type as if they
were subscripts will still conform exactly to the standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ An ALTER Horror Story (was: Re: help)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71ab6q$hbo$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com> <363b8caf.4344531@news-server> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-ySDdqFQYB6bn@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <3638A020.9F819A9A@home.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-edbemwWXTMvd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
The worst COBOL program I ever saw was a cross-reference-and-flowchart
utility for COBOL source programs written by a programmer who was clearly
fluent in FORTRAN, and who had equally clearly decided to make maximum use
of COBOL's ALTER verb presumably because using it was intellectually
stimulating.

Sometime after it was written, the flowcharting part was disabled and
largely ripped out, but the nature of the program was such that significant
pieces of this logic remained in odd corners of the program.

I actually managed to patch the program to correct a problem I encountered
with it, but nobody else could figure out well enough how the program worked
to permit the patch to be peer-reviewed.  Ultimately the problem was
circumvented by documenting it ...

    -Chuck Stevens

Thane Hubbell wrote :

>This Week's COBOL Tip:
>Alter is the most powerful Verb in COBOL, and if ever use it
>you may find your employment status altered.
```

#### ↳ Re: help

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1998-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36395847.781398E1@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3637FC43.EF006A87@cnetech.com>`

```
Kelly Wakeley wrote:
> 
> If anyone can answer these please let me know.
>               1.  If you move a I to a subscript, what will it contain?

Strictly speaking, you cannot move an I to a subscript, you must SET the
subscript to I. The contents will be the same, but expressed
differently. (I'm assuming you didn't intend this as a trick
question<g>)

HTH,
Bill Lynch
```

In message <<36395847.781398E1@att.net>> William Lynch <wblynch@att.net> writes:
> > 
> > If anyone can answer these please let me know.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> question<g>)

You are confused.  A subscript is simply a numeric item and
is only a subscript when it is used to access an array.
It is INDEXes which require the SET.
```

## Thread: Detecting when a file is in use by others — 8 msgs

### Detecting when a file is in use by others

- **From:** dblaze@merchants-fla.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>`

```
Is there an easy way to detect when a file is use by others and/or
tell how many users are attached to the file?  I've got an application
with a shared ISAM file that I'd like to be closed before I process
the records in it.  The app is written in MF Cobol 4.0.32.

Thanks in advance
Doug
```

#### ↳ Re: Detecting when a file is in use by others

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7180uj$sj5@sjx-ixn4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>`

```

dblaze@merchants-fla.com wrote in message
<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>...
>Is there an easy way to detect when a file is use by others and/or
>tell how many users are attached to the file?  I've got an application
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Doug

It is relatively easy to detect if it is in use by others, but I don't know
how to tell HOW many others are using it.  (There may be a way - I just
don't know any)

To detect whether the file is in use already, check out the "file
sharing/record locking" documentation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Detecting when a file is in use by others

- **From:** gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363976c8.2102583@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 16:48:47 GMT, dblaze@merchants-fla.com wrote:

>Is there an easy way to detect when a file is use by others and/or
>tell how many users are attached to the file?  I've got an application
>with a shared ISAM file that I'd like to be closed before I process
>the records in it.  The app is written in MF Cobol 4.0.32.

easy? you could have a program which intercepts open calls to the ISAM
handler, and gives them a hold error message, and a program which
during every *close* to the file, attempts an *exclusive* lock on the
file.

to do this, you would need a pointer to the function which opens and
closes the ISAM file. that, and the parameters taken.
you would also have the problem of whether the open function is
currently in use AND if so, how a bad call to it would be handled (a
stack based program counter, or a failed return which crashes the
entire machine?)

These are the most possible Points of Failure I can see, from a
computer scientists point of view.

the simplest option, however, is to constantly attempt an exclusive
lock on the ENTIRE ISAM file. sure, polling will take up alot of CPU
cycles, but most CPU cycles are not used anyways. 

on the other hand, you may not have to lock the entire ISAM file. just
lock 1 part at a time.

gvwmoore@ix.spam.netcom.com to reply remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: Detecting when a file is in use by others

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71897i$jbl$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>`

```

dblaze@merchants-fla.com wrote in message
<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>...
>Is there an easy way to detect when a file is use by others and/or
>tell how many users are attached to the file?  I've got an application


If it is in use is easy.  Open it and look at the file status.  How many are
using it is quite another thing.  You should be able to find the information
you need in the section on file-locking.
```

#### ↳ Re: Detecting when a file is in use by other

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3298301.65168.4840@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>`

```
In message <<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>> dblaze@merchants-fla.com writes:
> tell how many users are attached to the file?  I've got an application
> with a shared ISAM file that I'd like to be closed before I process
> the records in it.  The app is written in MF Cobol 4.0.32.

You can specify LOCK MODE EXCLUSIVE in the SELECT and then
when you try to OPEN it you will get a File-Locked status
(9A from memory).  Once you have sucessfully opened it others
will get that status when they try to access it.

If you need to signal other programs that they need to close
it you will have to be able to change them to check for a
flag that you set somewhere.  They would need to periodically
look for this.

Alternately it could be done by record locks.  Have a control
record in the file, with a key of, say, low-values, and only
allow updating of the file when a successful KEPT LOCK has
been obtained on this record.  This will allow readers of
the file to continue but updaters can be excluded (as long
as they all follow the rule).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Detecting when a file is in use by other

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71am1m$aom$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com> <3298301.65168.4840@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3298301.65168.4840@kcbbs.gen.nz>...

An interesting idea.  In fact, if you wanted to use a form
of user id as a key, you could even find out who was using
the file.

>Alternately it could be done by record locks.  Have a control
>record in the file, with a key of, say, low-values, and only
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>as they all follow the rule).
```

#### ↳ Re: Detecting when a file is in use by others

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0004b20a@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<3637484b.445042126@news.mci2000.com>`

```

d> Is there an easy way to detect when a file is use by others and/or
d> tell how many users are attached to the file?  I've got an application
d> with a shared ISAM file that I'd like to be closed before I process
d> the records in it.  The app is written in MF Cobol 4.0.32.

You can try to OPEN the file EXCLUSIVE and check the file-status whether  
you were successful or not. It is not possible to tell how many users are  
attached.

Best regards
```

---

<a id="t-afd44ea847"></a>
## Thread: Windows 95 VS NT 4.0 API Call Results — 4 msgs

### Windows 95 VS NT 4.0 API Call Results

- **From:** dblaze@merchants-fla.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36394c8b.446130341@news.mci2000.com>`

```
I have an application written in MF 4.0.032 Cobol that uses API calls
to access a COMM port to communicate with a modem.  I developed it on
NT 4.0 and tried to move it to a Windows 95 machine.  It works fine on
NT but on two of the API calls, I get back a difference result.  The
two calls in question are :  GetCommState and SetCommState.  In NT I
get back +1 while in 95 I get back +0.  Is this correct?  Does 95
return +0 as TRUE where NT returns +1 when making the API calls?

Thanks in advance
Doug
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows 95 VS NT 4.0 API Call Results

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<717nod$91l@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36394c8b.446130341@news.mci2000.com>`

```
dblaze@merchants-fla.com wrote:
>I have an application written in MF 4.0.032 Cobol that uses API calls
>to access a COMM port to communicate with a modem.  I developed it on
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>return +0 as TRUE where NT returns +1 when making the API calls?

Hi Doug,
According to the documentation for these APIs, a return value of 0 
indicates that the function failed, and you should call GetLastError to 
get extended error information. The docs don't indicate any difference 
between Windows 95 and NT.

Steve.
Micro Focus Development.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows 95 VS NT 4.0 API Call Results

- **From:** dblaze@merchants-fla.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<364389b9.73326728@news.mci2000.com>`
- **References:** `<36394c8b.446130341@news.mci2000.com> <717nod$91l@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Thanks for the information Steve.  It turned out to be my problem and
I feel really stupid because of it!  I didn't have a modem on the port
at the time I was testing.  I figured since the port was actively
defined on the system and was valid, up until I tried to send out an
AT command it should return good status values - stupid me!  I hooked
up a modem during further test and getting my InstallShield script to
run and it reported back with the +1 like NT did.

Doug



>Hi Doug,
>According to the documentation for these APIs, a return value of 0 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Micro Focus Development.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows 95 VS NT 4.0 API Call Results

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3298301.65435.5002@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<36394c8b.446130341@news.mci2000.com>`

```

> NT 4.0 and tried to move it to a Windows 95 machine.  It works fine on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

c-isam file.  The documentation says the filetype is 1 and can only be used
with the 16bit compiler or unix.

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

#### ↳ Re: Compile MF COBOL using c-isam?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71aj4f$km3@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<717oor$vu3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

glenn.thames@unisys.com wrote in message
<717oor$vu3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>How do I compile a COBOL pgm using Micro Focus Workbench/2000 to update a
>c-isam file.  The documentation says the filetype is 1 and can only be used
>with the 16bit compiler or unix.
>

Here is the reply that I received from my "usually reliable sources",

I think the documentation must be wrong if it says that.  It should just
work, even without the need for the idxformat(1) directive - when it opens
the file, it works out the type from header information and updates
accordingly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Compile MF COBOL using c-isam?

- **From:** "Sven Brueggemann" <Sven.Brueggemann@brem.ftn.de>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0004b20b@ftn.de>`
- **References:** `<717oor$vu3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

gt> How do I compile a COBOL pgm using Micro Focus Workbench/2000 to
gt> update a c-isam file.  The documentation says the filetype is 1 and
gt> can only be used with the 16bit compiler or unix.

Now - that's new to me. What is the exact version number of your compiler?  
I use c-isam files with 3.x and 4.0.x without any problems.

If WB/2000 really no longer supports c-isam, you will have to convert your  
file using rebuild (REBUILD infile,outfile /s:c-isam /t:mf).

Best regards
```

## Thread: How well do you know COBOL? Find out at new site! — 4 msgs

### How well do you know COBOL? Find out at new site!

- **From:** Michael Littman <mikelittman@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36378B17.DF4B84E6@erols.com>`

```
Dear COBOL programmer,

We are currently beta testing our web-based 'COBOL II' knowledge test.
This test has incorporated feedback form those of you who took the test
last month in it's original beta release.  The format is multiple
choice.  To take the test, simply go to www.123apply.com and select the
link,
"Take a beta test".  The rest should be self-explanatory.  Before taking
the test, check out
the 'top ten' list to see the best scores to date!

Upon completion of the test, you will be scored 1-5 (1-Novice,
2-Beginner, 3-Intermediate, 4-Advanced and 5-Master).  The test is
actually comprised of two tests of 16 questions each (that's two chances
to make the top ten!).  Any feedback you can provide us on the quality
of the questions and the test itself would be greatly appreciated.  You
will be given the opportunity to provide feedback on each question as it
is administered as well as at the end of the test.

The test is delivered in our production environment, so things might
seem a little 'strange'
prior to taking the test.  However, any information you provide will be
kept anonymous
(unless you make the top ten - we figured the bragging rights would make
it worth it!).

If you have any problems getting to the site, or have any questions in
general, please
feel free to contact me.  We hope that you enjoy the test and look
forward to your feedback!

Good luck!

Mike
```

#### ↳ Re: How well do you know COBOL? Find out at new site!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<718f4m$qlh@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36378B17.DF4B84E6@erols.com>`

```
The following was sent to the author of the test,

"You have the same compiler error in multiple questions. You canNOT use an END-PERFORM statement following an out-of-line PERFORM, e.g.
Perform abc until ....

End-Perform

is illegal. You either need to have

Perform until ...

inline code ...

End-Perform

or you need to have

Perform abc until ...

(with no end-perform)"

    Michael Littman wrote in message <36378B17.DF4B84E6@erols.com>...
    Dear COBOL programmer, 
    We are currently beta testing our web-based 'COBOL II' knowledge test.  This test has incorporated feedback form those of you who took the test last month in it's original beta release.  The format is multiple choice.  To take the test, simply go to www.123apply.com and select the link,

## Thread: Switch from Micro Focus to RM COBOL? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Switch from Micro Focus to RM COBOL?

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363711D6.DB82DA27@bdssoftware.com>`

```
I'm contemplating switching from Micro Focus to RM/COBOL.  After 15 or
so years I know where I stand with MF.  I don't have any serious
complaints against MicroFocus.  Rather I'm looking to do work with
production line devices and it appears RM is easier to work with in this
area without resorting to C.   Do any of you have experiences with RM?
My only experiences are on a Radio Shack TRS12 (8" floppy, 64MB RAM) and
an old NCR minicomputer I converted to Unix and MF COBOL about 8 years
ago.

Thanks

Scott
```

#### ↳ Re: Switch from Micro Focus to RM COBOL?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qLYOrLr1JI2j@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<363711D6.DB82DA27@bdssoftware.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 12:45:10, Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com> 
wrote:

> I'm contemplating switching from Micro Focus to RM/COBOL.  After 15 or
> so years I know where I stand with MF.  I don't have any serious
> complaints against MicroFocus.  Rather I'm looking to do work with
> production line devices and it appears RM is easier to work with in this
> area without resorting to C.  


Educate me - what is a production line device and what is the host 
where the COBOL application runs and what is the interface to the 
device?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Switch from Micro Focus to RM COBOL?

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36373D64.7D9AAAFF@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<363711D6.DB82DA27@bdssoftware.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qLYOrLr1JI2j@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
I meant scales, thermometers, tickers (counters), etc.  The host is Unix
(SCO), and the interface is a serial port.

> Educate me - what is a production line device and what is the host
> where the COBOL application runs and what is the interface to the
> device?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Switch from Micro Focus to RM COBOL?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-4yMdNmdzEvoo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<363711D6.DB82DA27@bdssoftware.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qLYOrLr1JI2j@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36373D64.7D9AAAFF@bdssoftware.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 1998 15:51:00, Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com> 
wrote:

> I meant scales, thermometers, tickers (counters), etc.  The host is Unix
> (SCO), and the interface is a serial port.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Thanks!

The way I have handled this problem in the past is to interface with 
an ASM routine, or in your case, a routine written in C, that is then 
called from the COBOL program.  Would that help you more than changing
compiler vendors.  While I am a strong advocate to the idea that you 
should be able to easily change vendors, I know that any such change 
is less than trivial.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Switch from Micro Focus to RM COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott Ramey <scottr@bdssoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36374CA5.6EAD1291@bdssoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<363711D6.DB82DA27@bdssoftware.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qLYOrLr1JI2j@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36373D64.7D9AAAFF@bdssoftware.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-4yMdNmdzEvoo@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
The more I look around the more I think doing a callable C program will be the
solution.  For example, AccuCOBOL works more or less the same as Micro Focus
and doesn't support intrinsic functions (which I happen to like).  RM/COBOL
works more easily with non controlling tty devices but doesn't support free
format coding (which I consider mandatory - I've had it with margin "A" and
"B" and after 30 years of COBOL am not going back to a format dictated by 80
column punched cards) and also doesn't support intrinsic functions.  Fujitsu
COBOL may or may not easily work with serial devices, I dunno, but it appears
they don't have a SCO Unix version.  And then there's Relia, now CA, and with
the reputation Computer Associates has I won't touch that one.

So, in the end, there's Micro Focus, kinda like my wife, still looking pretty
good after all these years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Switch from Micro Focus to RM COBOL?

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@infonet.com.py>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363848F4.78DD36C@infonet.com.py>`
- **References:** `<363711D6.DB82DA27@bdssoftware.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-qLYOrLr1JI2j@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36373D64.7D9AAAFF@bdssoftware.com>`

```


Scott Ramey wrote:

> I meant scales, thermometers, tickers (counters), etc.  The host is Unix
> (SCO), and the interface is a serial port.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > device?

RM/COBOL on SCO Unix has no problem reading serial ports using normal OPEN
READ CLOSE statements (at least until version 5.3) There may be some
problems with stty settings (baud, parity, etc.) but you can set those with
a SYSTEM call.

Regard
Albert Ratzlaff
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<36370E3A.118BF91E@pobox.com>`

```
Though I have no recommendation, I too am interested. The only
reference I found to a book with COBOL and Client/Server was
at Amazon.

Creating Client/Server Applications With Cobol (Bk/Disk)
by Julianne M. Edmunds Our Price: $40.00

Paperback Bk&Disk edition (January 1999)
Prentice Hall Press; ISBN: 0133973573

<
http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/0133973573/qid%3D909665906/002-570086
0-6927224 >

Synopsis
Many businesses are moving from large mainframe computers using
COBOL applications to client/server platforms. Using a hands-on,
tutorial approach, this book enables COBOL programmers to create
new client/server applications or redevelop existing legacy code for
the client/server environment.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Computer Consultants International has contract vacancies in Australia, USA
and UK.

This coming Friday through Monday our representatives will be in
Johannesburg and Capetown to interview IT people who would like to look at
new opportunities elsewhere in the world. We will fly you to your contract,
provide initial accommodation, look after visas and refer you to good
taxation advice.

intentions and resume to Jobfair@cca-cci.net

<a id="t-2cdb7bc008"></a>
## Thread: ISPF Training — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### ISPF Training

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19981028102115.16069.00000503@ng127.aol.com>`

```
Just wanted to indicate the availability of a new ISPF course: Advanced ISPF (2
days).

Originally I had a 1.5 day course for ISPF V4 differences, but management
didn't seem interested in paying for this (attitude: they can pick it up on
their own; might be true, but a class is often a faster way to see all the
options and learn what works best for you). So I took the 1.5 day class and
boosted it up to include topics that people who learned ISPF a long time ago
might not know even though they've been around quite a while, along with a
short introduction to edit macros and the ISRDDN facility.

The class is intended for use by programmers, not end users or dialog
developers.

Most the folks I've met on newsgroups don't really need this class, in my
observation, because they tend to work at keeping current; but you might know
of people in your organization who could use such training, and perhaps you
could point them to the web site below, which contains a course description and
outline.

Thanks,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-3961bcdda9"></a>
## Thread: An Event That Could Change Your Life — 1 msgs

### An Event That Could Change Your Life

- **From:** resume-cv@element-limited.com
- **Date:** 1998-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u$w1IJpA#GA.324@nih2naac.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```


An Event That Could Change Your Life

I would like to invite you to Visit. As a professional working in the Cobol field  
Visit (Vacancies in Systems and Information Technology) is a great  
opportunity for you. It is an exhibition where the best companies meet the  
best people face to face, with a view to encouraging you to join them.  Since  
Visit opened in 1994, over 1,000 people have moved to new jobs at the event.

Companies Attending Include:
EC Soft, Admiral, Sema Group, Cap Gemini, BACS, Synamic, Nokia, Crew,  
Friends Provident, Microsoft, Fulcrum, Abbey national,  Cedar Group, PMS  
Creative, Sainsburys, Royal Bank of Scotland, ICL, Sequent, CSC

Why Should You Attend
Quite simply this is your chance to take control of your career.  You get to  
speak face to face with the people you could be working with, and you  
choose who you will speak to and when.  You can find out for yourself  
whether these companies have something to offer you.

Jobs On Offer
The huge number of vacancies at Visit means there will almost certainly be a  
job you will be interested in no matter what your skills and experience.   
Virtually every job at every level in Systems, IT and Networking is on offer.   
These are located throughout the UK and across Europe and the world.   
There are over 1,000 jobs on offer in total.  

How To Get There
By rail, take the underground from main London terminus to Piccadilly  
Circus.  The Caf� Royal is 50 yards away in Regent Street.  By car follows  
signs to London, then West End, then Piccadilly Circus.  There is NCP  
parking in Denman Street.

What To Bring
If possible, bring copies of your CV, be dressed as if attending an interview,  
and be ready to explain what your main skills and strengths are, and also to  
discuss what is important to you in changing jobs.

The exhibition is open between 12 and 8pm on Monday 16th and Wednesday  
17th November.  You do not need to attend on both days.

If you have any further queries, please contact Kelly Baker, event  
co-ordinator, on 0171 413 4079.
```

<a id="t-6d9353ad81"></a>
## Thread: Visual AGE for COBOL and LINAGE clause — 6 msgs

### Visual AGE for COBOL and LINAGE clause

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1998-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<363ac9ca.17412107@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Hello Everyone

I am playing around with IBM's Visual Age for COBOL, and while I do believe that it is one
of the finest compilers available, sometimes it drive my crazy....

On the mainframe I know that I can use LINAGE clause to get eassy pagination in a report.
Trying the same thing in VAC (Windows) gives me a lot of headaches:

Either I specifify ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL, then the syntax is accepted, but the
resiulting file is not readily availablem as it does not CRLF record delimiters, and also
contains an STL file header (standard languge file sytem header)

If, on the other hand, I specify ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL, then I get CRLF record
delimiters, but I can neither specify the LINAGE clause, nor can I use EOP, AFTER|BEFORE
ADVANCING, and other formating clauses on the WRITE verb.

Most probably, I am overlookig something obvious, but IBMs documentation (while being
rather voluminous) is not a very helpful.

Any help would be appreciated
 
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<363ac9ca.17412107@news3.ibm.net>`

```

I am not familiar with this specific compiler, but
          ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
          ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
may be what you want.

>Either I specifify ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL, then the syntax is accepted,
but the
>resiulting file is not readily availablem as it does not CRLF record
delimiters, and also
>contains an STL file header (standard languge file sytem header)
>
>If, on the other hand, I specify ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL, then I
get CRLF record
>delimiters, but I can neither specify the LINAGE clause, nor can I use EOP,
AFTER|BEFORE
>ADVANCING, and other formating clauses on the WRITE verb.
>
>Most probably, I am overlookig something obvious, but IBMs documentation
(while being
>rather voluminous) is not a very helpful.

You need to use the recording mode clause. 'F' is fixed, 'V' is
variable shoulg get you through that one. 

Use recording mode is F 
to process SEQUENTIAL files.

-Paul

```

Volker Bandke wrote in message <363ac9ca.17412107@news3.ibm.net>...
>Hello Everyone
>
>I am playing around with IBM's Visual Age for COBOL, and while I do believe
that it is one
>of the finest compilers available, sometimes it drive my crazy....
>
>On the mainframe I know that I can use LINAGE clause to get eassy
pagination in a report.
>Trying the same thing in VAC (Windows) gives me a lot of headaches:
>
>Either I specifify ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL, then the syntax is accepted,
but the
>resiulting file is not readily availablem as it does not CRLF record
delimiters, and also
>contains an STL file header (standard languge file sytem header)
>
>If, on the other hand, I specify ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL, then I
get CRLF record
>delimiters, but I can neither specify the LINAGE clause, nor can I use EOP,
AFTER|BEFORE
>ADVANCING, and other formating clauses on the WRITE verb.
>
>Most probably, I am overlookig something obvious, but IBMs documentation
(while being
>rather voluminous) is not a very helpful.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>(BSP GmbH)

The reply that I received from my "usually reliable sources" was,

"The restrictions discussed in the attached note are largely correct.
i.e:
    You can't specify: LINAGE clause, BEFORE ADVANCING, AT EOP.
     You can specify WRITE ... AFTER ADVANCING though  (the note  implies
other wise).
These are documented around page 110-111 in the current LRM.

As for what the options are:
     1) use a LINE SEQUENTIAL file but handle page formatting
           via application logic using WRITE AFTER ADVANCING statements
        or
     2) write the report to a SEQUENTIAL file (with the LINAGE clause, WRITE
..... AT EOP, etc)
          and then copy the sequential file to a line sequential file.

The second choice may be better for this particular customer."
```
